question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I’m a 20-year-old white male of average looks (surprisingly, mine are not mentioned here and I’m inclined to believe they meant mine were average, but not average to the movie world, which would make sense), I’m not sure. I was born in San Francisco, CA, and lived there until I was 11, then I moved to Connecticut. I didn’t really have any friends there, and in CA I did have friends, so I think it was important to move back to CA to be near my friends. The reason why I’m so important is because I’ve had a dream, since I was a teenager, of being in Hollywood. For some reason I have this “dream” that I want to go to Hollywood. This is something I’ve been dreaming of for many years, but I haven’t been able to afford to live in CA. I have had some opportunities and I think I’m going to be very lucky if I get this chance. I love acting and I love the acting itself. I think my friends have told me that my acting is quite good and I’m sure I’ll do well if I can get to Hollywood. In terms of the movie I’m working on now, I’m not sure about the story. I don’t really know what to expect in the movie. I’m sure it will have a happy ending. My favorite movie of all time is “Good Will Hunting”. I don’t really know why this movie is good for me, but I like it because it’s a good movie. My favorite TV show is “Friends”. I don’t really watch that much TV, but if I get a chance to watch it I will. I really like the comedy part of it. I’m not good at comedy, but it’s fun. It’s also a show that you can watch with your family and friends.

I’m really into the film and television industry. My dream is to be an actor and film director. I’m a writer too and I think my biggest dream is to write a movie script. I hope to make my first film this year. I’m really looking forward to it.

It has really been a pleasure to be able to write this letter. I’ve had a wonderful experience here. It’s amazing to have been a part of this process. I’m glad I had the chance to write to you. I hope you like my work and find it helpful.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: Well, I'm 18 years old, I was born and raised in my mother's homeland. The closest to the capital, where I grew up in a tiny village, I'm really happy that I was born and raised in a small village. My childhood was so wonderful, we spent most of the time outdoors, my mother taught me to read at a very young age, I was the first in the family who learned how to read, and I started going to school at the age of 5. My dad was the head of the local school, I also went to school in my village and my dad was my teacher, he taught me a lot of things, but the most important thing he taught me was to be myself, to be true to myself, to always follow my own path, and I have been following my own path ever since. I went to school and I became a teacher myself, and I'm so happy that I did, because I became a teacher, and I'm so proud of myself.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I'm 20 years old from a rural town outside of a larger city in Louisiana. I grew up playing baseball my whole life and still play until I left the army.

I also grew up involved in scouting. (I know thats not really the best way to say that but I didnt want to write a run on sentence). I was fully trained as a first class scout when I left at 18 and I never went back until recently. I've never had an experience that can match the feeling of doing something I've worked my butt off to get but I always had to come down off that high. Being scoutmasters son was tough because it always worked on peer pressure. I would always do things because I wanted to but never because I wanted to be popular or fit in. But in the back of my mind it was a constant reminder that I didn't want to let my dad or my team down and I couldn't go through life being exactly what others wanted me to be.

I can't tell you one of my friends from when I was young but I can tell you how to do all the knots and how to tie them well. I don't know how many times I tied the jamaican hangmans knot on a stick in junior high. I really wish I could answer this question because being in the scout troop with my dad was so much fun. I remember the campouts and the trips to our scout island and we would play hockey on a small pond we had. In high school I got pulled out of my scout troop and that day I had no one to talk to at school. My dad would always send me to school but we would still talk to each other every 3rd period. I ended up getting fat and lazy so I ended up losing weight and doing my best to stick with my scouting as best I could.

I moved to Georgia when I turned 18 and thats when I started to get the best parts of scout programs. The amount of higher learning and overall confidence in self was incredible. I have no idea how to describe it but for a brief time I was fully engaged in a community of highly skilled people that were all engaged in something that would not only benefit themselves but anyone and everyone around them. I also ended up learning several different languages, enough to become fluent in at least two of them. It was like I was starting to discover myself but I was still in the scouting program. As a scout leader i knew there was a lot I needed to learn and I just needed the right people to do it with. Scouting always appealed to me because it allows kids to learn life skills in an environment that challenges them in a constructive and fun way but when you have teenagers it becomes difficult because the traditions of scouting have gone through so much change and those traditions used to be so important to keeping people alive in the woods.

I was 18 when I left the scouting and I didn't come back until I was 28. I went through a lot of bad stuff when I left and I never had another experience like what I had in scouting. No other community has meant the world to me as scouting has and because of that I stuck with it. When I went to scouting as an adult I expected to get a good job and work my way up through the program to the best I can but everything you need to do to do those things is over 50 percent politics. I know a lot of people make a lot of money when it comes to scouting because its a good time and a good place to do so. But for the people who actually want to make a living off of scouting like my dad did it has changed the way they try to run programs. Its a lot more like the army but in a business sense and I don't want to run it like the army, I want it to be fun but I want it to be fun while actually contributing to society. I hate the politics but I love the community.

I believe the army was a waste of time. When I was 18 it was really fun. The people I served with were great but I didnt learn anything about myself or the world around me. I was injured during combat and I was in a coma for a month. I didnt get out of bed and walk for a year but now I'm able to do what I need to do without any issues. I only really stayed in the army because I didnt have any choice and now that I am able to move on I want to really make an impact on things that matter.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I think this is the scariest question. As you tell people more and more about yourself, it’s a bit like coming out. You put yourself in the position of being judged by other people. It’s hard to convey who you are to other people without telling them embarrassing stuff. It’s even scarier to ask people to share who they are with you. It also has to be the most common question for me because it’s something that I have to come up with an answer to several times a day. I’m 27 years old, but I’m pretty bad at thinking about what I have to do to get older and what that means for me. I’m from Los Angeles, but I’ve never been a part of the LA “scene”. I’m a journalist at High Times, but I’m also trying to figure out how to move out of journalism. For me, questions that I’m wrestling with now are “what does it mean to be a real artist” and “what does it mean to be really poor?

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am 24 years old. I grew up in a beautiful place of California’s Central Coast known as Big Sur. As a very young child, I remember being happier and more curious with the world than any of the other kids my age. This was always a puzzle to me. I felt more like a grown-up than a boy with a child’s attention span. It wasn’t until I was around twelve years old that I was convinced I must be weird and to this day I have no idea why.

The part of me that remains a child lives in the rich imaginations and curiosity of my children. Children have the most profound imagination and curiosity. I believe that the cure to autism will be found in the minds of our children. As humans, we are so easily distracted by the moment. Autism creates an obsession with the moment that strengthens the imagination. The latest symptom of autism I hear about is the growing fixation of children on Minecraft and Pokemon. Every child likes these games because the nature of these games are to constantly survive and evolve. This is exactly how autistic people are forced to think. Why not turn this strength into a cure?

As I grew older and started to get seriously into drawing, I began looking at the world with the eye of an artist. A friend once told me my mind was like a great big book with a story stuck in one chapter. I felt strongly attached to the environment. It was something that I couldn’t wait to see every day. After a move to the East Coast, I found my dream job as an art teacher in Japan. It was a life changing moment. My dream was to become a successful visual artist, and to live in the jungle with a few wild monkeys and a tiger. Unfortunately, it seems that everything in my life is a dream, and none of it is real.

Most people do not know who I am because I rarely speak, or at least no one ever pays attention when I speak. The reason why this is, is simply because I say what I think. It’s about honesty and communication. I am not a good liar. It’s a gift and a curse. This also makes it hard to socialize with most people. Being polite is something that I find difficult in the sense that you must always lie and this makes it very awkward for me at times. My silence is also a source of great loneliness that I will someday overcome.

My love for nature and art are both equally strong in my life. It is my mission to help others with my knowledge. I try to be useful in any way that I can. I’ve always wanted to be one of the best people I can be. There’s a joy that comes from being able to spread kindness.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I’m 21 years old and I was born in Princeton, NJ. When I was growing up I read the Lord of the Rings about once a year. I came back to it recently and I wonder how I took it in when I was much younger.

On my ninth birthday, my mother gave me the Beren and Lúthien book. Of course it was about my favorite characters from the books, but it also had beautiful illustrations and it was the first Tolkien book that I read that had a strong point of view from a woman. This was very empowering to me, and it was my first big hint that the female experience, especially in epic fantasy is not all of the same. I’m glad that it showed up at that time in my life, when I was still forming my idea of what I wanted to be when I grow up.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I’m 27, I grew up in Palm Beach County, Florida. I have 3 siblings and I’m very close with my parents. I’m lucky to have the best family ever. I moved to London to attend culinary school when I was 17. I lived there for 5 years, and moved to New York when I was 22. I have no regrets about moving to London, and I’m happy I went through the culinary school experience. However, I’m a small town Florida girl at heart, so I’m looking forward to being back home. I think the moving to London experience will help me in the long run, and I will take everything I learned from my culinary school experience with me. I really do love the food culture here in New York, and it’s made me a better cook.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I'm 21 years old and grew up in White Marsh, Maryland. Both of my parents are alcoholics, and my mother also suffers from a mental illness that involves auditory hallucinations. As a result I have become increasingly independent as I grew up, never being able to count on my parents for emotional or financial support. After spending the last 12 months in a severe economic depression I have finally pulled myself back together. I feel as though I've run through a dog-runs nose hair on both sides.

I have always known that I could do a better job of sustaining myself if I didn't always depend on others for every little need. At the age of 15 I realized that I had a knack for computers, and by 16 had built my first website. By 17 I had taught myself C++ (still have some rust) and began work on a MMORPG, (I've since abandoned that project, however). It was during this time that I realized I could make money from my skills. After building dozens of websites for local businesses I began to start my own. I began hosting other people's sites, and by 18 had earned over $10000 and reached the highest status in GameServers.com as a contract client.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I’m a 22-year-old college grad from Houston, Texas. I grew up in a single parent home with my mom and sister. My mom works as a school nurse. My dad left when I was very young. My sister is a fun loving kid who is four years younger than me. We love going to the park and going out to eat together. I am a first generation college student. I am the only person on both sides of my family to have graduated from a university. This was a very challenging feat because of my lack of family support and education. After graduating high school with a 3.6 GPA, I was accepted into the University of Texas at Austin. I studied Journalism, focusing on creative writing. I found a passion for writing plays and decided to become a screenwriter. I have been accepted into USC and UCLA, and would be pursuing my Master's degree in Screenwriting if I don’t get an acting job. My mom supported my writing aspirations by forcing me to read a book and write a report on the book every other week. I’ve been an avid reader of plays and novels since I was a little girl, because of her dedication to my education. I learned to love reading because she would not let me graduate to the next grade without passing my class. She motivated me by taking away my favorite activity, going to the park, until I finished my work. I am an avid reader and I plan on reading something every day throughout my career. My sister and I love going to museums, parks, and family events. We are a very close knit family. I have a romantic relationship with my partner, who is one year younger than I am, and a dog named Zoey. I chose to stay with my partner in Los Angeles because he has a 3 year old son that I’m really close with.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: Let's see, I am in my mid 20s. I grew up in the small town of Miller. I have always lived around here. I am the oldest of four children. I have a younger brother who is about to turn 19, and I also have two younger sisters. I used to work construction in the summers and doing a lot of manual labor. My dad is a building contractor and I used to work for him for 6 summers during high school and college. At the time, it seemed like a boring job, but it has set me up for great success since I never saw it as work. I was able to focus on my college work and hang out with friends, while still earning money to have fun. It gave me the option to work and earn money when I wanted to. Now, my job is doing things that I would enjoy, only I get paid. I studied Psychology, but I really wanted to get into a foreign language. So, I started learning French on my own for the past 3 years. I have also traveled extensively. I did 6 months in an extended stay at the IEP in Paris. I really enjoyed it, the food, the culture, the language and the whole experience. I also traveled to Germany to explore on my own after the IEP. I recently graduated from Iowa State University with a degree in International Studies and minor in Business.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am a 25 year old writer and web developer currently located in Denver, Colorado. I’m divorced and my ex wife and I still have an amicable relationship. I am a big proponent of the child development field and my relationship with my daughter is a priority for me. I have no alcohol, tobacco or drug problems. I have had short relationships with sugar daddies but feel that it is more lucrative for me to model and film myself on webcam. I have diabetes. Two months ago I moved to a 24hr walking city from a suburban townhouse where I had lived alone for the past year and a half. Being single and available I have been trying to experience a more active social and community life.

I was born in Colorado and have been here all of my life. I can no longer go to my hometown without being shot at. This event has occurred a total of five times now and for that reason I am very anti-gun. I enjoy many of the things that a typical white boy from the middle of the country would enjoy: sex, the internet, model cars, video games and movies. I spent a lot of time in high school doing martial arts and wrestling and would still consider myself to be in good shape. I have never spent a night in jail (unless I can’t remember when I was too drunk to drive) and I currently hold an A+ rating with the Better Business Bureau. I worked very hard at the hotel and restaurant industry to achieve the status that I have. I was able to purchase a townhouse, several vehicles and to learn HTML and JavaScript. I’m very content with my life and feel that I have achieved quite a bit at a very young age.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
Below is my life story. If anyone has the patience to read it, I thank you.

I am 25 years old. I grew up in the Chicago suburbs. Born and raised. I live about an hour from downtown. I really don't like driving in the city, but love seeing big buildings.

My parents split up when I was really young. My dad was a drug dealer and on the run from Chicago PD. He left the drug business 10 years ago and is now drug free.

My mom left my dad and was a single parent ever since. She works 3 jobs because she has to. She has MS and to be honest doesn't make enough to live in the suburbs.

My first job was at Panera (I worked there for 2 years) then at Steak & Shake for 3 years. I left there to work as a warehouseman at a vending company. I worked there for 2 years and moved to another warehouse where I am today.

My life is terrible. My parents live 6 miles apart but hate each other so much they don't speak. Most days I don't know what I will eat. I barely have money for rent because my rent keeps going up and my pay doesn't. I wish my parents would get back together so they would fight less.

In high school I had a great group of friends, but they all went to different schools. After graduation, they never talked to me again.

I don't have any friends really. Sure I have coworkers, but my manager told us not to hang out because we will be called in on days off to work.

I went to college but I had to leave due to money. I am in debt and need to pay my bills before I can go back to school.

I can't find a second job because every job I look at says I don't have the experience (I have been told by the hiring manager in 2 interviews that my experience makes me too qualified to work there. 

My dream is to become an engineer. I love to build things with my hands and I love math. I am good at both. I can't go back to school for 4 years because I need to work to pay my bills.

In a few years my car will need to be replaced. I don't have the cash to buy it outright and I have no credit card because I never had the cash to put one.

I have been single for 2 years and my cat died from old age 3 years ago.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I am a recently turned 21-year-old, with no college education, working out of a cubicle in the Insurance Verification Dept. of the hospital. I was born in southern California, and grew up in a country with absolutely no streetlights, lots of free-roaming animals, and easy land space. I always knew who my parents were, but they weren't the best guardians I could have had (nor they were the worst). I am the first-born, and have one younger sister. I love to draw, I'm a perfectionist, a little OCD, opinionated, and an adrenaline junkie. After high school, my heart was set on living out of the country, in Italy, to be exact, and I actually got accepted into Florence University of the Arts; my parents were not excited about this idea at all, and I didn't get the chance to go. I couldn't afford college for a long while, so I have been working from 19, in a bad coffee shop I loved to hate, and have been learning what I could from the experience. I've always been a go-getter, and can't stand lazy people. I'm comfortable with being uncomfortable. After a year and a half of working 6 days a week, I decided to grow up and act like an adult and finally get my act together. I decided to sit down, talk with my mom, and look into attending a community college. And here I am, in my 3rd quarter of college classes, and I plan to go on with my life educationally.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I’m 27 and was born in Toledo, Ohio and grew up right on the Detroit border in Lambertville, Michigan. I’ve had a crazy life – my family lived in several different states, I lived with family members in California, Montana, and Texas when I was a kid – but I’ve been living in Idaho since 2004. And I’ve lived in the same place since 2013 when I was hired at BSU!

My husband is from South Africa and is the son of Christian missionaries who left the States and lived there for 22 years. We met in Montana in 2012 – we both worked for the forest service – and after 6 months of being together, I moved to South Africa. In May of 2013 I flew back to Montana, got married, then moved to Idaho.

I love the outdoors and the Great Falls area. I’ve always been a city girl and really hate the dirt and bugs here (such as ticks, and beetle-maggot-like larva, and thunderbugs). But a lot of things are really great about Montana and I want to move back someday.

I’ve lived on two small farms, and I’ve really enjoyed the experience both times. We had about 7 acres at our farm, and a small commercial garden, a small forest, some native plants, a food forest, about 10 fruit trees, and a big green house full of native and edible landscaping. My husband and I worked hard and have grown a lot of food.

I’ve been a vegetarian since I was 15, and more recently went vegan. I was a vegetarian for a really long time because I just did not like the idea of eating things that had eyes, or fur, or a face. I also didn’t like the environment consequences of farming, and I didn’t like the way meat, milk, and eggs were handled. My husband was vegan before I met him, so it was never an issue when we got married.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I am 27 years old, born in New Jersey and grew up in New York City. Growing up I spent most of my time outside. It was a way to escape the reality of the world. I liked riding bikes, hiking, and skating. I always wanted to help the community and I wanted to make a difference in the world. I am a political junkie and my friends will tell you I know a lot about politics. I always wanted to work in government. I had my sights set on going to college and work in politics. I applied to school to study political science and I ended up getting my degree in sociology. I was not sure what I wanted to do but I knew that I wanted to get involved in politics. I started working for a member of congress in New York. I then worked on a campaign and it was not until I was working on the campaign that I found out that there is a possibility to make money doing this. I decided that I wanted to go to law school and I applied to law school. I decided to go to a school in South Carolina because I thought that it would be a good experience. It was an interesting experience. I got a degree in political science and then I went to work for a politician in South Carolina. I decided to take a break from politics and work for a large law firm in New York City. It was a good experience and I got a lot of knowledge about the law. I decided that I wanted to go to law school and I applied to a few schools. I ended up going to Columbia Law School in New York City. I got a law degree and then I went to work for a large law firm in New York City. It was a good experience and I got a lot of knowledge about the law. I decided that I wanted to go to law school and I applied to a few schools. I ended up going to Columbia Law School in New York City. I got a law degree and then I went to work for a large law firm in New York City. It was a good experience and I got a lot of knowledge about the law.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I’m 24. I grew up in a rural midwestern farm town. I was raised in a well-functioning family with two loving parents who care about me. My parents were public school teachers, and they cared about me in that regard, as well. My sister is a year older than me. We were close as kids. As teenagers, our relationship fragmented due to an unhealthy combination of extreme independence, hormones, and living in different households. In college, I became close with her again. My parents divorced when I was ten. Since then, my family has been generally dysfunctional. My family’s dysfunction is mostly due to the fact that my parents and I don’t see eye to eye. My parents have slightly conservative moral values that they instilled in me. I became progressively more left leaning as I grew older. Since I turned 18, my family’s dysfunction has manifested as an endless stream of arguments. We seldom see each other. Only in times of acute family emergency do we come together. I spent most of my twenties happily independent in my own apartment.

I was an introvert growing up. As a young boy, I was always ashamed of that. When I was around adults, I acted confident. With adults, I was boisterous and extroverted. I was always desperate to be acknowledged and liked by adults, especially men. My whole personality was molded around getting approval from adults. I’ve lost that desire as an adult, as I went from being a little boy to a grown man and as I learned how to gain acceptance from other peers my own age. It made me conscious of who I was with no need to impress. Now that I don’t feel the need to gain approval from adults, I’ve become very quiet around my parents. I never speak more than a sentence at a time, so our relationship has dissolved over the years. I’ll go as far to say that my relationship with my parents may be what I refer to as toxic. I feel my emotions as absolute, right or wrong.

I grew up on a very rural farm with two brothers. My father worked in a factory, and my mother was a school teacher. I had a very pleasant childhood, and I love my parents. I enjoyed building things as a kid. I never loved school. I was very introspective, awkward, and shy growing up. I kept to myself quite often. I was surrounded by a lot of other kids, but I was always afraid of conflict. That’s the main reason why I didn’t have many friends. My childhood was otherwise uneventful. I played a lot of sports growing up, and I just played along with the other kids. I didn’t try to stand out. I did want to stand out. I always liked art, and I was good at it, but I never understood the correlation between art and doing well in school. The more I did art, the worse I did in school. No one ever told me that art isn’t a waste of time, and it’s a life skill. I lived in my own world, and I never tried to fit in. I thought it made me special, but it also made me lonely. I got passed by in school, so it caused me to be ashamed of myself. I learned how to show people things I did well in, such as art, in school rather than let people find out on their own. In all my classrooms, I sat in the back or a side chair. My discipline was quite poor. I had a terrible temper. I got in many fist fights in elementary school and middle school. I would often get expelled from school because I would not listen to the teacher. The best and worst thing for me was I had my mind focused on the future. If I looked around my home, I felt the seediness of life in my rural hometown. The rural life I lived wasn’t for me. I wanted more for my life. It was always a desire for more. I lived a pretty normal childhood. I went to a good public school, and it was relatively safe. I would go out with my friends in the summer at a park or on a dirt road riding our bikes. My friends were never the obnoxious kids. We would play army, do all kinds of imaginary play. I had a large collection of micro machines, and I enjoyed playing with that as much as anything else. I built tree forts with my friends when I was eleven or twelve. It was pure physicality. The most difficult sport I played was probably hockey, and I was pretty good. I also played football and baseball.

So I guess I was a quiet child, but I definitely had a sharp wit. We had my home’s outdoor TV which was just a screen with speakers on our covered front porch. I remember watching a show on TV and I would pretend like I was a star on TV. My brain was always running. I can be pretty entertaining if I want to. Being around my dad was fun because he would laugh at me. I grew up really fatherless. My dad doesn’t know how to be a dad, so I spent most of my life around my mom, my brother, my sister, and my friends. My dad didn’t care about most people.

I guess my family was relatively poor, but I wouldn’t know because my dad always made things look perfect. We had a garden that had fresh fruits and vegetables. We always had enough to eat. My mom used to tell me as a kid that we are not poor, but my dad also told me we are well off. I didn’t know what to believe. But I knew we weren’t exactly poor. They never took us on vacations. They didn’t go to movies. We didn’t go to a restaurant. But we lived a comfortable lifestyle. My dad likes to say that he can always feed a family of six for less than a dollar. And we lived off one income with two kids, so that was not a small feat. I’m aware of my family’s socioeconomic class. But I never felt poor. As I got older, the cost of living in our household became more visible to me. But not enough to strike me as poor. I felt poor when I moved to California, but that was because I lacked a car and a job. We also didn’t have a dishwasher. Mom did the dishes. She just hated to spend money on nice things.

As far as my role in the family, I felt like the ‘golden child’ at least in my early life. I was the first born, but there are a lot of personality differences between me and my brother. We did get a lot of family time, and our parents were pretty liberal with us. I was disciplined in a way I would describe as love oriented. And I feel we had a nice childhood. We didn’t get everything we wanted, but we had each other, and we were supported in every way necessary to grow as independent adults.

Growing up, I never saw my dad work. My brother and I would watch tv during the day while my mom and dad were working. I remember these commercials for pest control with this guy in a big mascot suit, and I would watch those, as well as the ‘red eyeglass’ fraud ads. I felt a bit sorry for that guy.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I’m a 29 year old man that’s very tall for my age and countrymen. I’m Polish-American, meaning that my biological parents are Polish and my adoptive parents are American. I grew up in a country where I was forced to go to school until I was 17, then was expected to attend some vocational program or university while struggling to survive. There wasn’t much support from my adoptive parents and the society as a whole for children in general or disadvantaged children. This sort of taught me to value my own life and to do what needs to be done, rather than to expect help from strangers. Still, living in America, I had a few contacts with some Polish people and with Polish culture while growing up. Being interested in other nations, I started visiting Poland in 2015 and attended an American-style Christmas, which I found interesting but rather banal. There’s almost 20 years between me and the older men. There’s a lot of Czech men visiting, all of whom are at least 5 years older than me. I don’t really have much in common with Polish girls due to my Polish education and culture, but American or French girls find me interesting and engaging. In 2016, during an International Ukrainian American Youth exchange conference, I developed a romantic relationship with a girl from Ukraine which lasted for over a year. In 2017 I visited Poland twice. One of the reasons I return to Poland on a regular basis is because of its cheaper and closer transportation to my hometown, which I can attend to via train or bus, but I also visit Poland because I found some of the Polish women attractive.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I’m 27 years old. I grew up in a struggling middle-class home in a small, down-and-out coal-mining town. My parents were alcoholic. When I was eleven, they got divorced. They left me and my brother with our grand-parents and we grew up with them. My grandpa was a heavy smoker and drinker. He was an alcoholic and suffered from dimentia and diabetes. He had heart failure and passed away when I was 18. My grandma, on the other hand, was a born-again Christian, and is the best person I know. She’s still living, but her health is poor. Although she is very religious, she is a Christian who lives by Jesus’ command to take care of the poor and other marginalized people. She took care of the children and elderly poor and she helped all the members of our city, always caring for the marginalized. Sometimes she feels guilty because of the treatment she received from her children, especially her husband. She feels they took advantage of her, but I tell her that I am the one who has to take care of her now and that she should leave her feelings of injustice in the past.

My brother’s in prison for manslaughter, even though I know he’s innocent. I don’t know how he got involved with organized crime. I think it’s because he was pressured into joining in the fear of being killed, so he joined a gang for protection. My brother was the breadwinner for my family, including my grandmother, but now he’s in jail because of me. My parents are both dead now and I have to take care of my grandmother. I left my hometown when I was sixteen to seek my fortune, but I didn’t succeed.

I studied advertising in college. It was my second choice and I didn’t like it, but my parents were too stubborn to allow me to study something that they called “useless.” I hated advertising, but even though I didn’t have passion or the will to get a good grade, I still got good grades. It wasn’t that hard for me. At college I joined an anti-war organization and I was thrown out of school because of my political activities and because I skipped classes to demonstrate. I was sent to jail, and my parents sent me to Asia to “rebuild myself.” I traveled to Singapore, Vietnam and Cambodia, and I loved it. I discovered myself in these places, and it was my political and social activism that made me love traveling and meeting people of different cultures.

But after two years, I came back home to my family because I wasn’t being very successful. I wasn’t making money for my grandmother and she’s dying. I’m not as smart as my brother. I did all kinds of work after I was sent home from college. I worked in a warehouse, a call center, and I even got into drugs because of the stress. I found out that I did enjoy working with computers, though, and that’s when I decided to start learning about computers. I learned some things but I don’t know where to go from here.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I was a happy kid despite being part of a very large family (I have 9 sisters and 3 brothers). I currently live in Round Rock and have lived here since I moved out of my parent's house at the age of 17. I left high school my junior year and worked full-time at a foundry to support my family (My Dad got very sick with cancer and so I decided I needed to step up and get serious about the family business, supporting my Mom). However, when I was 18 years old, I went back to college and completed my AA degree as well as earned a Bachelors Degree in Applied Arts in Business Technology and a Masters in Accounting and Finance - all while working full-time. I've also completed more training and coursework over the years through different universities, including a couple of real estate courses. My parents divorced when I was about 5 years old, but that wasn't a traumatic event for me, I really didn't miss having a father when I didn't have one, it was the same. Although I am not a believer in the traditional value of a nuclear family, I do believe that family is very important. Family is definitely who I am. After getting my life back on track, I began to really focus on rebuilding my relationship with my parents, especially my Dad who had now finally gotten better. My Dad is not a very emotional man, and he never said "I love you" to anyone in my family - that is, until he died. And then he told me that he loved me. I have been with my current wife since I was 17 and married for almost 30 years now. We are both very happy together and are busy raising our boys. I am fairly active in the local school system and am a firm believer that every student should have the ability to succeed regardless of their circumstances.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I am 22 years old, born in South Africa to a family of six, and immigrated to America when I was nine. I have lived here ever since. I am very close to my family, as they mean a lot to me and as a unit we go to Canada or Mexico every summer. For me it’s important to spend time with the people I love. Life is short and these summers are very important to us.

On the other end of the spectrum, I also enjoy spending time by myself and exploring. I love hiking and finding myself alone in nature, or just being alone in my own apartment (which is pretty small).

In terms of education and interests, I graduated with a Bachelor of Arts in Economics and Environmental Policy from the University of Michigan last December. My passion and goal is in social policy and I intend to pursue a graduate degree in public policy in the next few years. I plan to write an essay on the current policy that targets stem cell research for my graduate admissions.

My knowledge on policy really comes from the Economic Policy Analysis project I completed for my senior year. It was funded by the State of Michigan and we analyzed 75 new policy proposals that appeared during the 2010 elections. This was an amazing opportunity that allowed me to travel to our capitol building for a week to meet people from every aspect of government, the private and non-profit sector. It was truly a fascinating experience and I received a paper outlining my findings published on the department’s website (under “Economics at Michigan”).

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I am 18 years old. I grew up on Oahu, one of the 50 states of the United States of America. I was born and raised on this island where I developed skills as a composer, singer, and song-writer. I composed original songs, played in bands, and collaborated with musicians and directors to write, record, film and broadcast music videos. By the time I was 16, I had traveled to New York City for the New York Musical Theatre Festival as well as West Palm Beach, Florida for various songwriting and singing workshops. I’ve been involved in events and competitions with the Hawaii Community Foundation, Songwriters Guild of America, Youth Speaks, And Creative Connection. More recently I composed “Flight”, which I performed at the American Idol auditions in Honolulu. But it is not only music that affects me. In many ways, I have always been affected by my experiences with my family. As a child I was, unfortunately, separated from many of my family members who live in California and Japan due to my father being an Air Force serviceman. In the past years, however, this separation has been made up for. In my earlier years, I was always the kid who dreamt of traveling the world. I can now say that I have my feet firmly planted on the ground. As I spent most of my school years traveling back and forth between CA and HI, my educational record is largely that of a Cali-girl with two associate degrees. Nevertheless, Hawaii has brought an abundance of positive influence into my life. It is through my friends and my fellow musicians in Hawaii that I have found guidance and support as well as an eagerness to learn and grow as a musician, a person, a friend, and a human being.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I am 25 years old and grew up in ____. I would say the biggest thing to shape who I am is my upbringing. My mom was pretty hard on me growing up but not as hard as she could have been. I was kind of a really anxious kid and she would consistently shout at me about wanting to beat me into submission. I never told her I was on meds because I was afraid she would stop them, and the thought of it just breaks my heart. I still struggle with a lot of anxiety as well. It would be so awesome to go back and be a kid again and make decisions that would allow me to get the education and work I want. As for people I hold in my heart, I don’t really have many but I do remember fondly the girl who was just getting into it with me when I met (2nd person on the team). Despite our vastly different perspectives, we were able to connect on a few things and actually come together over a project we’re starting with her husband and a few other people. I just wanted to give a little background on myself, answer what you wanted to know. I’d say I’m most proud of my ability to stick with things I start. I kind of quit a lot of stuff that I start, whether it be learning how to run or a university, but I don’t think I’m a quitter. There’s just only so much time in life and sometimes you just have to decide between a million different things you want to do and stop worrying about it.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I am a 28-year-old heterosexual male from San Diego, California. I grew up in an affluent, white neighborhood. I was raised by a single mother and had little contact with my father. I did not have a lot of friends and was picked on for my love of video games, particularly the game Everquest. I remember being called “gay” for playing Everquest and feeling hurt, but I still played it because I loved it and because I wanted to be an adventurer. It was my dream to be an adventurer. I went to school and college, graduating with a degree in political science, and began my career in banking. I worked in a number of places, including Boston, Los Angeles, and New York City. I worked for a number of banks, including Bank of America, Wachovia, and Deutsche Bank. I was a bank teller, loan officer, and investment banker. I was in the latter two positions when I became aware of the plans to destroy the World Trade Center towers. I saw the attacks on the towers and immediately became aware that the U.S. government was behind it. I quit my job and went to live in Washington, D.C. I saw the towers fall and saw that the government had done it. I went to the FBI and told them what I knew. They told me to shut up or else. I shut up. I spent the next few years doing nothing. I did not talk to anyone about what I knew. I was afraid of what might happen to me if I did. Then, in 2006, I was contacted by the FBI and asked if I would testify before Congress. I agreed and the FBI provided me with a lawyer and security. I testified before the House and Senate Intelligence Committees and told them what I knew. I did not name names, but I did name dates and places and provided a detailed account of what I knew. The hearings were televised. The hearings were broadcast all over the world. People were horrified to learn what had happened and how it had been done. I went back to my life in Washington, D.C. and resumed my normal activities. I had no further contact with the FBI or the U.S. government. I was not interviewed by any news organization. No one asked me to go on television or to speak to the media. I was left alone. I went back to my life in Washington, D.C. and resumed my normal activities. I had no further contact with the FBI or the U.S. government. I was not interviewed by any news organization. No one asked me to go on television or to speak to the media. I was left alone. I went back to my life in Washington, D.C. and resumed my normal activities. I had no further contact with the FBI or the U.S. government. I was not interviewed by any news organization. No one asked me to go on television or to speak to the media. I was left alone.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am a 23 year old female who grew up in a small suburb in the west valley of Phoenix. Because of this, I never wanted to be a small town girl. As soon as I turned 18, I had the opportunity to move to California for 6 months and I have never looked back. I love new experiences and have been privileged enough to visit 32 countries. And as the US citizen, that has been the most valuable lessons that I have learned while abroad. Moving from country to country can be difficult at times but will never stop me from making a move when opportunity knocks.

After returning to the states, I traveled to New York, Las Vegas, Washington D.C, New Orleans, Los Angeles, and San Diego as a tour guide on on a 3 month road trip. I was able to complete my undergraduate work with a double major in Journalism and English with a focus in Professional Writing. Over the past few years, I have had the opportunity to hone in my skills and help numerous people. From teaching history to high school students to one on one mentoring to younger females, I have loved sharing my life experience with them.

As I travel and work towards being an English language teacher, I am hoping to prepare them for life in our ever changing and sophisticated global society. Furthermore, I have aided both graduate and undergraduate students with their essays. I have tutored children aged 7-17 in English Language Arts. And I also have a background in proofreading for numerous businesses in England.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
Sure, I am 23 years old. I was born in Zagreb, Croatia to American parents. My father was stationed in Zagreb during his tour of duty with the Air Force. I was back here in the states during my school years and also for the better part of this last year.

Last year my girlfriend (now wife) and I moved to Shanghai where I worked as a web developer for a Chinese internet company. Unfortunately, the job did not work out well because of a combination of factors. For one, I was not doing what I thought I would be doing. I was essentially a Java programmer when I was promised a more diverse web development job. Secondly, the company lied to me about there being free housing on site. I had to come up with my own accommodation. Lastly, my contract stated that I would be paid once every 60 days and they didn’t follow through on their part of the deal. The timing of my paycheck was a little ridiculous. I would leave the apartment on Monday mornings before work and come home on Friday evenings. The company didn’t give me my paycheck until Sunday evening. Since the companies that I work with require paid invoices on Fridays, I was sometimes unable to do business until Monday since the money would not have cleared until then.

Anyway, I quit about a month into my job and moved back home to start on a private programming project that I’d been working on for the last several months. I was trying to see if I can complete and launch a web based Sudoku site and make a little extra money from it. While I was back home, I was able to learn and understand US tax law and make some notes for myself for later use when I will be filing taxes this year.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: Well, I’m 29 years old, I was born and raised in Illinois. I have one older brother, two cats and a bird. My father is an alcoholic and my mother is a Nurse. Because of these circumstances my mom was left in charge of raising my brother and I. She did the best she could as she was just a nurse and could barely afford to care for herself and we got welfare from time to time. My brother and I did without a lot of things though and we spent time with our mom in homeless shelters and sometimes with our grandparents or cousins. I didn’t know that my parents fought a lot until I went to counseling as a kid. Our dad would come around sometimes and try to beat on my mom but my uncle always pulled him off. After my parents divorced, my mother left Illinois and I went to live with my grandparents. It made things easier and we didn’t have to fear for our lives. I was fourteen when my grandparents kicked me out because of my parents. There are some things you just don’t tolerate as a grandparent. I think they should be sent to prison for letting that happen to me. I don’t talk to them anymore. Things are different than when I was in the shelters though now. It’s not as bad because there are a lot of support systems set up for the poor. I had been going to an after school program called the Big Brothers/Big Sisters for a few years and the workers there saw how much I needed help and connected me with Family Action Development. They took me in, made sure I was going to school every day and on time and made sure that I had clean clothes, food and friends. My mom is trying to clean up her act and my dad is trying to avoid the law so I haven’t seen them for a long time. I’ve got no reason to go back except to pay my final respects when they are dead and gone. My life is not the same as it was before. I’m trying to be grateful for what I do have and I’m really hoping that I can make something of myself and stay out of a homeless shelter in the future.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am a 27-year-old Filipino-American male from Modesto, CA. My parents are divorced and my father is a drug dealer. I'm the oldest of three and the only one who has received a college degree. My parents' lifestyle has made me want to set a different example of what kind of person is capable of creating more than is available to the impoverished community.

I grew up playing mostly sports and always spent a lot of my time outdoors. I lived an average middle-class lifestyle with only me, my two younger brothers, and my mother. When I wasn't playing sports I was at home playing video games or working on a school project. A form of entertainment that I really enjoyed was going to the movies or the theater. This helped me realize that I wanted to be the person who created these enjoyable and engaging worlds. I believe my goal is to convey emotions with whatever story I can convey.

I feel that I am the kind of person who people gravitate toward as I have learned to become an empathizer that listens to what people are telling me. I generally am more of an introvert, but I do feel very at peace in conversation with my fellow men. My interests outside of my career goals are to always be an ear for someone and to create art as best as I can in the free time I have. To be an artist that can engage others with the story he wants to create.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I am 24 and I’m originally from Virginia. I spent the first 18 years of my life as a small town girl who did not know what it meant to be poor, until I moved to DC where I quickly learned what it meant to be poor, and then, eventually, homeless. My mother died of a drug overdose when I was 4 and the rest of my family did not care to raise me so I was put up for adoption shortly thereafter. My adoptive family was beautiful, loving, supportive and supportive people but in my adult years, I was able to come to terms with the fact that they were not a black family and I felt very different from everyone around me. This began my journey of learning to be proud of who I am as an African American woman and I now whole heartedly believe that my story is a gift and a blessing to whoever ends up with me because of how much happiness and strength I have been able to find through my journey.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I am 23 years old, I am from the small countryside town of Brogueville Pennsylvania. Growing up in a small town, everyone knew each other, and that made me shy to talk to strangers. I felt like there was always someone watching my every move. Every decision I made someone was there to see it. At the age of 10, My grandparents had passed away when my sister and I were young. It really took a toll on our family. My Mom needed someone to help look after my sister, so I stepped up to the plate. I took on the responsibility of 24 hour care. It changed me and made me who I am today. I am proud to say I helped my mom take care of my sister. Family and honesty mean the world to me. If I was being honest with myself the reason I joined the military was for a paycheck. I wanted to be in a position that made me feel safe. I wanted to feel good about myself knowing that I was doing my part for our country. Since I have been in the military, I have learned who I am and what I want out of life. Being in the military showed me the value of a paycheck. Money was not the most important thing to me anymore. I wanted to be in a position to help my peers. Show them the way, help them grow into the individual they want to be. Whether or not they would like to admit it, I am a leader by example. I am in charge of what I do and how I approach my tasks. I am a hard worker and I am always trying to improve.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I’m 20 years old. I was born in Chugot Nasirot, Israel. In response to the Palestinian Arab uprising, my parents and I emigrated to the United States in 1996. As a child growing up in an Orthodox Jewish neighborhood, I was constantly at odds with my peers about my beliefs, my values, and my intentions. I lived in a world where there was little time or room for introspection or rest, and I therefore tried to escape that world by becoming a conformist. During my high school years, I learned to avoid having to take a stance on the concerns of the people around me and I never dared to ask questions regarding the beliefs I had about myself or about the purpose of life. In college, however, I discovered the importance of being true to oneself and the duty we have to make use of the power we have to make this world a better place.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I am 28 years old. I grew up in Queens, New York. I spent my early years with my maternal grandparents in Jamaica and went back to school there. Moving back to the US from there, we moved back to Queens and lived a life that was vastly different from our experience growing up in Jamaica. My parents were born there and we went through a lot of the same struggles they did. However, I was fortunate enough to see the world, attend college, and pursue a fulfilling career. My parents opened their home to 24 foster children. By seeing this, I understood the compassion in people to want to help someone less fortunate than they are. It also showed me how precious my own family is. They showed me the value of volunteering and gave me the opportunities to serve. I want to instill that value in my own family now. I understand the importance of understanding and supporting all people for the differences we hold. In my short adult life, I've had to adjust with so many life changes and make difficult decisions. I've also had the incredible opportunity to move across the world to the United Kingdom. It's where my wife is from. I had to leave my career, move to a foreign country, and make new friends all while finding a job and setting roots in a new country. I do not believe this is a far-fetched example for Americans. As I see my wife, her family, and friends fight to secure the rights for other European citizens who have not had the chance to do so for their own families. My life experiences give me the skill set I would need to serve as an immigration judge. I will focus on the individual and look at each case and make a decision that is just and compassionate.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I'm a 24 year old straight edge, "soccer mom," by trade American nurse. My experiences span from being a soccer coach for girls, to a US Coast Guard Reserve, to a volunteer EMT in a volunteer fire and rescue squad, to the home healthcare industry I work in today. 
In early high school I was introduced to punk rock culture which exposed me to animal liberation, ecology, anti-racism, and anti-misogyny which I felt could only be echoed through the exhortations of President Kennedy. I participated in projects that would likely offend your employer, such as protests and music events, while working night and day to pay for tuition, books, and housing, without any help from my family. I became depressed because of the inconsistency I had with my employer and, after an unrelated accident, physical and emotional exhaustion. The diagnosis of chronic migraines caused me to quit my job, and to sell the last of my possessions to ease my suffering. 
My old coworkers heard about my situation and brought me into a local restaurant chain's "round table," where I worked for $7.50 a hour making nothing but tips, but received an extraordinary amount of support from not only my employers and coworkers, but also from the customers. I eventually saved enough money to continue my education and succeeded in earning both a bachelor's and a master's degree in Nursing. 
My family never really went through hardships growing up because of circumstances from when they immigrated here (to whom I have never met). It's fair to say that I was spoiled.
I believe that I'm a survivor in this world and I want to make the world a better place to live in, and to share it with other beings, our home, which will always be here, no matter what.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: Hey I’ve got five hours for this. I’m 25 years old. I grew up in western Montana. My parents decided to move from Montana to Texas to have a large family. I grew up all over the state of Montana with my parents living in Texas part of the time. I graduated from Wayland Baptist University with a bachelor’s in education. I have a friend who introduced me to the Board of Christian Education, the kids. They are God’s kids. They come to the board wanting to be teachers, and they leave with the ability and the confidence to pursue their dreams. I know what you mean. Our kids want to make a difference and this is the way to do it. I give people the freedom to speak their mind. They feel I know who they are. I know what they’re capable of. That builds trust. I relate to God’s kids on their level and it really feels good. I have lots of friends and I maintain a healthy work ethic and a sound mind. I take time to live life and have fun and I’m really good at it.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I am currently 24 years old and was born in Long Island, NY. In my younger years, I grew up with a lot of struggles but I have always believed that there’s always a light at the end of the tunnel. This light would push me to the right direction no matter how dark that tunnel can be. Therefore, during my young years, when I saw the possibility of a brighter future I would always try to reach for it. School was always a fun and enjoyable place for me because that was where I developed new friendship and established new relationships. By being surrounded with people whom I can relate to and can understand how they feel and deal with things really drew me to being social and caring for my friends, family, and my community. I have always grown up in a community that was really aware of the important of family and never let them go. I also love to travel and have new adventures and experiences. Recently I have been living in Saudi Arabia where I would do volunteer work and do small activities to help the communities in Saudi. I also visited the Souq Waqif in Qatar to get some real Arabic food and experiences.

In the end, I was always interested in the positive changes and experiences that my community was going through because I wanted to be a part of it. As I grew up, I learned to become more mature and learned how to handle stress and tough situations. My experience in dealing with stress taught me to have new ways of managing my stress by channeling them into activities and positive thoughts. My family inspired me to become a better version of myself and to do the things that they have never had the chance to do. Family is always my priority in life and I have always made sure that I do everything that I can to keep their hearts warm.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I'm 21 years old. I was raised and currently still live in Frisco, TX. I have always been quite, keep to myself unless it comes to fixing things and technology. Everything makes me who I am. Im a good person deep down even though I look like shit. It matter's to me if i can help people but right now I don't want to live the restraunt life any more. I have a godson who is a year old and he matters to me more then anyone or anything else in the world. I enjoy watching him grow up and learn new things in the past year. After being a host for almost 2 years, i saw that even if i want to I will never go anywhere with that. I don't like it one bit. I like helping people I have been working since i was 16 and all I have learned is how to deal with people to get what I want. Be polite, patient and work faster and faster to move up the ladder. So thats just another reason why I'm staying put.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I am a 29 year-old writer, speaker, and former Democratic staffer. I grew up in Falls Church, VA. My parents met during the Vietnam War – my dad was in the Peace Corps, and my mom was a refugee from North Vietnam. They met in Hanoi and did not see each other again until she fled the country and resettled in Miami. They married and had two sons before my dad died tragically young in 1995. Growing up in my family, political advocacy was a constant and sometimes controversial subject. (In my household, it’s very pro-immigration, pro-worker and anti-war.) We grew up with a right-of-center orientation that emphasized the importance of hard work and education. While we were right-leaning, my family was always supportive of Democrats and participated in community organizing. I studied political science and government at the College of William & Mary, where I was active in politics and student groups. After graduating, I was a Staff Assistant in the Washington office of then-Congressman Mark Warner (D-VA), who is now our Governor. In the fall of 2005, I was recruited to work in the Senate Finance Committee. I left government service in 2007 to work for an international consulting firm and in 2009 joined the political team at Ron Wyden for Senate. As the Deputy Political Director, I oversaw day-to-day political operations. In 2010, I founded On Location Marketing, a consulting firm that works with presidential candidates and ballot initiatives. In addition, I serve as a spokesperson and strategist for Blue Virginia. In 2011, I spoke at several Democratic Party State Conventions. My personal interests are writing and advocacy. I recently became interested in climate change, but have not always been engaged on the issue. Before my position at On Location, I did not have the influence to speak out.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am 19 years old, born in Miami, Florida. I grew up with 4 sisters in a not so well off household with strict parents that pushed us to strive for more. Family to me means love. Love is something I have never had an abundance of and I have never known, until recently. My family has never been a very affectionate bunch. I have two loving sisters who I consider to be the most important people in the world to me (my sisters and I are 12 years apart). My parents split when I was about 5-6 years old, leaving my mom as the only breadwinner for a family of six. My mother never worked while my sister and I were in school, so this led to neglect, argument, and abuse (abuse is never an okay action, but it has given me something to learn from). Growing up my father was an abusive alcoholic that didn’t live in my household (he lived in a halfway house for 15 years after my mom got a restraining order and divorced him, he drinks on a rare occasion now that he has recovered). I could go on and on about how my home life was more than bad, but I believe I made it my own, and found happiness in a lot of the despair I experienced as a child. I found solace in my dog, on the side walks around my neighborhood and the empty riverbed I would walk past when I walked to the local convenience store. I found a solace in the leaves and trees that turned blue after the polluted rain. I discovered love in the way that love has no attachment. When love lets go it’s not because it’s bad, it’s because there’s only so much space in one person’s heart, and you can only have so much love to give.

As for my past events, I have a lot of them. I have been homeless for 6 months in Phoenix, Arizona and survived it. I have been (and always will be) a huge advocate for helping the homeless. Most people see them as a drain on society, but they are people like you and me. Without them, the city would not be so vibrant. It takes all kinds to make a world that we can all enjoy. Being homeless taught me that helping other people doesn’t take much. You can give up to two hours of your day handing out food and water and that’s enough. Something else that really matters to me is getting better at something each day. If I say that I will learn how to play 3 different instruments I make sure I practice on one of them at least 30 minutes a day. You can’t do much in life, if you don’t push yourself to get better each day, it’s as simple as that.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I'm in my mid-20s and was born in the East coast. I grew up as any stereotypical American kid playing sports and playing with videogames. My parents let me drink a little alcohol, let me have sex with girls, whatever I wanted. I went to high school, did very well in honors classes, was on the football and wrestling teams, dated a bunch of girlfriends who were all studs but still losers, dated some cute girls but all of them were friends with my exes, and partied a little bit. We all thought we were going to be rich. I went to community college and chose to keep my partying ways going, and continued to excel at school. Then a girl I had sex with accidentally fell pregnant, it was a false alarm thankfully, and I dropped out to try to help her, but I got into alcohol and marijuana abuse, and started selling meth. Eventually the sex girl left me, with my own children now in the picture, and my roommate helped me find salvation in Jesus. I went to church, got a job at McDonald's, and became pregnant with my second wife. My kids with that ex are with my mother now and it is happy, as my mother is a very wealthy woman. The girl I married earlier this year is my salvation; she is a believer too, and she forgives me for my past. Jesus is my life now and I am happy with my family.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I'm in my early 20s and I went to a normal high school. I'm from the city and I grew up playing video games and enjoying things that don't benefit me in real life. Making my own realm on Runescape is probably the most achievement I have in real life. It doesn't really do anything good for me, but it's one of my prized possessions. Seeing that daily hour meter tick up and watching my death count slowly go down is comforting, I don't get that luxury in real life.

I also enjoy doing nothing all day and eating take out three times a day. I used to like pizza but I've grown tired of it because I have it every day. I enjoy hooking up with girls, but I haven't felt the need to in a while. I'm past that stage of my life.

I also have a fancy looking room. I bought this floor lamp at Bed Bath and Beyond and hung this fake mirror from the ceiling. It's just the mirror to a frame that I cut and put over the light, and I shine light through it so I always have some sort of glare over me.

I have a collection of guitars on this wooden stand. I have two beautiful Fender Stratocaster guitars. I paid $800 for the turquoise one because it was a limited run model and it came with Fishman pickups. $750 for the red one because it was an American Elite Stratocaster and it was on clearance for $150 off.

I'm trying to collect guitars, but I don't know what to get. I own two custom STRAT guitars. What do I get next? A Telecaster? A Gibson Flying V?

The MOST important part of my room is my monitor. I have my computer hooked up to it, so I can surf Reddit, watch YouTube, watch Netflix, or play games on my screen while I talk to the family. I also have headphones hanging out over the computer so I don't disturb them when I yell obscenities while gaming.

I went to job interviews in 2016, but none of them have called me back for a second interview.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I’m 26, middle child of 4. Born in California, moved to Indiana at 8. I played baseball and golf. Got my first job at 13 selling candy bars to fellow students. High school was easy, I was a dumb jock. College was hard, it was the first time I had to manage my own schedule, do my own studying, make friends. I didn’t know how to balance fun and education. I failed some classes, had to take some extra time, but I graduated. It was a small Jesuit school so we all called each other brother. We were encouraged to see each other in our entirety and grow with love. I was not strong in my faith then but being at that school started a little bit of a faith journey. After graduating, I moved back to California to work for a communications company where I traveled internationally for a few years and had my first real challenge; growing in patience and flexibility. I enjoyed it but decided I needed a steadier base in the US so I moved back to California and got a job with AT&T. Life wasn’t so interesting then, so I started to wonder what else was out there. As chance would have it, I found an opportunity at CSN with Beth over 3 years ago.

This is pretty much the easy part, usually. That said, all the stories that we relay are with a purpose. There are examples we use to show which values and personality traits we have.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I am in my mid 20s. I grew up in an environment that had a heightened sense of competition among everyone. I felt naturally competitive and I think I felt this way because of this culture around me. As I grew up, I valued performance more and more. At the same time, I began to learn more about who I am and things that I enjoy doing. I started to realize that I value, probably most of all, self-fulfillment. More than anything, I wanted to do something that makes me feel fulfilled. I know this is not a tangible thing but it is what has guided me thus far. Growing up I really loved playing basketball, guitar, and video games. I also enjoyed math and thought that I wanted to pursue a major in this. I thought at first that I wanted to be an engineer of some kind and then chose chemical engineering. While in school I kept playing basketball for fun and enjoyed working out and being active. As I got closer to graduation, I realized that I was unhappy in my major and with my path. I decided that I wanted to do something to challenge myself physically, emotionally, and mentally. Something that was going to teach me a whole lot more than engineering. I had always enjoyed the idea of traveling and meeting people, so I signed up for the Peace Corps. After going through rigorous processes, training, and getting to the country I am now stationed in, I know that this experience has made me feel fulfilled more than ever. I am very thankful to have made it here.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
This is a broad question. I would answer in a story telling way. I grew up in Chicago suburbs with parents who divorced at a young age. My dad was in and out of my life, he died in prison. My mother is a major alcohol. My sister and I were raised by my mother and her mother.

As a child I was angry and suffered from mood swings and even suicidal tendencies. I struggled through my life being the best I could be. However, I needed counseling and struggled to understand myself. I even joined a suicide prevention group when I was in my early 20s. I never got better in many ways, as I was diagnosed with bipolar disorder in my early 20s.

Now, I have been sober for 6 years. And I am still learning about myself and my identity. Mental Health is a struggle. Even with a strong will to survive, the mental illness is still there.

I work as a mail carrier with the United States Postal Service. I joined the United States Postal Service and currently work as a letter carrier because of mental health struggle. I needed a job that would allow me to take any time off when I was having a bad day. The Postal Service is very flexible with their employees.

My job experience also includes child care where I worked for 6 years as a babysitter and then a home care provider. I worked for a family with 3 children of ages 1, 4, and 7. I also provide in-home services for children and disabled adults.
