I look at my job as a sportswriter like most people would a painting or a piece of furniture they’d build. I take pride in every piece I put together. There’s no greater feeling for me than hearing my name read off as a byline when watching a press conference or post game interview. When I leave work, I bring that same pride and intensity to the cage.

In the ring, I’m super driven. I don’t like losing and I do whatever it takes to make sure it never happens. At 180 pounds, my wrestling is a huge strength for me. I’m super conditioned. I’m 6 foot with a long reach so I can keep my range, reach and power. However, I do tend to have a bit of a glass jaw. I’ve been knocked out in competition. It’s not as frequent anymore as I used to fight the much heavier Bobby Brents, but I do lose concussions from time to time. For the fights that matter, I make sure to rest up and catch my wits. After every fight, I like to make sure I have some IRLs to talk to about what happened, how it felt and how I can make sure it doesn’t happen again. Talking about it helps me understand what happened.

In 2014, I began pursuing the UFC after I won the WSOF middleweight title. I went on a huge winning streak to get the belt in my division and the UFC called after that. I had a few chances after that and it didn’t work out, but I always stayed in the gym and kept working. In 2017, I became the RFA welterweight champion as I went out to LA for my fourth shot at the big show. After finishing Mike Rhodes in the first round, I knew I was in for another wait. In the summer of 2018, as I was preparing to make the first defense of my title, I started getting text messages from Nate Shaw, my manager. It turns out that the UFC had made a small mistake when they were sending invitations for the World’s Scariest Martial Artist show, where they pit MMA fighters against pro wrestlers. One of the script writers had accidentally submitted my name instead of Johnny Case’s to the show. I wasn’t sure what to think. If they wanted me for a reality show, it might mean good things.

I didn’t have time to discuss it right away as I needed to fight for my belt in a couple weeks. However, I agreed to do it. I didn’t have a lot of confidence in my pro wrestling abilities. However, I had been competing in freestyle and Greco-Roman wrestling since I was 14. I am no longer in the shape to compete as a Greco or folkstyle wrestler, but I have no doubt that with some training, I could be competitive in the world of professional wrestling.

I wound up winning that title fight, and I had all sorts of offers from the WWE after the show, but they wanted me to pack on some more muscle and not do pro wrestling. I told them it was wrestling or nothing. I wanted to finish up my work at RFA and then I wanted to get to work on my own contract. No wrestler, former or otherwise, with an 8-0-1 record and a well-rounded toolbox should have to wait. After that, I was in negotiations for a little while with Noah Grabeel and the members of the crew and I signed a 3-month contract to try out for Impact Wrestling. To date, I have been at several Impact tapings, but I haven’t heard anything back. I’ve heard that there is one official negotiation I need to sign and I will be under contract with Impact. The third letter of the alphabet is a direct reflection of the only thing I hold more than me, my wrestling, and my family. It is KILLED, and that is the essence of my fighting style. I never tap, but I have been out cold once. If you want to kill me, you have to put me down for good. A few concussions does not make me question what I’m doing. In fact, I have a healthy amount of concussion symptoms all the time. I make sure to rest and try to take care of myself, but make no mistake. The moment I hit the mat, I want to take off their head.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: That can take a long time but I'm not even 20 years old at this point. I was born at my home and grew up with my three older brothers on 200 acres in the Colorado Rocky Mountains. When I got older and finally was able to leave the house, I went to a couple different schools and in the seventh grade, I finally made it to high school. A year later, my brother at the time took his life. I could only think about how I wished he was still alive. Shortly after that, my mother came into my room and was frantic. She informed me that my older sister had been shot in the head 30 minutes prior. I ran outside screaming and crying. I managed to get a hold of a family friend who rushed me to the hospital. I couldn't move my sister's body so I took my jacket off and laid it on her face to hold her head up to keep it from falling to her side. She was my everything. And I had to be the person there for her as she drifted away from this world. I didn't know how to help. This became my biggest pain. But the real reason for this came down to the decisions we make. My sister killed herself after spending a year in prison for murdering my brother and therefore I have been moved around through different homes in order to remove me from that tragic event. I'm sorry, this is going to keep going like this. I'm not okay. I'm really not okay.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
Well, I’m 19. I grew up in Bethel, New York. When I was 3 or 4, my family moved from Rockland County, NY to a town that no longer exists. Because they built Interstate 84 through it. We moved to Pocono Pines, Pennsylvania in a house that’s since been torn down and been reincarnated as a condo building of almost a dozen units. I spent a lot of time in the Poconos as a kid. Mostly spent in the woods of my grandparents’ property where I developed the love of trees that will probably not die for some time. Summer camp, I was a Girl Scout, and lots of other things meant that there was always some form of outdoor activity going on. I also had a pretty standard childhood. My parents have been happily married for a number of years and have a good relationship. My grandparents have also been married for a long time and are still together. My dad was (and still is, I presume) a cop.

Now I’m a student here at BC. I’m studying sociology and anthropology. I want to study linguistics, philosophy, and religion in the future. I love science fiction, hiking, and being out in nature. I’m a bit of a pragmatist. It’s hard to get me to care about whatever thing is happening these days. At one point I used to wear a lot of black clothes, listen to a lot of [sic], and say a lot of radical things to shock my ultra-religious grandmother. Those days are largely behind me. I love my family. I love nature. I love my friends. I love random acts of kindness. Mostly though, I love learning.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I'm 25 years old. I grew up in Independence, MO. I attended Independence schools from pre-kindergarten through high school, so I basically grew up and stayed in my hometown my entire life. My grandfather influenced me growing up. He taught me the value of hard work, honesty, and integrity. He was a businessman. He also was active in the Republican Party. He had me help with phone-banking, register people to vote, and so on. I was 7 years old at the time and learned a lot from him. He recently died, unfortunately, but I remember him very well. We would run into people who knew him all the time and I would hear people say "Hey, that's Mike!" Not to mention he had more pictures in news coverage than a celebrity does! He was well-known in the area and still is. We always went to see him at the office, we were always with him when he went out doing business, and I would go on his travels with him sometimes when I was a kid. When I was 15, my father passed away after a battle with leukemia. I was then motivated even more to make something of myself, work hard, and be a good man. My father would have wanted me to. That same year, my older sister, who is married and moved to Kansas City, MO, died after a massive heart attack. She was only 27 years old. Shortly after her death, my wife and I started going to church together and got serious about our relationship and our religious beliefs. It was a tough year for both of us. Just recently, she gave birth to our first child. I'm so proud of her for being a great mother and mother to our family. I'm grateful to God for blessing me with her.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I am 28 years old. I was born in France but moved to the US at the age of one. My family (mom, dad, and my older brother) live in New York. I consider NY my “home” even though I was only raised in the US. I currently reside in Southern California. I’m independent but still a mama’s boy. My mom raised us by herself after leaving my dad when I was just three. She was extremely hardworking, empathetic, loving, fun, and simply put- a great person. She wanted the best for her boys and we were never lacking. Her death on February 23rd, 2011 was a complete shock to me. She was in really good health and I did not see it coming. She passed away from a heart attack caused by a brain tumor in her heart. She was 59 years old.

The brain tumor itself was benign but caused pulmonary hypertension and heart failure. The tumor literally increased pressure inside her heart and blocked circulation. My mom was hospitalized for a week before her death and despite trying to prolong her life with a tracheostomy and pulmonary artery stenting, the heart tumor was still there. Her love was everlasting and I hope to continue her legacy. This is one example of why I live so selflessly and don’t want to do anything to hurt others.

I love “living in the moment” and doing “impulsive” things. I enjoy waking up early in the morning for a run in the park. I love going to the mall to window shop. I also enjoy running errands. I’m a big fan of food. I love eating. (who doesn’t?) For example, I could never resist a good set of sliders. I love simple but good food. If I’m going out to eat, I often buy high-calorie fast food (gourmet burgers, fries, and milkshakes). I love going to a bar with a friend(s) and just get a drink. I also enjoy nature and volunteering. It doesn’t matter what activities I’m doing, I’m extremely social and love meeting new people. The best type of friend I can have are simply trustworthy and kind. It’s as simple as that.

Another thing that matters to me is family. My family and close friends are what I would classify as my “glue” in life. My family is always going to be there for me to lean on when things go wrong. Even though I’m only 28, I hope to have the same amount of success that my mom had in life. The amount of strength, love, and care she gave me is something I will always remember and seek to achieve. I am also very religious. I’m a Christian. I have a strong belief in God and have faith that my family will be in good hands with him. I’m also extremely driven in my life. Nothing can stop me from achieving my dreams and achieving success.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I am 25 years old and I grew up in Phoenix, Arizona. I was always interested in computers but wanted to become a concert pianist until I discovered that my hands were too small. I later played in a rock band. With a love of music and computers, I soon decided I wanted to become a sound engineer. I attended many CD Baby workshops which were led by musicians who were also awesome teachers and learned how to mix and master recordings. Soon after, I became a teacher myself and began teaching musicians everything I learned on the fly. These were wonderful teachers who asked me a thousand questions and encouraged me to keep pushing myself. In 2008, I decided to push myself even further and spent a whole summer completely honing my craft as an engineer before graduating from Full Sail University with an M.S. in Sound Design in 2009. After graduation, I moved to Orlando where I continued to teach and mix projects while perfecting my career as a mastering engineer and general sound designer. In 2015, I decided to further my career and skills by accepting a contract with Dubset Media as a senior mastering engineer for one year and I also accepted an internship with mOcean in LA where I further developed my skills in creative engineering. Currently, I am happily employed by United Masters where I get to mix and master songs for some of today’s biggest stars and I also live in Los Angeles, where I continue to work with mOcean as a senior audio engineer.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: Well, umm, err. Ah, I am 27. I grew up in a modest home in the mid-west. My father (a farmer), my mother (a store clerk) and my sister (my best friend) made our lives a happy one. My father and mother were wise and wise people taught me how to be wise, to be honest, to be humble. I decided when I was young to, well, let's say I decided that I wanted to be a star. I wanted to be the best at something, and I wanted other people to know it. Perhaps because of my physical attributes, I really excelled at sports. My high school years were great as I felt that my abilities were something to be admired, respected, enjoyed. I played baseball and football, and yes I was the best player on my high school team. My coach was a jerk, though. He was a racist. I tried out for this team, the Kansas City Royals. I didn't make the team, although I really wish I would have been better than the guy that they put in my position. I didn't make it because, well, I guess there are some things I just shouldn't talk about here. Anyway, I then tried out for the Kansas City Chiefs, and again, I didn't make the cut. I guess that's what a guy gets when his father is a farmer and his mother is a store clerk and his sister is just sooo jealous. In high school I became involved in acting and even performed in a couple of small television commercial roles, and even won a small competition which made me really believe in myself. At that point, I knew for sure that I wanted to become a star.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I am a 25-year-old woman who grew up in Washington, DC. The events that most molded me into the person I am today were definitely my early childhood experiences. It was during this time that I became the human I am today. I truly loved life and looking back, I see how my parents raised me. I was free and my needs were being taken care of. I was loved. After that age, I grew more and more protective of the life I had with my family. I was frightened of the outside world which was filled with drug dealers and horrific murders on the streets. When I turned 8, my mother started an affair with a drug dealer. It was at that time, while I was at school, that my father was shot 19 times and later succumbed to his injuries. My mother knew what she did the day of my father's death, but yet she has never shown any guilt. I, and my siblings, were left with relatives. After a week, our mother collected us and we were returned to her. My brother and sister never spoke to their mother again. My mother, a social worker, still resides on the same block. The neighborhood I was raised in, has become more violent than ever. Mothers cannot even step out of their door without worrying that their children will not make it home. Living in the same environment, has either taught me how to stay away, or how to adapt in a similar way. I have chosen the latter, a smarter way to survive.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I'm 26 years old. I graduated from high school in 2009, and went to college to study music. I failed out of college and moved to Atlanta, Georgia, where I lived with my dad for a few years and worked odd jobs. I moved back to Florida and worked for a while to save up to move back to Georgia, and eventually I had enough to afford an apartment and a car. It took me two years to save up and move again. I became a gas station attendant and eventually my brother got a job and a place of his own and I was able to move in with him. I continued working at gas stations and eventually I became a manager for a market I had transferred to. I became a lead attendant at the airport and oversaw a crew of several attendants. I was promoted to Operations Supervisor and trained 35 different airport locations across the state of Georgia on the products and services they provided and how to manage their crew. I moved to Jacksonville, Florida and worked as an operations supervisor, and took a job in West Virginia as an Operations Supervisor, overseeing the networks for towns with populations under 1,000 people. Eventually I came back to Florida and took a job as a gas station manager, but I eventually got fired for reasons I still do not understand.

I guess you can say I've worked my entire life. I made good choices in what to study in high school, the college I went to, and I eventually managed to get the job I want most in life. Now, without any more good choices to make, I'm just waiting for God's plan.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am 27 years old. I grew up in a little southern town in Oklahoma, population 5000. I was homeschooled until I was 12 and then went to public school. In 11th grade, I moved away from my small town, and was able to enroll at my local community college. This experience taught me how to manage my time, do my work without anybody telling me what to do, study properly, make friends, live on my own, and be an independent person. I found that I am really independent, and I can do a lot on my own.

I moved back to my hometown for the summer, and still worked on my degree while teaching at a learning center in town. I moved to Tulsa for one year to attend a community college, but I dropped out due to family issues. I enrolled at a different community college in my home town and enrolled in classes.

I have had jobs that were very relevant to the events and topics that interested me. Most of my jobs have been in the automotive industry. This industry has been a recurring theme in my career; several of my jobs have been for the same company or very close to each other. When I first started working, I was really interested in this industry; it didn’t matter what job I had, I would take it because I wanted to learn about the industry.

In 2016, I was pregnant with my first child and I was laid off from my job due to the economic downturn. I decided to continue my education and obtain a degree in a STEM field. I enrolled at a four-year university and took prerequisites until I could transfer and pursue my degree.

During the time that I was pregnant, I had plans to go back to work and continue my education. I was even interviewing for jobs in order to save money for childcare expenses. I really loved the engineering department at my school; every single person seemed to be focused on improving their skills and helping others improve their skills. It was really encouraging for me, and it showed me that I wanted to become part of that department. It’s very motivating to me, and it helps me to want to take the next step.

I graduated with my engineering degree in May of 2019, and I moved back to my hometown to begin my career in the automotive industry. I have been employed with this company for about a year now, and I am in the leadership development program. I really enjoy my job and the people that I work with. The company treats its employees well and I see a lot of opportunities to grow with them. This job was a good opportunity for me to put my knowledge and skills to the test.

Many of my friends are in the business management field and they have been extremely supportive of me. My friends have told me that they are very proud of me and that I have the potential to go far in my career. It’s been a really good experience to have a group of people that believe in me and want me to succeed. I wouldn’t be where I am today without their support.

The events that made me who I am, were really important and impactful, especially the events that happened in my childhood. My parents did a great job raising me. We moved frequently, which gave me the ability to adjust to any kind of environment. It also made me more independent and helped me to make friends quickly.

I also had a lot of responsibility in my childhood. I had to take care of myself and help my parents when they needed help. I remember the summer that we moved back to Oklahoma. My dad had a series of knee surgeries and he couldn’t walk; my mom had a lot of work to do to catch up on all the things that she put aside to take care of my dad. My brother and I had to help my mom with everything that needed to be done around the house, and then on top of that, I had to work and go to school. I learned a lot of important lessons during that summer, and I think that it taught me that I need to be responsible for my actions and for taking care of others.

It also gave me the opportunity to help my dad out, and for him to appreciate me. I helped him learn how to get in and out of the car when he couldn’t walk. I helped him prepare his food. I helped him with his exercises. It was a really rewarding experience for both of us.

My childhood was really fun. I have two brothers; we always played in the woods and played around the house. I enjoyed every minute of it. I wasn’t too concerned with school at that age, so I had a lot of free time to explore and learn about the world around me.

My parents were not afraid of letting me explore my own ideas. They didn’t have a set curriculum for me; I had to explore, read books, watch movies, and talk to people. It really helped me learn how to communicate and express myself. It helped me to think critically and to come up with my own ideas and plans. I also learned about the world around me. I learned that there were a lot of amazing and wonderful people in the world.

I also learned that there were a lot of challenges that I would have to face. I learned that people were not perfect, that life was not easy, and that I needed to be a positive person. I needed to have a positive attitude and positive outlook on life.

I also learned about my interests and what I was good at. I learned that I liked computers, video games, and sports. I was a really good athlete growing up. I also learned that I wanted to make a career in the science field.

My hobbies and interests have really shaped my career goals. I am very interested in STEM, and I feel that this is a great career to pursue. I enjoy reading and learning, and I think that this will help me to achieve my career goals. I also enjoy working with a team to achieve a common goal. I would like to continue to pursue my career goals and to grow in my profession.

My professors have been really supportive of me. They have encouraged me to take the next step in my education. I believe that I have the skills and knowledge to be a successful engineer. I have taken advantage of all of the resources that the university offers me; I have been involved in many clubs and organizations. I have also been part of the Alpha Sigma Tau sorority. It’s a great opportunity to get involved in the community and to learn more about other cultures.

This semester, I will be taking a STEM-related class. It will allow me to expand my knowledge and to apply my engineering skills in the real world. I feel that this is an excellent opportunity to be a part of the STEM community. I hope to make the most out of this experience, and I look forward to the opportunity to apply my skills in my career.

I believe that I can really excel in the engineering field. I have been given many opportunities to grow as a person, both professionally and personally. I am excited for the next step in my life and to continue to improve myself. I believe that I will do a great job in my career. I also believe that I will be happy and successful in my career.

I am also very interested in travel.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
Hey, I’m 25 and I was born in Medan, Indonesia. I love this country because this place makes me and who I am right now.

I moved to Jakarta, Indonesia when I was 18. I moved to Toronto, Canada when I was 19. I’m studying software engineering right now. Right now, I’m still searching for who I am and I’m still trying to find out what my purpose is.

The most important thing that I want is to create happiness to people around me, to share my happiness, and to give something to the world.

I enjoy playing video games, like, lol, even though I’m not good at it. I also enjoy video editing, and also making videos. I love to listen to music. I don’t have any favorite genre. I just like to listen to the music. I enjoy sleeping and eating.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I am an 18 year old citizen of the United States. I am from the state of Utah. I enjoy ice skating, skiing, flying and parachuting and I hope to one day jump out of a plane. My hobbies include racing (both in RC cars and for the masses) and reading and I enjoy stargazing on occasion as well. I do enjoy driving for myself but I am partial to drive a Honda Civic or a Volkswagen Jetta.

I worked at Rocky Mountain ATV/MC to get more driving experience and I also am qualified to be a cashier there which should show my ability to work with customers. I also worked for HobbyTown USA as a cashier for 2 years and that gave me additional experience as well. I am currently the manager of my local Scouting Club. This requires excellent management skills and the need to prioritize properly.

When I was 16 my father was in a car accident. He suffered a traumatic head injury which left him unable to do some basic things like speak or walk and he needed constant supervision from me to prevent him from hurting himself. It was an experience that taught me patience and humbleness as well as how to handle and care for others who were injured.

Studying engineering requires using math frequently so I have always had to be able to do more than just read words and follow directions. I have always had to understand the “why” and not just the “what”.

In addition to my schooling, I feel I can maintain a positive attitude and do well.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
My name is Robert Pedersen and I am currently a 25-year-old attorney licensed in Wisconsin. I was born in Columbus, Ohio and grew up in Westerville, a suburb about 20 minutes outside of Columbus. Both of my parents are from North Dakota and have lived their entire lives in Westerville. As a child, I was very active in sports and greatly benefited from the kindness and commitment of my coaches. From my coaches, I learned the benefits of hard work, sacrifice, and service to my team. Sports taught me so much more than just how to throw a ball. From a very young age I knew that my life was going to involve sports somehow.

My parents divorced when I was in middle school and my brother and I moved in with my dad. This was one of the biggest events of my childhood. My father is a very humble, hardworking man who got me my first paper route, my first car, and taught me how to be a hard worker and have strong morals. He also helped to shape me into a man that enjoys hunting and the outdoors. My mom remarried when I was in high school and it was a great experience for me to get to know and spend time with my stepdad. Having two good men in my life at the same time was a great experience. I am close with both of my parents and both of my step-parents.

My grandparents are my heroes. They have always provided an abundance of love and wisdom to me. From my paternal grandparents, I have learned how to provide for my family and to take care of my children. They also taught me how to have a strong relationship with my faith. My maternal grandparents are just fantastic people that I love very much. They were teachers in Westerville and have always taught me the value of education. All of my grandparents have worked hard to be the people that they are today. My grandparents have helped me to become the person that I am today and I hope to be as good of a grandparent as they have been to me.

As I mentioned, I was very involved in sports as a kid and I played football through high school. One of the best experiences that I had in high school was playing football for a team full of outstanding young men. I had some coaches that were really great leaders and provided an example for me to follow. I always tried to do everything that I could to be a good teammate and I have kept that mindset since then. The lessons learned from being a part of a team in high school will stick with me for the rest of my life.

I was very fortunate to attend and graduate from The Ohio State University. Ohio State was an absolutely amazing experience and one of the greatest decisions that I have made in my life. My goal of getting into law school was to become an attorney and to be able to work as a prosecutor in order to protect the rights of children. Although I was an undergraduate at OSU, I knew that I wanted to work with the children and families of central Ohio. Working as a prosecutor would be the best job for me because I want to use the law to help make the world a better place. I will always advocate for children and families and be someone that they can trust.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: Well to start I am a 22 year old Caucasian American male from New York. I grew up fairly poor on the lower east side of NYC and my parents got separated when I was 4 years old and I was with my mom. I was enrolled in public school until I was 9 years old when they had difficulty getting financial aid for me. My mother got involved with more crack-cocaine and was eventually incarcerated and she claimed to be straight-edge after that and never talked to me about my dad again. I was left with relatives on Long Island who held multiple household jobs to keep us there. I had several families who cared for me but they were always strung out or too unorganized to keep things together. I was finally enrolled into private school as an “at risk student” and was enrolled into a residential program when I was 14. I saw a therapist, psychiatrist, and a supervisor constantly through that time. I was able to see my mother sometimes and she told me her ex-husband had her put on anti-psychotics but I know that is an outright lie since my mother has never had a problem with delusions or hallucinations even before she did crack. I was able to take medication that was easier to manage and I received both emotional and educational therapy for that time. I moved back to the city when I was 17 and have had several jobs since then, including working for the local McDonalds where I was harassed frequently and treated as if I were 10 years old. I was then hired in customer service because of my good voice and facial expressions. I started going to college since then but got depressed and stopped attending.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I was born in in 1984 and grew up in a small Missouri town where most everyone was born, grew up, and then buried themselves. About 10 years ago I moved to the east coast and have lived in Washington D.C. for the last 3 years. I am currently 26 years old and still haven’t figured out what I want to do when I grow up. For years I held the notion that I would go to law school, I have taken the LSAT and got a mediocre score that probably wouldn’t help me even get into a third-tier law school. Like most early career-advancing folks I had to develop an answer to the “What matters to you?” question. Well, I was hoping that maybe my current job had something to do with what mattered to me, as I work as a case manager for individuals with developmental disabilities. This field of work was relatively new to me, and I had never encountered folks with intellectual disabilities, much less have any former experience with mental health diagnoses. Working at a job that both challenges me and pays me has been immensely rewarding. In many ways, this career has transformed my life. I am trying to work towards, and overcome, many personal issues and am honestly trying to find some happiness in my own life.

Currently, much of my life is consumed by a fight against depression. Fighting an ongoing battle against the condition is exhausting, especially when the struggles are so out of my control. In recent years, depression has, rather aggressively, taken over my life. It has created the anxiety and hesitation in my desire to pursue higher education. I can’t even force myself to apply for jobs that would be within my skill set, mainly due to the fact that my physical and mental health isn’t allowing me to do anything else. What matters to me is, literally, having the energy to get through each day and that means doing it alone. Most days, I cannot overcome my own struggles, I cannot confront the loneliness, boredom, or sadness that constantly lingers in my life.

Some days are better than others, some days I can get out of bed and take a shower, other days I am down for the count. Currently, I am working part-time as a direct care professional for an organization that supports folks with developmental disabilities in their homes and communities. I have been working as a personal care assistant for the same organization for over a year. I want to take advantage of the opportunity I have been given and I would like to develop as a direct care provider, and move up within my organization. I believe that this job is important in my fight against depression as well as my overall happiness. Most of my free time is spent either playing video games or sitting on my couch doing nothing. I don’t have many other hobbies or talents other than volunteering my time to help out at my local Veteran’s hospital. I know, that if I want to make a difference in my own life and in the lives of those around me, I need to start working to get past the major roadblock, my depression. I hope I have answered your questions in a complete manner and with appropriate detail. I appreciate this opportunity to tell my story and I hope that I am the best choice for the opportunity that you seek to fill.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
My name is Brandon Wu. I'm almost 26 and live in the town of Greenfield, Massachusetts. I grew up in Greenfield but have an older brother who lives in Worcester. (The word Worcester must have struck a chord with him because he said the same thing when I told him that.)

In Greenfield, I live with 2 roommates. One is a social worker working with youth, and the other is an illustrator who also paints pictures for toys and clothing. Growing up I was extremely musical and had a passion for sports as well. These two things have remained to be as important as they were growing up. I was in 4 different symphony orchestras, and 2 jazz bands, was involved in sports for the majority of my life, and continued to play music. I also graduated from Berklee College of Music, where I studied jazz composition and guitar.

I really think it was childhood me who cares about things more than anything else in my life. I'm still an avid sports fan, I still love music, and I just have a simple human curiosity which makes me want to learn about other cultures and people. I have spent a significant amount of time abroad as a volunteer, backpacker, and worker. I've been to Sri Lanka, Nepal, South Africa, New Zealand, and all around the US working different jobs and traveling in all of those places.

I'm also extremely proud of my family. My younger brother was just named the winner of the World Fireball Windsurfing Championship in Nicaragua. I also just found out this week my brother in law John (my sister's husband) has cancer and is starting treatment this week. This is honestly the biggest tragedy in my life, and really makes me feel helpless because I want to fix it. My brother is the most genuine and kind person that I know, and I want to fight for him and help him in any way possible.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: Sure. I’m a 21-year-old student majoring in engineering from Portland, OR. I grew up fairly unstable, my mother worked hard for an abusive man, and I’ve been caught between having a normal relationship with my mother and a dependent relationship with my father ever since I can remember. I grew up with two stepdads, and my first grade teacher was assaulted by one of them. As a child, I struggled with suicidal thoughts after once thinking about how a bridge across the Columbia river would make a good solution for my family problems. My mother’s parents tried to tell her how bad they thought he was, but she wouldn’t listen, and eventually I lost trust in my mother. I was like a child soldier with depression. I tried to be a kid, but my parents had problems that didn’t allow me to. I tried to tell my dad how worried I was, and he said, “I’m concerned, but what do you want me to do?” I saw my stepdads hit my mother, but I was powerless to stop it because of my mother. My stepdads did a lot for me, and I lived with them to keep the money to pay my way through school, and I would be on my own soon enough, but I was hurting. They weren’t horrible. I learned how to help people from the mental and physical abuse I experienced growing up. From my perspective, I have a really weird life story. I’ve lived with two really different people, but I’ve seen both sides of the world: dysfunctional and functional. But through all of this, I worked hard, and I made it. I’ve lived alone for a year now. I need a job to keep on top of my college debt. I chose job as a greeter because it seemed easy to me, and I love interaction with people. I’m also a great listener and I think that’s a key component to keeping an emotional high while working a low skill job like this.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: Okay, so I'm a 20-year-old male living in Texas. I grew up in a low-income suburban household with strict parents. I would constantly get in trouble with my mom whenever she found out I was going out with my friends, she always called the cops whenever I came home from school late. (In my defense, I had some of the most dedicated friends from my hometown, who would never just leave me behind.) I grew up with all sorts of influences that formed me to who I am today. I wanted to be like Chad Brocks, who was a former pro wrestler, he also hosted a TV show called America's Funniest Home Videos. People at school liked him, he was the cool guy of the class. I remember countless days of me getting trash talked from all of the people at school about his misfortunes and how he screwed up, but I defended him and said that he is great. As a result, people started picking on me, I never cared, I only had one goal in mind. I didn't care about all the fights that I had gotten into with other kids, I only cared about serving them payback by being the best at school, I didn't care about all the times when I had to get in trouble with the cops, I only cared about making sure my friends were safe, I didn't care about how many people didn't like me, I only cared about Chad Brock. Chad was what kept me afloat and so far, I was having quite the year, while some of the dumbasses who mocked me had all their girls leave them, and some of them failed while I succeeded. Anyways, what I've been doing over my time living is knowing what you are capable of and strive for it, something that I want to accomplish now is to study a hard course in the career of law and eventually become a lawyer. The only way I can do that is to achieve the GPA I need to get my scholarship and just keep it up.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I am a 24-year old man. I grew up in a beautiful small town called Montpelier. I lived with my dad and mom until about a year ago, when my mother passed away. Losing her has been one of the hardest things I’ve ever experienced, but it helped me to realize that, life’s journey, while it involves a lot of hard work, and stress, and hard decisions, it’s also a wonderful gift. It’s a gift that God has given to us, and when we’re not focusing on the hard parts, and we’re looking at the things that matter, we get the joy and the privilege of really living the life he wants us to. To me, that means balancing work and school with living life and enjoying family. I’ve also learned to laugh a lot, and even in the hard times, finding ways to laugh are so important to help you keep your soul and your spirit strong, so it can endure. I go to work, I finish school, I take care of family, I try and make myself a better person, I try to understand who I am and what my purpose is, and I try to follow God’s commands and make sure I’m doing his will. Outside of that, I love to spend time with family. I’m a TCK, I’m multilingual and I’m multi-ethnic, so I have a lot of different cultures that I feel connected to. I still enjoy watching a good T.V. series sometimes, playing games with my brothers, and staying busy outside, even if it’s just riding around the neighborhood. But, I’m at a point where I just need to take care of myself, take care of my family, and work hard so I can leave this world a little better than I found it.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
My name is Emma. I'm 22 years old. I was born in Rhode Island and moved to Michigan after a few years. As a child, I suffered from a lot of bullying in school, and this caused me to sink into a deep depression. I felt worthless and like my life had no purpose. I contemplated suicide many times.

I had to move to another school, and I immediately saw that things would be different. I was heavily bullied for my skin, I was immediately ignored and I was bullied for everything that made me. They would make fun of my skin, my face, my appearance, my personality, what I said. When they weren't saying something, they were laughing at the people around me to make them feel bad. I quickly became the scapegoat of the entire school, people telling me I was irrelevant and that I would never get anywhere. There were people in school at my age who were gay, and they were fine. But if I stood up for myself, they would say that I was one of "those kids" who just wanted to commit suicide because "if you kill yourself, you go to hell" and they did this as a joke. This eventually turned into rape jokes, sexual harassment of other students, and was not only blatantly racist, but misogynistic, and homophobic. This led to physical assaults.

I reached out to the school counselors and tried to stand up for myself, but I was dismissed. One counselor said "I mean, it happens". They suggested that I take a drug that I didn't know what it was for, but she was offering to prescribe me anything that would help me feel better. They told me that I had to attend classes instead of one-to-one meetings.

I eventually got out of my depression, and I still talk to these people every day (I'm now friends with them). I realized that my goals in life were to be a counselor, a psychologist, a journalist, and a successful executive.

I had to leave school because of health issues. I was diagnosed with Cystic Fibrosis, a disease that could kill me at any time. I learned to appreciate life, and I began to feel like I was growing up. I have now moved to California to work with teenagers and to work as a counsellor and as a psychologist. I've had a lot of good experiences, and my life has really turned around, and I think you should know it.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I had a pretty normal childhood, a little more than a few years in Southeast Texas, living in a small duplex in a development called Innsbrook Forest, just south of Beaumont. I graduated from Central High in 1995, but was captain of the wrestling team in my sophomore year. This is probably the most notable part of my high school career. I'm not a fazeio person but I had some really good friends. I was a year younger than most, so I was a junior until I was 18 years old. I went to work at Auto Zone in 1996 with a friend who had just graduated from Texas A&M, though I really wanted to go somewhere, anywhere, with no intention of actually staying in Texas. My first experience out of Texas was in Jacksonville, Florida where I worked for Superior Food Service, running chain samplers and catering. I won't go into that but it was only the beginning of a lot of difficult jobs. My relationship with this friend didn't end well, so I left Jacksonville. I came back to the Beaumont area and lived with my parents for a while. I got a job at the front counter at Hobby Lobby and used all the money I was paid to upgrade my car to a 1994, one of the coolest in my collection. I got another job at Auto Zone, and the next place I went, it was Idaho Falls, Idaho. I had applied to the local auto dealership in town, but it was sad to live in a car dealership all winter, with the cold, it was crazy. In the summer, I went to Bear Lake to go water skiing with some friends, had a blast, but knew my time was up there. I got married and moved back to Beaumont and returned to Hobby Lobby. That's when I moved back to Omaha, in January, 2001. I worked for a grocery store chain. It's sad to say that I don't think that I've had any job that has paid anything close to what I made then. It was a grand total of $21,000. I moved to Liberty, New York because the road to the Lake Winnepesaukee area was beautiful and I made really good friends. This is where I met Andrew, who was from Ann Arbor, Michigan and at that time, I moved to Fort Gratiot, Michigan for a job. My parents have lived in the Marysville area since 1996. My parents got divorced in 1998 and I moved back home to be with them while they dealt with their changes. I've since spent more time with my father than my mother. I've had serious relationships with almost all of the women I've dated and Andrew has always been the exception. Since moving back to Texas in 2004, I've worked for Reliant Energy until 2010 and spent some time with Logix Communications before I went to work for the United States government on 27 July 2010.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: My name is Jane Jones. I am 27 years old. I grew up in a suburban neighborhood in Pennsylvania. I come from a middle-class family. My mom was the first generation in her family to go to college, where she majored in Psychology. When she met my dad, they got married and moved into a small town. They were very religious, and my dad was very passionate about being supportive of his community. In college, my parents were very passionate about going to church every Sunday. When I was a child, I went to a very small preschool. My mom wanted me to have a good social life, so I spent a lot of time after school with other children. Since then, I have always been involved in many social activities. I have been involved in many volunteer organizations. Some of them are Habitat for Humanity, Mission work at my home church, and any local social outreach opportunities that my neighbors have invited me to. While in college, I started as an undecided major and slowly narrowed my interests down. I was interested in many things, but I finally settled on English and then History. I was not sure what I wanted to do after college, so I majored in both, and when I got my bachelors degree in History, I switched it to English. I decided to go to graduate school to earn my master’s degree in Education to become an elementary school teacher. I then did some teaching internships, and I decided that I could combine my interests and become a high school English teacher. After graduating, I was offered a high school teaching position in a small town in California.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am a 22-year-old graduate from the University of Wisconsin Milwaukee with a BFA in Acting. I was born in Columbia, Missouri and spent my childhood on farms in Northern Arkansas. I grew up around lots of uncles and cousins. I loved growing up in a family full of people, so after I graduated high school I decided to join the Army and meet as many people as I could. I went into the Army at the age of 18 as a CBRN specialist. One of my most memorable moments during my three years of service was when I went on a U.SO. mission to the west of Baghdad, Iraq and met a group of little girls who were so happy that I had brought crayons and coloring books for them. I was also a Community Action Officer and worked in a clinic, performing HIV testing and teaching people about the importance of drug and alcohol education.

I served two tours in Afghanistan and one in Iraq. I received awards for outstanding service in Iraq and Afghanistan, including Army Achievement Medal, Army Commendation Medal, Army Good Conduct Medal, Combat Action Badge and Expert Field Medical Badge. After my second tour in Afghanistan, I decided to leave the Army because I could not handle being away from my fiancée for so long. When I left the Army I decided to pursue acting full time. I am currently a professional actor, fight choreographer, voice-over artist, and speaker and I have a couple of feature films and television shows coming out soon.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
Sure! I’m 23 years old and grew up in Omaha, Nebraska. I went to public school K-12. In high school, I joined the debating team, the Academic Decathlon team, and the acting club. I believe this experience helped improve my communication and time management skills, as well as increase my confidence and general self-esteem. I went to UNO and earned my degree in accounting. I then joined AmeriCorps, which exposed me to real-life hands on teaching. I interned in the 1st and 3rd grade. I was given an opportunity to run an after-school program to prepare students for state tests. Eventually I was hired as the afterschool program director. Because I ran the program, I was able to come up with ideas on how to motivate students. One event I developed was a live game show at the end of the school year. I thought this would be good for my students as they always loved watching game shows. It really worked well, and I had a lot of fun. In addition, I had the opportunity to plan a vacation for 50 students to get away for a few days. It was just fun to see them have such a good time and build bonds that probably wouldn’t have happened otherwise.

I also got the chance to see how schools are affected by budget cuts. Some of the best teaching resources were axed, and the principal was going out of his way to buy things for his students. This inspired me to work in education even more. It also inspired me to figure out a way to help students succeed and still be able to do great things in my life.

So when I applied for Teach for America, I wanted to develop new tools and knowledge to reach and teach students. As an educator with TFA, I have learned many new skills that are essential to being an effective teacher. I am able to use those skills to impact my students. I have a better understanding of different educational and learning styles. My grammar is much better, and I have gained a better understanding of the teacher’s perspective in a classroom.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I’m 23 years old, born and raised in Mexico. I went to a very traditional private elementary school. However, my parents thought that we should have private English lessons, so we all had 2 English classes every week from 2nd grade till the end of high school. When I was 14, I was an exchange student for 2 months in California. I know it was a very short time but I loved it. My host parents were amazing. I loved the freedom I had, the trips I went on and just the way life was like there. I wanted to go to the US. My parents did not let me because they thought it would make me a lazy person who depended on others. After graduating from high school, I started college. After a year and a half in public university, I didn’t like it. In Mexico it’s almost impossible to get a scholarship in a private university or college if your family’s income doesn’t meet the 80% minimum. I decided to come to the US. I learned a lot of people come on a student visa and use it to get a Green Card or even citizenship. It seemed like a good option for me so I decided to come. The first time I came was in 2010 as an exchange student (F1) but since I did not complete my 1 year of English (EAP) at my university I was forced to return home. Now I am here in the US for the 2nd time. I am now a US citizen.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: My name is Taylor. I am 29 years old and am originally from Scott County, Virginia. I grew up with my parents and five siblings in a small town called Weber City. My mother is a special education teacher and my father is an administrator at a local hospital. Some of my most influential memories come from my childhood. I spent a lot of time with my younger brother and one of my older sisters going swimming and walking on our family farm. My parents, my grandparents and my siblings have always been a source of love and support for me. My father especially has been a major influence in my life and he has passed on to me a strong work ethic and sense of responsibility. I have always worked hard in school, and while in college I met my wonderful wife. When I graduated from college, I felt I had to work hard to accomplish my goals. I went to work for a local pharmacy and soon my position was outsourced to India. I was devastated because I felt like I wasn’t getting any where. I was determined to change things for the better and decided to start working on my Masters. After completing this program, I soon was promoted to regional manager. I work hard for my family and for their future. I enjoy spending time with my wife, our two dogs, and my family and friends. My wife and I enjoy helping others in any way possible. I enjoy reading and traveling.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I’m 24 years old, I was born in Ukraine, but moved to the United States with my family when I was 6. I grew up in California, went to a private school, and started my college life at a local community college, where I initially majored in Psychology, then Business, and in the end received a degree in Information Technology, all while working part-time at the on-campus student radio station where I used to get all my groceries paid for by managing a weekly radio show. The same year I graduated, I started my first entry-level tech job in the cloud computing business and later received an internship offer from Netflix to work on their video player. As I got older, I got into the habit of reading nonfiction books like you said (mostly business books), and reading helps a lot to improve your productivity, decision making skills, and makes you a better, more confident person overall. One way to remember this quote:

“Wherever you go, that’s where you are.”

Many experiences shaped me as a person, but two of the main events that I want to mention. The first was when I moved to the US at the age of 6, and the second was when I decided to go from a small university in my hometown to a new one thousands of miles away.

At 6 years old, I knew no English. When we first arrived, I thought it would be cool to say my first and last name in English because in Ukrainian we say it more like pronouncing a single name. My parents were like: “no, that’s a full name, and you can’t just say your last name.” I was like but it’s a cool one!

My last name, Yatsenko, sounds like the name James Bond because it’s pronounced similar, and it doesn’t have a separate first or middle name. We are all immigrants in the US, and even though I was raised here, I am not a typical American kid, and I will always have different traditions or views on certain things. Having a childhood like that made me have a combination of several views and cultural identities. For example, I am always very appreciative of people, even if they have a bit of an attitude, and it’s because of my Ukrainian heritage. That being said, we all know that being an immigrant in the US can be a rough journey, especially financially. I am always grateful for the opportunities that we were offered, and I don’t take things for granted.

The second thing I want to mention is, back when I was in my first year of community college, I was going to drop out. I was a few credits short of being considered a full-time student and because of that, I was not able to finish my classes in one year. I told my parents about it, and they explained that finishing my classes and getting a degree would be a good choice for me, since I could always work on business and money later.

The choice I made was to stay in school and finish up my courses, and it was probably the best choice I’ve ever made. In the end, I ended up getting much more than my degree, including the amount of wisdom, knowledge, and experience I gained during my time in college. As cliche as it sounds, you don’t know how smart you are until you really try to study and get something done. Don’t make excuses, do your best to get a great education so you can always fall back on it when needed. Your knowledge is your true wealth.

Now I’m at Netflix in San Francisco in a cool office, walking distance from the best restaurants, coffee shops, and other attractions that Silicon Valley has to offer.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am 20 years old, I was born on July 1st 1993. I was born in Fort Myers, Florida. I grew up around the beach, never out of Florida, so that’s basically all I know. I like to go to the beach, listen to music, hang out with my friends, and really anything that gets me out of the house. I’m really quiet so when it comes to hanging out with people, I enjoy it but I don’t talk much.

I actually wanted to be in the army all through high school, and that’s mainly because my family is so full of military. My grandfathers, uncles, cousins, sisters and I, were all in the military at some point in our lives. I spent a lot of my time around the military so it seemed like the perfect job for me. Unfortunately, my family always expected so much out of me because I was the third son and I felt like I disappointed them. They felt like I let the family down. So I lost interest in the military, it just seemed like a dull thing to do in my eyes.

My mom worked as a nurse for 30 years, and I always looked at her and figured that I wanted to do something that would help people, so that kind of led me into nursing. I love helping people, and I figured it was a nice way to make a living without having to kill anyone or feel like I failed everyone when I grew up. After doing some math, I realized there would be some pretty good jobs for nurses when I finished school, and now here I am.

When I first came to HCC I was so lonely because I had no idea what I was doing, I was always lost, I looked like a lost puppy. It was hard for me at first because I was trying to change myself for my family to make them proud, but I didn’t want to change who I am. They wanted me to change who I was to satisfy them but it made me unhappy. So I just fell back on what I actually wanted to do and here I am. After my first year at HCC, I took a break and thought about my life. I didn’t see a purpose for myself anymore because I was working 6 days a week 12 hours a day at the grocery store. When you’re working hard for something with little reward, then it feels pointless. After I quit the grocery store, I found out about aviation in a class I was taking. I was studying aviation, so I decided I would do something about it. I enrolled in HCC’s Aviation Maintenance program. But then my little sister turned me on to alternative medicine. Now I’m taking classes for the Paralegal program. I’d say that I’ve come along way.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I am a 21 year old girl from Upstate, NY. I grew up in a traditional Italian-American family. My father died when I was just 4 years old, so my mother is all I have. I am a senior at University of New Hampshire pursuing a B.S. in Mass Communications. After I graduate, I plan on attending law school at Suffolk University in Boston, MA. One very important event that made me who I am today was the passing of my father when I was only 4. I was very close to him. He worked for IBM and had many stories about working in an all male environment. I would always ask him questions about the different things he would see and experience and he always took the time to answer them. I remember asking him one day why there weren't more women working at IBM. He said, "We need to elect more women to congress and state legislatures because when that happens we'll see more women in powerful positions in the business world." I thought that was amazing. He truly recognized the powerful nature of women and he valued the intelligence that my mother and I both possessed. His passing was so shocking and difficult, but I took strength from his memory as I grew older. Now that I am a young adult, I understand that he loved me more than anything and just wanted the best for me. He would be proud of the women that my mother and I have become, and I am happy to follow his lead.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I’m in my mid 20s. I was born in DC, raised in NY. Both my parents are immigrants. They moved to the US in the 70s to be away from the Soviet Union. They lived in a Russian immigrant community in Brooklyn, but my father worked in finance in Manhattan. My parents met when my mom was a model and my dad was a photographer. My dad was one of the first photographers to document the Russian immigrant community in the 70s. He moved to Manhattan and became an artist. I think I inherited his talent for making art, and also for running away. He always had a wandering spirit and that spirit runs in my family. I grew up surrounded by books and art and music. I was constantly looking out the window, seeing what’s out there, dreaming of the future. I’m from a big family. I’m one of 8 children. We all lived in Brooklyn in a small apartment. It was the 90s and we were always walking down the street, hanging out with friends. It was really fun and great. And it also meant we didn’t have much money. We didn’t have much space. My mother cooked for us all, she sewed our clothes, she bought us books, we played in the park, and that’s what we did. We didn’t go to the movies or the mall or spend time at home, because there wasn’t space. We were also constantly traveling, because my parents wanted to show us the world. They took us to the beach and the mountains and museums and we traveled to Russia a lot. I was always with my mom and sisters, looking for adventures, getting into trouble, getting lost. We spent a lot of time together, and I think that’s what made me who I am. Family is really important to me. When we’re together, we’re close and it feels like a really good thing. I also care about equality and diversity. I think there should be more people of color in positions of power and more women in politics. I think that because we’re in a political climate right now that’s really divided, and I want people to come together and work together. I think we’re all connected, so it doesn’t matter where you come from, or what you believe. I don’t know, I think I’m just really lucky to have been born in the 90s, when there was a lot of change in Russia. I grew up in the 90s, but I also grew up in the 80s, when things were really bad in Russia. My parents left Russia because they were afraid they would be sent to a labor camp. They were afraid of being sent to a labor camp because they were anti-communist. My dad had been in the army, but he defected to Israel. He wanted to leave because he wanted to study art. My mom was a model, but she didn’t want to be a model. She wanted to be an artist. I don’t know, I just feel really lucky to be here and to be who I am. I’m so grateful for my parents and my sisters and all the adventures we had.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I am 24 years old and was born and raised in a suburb of Los Angeles, California. My mother’s side of the family was from Germany. A few of my cousins still live in Bavaria and I have always felt like Germany was my second home. I feel a strong connection to the German culture and the country. A small number of my father’s family still live in Italy but the rest of them have migrated to southern California. I was born in the summer, approximately 2 weeks before my parents’ anniversary, so I am an only child with two loving parents. My father worked for the same company for over thirty years and recently retired. It was a local company near where we lived and it gave him security. My mother had an ability to adapt to a wide range of circumstances, so she was always able to find employment. Both my parents believed in the value of a college education and were instrumental in my success in school and preparing for college. I enjoyed going to school. My favorite subjects were Mathematics and History. I enjoyed learning about other cultures, places, and people. My family and my friends are the most important things in my life. They are the people I relate to most and it is these people who are my family. We always spend Christmas together and I also enjoy getting together for BBQs during the summer months. I enjoy running in marathons, especially ones with history behind them.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I am a white, female, born and raised in Anderson, Indiana. I am 24 years old and graduated from Anderson University.

I grew up pretty normal. I went to church, to school, and through home school. I grew up with two sisters, but no brothers. My sisters and I have a close relationship and have been best friends for as long as I can remember. I have a close relationship with my family and a good relationship with my parents.

I attended Anderson High School for 9th and 10th grade, and then finished school with home schooling. I ended up taking on a job at a fire fighting academy where I became a firefighter.

I grew up hearing the stories from my dad about the Vietnam War. My dad was a navy pilot in the war. I also had a stepmother who was a nurse in the war. She ended up losing her husband during the war. Also, I have another friend who’s father was a navy pilot in the war. Her father was a second lieutenant, who was shot down and killed during the war. These personal stories stuck with me and shaped me as an adult. I joined the US Navy Reserve after High School and currently work as an EOD Technician on a firefighting crew.

I have two dogs at home which I enjoy taking on walks and teaching tricks to. I love music, I especially enjoy the drum sounds. I also enjoy shooting guns. I have many different guns that I enjoy shooting and spending time with friends shooting.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I’m 28. I grew up in Long Island. New York. I was raised by teachers. My stepfather is a former principal, my mother is an educator. My sister’s a teacher and psychologist. My father was an engineer. He wanted my brother and I to study engineering, so we spent all our time in workshops, doing computer simulations and electrical experiments. My mom loved sports and was an athlete. She has a great heart. She used to speak for me before I even knew what the words “fine” and “hello” meant. That all that we’ve taught are gone, but I feel lucky that I have such role models.

I don’t do so many experiments anymore. But I love to read. I’m a book worm. I’m reading a lot of eastern and western philosophical literature. I’m trying to make sense of the world, trying to figure out what role I’m supposed to play in this universe.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: Well, let me see. I am currently 26 years old. I was born in Los Angeles and grew up in the wealthy suburbs of Encino. I attended the finest private Christian school in the area. When I was in the ninth grade, my parents got a divorce. It was a really difficult time for me. My mom had been abusive towards me as a kid, and she left the country when I was 16. I needed to find a way to pay my way through college because my parents were either too embarrassed or too scared to help me. Then I heard that Branson Marcus was hiring for the local Hunt Club, so I got an interview and was hired for the job. I have continued working at Hunt Club for three years now, and I would like to be made an equerry to Branson because I have known him since I was a child, and it seems fitting that I give service to a man like him. What I enjoy is hunting and anything outdoors. I have a passion for unique sports, such as navigating caves, using a scuba suit to dive in rough oceans, and mountain climbing. What matters to me is giving. I would love to give service to a kind man like Branson, and I think this job would let me do so. I also want to give back to my country. I want to pay my citizenship dues and be allowed to do that. I also believe it is my duty to defend the rights and freedoms I have been given, and I believe Branson will give me the chance to do that.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I am a 24 year old senior, studying Asian Studies at the University of California, Santa Barbara. I have lived in San Diego for the last four years, and I am originally from Idaho. I am scheduled to graduate with a degree in Asian Studies in June, and I plan to stay in San Diego to pursue postgraduate work in Japanese language and culture. I was raised in a conservative Mormon family that believed in the “rugged individual”, self-sufficiency, and law and order. I grew up under the authority of the church leadership, hearing about the divine right of the prophets. I was subjected to the teachings and ideals of Joseph Smith, Brigham Young, and Thomas S. Monson. I was taught not to ask questions of anything my leaders taught me, because that would imply that my leaders could be mistaken. I was taught that the only way to salvation was to become like the Savior, Jesus Christ, and I was taught the doctrine that the original people on the American continent received the gospel and were taught by the Savior himself after his death and resurrection. I was a missionary in Fresno, California for two years, where I learned to “proselytize” and go house to house, meeting new people and converting them to our religious cause. I have three siblings, one younger brother and two sisters, and we all went to the local schools before I went to the Mormon university that I attended for two years. I am now living in an apartment, renting a room with another Mormon girl. She is from Vietnam, but we share the same religious values and ideals. I have also become friends with many other people from Vietnam, Laos, and other Southeast Asian countries. I have come to be open-minded and welcoming towards these people because of how open they are towards me. I have come to see my upbringing as cultish, and I can’t wait to leave this place after I have completed my degree. I am eager to grow, learn, and move on with my life, and I enjoy reading about other cultures, so I am sure my life will be exciting from here.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: Me: “I’m 23 years old. I grew up in a small town about 5 minutes outside of a small city about an hour north of Buffalo, New York. We lived in a small home on a little farm with my brother, mom and dad. We had one dog at the time. That dog’s name was Chloe. She’s the reason I don’t have a dog anymore. As a kid, I spent all day outside having fun with my friends. I used to live for vacations because I love Disney world and fun summer days spent at Cattaraugus State Park in New York. My best friend lives just down the street from my childhood home and I always say that to me – home is not a house but a place that is surrounded by the people I care about. When I graduated high school I went to college full time for a year and a half but after my first year I took a year off and worked full time for a company called Tesoro in Maine. After I took that year off I moved to Alaska where I got an amazing job as a casino dealer. My job kept me busy and living in Alaska helped me grow as a person. But after a couple of years, I was ready to get back to the lower 48 states where my friends and family were. I have lived in California now for 3 years and have started college online to get my degrees in Business and Marketing.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I am 27 years old, born and raised in Chicago, US. From my early years, I had trouble fitting in. I grew up as a unique individual from a mixed parentage: from my father, I inherited a Russian Jewish mother. My father is from a working class neighborhood with a mother who only finished the 9th grade and a dad who never graduated high school. On the other hand, my mom was born in the Bronx into a family of doctors and lawyers; so naturally she did not fit in in the schoolyard. I did. I went to an elite private school, where I read every single encyclopedia from A to Z, graduated in the top of my class, and was immediately accepted into the most prestigious college in the US. I majored in Economics and felt that I fit in right at home in the library where I spend 90% of my time. In college, however, people started to label me – I was a Russian and had a Russian mom, the geek who never drinks, the political activist who talks about how America’s evil. In high school, all my friends were Caucasians with Christian or Jewish backgrounds, and I was the only black and Jewish kid, so college felt so safe in comparison. My college friend broke my safety bubble; we were walking around Boston, when one of the homeless people started yelling at us for whatever reason he had, but my friend yelled back in a scary way and made sure everyone understood that he is Jewish and this is his religion. I almost forgot that not everyone was so passionate about God, believing that everyone believes in Jesus – in a respectful way, of course. In retrospect, college and grad school were some of the happiest years of my life, but at the time I couldn’t really enjoy it; I missed my home so much, and being in the shadow of my perfect parents – the image I created in my head – was becoming unbearable. That is when I took a gap semester to travel. With little money, I went to Asia alone, lived there for a year, worked as an English teacher, and became wise from all the cultures and people I met. I never felt so happy. When I came back to the US, I finished my Master’s degree, moved to New York City, and got a job. At first, I thought the gap semester broke the ice of socializing for me and I made tons of friends, but then I found out that all of them were fake, and everyone there was consumed by ego and money. These people were nothing like my college friends and those from my neighborhood back home.
