As one of my only friends, Dave, put it: “You are the enemy.” I felt so lost at work and decided to quit my job after a month. I did not get the usual “out of the box” responses that I always got in the interviews. After two years of searching, I started working for a very small food company which was based in Chicago and did not like Chicago at all. People who work there complain about the same things – not getting enough attention from investors – and in a way, they are quite right. After two years, I felt comfortable enough to ask for another offer, but this time the market was different and I was not getting any responses. I started to Google how to improve my resume and found your website and emailed you for a consultation. I did not know what I am going to do, but I knew I needed help. After working on my profile for you, and taking your advice to improve my interviewing skills, I received two phone interviews, and they were just the right timing for me. This time, however, I only applied for something that I can feel for. No more soulless tech startups, but a food company, that makes delicious cookies. I received two calls and the second one resulted in a job offer, and I love it. I am in the best state of my life, I’ve never been happier or more relaxed. Thank you for helping me improve my profile, for giving me advice and encouraging me. Now I can finally go back to travel and world explore, and continue what I left off when I was in my 20s.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I am 29. I am from Trinidad & Tobago. My wife of 5 years immigrated here from Trinidad in 2014 and I joined her in 2015. We currently live in Connecticut. We are expecting our first child soon. I grew up on the Eastern Caribbean island of Trinidad. I was raised with a strong work ethic. I come from a middle-class family. My parents are both government employees. My father is a third-generation Trinidadian, while my mother is a first-generation Trinidadian. My maternal great-grandfather immigrated to Trinidad in 1910 from Barbados. I am from a mixed-race family (african, indian, and chinese descent). My first language is English but I am fluent in our national language, Creole (English-based). I completed my Masters in Computer Science at the University of Connecticut. I taught as an adjunct professor of Computer Science for 3 years while I was completing my Masters. After graduating from UConn, I began working for the US Army (Transcom) in 2011 as a Contractor for a subcontractor of General Dynamics. I have 8 years of experience as a database developer and DBA. I have done web development for about 4 years. I am very comfortable with all things related to computers. In fact, I knew I would be working in the IT field by the time I was 8 years old. Also, in my spare time, I like to grow fruits and vegetables in my backyard.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:

Then again I can easily play at all and have fun with crowds as much as with individuals. Everything I want is in my hand and I become a happy being when everyone around me is equally happy.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am 24 years old. I was born and raised in Orange County, California. I am part of a blended family with a loving father and a great mother. They supported and guided me throughout my life until I joined the Air Force. My parents met when they both worked in the Navy (my mom as a nurse and my dad as a helicopter mechanic). After serving in the Navy for a few years, they decided to move to Arizona so they could pursue other opportunities. In our free time, my mother played the piano and my father was an avid golfer. This developed my love for music and sports. We were always doing something outside. When we weren’t playing in the backyard, we would go to concerts and baseball games. I was brought up in a sport-centric family. That’s where I was raised, in a baseball field.

Music has always played a key role in my life as well. I started taking piano lessons when I was about nine years old. I did it to become more well-rounded and to express myself without having to play a sport. However, while playing the piano, I have expressed myself through my music. My piano teacher was an older lady who was Russian. I liked her because she introduced me to classical music. Through that, I learned the relationship of classical music to operas and jazz. I’ve read a few books about the history of jazz and it has inspired me to start playing again. Not only do I like to play the piano but I also like to listen to jazz as well. Jazz has always been a part of me, ever since I was a kid. Some of my favorite composers and piano players are George Winston, Dave Brubeck, George Gershwin, Duke Ellington, Jerry Lee Lewis, and Jerry Garcia, who I got to see in concert when I was about 10 or 11 years old.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I am a 24 years old (turning 25 in July) single male Caucasian. I was born in Los Angeles, California but spent the first part of my childhood in Carmel, California. Carmel was such a beautiful place and the beach really influenced me to get into computers. For most of my childhood, I always saw people playing computer games that looked fun. I started using computers and looking into my first email account at age 11 which made me start to enjoy computers and the Internet. For most of my childhood I went to Carmel High School and really had a good time there. In fact, I met most of my friends there and while it wasn’t that hard of a school, I still managed to learn a few things. In 2009, I graduated high school with a 3.2 GPA (almost a 3.4 but 1 semester I had some issues and got a 2.1 GPA.) While going to school in Carmel, I also learned a lot about business. My dad owns his own business in Carmel and I would help him with small errands and stuff. He really inspired me to learn more about money and business since he did so well. I went to college for about 1.5 years and then decided I should come to Phoenix, Arizona. Here I enrolled to ASU and got my Associates degree in Arts. During this time I did a little part time programming and learning about computers. Upon graduating I decided it was time to go back to Carmel, California and work for my dad and continue learning about business.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I am a 24 year old College grad. I grew up in a very poor household. The surrounding neighborhood in which I grew up in isn’t exactly known for being the best. I had some rough experiences there but regardless I pulled through. I love to travel, in fact, the airline ticket I will receive will be from visiting Australia for three months. During my trip I was able to explore the world and learn about different cultures. I couldn’t begin to describe what it means to be an American, I feel as if you must live here to understand it. America is a beautiful country that doesn’t get the recognition it deserves. When I travel overseas, I experience the cultural differences and how our culture is diverse. I also learn about how things are done differently in other places and not better, just differently. On my travels I learned not to judge anything until you understand it. I lived in L.A. for 4 years at university and learned about a lot of the cultures within the world, I even visited Mexico for a day and was able to learn about a third culture. The one thing in which I feel is most important to me is the right to freedom of speech. If this right was taken away in the future I would be extremely displeased, as I have a lot to say. If this was taken away I would try to fight it until the very end. I also love my cats. I have four of them, they’re adorable.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: Well, let’s see. I am 27 years old. Born and raised in Canada. My father was a professor in physics and mathematics at a local university. My mother was a nurse and was always home when I was young. I have two brothers and sisters. One younger brother and younger sister. When I was in the 9th grade, my parents filed bankruptcy. I was angry and felt abandoned. During this time, I taught myself the saxophone and my mother supported my efforts. I had the possibility of auditioning for the Toronto Conservatory. I was accepted to the Toronto Conservatory of music in jazz and classical saxophone. It was one of the top ten schools in the world for that time period. I was accepted with full tuition and room and board scholarships. This was the best thing in my life to date and turned me into a different person. Suddenly, I had to deal with jazz greats and professionals. The pressure of being one of the best musician in the world at a very young age was too much. I began using drugs to cope with the stress. This led me to stealing and loss of self worth and self respect. I put the blame on my parents and wanted revenge for the financial hardship they brought upon the family. My continued resentment for them made me feel that they caused me to enter the drug world and I got deeper and deeper into drugs and my life began to spiral out of control. I was deeply hurt. My life was very colorful, exciting, and also totally messed up.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I am 27 years old. I was born and raised in Nashville, Tennessee and I still live in Tennessee today. Currently, I am living with my fiance in the Nashville area. In regards to personal, short term, goals, I hope to marry my girlfriend next year, buy a home of my own soon and be a father within the next 10 years. I also strive to make sure that my grandmother and my grandmothers first husband, my biological grandfather, see our child in their lifetime. My grandmothers first husband passed away in 1995, but I am sure that she would love to see a grandchild. And if she’s able, she would love to be a great grandmother. In addition, I hope to travel at least two new places in the world, visit my long time friends in Ohio who I have not seen in five years and start my own therapy practice in ten years. I hope to help children as well as adults. And in regards to my strengths, I was raised by a woman with a great amount of dignity, bravery, selflessness and a self made value system. The way she had to raise her children made me the strong, hard working, caring, tenacious, compassionate, thoughtful, people person that I am today. And I think that being able to understand, recognize, have empathy for others needs and feelings as well as abilities, and work towards balance in others lives made me a better therapist candidate. Also, I believe the change that I experienced in my own life from suffering great personal loss as a child to becoming very productive and successful in my own endeavors will resonate well with others who may be experiencing the same types of losses and/or struggles. I am very proud of my resume and my level of education, professional work experience and personal accomplishments and I believe that makes me a great candidate for the school psychologist position at Shelby County Middle School.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
Most children grew up in this society within the United States. I am one of those children, born in Houston, Texas. I am 23 years old now and I’ve enjoyed being part of a great culture. Our influence on others is immense. There is always something fun to do, so we have a great population of loving, caring people and I’m happy to have been born and raised here. Growing up in the US, living in one of its biggest cities allowed me to experience and understand how life is outside of the United States. So, I am aware of some other societies, their cultures, and people. I’ve met a lot of people and I find that US citizens are some of the most caring and helpful individuals. They always have a good attitude.

The care that US citizens have for their close ones is immense. My parents have created many memories for me, making sure to keep me around them, and always providing me with what they know I need. My family has and continues to be a very big part of my life. They shaped the person that I am today. I grew up in a very fun environment, always finding new things to do, new sports to try, with many friends and family to keep me active. I lived in the big city, but didn’t always stay inside the city limits. I wanted to explore and find new places, so I’d always tell my parents we were going on a road trip. There are still so many places I want to see, things I want to do, and many many people I want to meet. I love to travel and having two loving parents that teach me about the world as I go is truly a gift. I would love to have my own children someday and I want to provide them with the same guidance, and the same attention to teach them about the world as I have had.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I’m a 24 year old single mother who’s divorced. I moved to New York from Miami when I was 16 in 2005. In this order, I’ve worked as a cashier, a waitress, a kindergarten teacher, a freelance fashion writer, a bridal consultant, and I am now back at the restaurant where I started. While my favorite childhood sport was baseball, I had a lifelong fascination with shoes. When I turned 21, my parents gave me the gift of a Jimmy Choo bag, and I bought a pair of blue Choo’s. Over the years, I’ve often found myself analyzing friends’ wardrobes, referencing dozens of magazines in order to create the ideal ensemble, and dreaming of opening a shoe store. I’m also known by my friends and coworkers for my sharp wit and impulsive behavior (I’ve been described as “magnetic”). Despite these apparent strengths, I’ve had my fair share of failures. I was kicked out of one prep school, expelled from a second, and dropped out of a third. At 17, I ran away from home because I didn’t get the internship I wanted. At 19, I decided to return to a very difficult school, and I didn’t do well. I’ve had periods of depression, anxiety, and hopelessness. What’s made me who I am today is the ability to overcome my failures and try again. Even though my work ethic may be questioned by interviewers, my persistence certainly is not. I don’t allow myself to stop and I never give up. My parents abandoned me when I was 9, and they are no longer in my life, but I stay in constant contact with them because despite how I feel about them, their failings were never meant to destroy my life or my self-confidence. They were simply human, and I can only do my best to continue to grow and not follow in their footsteps. I am more myself when I’m with friends or alone. Among strangers, or when I’m not 100% relaxed, I can be self-conscious and shy, but not to the point that it impacts my ability to do what I have to do. What matters most to me are the people around me, and I always consider what they would do before acting, but I don’t usually listen to their suggestions. In everything I do, I focus on trying to be the best I can, and I treat others the way I would want to be treated. What I enjoy most are shoes, the outdoors (I used to ride horses), riding my pink Vespa, a good book, a glass of wine, or a latte.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: Well, I’m 26 and I grew up in Wisconsin, specifically Green Bay. I went to high school at Pulaski High School (my dad is a retired Pulaski High coach). I played hockey all four years of high school and was on the varsity team three years. My dad stills owns a home in Pulaski. I went to UWGB from 2011-15 and graduated with my undergrad degree. I was supposed to do a fifth year of school and got an internship in Middleton that went really well. I then worked my way up to assistant director in fall of 2018. I put in my two weeks to leave and go to work in Minneapolis, but I changed my mind and decided to stay here. I took a chance and got a different job at Blain’s Farm and Fleet, and worked my way to becoming a corporate trainer. In 2019, I had to go to Minneapolis for training for Blain’s. It was here that I got offered a better job and decided to work my way up to a manager job there. In December 2019, I quit that job and took a job in local home décor called Ramblin’ Rose, which I was assistant manager of. I had a chance to get the manager job there, but I left to take a better job, and I’m still at this job. Working my way up here, I became friends with the director of marketing and found out he was a Packers season ticket holder. This led to me reaching out to him on getting tickets to a game and asking him to help a season ticket holder out. He allowed me to come with him and I even got the carpool pass for the day to park free.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I’m 24 years old, recently engaged, and working towards an MBA in strategy and entrepreneurship. I have a background in music and have freelanced in a lot of different areas. I was born in the US and grew up all over the world.

I am starting to look into an extended world trip with my boyfriend, with the intent to live abroad for as much time as we can afford to sustain it. Currently that means renting out my apartment in favor of a more nomadic lifestyle, more travel than usual, and (hopefully) opportunities to freelance if we fall below our financial means.

Interests change over time, and I definitely have a wide range. I am a member of the Venture Out club at UC Berkeley, so outdoor activities like backpacking, rock climbing, skiing, and sailing are on the agenda for me. I like studying languages, baking bread, and getting to know how people’s mindsets differ from one place to another.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I was born in Mexico and came to the U.S. when I was 11 years old. I am currently 27 years old, working on my Bachelor’s of Science Degree in Criminal Justice from Suffolk Community College. I worked for 5 years at the Governor’s Office for Employee Relations as a Fraud Investigator, specifically in welfare fraud. I have over 8 years of experience in investigations and cold case management. I am highly skilled in investigative analysis and intelligence operations in order to work effectively with local and national law enforcement agencies, the community and target population in order to meet arrest and prosecution targets. In a team or individual capacity I have a proven ability to liaise at all levels within an organization, ranging from senior management to law enforcement agencies and external groups/individuals.

I am highly motivated, with a strong work ethic and respect for the structure of organizations and networks. I am able to accurately plan and manage workloads to ensure achievement of objectives with a proven track record in planning, arranging, organizing and carrying out complex investigations. I am innovative, creative and enjoy problem solving.

I am interested in this position because I believe that my skills and work experience will assist me in executing each task presented to me. I currently work as a Civilian Operator for the New York State Police, but I do want to further my career as an Investigator, and this opportunity excites me because I can see that I am a perfect candidate for the job.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I...I'm sort of shy so I don't really...well, I'm 20, and I grew up in Houston. I'm an only child. I got an allowance of $5 a week, and at 13 I took that $5 and I invested it in some stocks and I bought some soaps for businesses and things like that. I realized I really enjoyed businesses...so I've tried my hardest to get into the business field. I guess what matters most to me is my family. Growing up, my mom was usually divorced...so most of the time I'd stay with my dad. He worked a lot and was gone a lot, but he was pretty strict about making me to do my chores and get good grades. He always told me I could do anything I set my mind to, so I've always strived to be the best...I want to be somebody so people look up to me...I want to be the leader of the...for my family, I guess. My wife. My boss. And eventually, if I have kids I want them to feel as if I can trust them, and let them know that I'd like to help them in anything they do. So I really think that makes me who I am.

My 4th flight was yesterday. There was a girl there who looked to be my age. They put me in the plane with an instructor who was apparently just as uncomfortable as me about the whole thing. I think he didn't say much because he was learning about me, and I barely said anything because I didn't want to look stupid. We did the parallel wings thing, and honestly, I think I might have done better with my old instructor...That guy didn't pressure me.

Yes, I learned how to drift....but that's about it. I can't say I'm really looking forward to going back to do more, since I think I did bad and it made me realize I have a lot of room for improvement.

It would be great if any of you are pilots...just for kicks I want to know what you think of my performance.

I heard they're closing down the driving school we used to use, and after 3 failed attempts, there's no way I'm going to find another in time to find out if I'm still eligible. The deadline is September 1st or something like that. They're doing a last screening of qualified applicants on July 16th. I'm not a bit ready for it....I just want to have something lined up before I come home. Just keep your fingers crossed for me...

Miss you guys.

Jerik

If I had a dollar for every time I say sorry in this post, I would have enough to buy enough food to survive a week in Alaska. Let me start out by saying that I knew Alaska was a big deal but I wasn't exactly sure of just how big a deal it was. I don't think I ever really grasped just how far reaching the reach was. Just in the program I was in for 14 months, I knew that there were another 400 or so students at a location less than 100 miles away from me, and that they all went through the exact same program. I'm not sure of how many that would be, but there were a lot. I also knew that there were about 1600 to 2000 students at that location alone.

So what does it mean to finish a 14 month truck driving school and not have a job in 3 days? Well, let me put it this way...You finish school, come to class and take a test called DOT or Department of Transportation. The test isn't over until you get a passing grade. After that, you have to fill out some papers and take a drug test. (This is just for entry level driver jobs. For longer hauls, this process can take up to 10 days). You then turn in your papers for your complete physical, they get dispatched to the doctor of your choice. At the end of that 3 days, you are expected to get called for a job. Because there are so many jobs with trucking companies, they usually start calling the more experienced drivers who are already CDL certified first, and slowly trickle down to drivers who just got certified (by the way, training costs about $5000.00, which isn't something any high school student can afford, so they'll usually take the younger guys first). The thing is...just because you are already there and working doesn't mean that you're guaranteed to work a certain amount of hours.

So basically, being certified doesn't guarantee you a job. So here I am, able to work, but I have no idea where I'm going to be. Even though my instructors were nice and they were sure I'd get a job as soon as possible, it's still up to the trucking companies to hire me. So you can see how that's frustrating. Meanwhile, it's been a week and I still haven't got a place to live, and I'm getting sick of sleeping on people's couches. And I won't even go into how much of a money pit it is to have a truck, I mean, my spare tie rod snapped, and that alone costs $80 to fix, plus labor of course, and the tow. I'm not even sure if I'm going to get that fixed right away, because I'm expecting that to be one of the many expenses that will pile up. And I feel bad taking advantage of these people who have let me live with them so freely and without expecting anything in return. I mean, my goal was to save money and that has yet to happen. I'm going to let my generous hosts know that I have to get my own place soon and ask them to cut me off so I can start saving money to at least get a deposit on a place and maybe furnish it.

To top it all off, I have to submit my permit by July 1st, and if I'm not working and have no place to live, I'm not going to be able to start training immediately because I can't really say if I'll be allowed to move in with them for a while. As much as I want to go to training, it will depend on whether or not I can find a place.

I appreciate all the great comments you guys are sending, but I need some advice. In case I'm unable to go training right away, should I go out and drive anyway (make some money)? If so, then I guess I will lose out on training, but if I can't go, then I'll need to make money somehow.

If I've done wrong to any of you somehow, please feel free to call me on it. It's been a long time and I don't always remember how I treat people. Even if I have acted civilly, just the slightest hint of offense will do. I'll do what I can to make it right. It's not that I'm expecting forgiveness, it's that I want to learn to be more respectful. I appreciate your help.

As for your comments, thank you so much. Seriously, what is wrong with you people? How can someone put up a post asking for help in a time of trouble, then say he appreciates the positive feedback?

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I am 23 years old, and a senior at Lafayette College. I grew up in Frederick, Maryland. I enjoy playing soccer and music, particularly drumming. I became interested in public policy and sports through my various life experiences, which I will describe further. Growing up in Frederick, MD, which is located right between Washington, DC and Baltimore, I had the opportunity to observe lots of politicians and other politically powerful people, such as presidential candidates, at the nearby Washington National Cathedral, Washington, DC, or Baltimore. I also had an after-school job since I was 14 years old until senior year of high school, where I worked in a printing business called PhotoTree and was involved in every aspect of the business, from sales, to packaging, to printing, to book-binding. Through my different jobs, I developed a vast amount of experience with serving customers, marketing, and technology. The photo book printing business had its own Web site, which I was involved in designing. I also became particularly fond of exercising on a regular basis. I played competitive high school soccer as a keeper until my senior year, and continued to play recreationally in college. I enjoy soccer, and work hard to constantly improve my game. I like to listen to music and play drums, and I do so whenever I can. I always try to keep a positive attitude, as a more positive and confident person, I believe, will be more effective. I think its important to try to stay objective, but not detached. Its important to always think critically, but not believe just anything. Through my broad and varied experience in public policy and sports, I think I have a good background to understand the international nature of the problem, and thus work effectively to deal with the issue on a global level. I also want to pursue a career in public policy, and I think this internship would provide an excellent starting point to begin a public policy career.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
Well, I’m 23 years old and was born in India. I lived there for 10 years and had a very loving family. Then, my parents decided to move to the US. Growing up in the US was easy. I was always encouraged to study hard, I had many friends, and I enjoyed going to school and learning a lot of subjects, such as math, science, and English literature. My favorite subject was literature and I liked to write short stories and poems.

While I was in high school I received a scholarship to help pay for my college education. I chose computer science for my major at university. This was important for me because I believe that women should be encouraged to work in math and science fields. After college, I worked for a company that made software for students to use in order to learn English.

At the same time, I taught at a small school for disadvantaged students. My years of working with high school and university students taught me a lot about careers and earning money. After I taught students, I decided that I was interested in becoming an accountant. I received more training in accounting and became a full time accountant for a company. I also helped other students at my school learn math and English so that they could take college entrance exams and do well.

I love working with students and I enjoy doing my accounting job. I get paid a good salary and I feel that I am doing something important in my community. The most important thing to me is not money but the feeling that I am doing a good job. If I were to become an immigration judge, I would know that I was helping people. This would make me happy.

I feel that we are all connected to each other. I’m a person who believes in community. I feel that each one of us is a link in a chain that goes back thousands of years. In India, there are many stories about linked chains and the importance of community. For example, the Mahabharata is a well-known Hindu text which tells the story of a war between two groups of family members.

An Indian lawyer named Maya explained that if you were to take the largest sphere of gold in the world and attach a small pebble to the bottom, you would see that the sphere would sink. However, if you added many pebbles to the bottom, the sphere would rise to the top. She said that this means that every person matters. She said that if you don’t do your job, someone else’s job becomes harder and it has an impact on other people. She explained that it is important to do your job to the best of your ability so that all of us can enjoy life. I try to live by these principles.

In summary, I was born in India but grew up in America. I am 23 years old and I have lived in a number of different cities. I like working with students and I enjoy doing my job as an accountant. I am a link in a chain that stretches back hundreds of years and my job is important because if I don’t do my job, it is more difficult for others. The most important thing for me is to enjoy my work and to make sure that my work is done well.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I am 24 years old, born and raised in North Jersey. I grew up in a mostly white area where everyone goes off to college after they graduate high school and generally settle down. The dominant sports are soccer and hockey because of the high level of European immigrants living in the area. My mother was born in England and my father was born in Puerto Rico. I spent the first three years of my life in England, but after my grandfather passed away I had to move back to the US and this is where I grew up. As for my father, he served in the military for over twenty years and even spent time in Iraq. The first eight years of my childhood I had a good friend who lived on a farm next to me. He taught me most of what I know about farming. Later on I was diagnosed with cancer and now I am receiving monthly treatments for it. I live with my mother and I am currently living with her on the farm that we bought several months back. I wanted to get a better understanding of where my food comes from and it just so happens that my mother is vegan and a huge animal lover and this is the perfect place to live. She also agreed with me that it was important to have a career and to learn skills and that I should not put it off any longer and be free from debt.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I am 25 years old. I was born in Bilbao, in the Basque Country of Spain, and raised in a small town near the French border. My parents come from a small town near the Italian border, so my family is originally from Italy. My cultural influences and upbringing can be seen in the dialect of my Basque Spanish, or Euskera. I have always enjoyed learning about other cultures and speaking other languages. I am currently studying French, and I studied Spanish and German in high school. My passion for other languages and cultures has been a driving force in my career path. After graduating with my undergraduate degree, I went to Paraguay to teach English for two years with the Peace Corps. I loved getting to know the people and culture and share my own knowledge, especially when I got to do it all in Spanish! I am very happy to be able to share my experiences with the students at Colorado College, and especially as a native Spanish-speaker, I hope to help them become as passionate as I am about learning other languages.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I was born at New Orleans, but when I was 7, my parents divorced, and my mother sent me to live with my grandmother. So I moved to Portugal, the same year my beloved grandfather died.

There, I got a scholarship to attend University. I met my ex-husband there, so I lost my scholarship after I got married (I was 21 years old). I was pressured into getting divorced, and after that, I did not think I would ever find anybody in Portugal, so I went to Cape Verde.

I worked as an accountant there, and I got a visa to enter the US, where I met my husband. He was here on an H1B and I was here on a O1B. I was able to extend my visa, so after a couple years, we got married and my green card application went through. We divorced a couple years after, he was a jealous type of person, and my boyfriend at the time asked me to live with him in Japan.

He worked in a movie company and I, I continued my career as a translator for movies. I worked as a translator for a couple years in Japan, then came to US again, this time on a tourist visa and have continued my career as a translator. My husband-to-be and I went to Canada to get married.

He works in IT, and I made it through difficult times, then after a year my brother got sick and started a small animation business. We moved back to Japan, to help my brother. In Japan I kept on working in the animation business, and here I am now.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I am 28 years old, and grew up on the south side of Chicago. Our house was surrounded by other houses, but there was a forest like area across the street. It was green and I loved going there with my friends. We used to go in the creeks and just explore. Of course, as we got older we ventured farther into the city, around McCormick place, Soldier field, and further downtown. I was there in the 90’s and Chicago was not a great place to be. I’ve seen gun shots, car chases, and I’ve been stalked. I’ve had the scary experiences of breaking up a fight between men, helping women out of abusive relationships, and jumping in to break up altercations. We’re the generation where we know how to have a good time, but also know how to take care of business, if necessary. We know how to be tough, but still honor our elders and our mothers. I knew when I moved up to Michigan that I would have a good life, but I didn’t realize just how great it would be. I was telling my mom about this place that I live in and she could hardly believe it. Her house was in Chicago, across from garbage dumpsters. So when I say that I didn’t realize just how great it was, I’m serious. Being in the same place your entire life would have made me much different and I probably wouldn’t understand just how much I really have.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am 22 years old. I was born in Sumter, South Carolina, and moved to Acworth, Georgia shortly after my birth. However, after a divorce, I moved to Florida for a few years with my mom, and then to my current home of Atmore, Alabama.

Growing up, I was a big tomboy. One day my grandparents got me a pink bike, and I was furious. I cried and screamed and threw a tantrum that lasted the whole day. I never quite found the bonding experience between little girls and dolls to be for me, and I never got into many games. I played outside, worked around the yard and helped my grandparents as much as they would allow. If I got the chance, I read books. By my senior year of high school, my mom and I had taught ourselves the basics of repairing houses, and we would buy a house fix it up and then put it back on the market for a hefty profit. Unfortunately, this did not work out so well after the economy crashed.

My life was not terrible, but it was not good either. I did not have a bad home life, nor did I have a great one. I had a pretty average life. Looking back, I see a lot of events that made me the person I am today.

My family does not take very well to change. It is especially hard for my mother to make changes. She was in the military for about 3 years, and served a tour in Iraq. Her job was as a Scout. This was in the 1980s and 1990s, when there were almost no women in combat, and even less female marines who could travel with the Scout teams. I once asked her why she chose to be a Scout, to which she responded, “I could have been just as good a cook as everyone else, or as a typist. Why would I want to be that? If they were going to be in combat, I wanted to be there too.” If you live with a person like that, you eventually become a little like them.

Because I was a girl, I was expected to always be docile, kind, and interested in girly things. My mom never learned how to beat hair into submission. I wore my hair the way I wanted from the time I was five. If my mom did not like it, tough luck for her, that was what I was doing. I was not arguing. I was not kicking, screaming or behaving any differently than usual. I just did not want the choices my mom had made for me. Most of the time I was in the best case scenario. Most of the time I had long, beautiful hair. I had beautiful hair, when I decided to wear it down. My mom let me keep short hair. I have always been able to do my own hair, and a talent like that is very much appreciated in my house.

I did not only fight with my mom over hair decisions. I had to have control of what I wore, in general. I have always been a slightly quiet person, and very observant of the world around me. I did not talk much in middle school, mostly because I was always busy thinking and noticing. When I was young, I missed a lot. Now, I miss very little. When I was in middle school, this caused some issues. My peers thought I was not worth talking to, and that I was probably stuck up. I came up with every excuse I could think of, to keep myself from talking. Not many kids talk to themselves in middle school, but I sure did. When the urge would come, I would quickly think up a new excuse as to why I was not talking. After a while, I was able to convince the class I was a sociopath. The simple matter of the fact is, I am an observer. I observe. I listen. I make sure my stories are accurate. That is what is going on right now, and that is what I am here to do. I am not the most talkative person around. I am a good listener, and a good observer. I do not have the tendency to just say things willy-nilly, because they are usually not the most accurate, and they could end up causing trouble if other people find them false. I do not talk much, but when I am not around a teacher or some other authority figure, people might be surprised to know I can hold a conversation pretty well. Maybe not as well as my mother or as eloquently as most of my teachers, but for the most part, the conversation flows. The only reason I want to go back to school is so I can have a steady supply of new and interesting information that may not otherwise make its way into my field of view.

Next came the intense curiosity. I started questioning things about the world, not out of a desire to be difficult, but out of a desire to know and explore. I was not satisfied with just what I had already seen, I wanted to experience more, and I wanted to know more. This curiosity was an obstacle when I decided that I wanted to go to college. My mom and I have a different opinion on college. I want to go, she wants me to get an apartment and make a life for myself before I waste so much money. We have always had this problem, and I have already accepted the fact that if she refuses to take me, I will have to find other means of getting there. I have always had to provide for myself, and I will for the most part. I have always had to support myself, so getting my own apartment is not that big of a stretch, but there are some other factors. My mom is working on coming up with a compromise. If I had my way, I would have been able to go straight to college, and become anything I want. Sadly, with the cost of tuition being what it is, I have to get a job before I head off to college. By working, I can help pay for my tuition, and be sure that I do not have to accept financial aid. I have worked retail for some time, and I know I can make a fairly decent living at it.

I was a bit of a loner in high school, and that was a huge factor in making me the person I am today. I did have friends, don’t get me wrong. We used to go to the river on the weekends, and swim, sit around and watch the boats and drink. I have never drank before, and I do not like it, but to each their own. I had friends, but we did not always hang out in groups. My personal taste in friends is that they have to be more than a group of friends, they have to be a tight group of friends. I do not like to go out in the same social groups all the time, I like to change things up. I enjoy talking to people who have different ideas, and different goals. I tend to keep a lot of the information I get from my friends on the down low, just because I am an observer. I observe people, and usually come to conclusions about them. These conclusions are not always what people want to hear, so I do not talk about them very often.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am 27 years old and live in Madison with my family. My parents both grew up in Mississippi and I am the third child of four. I have a twin brother who is 26 and I love to play guitar and play games.

Do you have any pets?

I have three cats. We call them our clowders (meow-der is the plural for a group of cats). I have a Tuxedo named Kato, a Boxer Cat named Seymour, and a Calico Cat named Marty. Seymour and Marty both have fused claws (no use of claws), so it makes it difficult for them to scratch or climb. This has made me a believer in the benefits of kitty condos (a stepping toy with a rope), and I highly recommend them for people with cats.

Do you have any household responsibilities?

I have to empty my dryer about every three days because my cats like to “weasel throw” (squeak) little bits of fuzz into the lint catcher. It’s like watching Nyan Cat but without the bouncing.

Do you like pizza?

I like pizza. I like pizza with cheese, pineapple and ham. I think pizza with pineapple is one of the best inventions the world has produced since sliced bread. Hawaiian pizza is best served at room temperature. Let it get cold. The pineapple becomes like a fruit-based meat.

How much garlic do you like on your pizza? Favorite kind?

The more garlic, the better. I’m no herbalist, but I just know that garlic is a super food with all sorts of power-giving characteristics. I am fairly well-endowed for a person of my age.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I am 25 years old, but I am young and idealistic. I have an O-1 Visa (Entrepreneurial Artist), which I obtained from SVA’s residency program. I currently live in the Upper West Side of Manhattan. I grew up in Brooklyn, New York. I began drawing at a very young age. My mother is an art teacher, so I was exposed to art at a young age. I lived in the Far Rockaway in Queens for a good portion of my life. I recently graduated from Parsons the New School of Design. My parents were immigrants from Trinidad and Tobago. I grew up in a multi-cultural family with a myriad of activities in the area. My sister and I attended a French Immersion school. Growing up in New York City has been an enriching experience and opened me up to the world. Art has been my passion and I aim to use it to promote different forms of expression while creating a more cohesive society. In terms of current events, I was accepted into the Design Trust for Public Space’s FutureFest fellowship. It was a really cool experience to be a part of a community working on issues that matter to me. Lastly, I greatly enjoy movies, cartoons, comics, books, and action figures. I also enjoy the smell of books and gathering them in my arms. I love to draw and watch movies. Watching movies is a form of research for me. The more movies I see, the more I gain access to a variety of frames of reference. Movies aid me in my creative and mental development. They offer rich stimuli from which I can draw and interpret. Having my imagination stimulated gets me excited about art. Furthermore, cartoons are awesome.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I’m a 28 year old man, from northern New Jersey, born and raised in a mid-size city in the suburbs. I’m one of four kids with both of my parents currently alive, though some of their siblings aren’t. Though they aren’t living with me, I have two nephews and a niece who are much younger than me that also have an impact on my life and the person I am. As far as my social life goes, I’ve always tried to keep it simple and meaningful. I don’t have many people in my life that I can consider as a “friend”. All the relationships I have with people are quite meaningful. There’s certainly a small but incredibly precious group of people in my life that have meant a lot to me through the years and are the strongest people to this day, my parents.

As far as schooling goes, I’ve been and still am fortunate enough to have had a great education since birth. In terms of extracurriculars, I’ve been very sports-focused my whole life and, though I no longer play any sports recreationally, it’s something I’ve always had to keep in my life. As far as politics and social issues go, I can’t say I’ve spent a lot of time exploring the depth of these topics. Through high school, I was focused heavily on music and community service, which a lot of adults appreciated, including those I worked alongside. I can’t say I’ve ever felt the need to take any stance on anything political, not even when given the chance. This may be a result of my family’s cautious distance from politics, religion, and other similar topics. There is an incredibly respectful gray area in my mind for most topics or issues, allowing me the ability to focus on relevant issues, but not deviating too far from their gray boundaries.

As far as my job at Montgomery County Human Services, it is both one of the more meaningful and difficult jobs that I’ve held. As I started it around eight months ago, I was very intrigued by the position, being a relatively new face in the social work field. However, as I got into it, I was quickly met with one of the most in-depth and difficult job descriptions I’ve ever read. After some initial hesitation, I took on the challenge as I was hoping to expand my knowledge on mental health and substance use. Though I understand the distinction and the reason for the two distinct categories, I’ve never understood why a combination of the two subjects couldn’t just fall under one umbrella. Even if you can decide who fits under which category, it is nearly impossible to take a person who is dealing with one and not the other. As far as my daily activities go, I spend most of my days at work “processing” and distributing the applications my coworkers and I receive. While I don’t do a lot of hands-on work, interacting with people who are dealing with mental health and substance abuse challenges is one of my favorite things about the job.

As the dialogue shifts from a “deficit” and “medical” model to a more strength and empowerment based one, it is my responsibility to bring a calm and level-headed perspective to my coworkers, friends, and most importantly, my clients. As the smallest of the three, I usually keep the peace, and in doing so, I have developed a deeper respect for the impacts that intense stress and emotions can have. If I can’t help resolve a situation, I try my best to be available when people need advice. I feel I’m very good at easing the minds of those around me by helping them find the best ways to tackle things together. Much of what I do is being an advocate for those with a little less power. People appreciate and trust me because I usually steer clear of any past experiences or bias when giving my input or advice on certain situations. My boss calls me a “resource wizard” for a reason. This job has allowed me the privilege to really see how much I do know, and more importantly, how much more I can learn.

My biggest personal flaw is that I have a hard time letting things go. When I see or feel any sort of injustice, I usually do anything in my power to reverse it or attempt to prevent its relapse. However, this can make it hard to manage my own personal stress. Though I consider myself in control of my stress most of the time, I can sometimes have difficulty achieving this.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I’m 24 years old. Born and raised in El Paso, Texas and still living here. It is a small city (pop. 640K in the El Paso Metro Area, and 840K in El Paso and Juarez, combined). It is situated on the New Mexico and Mexico border, and it has the bad reputation of being a dangerous city (it is ranked #6 for most dangerous in the US). It is truly a city divided in half by the Rio Grande, with Juarez on one side of the river and El Paso on the other.

Things I love about my city are the people, the weather, the food (like fajitas, birria, carnitas, tacos, tamales, etc.), a city full of great musicians (Scott Shannon, Tish Hinojosa, Grace Pettis, Los Cenzontles, Iron & Wine, Los Lobos, Lila Downs, etc.), the prices for real estate, clothes, and groceries are cheaper than elsewhere in the US, and it is close to nature and tourist spots (60 miles to Big Bend National Park; 180 miles to Sierra Blanca, where Willie Nelson lives; 125 miles to Fort Davis; 212 miles to Carlsbad Caverns).

Things I don’t like about my city are the country music, the high rate of unemployment, the undereducated and unemployed, the restrictive and provincial mentality, the high rate of alcoholism and drug addiction, and the limited cultural institutions.

When I was 17 years old I went to Juarez, Mexico for the first time, which marked a first for me in that I went into another country for the first time (the first foreign country I had been to was Mexico in 2000). Over the years I made trips to Juarez with my parents who would take me to go shop at the t-shirt and taco shops on Avenida de las Americas and in the Mercado Juarez and Las Peñas del Sol.

In 2003 we made another trip to Juarez and had lunch at Tacos Las Magdalenas which was the introduction to my mom to chicken tacos, which she had never tried before. It was a half order of 6 tacos which were topped with a spicy green sauce (exactly like the green sauce in that Taco del Rio commercial from the 1990s). Later on that evening, my dad found out that he was to be transferred to Oklahoma City because of his job. He had originally planned to retire in El Paso, but he felt too much change in one year, 2002-2003: the capture of Saddam, the transnational drug war, losing our team of the El Paso Rhinos to Fort Worth, the 9/11 terror attacks, and his wife getting diagnosed with Bells Palsy. It felt good to have some good news.

So we moved to OKC and eventually I went to the University of Oklahoma, where I majored in foreign language (Spanish), history, and music. By the time I graduated from OU in May 2011 I had established a solid professional network of around 300 alumni and colleagues, and I thought it would be very easy for me to find a job in another city, like Phoenix, San Diego, San Francisco, Portland, Austin, Seattle, or New York City.

I was wrong. I interviewed for nearly 60 jobs from Phoenix to San Diego to Philadelphia to New York City, but I only got offers for two temp jobs, one in Boston (which I turned down) and one in Austin (which I took). After 9 months at the temp job in Austin and after 7 months back in Oklahoma, I resigned because of personal reasons (was homesick for El Paso, so I took a brief hiatus to heal, then came back to El Paso). At this moment, in 2013, it has been a difficult challenge to get back on the working world.

In 2011 I decided I was going to make my last attempt at being a music journalist, writing about my concerts, my travels, and also reviewing albums. It was something I had wanted to do since I was a kid but was too afraid of rejection (I thought that as a Texan, I didn’t have much chance of becoming a professional music writer). I looked around and realized that there were very few music blogs by Texans or based out of Texas, so I started Texas Music and Beyond. TMB became the very first blog in Texas that was dedicated to jazz, classical, and music criticism, the roots of the concept of “serious” music journalism that later on turned into Nippertown (based out of upstate New York) and the New York Times blog called The Front Row.

At the time I didn’t know where to find a publisher or a space where I could go and meet people, so I went on Craigslist and found an ad for a coworking space called LAB. This is a space where you can do your work but also have access to a community of like-minded people, creatives, and entrepreneurs, and it was around that time when I met Rafael. He introduced me to his network of over 200 creative entrepreneurs, and I became a member of LAB. It was also at that time when I knew that I would have to take Texas Music and Beyond one step further because I knew that Texas is one of the most fertile territory for diverse and exciting music, and nobody was writing about it.

So I had two challenges at that time: how do I cover more shows and concerts without increasing the cost of living; and how can I maintain quality articles without having to get a day job? My thought was to increase revenue by getting more than 200-500 monthly readers, and I succeeded by getting around 6,000 visitors per month. It had been a long time since I had that many people reading my articles, so it was a huge motivation and confidence boost.

So after this all happened, it seemed like I was on the right track to success, but there was one missing component, which was an editor. In the spring of 2012 I met Joanna Klinck, then a freelance arts journalist and blogger based in Austin, Texas, via email. She was attracted to the music blog Texas Music and Beyond because it seemed like the only Texas music blog from the perspective of a Texan, something that no one had ever done before. It was the music blog dedicated to Texas, about Texas, written from the perspective of someone who lives in Texas. We decided to meet at LAB, but little did we know that Joanna had been experiencing some critical health issues which later on had to be treated.

Meanwhile, Joanna wrote some inspiring articles that got the attention of my editor-in-chief at the online arts and music magazine Nodini, Einar. He liked her work so much that he asked her to contribute her articles as a freelance writer. Joanna and I agreed that I would have the senior editor position and she would be the junior editor, and from there we grew the music blog from 200 readers to 6,000 readers per month. Things were looking bright, the right people were coming into my life at the right time. It was a good sign.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I'm 28 years old, growing up was very difficult. When I was 12 years old my parents got divorced and my mother decided to go to college. My father (the sole provider for the house) didn't want to take care of me by himself anymore and dropped me off at an orphanage.

The first year at the orphanage was the hardest. My whole life I never saw myself as a boy or as a man. With everybody calling me "she" and pointing out that I should watch my "dresscode" I found it impossible to adjust to the culture.

When I was 13 my new stepfather changed everything. It was him who taught me that we are not the gender society expects us to be, we are the gender we want to be, and that it's our duty to be the person we were supposed to be.

To express my true self I began to explore the very boundary between man and woman and I found out that my body would never be the man's body I was expected to have. To make it worse, my mother and stepfather were always supportive of me becoming the man I wanted to be but the girls at school thought otherwise. Instead of listening to me I often found myself listening to lectures about how I shouldn't wear certain colors, shouldn't wear clothes that belonged to the "wrong gender". The kids at school also came up with some terrible names for me, insulting me the way only children and teenagers know.

It was when I was 14 that I began to feel weak, I lost my hair and I found it difficult to stand up for myself, so I turned to self-harm. To cope with depression, anxiety and panic attacks I started eating in order to cope with the depression, at the age of 15 I was in the hospital because of breathing difficulties. But when I returned to school I felt the urge to cut open my arms more often.

I don't know if it's related or not but one day I was looking through the history of a old textbook and found a collection of letters from soldiers who received physical or mental trauma. I didn't know that men could suffer from mental disorders, I assumed it was only women. I wrote the next letter myself. I didn't know whether I wanted it to be a letter to myself or to my friends and family, but I found myself writing it to them in their anger and frustration. It was like I was my own best friend, I wrote letters for myself to explain my flaws to myself and to help myself get through the worst parts of the day.

After finding out that other people can suffer from mental illness, I looked up suicide and that was when I became aware that I had been feeling suicidal for quite some time now.

After returning home from the hospital, I still felt like there was something wrong with me. My friend recommended that I should look at the link between depression and suicide, because he was concerned about me. He had me take an online quiz and the results were pretty bad. But when I went home I found the idea that my bad scores could be associated with mental illness rather depressing.

At that point, I knew I had to change something in my life and I started looking for a therapist who could help me get through the worst parts of my life. I found that by changing my diet (not drinking coffee, not eating sugar, not eating meat, not eating processed food, etc.) I was able to fight depression better than before.

Now that I'm able to think more clearly, I started reading up on self-harm and depression. I found out that self-harm was mostly done by girls who had not been given the proper emotional guidance in their childhood.

For the first time since I was 12, I felt like a man.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: Well, right now I'm 25 years old. I grew up in the small city of Casa Grande Arizona. I've lived there my whole life until now. When I was 16 I moved to a tiny place called Leupp Arizona and lived there for 3 years, I would say that the residents at Leupp had a significant influence on my life. Here in Leupp, I had my first job at the DHL drop off center. I also began a relationship with a girl who I truly loved. In my relationship, we both had a lot to learn. This included important lessons regarding mental health and self care. Eventually I realized I couldn't give her everything she needed at this moment. I realized I needed to learn more about mental health and self care myself. I graduated from high school, got my GED, and found a job as a line cook at the local McDonald's restaurant. We broke up about a year later. After the breakup, my mother kicked me out of the house and sent me to live with my father in Denver, Colorado. I quickly got a job at the local Kohl's department store. And then I applied and was accepted into college. I had a fairly low GPA because of my time at the Leupp High School, but was able to get a waiver and the school accepted me. After getting to college, I met a young woman who was a great partner. Very supportive, very loving. It was everything I needed. But I needed more, and she needed a change. After college, I moved back to Arizona to be closer to my family, and this time my mother accepted me back home, though grudgingly. I got a job at a local car wash. In the car wash, I realized it was where I belonged. It was where I was needed. And it was what I loved. My managers noticed this and helped me get a job at an Applebee's restaurant. There I learned the ropes of being a server, and this is where my passion for management and leadership began. My manager at Applebee's noticed my passion for learning, and I got my promotion to server trainer. This is where I grew to love managing people. Now I've gotten my promotion to general manager. I'm looking forward to continuing my management skills and leading my employees as we strive to provide the best possible experience for our guests. I am grateful to Applebee's for the opportunity to begin this position.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: Sure. Well I’m [obviously] 26 years old and was born and raised in Madison, Wisconsin. My parents and extended family all live in this small mid-west city, so it’s been good being so close to home. By way of brief summary, I’ve had two main phases of my life: Elementary and High School. Most of my elementary years were spent in very rural areas near Lake Michigan; those places formed the heart of who I am today. My high school years were spent in Madison as I went to this little private high school a friend told me about. These years brought me out of my shell and exposed me to a lot of ideas, interests, and people who would become huge parts of my life. Currently I work for the University of Wisconsin here in Madison. I study cannabis and take care of a lot of the cannabusinesses on campus in my administrative role. This is my third year with the university. If you want to know more about my interests I encourage you to check out my Q&A. As far as what matters to me I’m for sure a big proponent of science, a huge believer in human rights, and a huge advocate for the environment. I definitely don’t like the word “enjoy” and prefer the word “love” because it implies more than an action for me. Life’s about loving people, places, and things, that’s how I was raised, that’s how I think. If I can help you love your life better or this planet, it’s my duty to do so.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
Thanks, you're a friend indeed. I'm getting a little nervous though. I hope I'm prepared to answer these questions.

Well, I'm 22 years old, just graduating college (finally). I grew up in a very large family, and the oldest of several younger siblings. I grew up in an extremely academic environment, and its made me who I am. I'm the kind of person that puts pressure on myself that I don't need to put on myself. Even when everything is going great, I have this feeling that I should be doing more, I should be doing better. I've always put myself through a lot of stress, when in fact, I can relax and everything will be ok.

As far as things that I enjoy, I enjoy spending time with my friends and my girlfriend of 5 years. I enjoy hanging out and doing nothing with them, talking about almost anything, or going out to do something with them. Having gone through the past 5 years of college I've developed a love for technology, especially computers. Computers are the one thing that I can think of that I know more than anyone else in my family, because I enjoy them, I've played with them, and I've helped people with them. I like hanging out with people that share my love of technology, because we all know what we're talking about and we can talk about it without having to dumb it down or wonder if the other person is talking about the same thing that we are. I enjoy learning how to do new things, I enjoy applying what I've learned to real life situations. (I've done everything from tinker with cars to overclock CPUs to write my own os). I enjoy video games and although I don't spend a lot of time playing them, I enjoy spending time with my friends playing them. I like reading, especially science fiction and fantasy. I like spending time with people, but I'm also fine with spending time by myself. I love reading the directions on things before I use them.

As far as what matters to me, my family and my friends have always been at the top of the list. I spend time with them all the time, just hanging out or doing something. My family is extremely loving and my friends are extremely understanding. Its to the point where I know that I can call them at any point and they will always be there to talk. Especially my girlfriend, she's gone with me through thick and thin, and just listening to me and supporting me at this point makes her a keeper. I look at my friends and I want to spend time with them, I look at my family and I want to be with them. They are both like my parents. I come from a rather large family. My mom has three brothers and a sister, and my dad has five brothers. I have three sisters and four brothers that I have grown up with. We spend a lot of time with our cousins, and my siblings and I are extremely close. I'm very thankful that I'm part of my family because they give me so much support, and I feel like I belong with them. They keep me grounded, and I feel like I can depend on them for anything.

I'm at the point in my life where I need to figure out who I am and what I need to be. I've done a lot of soul searching, and this is one of the next steps. I'm not at all dependent, but I want someone that I can depend on, and someone that can depend on me. Its hard for me to find someone that can really meet those requirements, but its not because I have particularly high standards, its because most people can't live up to them.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I'm a 23 year old man who was born in Scranton Pennsylvania and was raised by my mother and aunt. My mother worked as a server in a restaurant and then later at a hospital. My aunt was a single mother with two children. We lived in a small house on the north side of Scranton. When I was 12 my mother and I moved from Scranton to Lancaster. I attended Daniel Boone High School. A large portion of my classes were the usual courses that are expected to be completed in high school, but I also took classes that would prepare me for the real world.

I attended the local community college and completed the requirements for an associates degree in business administration. As soon as my classes were finished I began my career with a local cable television company. Since I was a very young man I have always had a strong work ethic. I wasn't willing to let anything hold me back. I wanted to succeed in life. I've had 7 different jobs since graduating from high school. My first jobs were server positions at buffets. My favorite job was working in a waffle house. I would make some of the best waffles the customers had ever had and then I would always leave a big tip. That was when I met my first wife. I've had 4 different server positions and I've worked in cable television since June 2007. This is my first job where I was invited to management. I got promoted to the position of channel support technician in February. I'm a hard worker and I believe I am qualified to perform any job that is necessary. I'm extremely thankful for my manager giving me an opportunity to be a manager. I understand that managers are responsible for the performance of a group of technicians and in some cases the operations of a larger organization. I'm eager to learn more and I am willing to do what is necessary to achieve. As I continue to work harder, I will become more valuable to the company.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I’m a nursing major at the University of Georgia. I’m 21 years old and I grew up in Athens, GA. My parents divorced when I was two, so I stayed with my mom in an average income, two-parent household. My dad was never in my life, but my mom remarried when I was six and she is still with that man now. I enjoy listening to all sorts of music, but my favorite genre is country. Country music reminds me of my grandpa who passed away when I was 15. Country music always brings me back to my teenage years and has emotional attachment for me. That is my favorite thing to listen to. In the beginning of 2011, one of my best friends was diagnosed with an inoperable brain tumor. After more and more scans being done, my friend and I concluded it was anxiety. We went to our counselor’s office together to talk about our feelings and what we were experiencing. We sat down in the office and the counselor started yelling at us, claiming we were fine and didn’t need help. I was never able to get help for my friend because our counselor wouldn’t accept that he was sick. That is another thing that is important to me, getting help for people when they don’t even want it. This experience is my first instance of dealing with a patient that won’t let me help. This incident made me extremely determined to become a nurse and continue to make my patients feel like I am someone they can talk to and I am going to get them help when they need it.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I am 28 years old, I was born and raised in a North-East suburb of Cleveland, Ohio, USA. The name of the city is Westlake, Ohio. Since the time of my birth, I have had the extreme privilege of being under the custody of a wonderful, loving, and supportive family. By that I mean my biological parents, and my step-parents. My parents have never left the my side and as I was growing up, they made it a point to instill in me what is vital for one to be successful in life. My siblings taught me how to have fun and how to make new friends. Both my biological parents and my step-parents educated me on how to act appropriately to friends and family and it should also be mentioned that I was also presented by both of them on how to act appropriately to my teachers, who in turn, will then educate me. And they have also educated me on what it truly means to not judge a book by its cover.

I could honestly say with every fiber of my being that I have the best parents a person could ask for. I have been faced with challenges that have taught me the value of perseverance, they have also taught me how to be patient with people who are not ready to make good decisions or those who are not ready to make decisions on their own and I do believe that I can convey to you that I have applied this in every aspect of my life. What else matters to me? Loving my friends, my family, and my girlfriend would make a good start.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I am 25. I grew up in New Orleans, in the French Quarter. I went to a Catholic school for much of my early childhood, but we moved out of New Orleans for a while, so I had to go to a Catholic school in Metairie, before my family moved back to New Orleans and I finished school in New Orleans. I have three older brothers. They're older, so they weren't around much when I was little, but as they got older and as they wanted to get rid of me, they would invite me to do all kinds of things, which I would never say yes to if my family wasn't helping pay for it, like the zoo or swimming lessons or piano lessons. My dad taught me to tie my shoes. So it was pretty obvious I came from a great family, but growing up I never wanted anything to do with my family. They were bohemians and just a nightmare, to put it politely. I didn't realize why things were the way they were until I was out of college, started working, and I realized what parents are actually for. They're supposed to be your role models, a good example, people you aspire to be like. Not something you want to escape from, at least not entirely.

My mom didn't work much when I was younger because she was raising us, but now she has a job, and a good one. My dad died when I was 12, and we didn't find out about his illness until three months before he died, so it was a real shock to all of us, and it was an ongoing thing until his death. Right now my brother Albert and I are trying to do what we can to support my brother Chuck and his family. We all help with groceries and things like that. I won't live with Chuck and his family. The older I get, the farther from Chuck I get. I don't necessarily understand him or his family, and I don't agree with his political views, and I don't want his kids picking up some of those habits.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I'm 26. I grew up in Topeka, KS. I enjoy movies, my friends, gaming, books and make-up. We are divided into two groups of women, grouped A and B. Group A has a decade of experience in their chosen field and group B just barely have the qualifications to be allowed in the building. We all go through the same exercise where we are asked to tell our favorite movie, what we do in our free time and describe in details what our perfect day would be like.

My favorite movie is a toss up between Juno, Avatar and Titanic. I love my friends because they are my support system. I have a perfect day off (such as today) where I get to sleep until noon, watch cartoons, have coffee with my best friends, play Call of Duty and have sex (this part was listed specifically) all day.In our combined group we are asked if anyone actually watched Juno. I am the only one that admits to having seen it, probably because it is one of my two favorite movies.

I am then asked to leave the room so they can discuss who the best women are. I am also told not to get my hopes up because group B is more likely to go first.

After an hour and a half I go back in and answer more questions, this time it is about setting someone up on a blind date. I don't think anyone in the room particularly liked me.

At this point, I am considering quitting but somehow I convince them to let me move on to the next room, in this room there are about eight other women waiting to be interviewed.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am a 28 year-old Puerto Rican man born and raised in Boston, MA. It’s one of my favorite cities in the world and is on my shortlist of places I want to live in one day. The food, people, and sports teams keep me close to home most days but I love to travel. My two favorite countries to visit are Japan and Spain.

One of the first places I have ever travelled was to Nevada to visit a friend that moved to Las Vegas for his job. Ever since then, I have loved Las Vegas and I can’t wait to visit again.
