This question will really stump you: What if you gave a test and no one showed up? Well, that question would stump me, but only because it says ‘showed up’.

If I was asked what I’d do to take over the world, my answer would be: to make the world know I exist. It’s the most selfish reason to want something, but in the end, if I can’t be the person I want to be, then my name will never be spoken of again. Maybe that sounds selfish, but who are we if not thinking of ourselves? It’s better to think of ourselves and others instead of being a selfish prick. Look at Paris Hilton. No one knows who she is, and nothing she did changed the world in any way. Not even her twin sister, since she has the exact same personality. (except for her name, of course).

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: My name is Bob. I'm 32. I was born in Fort Lauderdale, Florida. I spent my youth in a suburb of New York. I have three sisters. I played baseball until I was 17, and until an injury put an end to my career. I've always liked fighting in mixed martial arts. My father died when I was 16. He killed himself after suffering from depression, paranoia, schizophrenia, and migraines for years. My father's death affected me deeply and I've been searching for answers as to how it happened. I believe my father wanted to go into business for himself. I have hopes of doing something similar, but something more meaningful. People need jobs. People need tools. I am considering opening a woodworking business, though I'm not sure yet exactly what kind of business I will open.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I am 38 years old, I grew up on a farm in Central California and attended a small Baptist School in Merced County. I went to the college with the oldest running water park in the country. I enjoy extreme snowboarding, anything water related, and spending time with my family. I am devoted to my Christian Faith and love talking about Jesus with people. I also enjoy being outdoors and spending time with people, life is too short to live it in a cube. So I will probably not be the best person to talk to if you are into the 9-5 life. I prefer the 24/7 life, the one Jesus lived, being where people are, and telling them about how great a Savior we have and what He has done for us.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I am a white, able-bodied, male in my mid-thirties who was born and raised in central Florida. I grew up in a rural farming community in central Florida with a population of just over 250. I was the youngest in my family and only had sisters. I was very sheltered as a child and very unsure of myself. Having sisters taught me a lot about myself because the family functioned like a democracy. I felt voiceless in a patriarchal household. The family didn’t have enough money for any physical activities so I wasn’t physically active as a child. I loved dinosaurs as a kid and my hobbies were fishing, science, and the natural world around me. As a teenager, I was fairly uncool. I had braces as a teen and very little experience with romance. My family did not have a great understanding of mental health so I just felt depressed and alone most of the time. I was enrolled in a vocational agriculture program in high school and learned how to be a Dairy Farmer. I got into 4-H and took farm management as an advanced program. We visited other farms in the area to learn about new innovations. I learned how to operate and repair milking machines, herd identification, and how to take milk samples to measure quality. After graduating, I was allowed to enroll in a dual enrollment program and attended community college while still in high school. This enabled me to earn an Associates of Arts degree by the time I graduated high school. My family moved to Western Pennsylvania in search of better employment opportunities because my mother’s health deteriorated and she was no longer able to work. I gained experience in customer service and early leadership through my work as a cell phone customer service representative while I was in college. I am deeply interested in space exploration and aerospace engineering. Currently, I am an aerospace engineer at NASA and I do quite a bit of work with crews preparing for mission. I am heavily involved in popular media channels that have discussions about topics in politics, science, philosophy, and engineering. Finally, I enjoy hiking, rock climbing, and spending time outdoors.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
Here is my answer to the first question: I am a 35 year old independent professional nurse who has worked for the National Health Service in the United Kingdom for 14 years. I gained my registered nurse qualification (RN/RGN/RNID) from Stirling University in 1994. In 2002 I was selected to be one of six nurses from the UK and Ireland to attend a 3 year postgraduate course on General Nursing Practice Management at a university in the Czech Republic. During this time I also visited Bosnia on numerous occasions to help start up an independent midwife training college and a birth centre.

Before going to the Czech Republic I spent two years working at the world famous Christie Hospital in Manchester in England working with women with gynaecological cancers, where I would very often have to travel to St. Petersburg to attend oncology conferences. I would talk regularly about alternative treatments for cancer and problems associated with modern treatments like chemotherapy and radiotherapy. It was from this time in Manchester that I decided I had to work to make birth normal again.

I then left the NHS to study homoeopathy at the four year Pukkhamrut Institute in The Hague in the Netherlands, however during the four years I worked part time as a midwife at the Macmillan cancer hospital in St. Petersburg in Russia.

During my time in the Czech Republic I worked at the Hradec Kralove, Pardubice and Kladno cancer hospitals. This time allowed me to meet some of the biggest names in holistic cancer treatment from all over the world. To my enormous shock I was not allowed to return to Russia and work in the hospitals due to a change of government. However, it did give me the opportunity to expand my homoeopathic practise in the Netherlands.

After completing the homoeopathic course I decided to study acupuncture and then later, reflexology. As a nurse I had studied the theory but was told in great detail how acupuncture worked by Hans Fischer, a German acupuncturist who lived and worked in the Netherlands.

He told me that acupuncture can have remarkable affects on disease and it can even help heal the body and mind on a spiritual level and can allow the vital life force or “chi” to flow. He told me how it works and how the meridian lines meet each other at acupuncture points. It was from Hans that I also learned how the pituitary gland functions in control of the adrenal glands and the cranial nerves. I was not surprised to later find out that the pituitary was located at acupuncture point B-52 on the master channel.

It was also during this time that I studied Tui Na, which is a Chinese massage system that works on acupuncture points in a therapeutic way to affect the entire body and its systems. Tui Na literally means “pushing and grasping” and means the same as Shiatsu and Ashiatsu which both use the weight of the body to make contact with different parts of the body. It is amazing how much pressure can be given using the feet, knees, thighs and buttocks and good pressure can be given at the wrists and elbow as well. I currently use this technique every day as I believe it to be very effective at making new acupoints available to heal.

In 2009 I was given a full blown allergic reaction to a patient I was taking care of. I was given a steroid called prednisolone to prevent this reaction getting worse. This drug caused a massive problem with my heart, kidneys, liver and bones. My kidneys were getting damaged, my liver was getting damaged and my heart was becoming enlarged.

To stop this happening I was then given a little smaller dose of prednisolone. This however did not solve the problem and the same problem was repeated. To counteract this I was given another drug called Digoxin to stop my heart muscle from working harder, however this medication was also causing problems with my bones and my eyes.

Now to help prevent my bones from dissolving due to the corticosteroids I was prescribed a drug called Fosamax. Fosamax damages the kidneys and other body tissues by decreasing the uptake of phosphate. My kidneys were already damaged but I refused to stop taking Fosamax.

To stop my body breaking down the vitamin D and calcium required in my bones I was then prescribed a drug called Reclast which is similar to IVF medications that block the effects of sex steroids such as progesterone, estrogen and testosterone. This drug has a certain level of risk associated with it and it was found to be more effective if it was given every 5 years instead of every year. Unfortunately, during this time I was regularly visiting patients with osteoporosis and bone fractures and in the past I had seen thousands of patients of different ages and with different degrees of osteoporosis due to the hormone changes associated with starting and stopping periods and during pregnancy.

I read the nurse teaching material on osteoporosis and the problems associated with the two different drugs I was taking and then quit both of these drugs. Unfortunately, stopping these medications is not an easy thing to do and is not recommended.

However, this did stop the progression of the bone damage associated with osteoporosis and the progression of the kidney damage associated with Fosamax. There are 7 phases of kidney disease that can develop.

The first phase is the kidneys still work but are in decline. The second phase is when they lose their capacity to filter the blood properly. The third phase is when they are no longer filtering the blood correctly. The fourth phase is when the kidneys filter 15-29% of the blood correctly. The fifth phase is when the kidneys filter 10-15% of the blood. The sixth phase is when the kidneys filter 5-10% of the blood and the seventh phase is when the kidneys filter 0-5% of the blood.

The last stage is where the person will need kidney dialysis and I didn’t want that and I would rather take my chances on a kidney transplant.

I have to say that I was shocked at my condition when I returned home to my brother in Leeds after my time in Russia. I had never before felt or seen such a profound shock to the body before and I was far from well. I was in bed for about 1 week or longer.

The walls of my bedroom had been extended to house my entire hospital bag. I had bags filled with gloves, protective pants, gloves for my hands, goggles, masks, shields and many other pieces of equipment to prevent cross infection.

It was this day that I decided to change my outlook on how I treated people. I would not give corticosteroids as an eye drop anymore and would try to avoid prescribing these drugs in any other way. I would not be feeding pregnant women a diet that was high in proteins and I would stop eating so much fish and meat in general.

All patients would be looked at holistically and any oral medication with an expiration date was seen as an opportunity to improve in my skills of pharmacy. My brother is a pharmacist so that skill came from him, however I continued to get better at counting tablets and pills. Now that I am 35 I think I have a little more experience than my 20 year old self.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I was born in Montreal in July 1971 and I am 43 years old. My family settled in Calgary in 1975, when I was 4. I was raised Catholic. However, in grade 11, I became a born again Christian and attended church weekly with my parents until after I moved out. While I was there, my father served as an usher for over 25 years. He still serves the church as an usher, while he helps my brother manage the church, which he bought in 2008. I worked for the local church for almost 10 years and helped manage the bookkeeping on a volunteer basis in 2000. My favourite subject in school was history. My favourite book was “The Prophet” by Kahlil Gibran, which I read as a young teenager. During high school, I was a member of the drama club and used to write a story about my classmates every day. I enjoyed creative writing, also known as “composition”, where the teacher gave us a topic and I would just make a story up, based on it. I would then read it to the class for extra marks. I started writing serious poems in the fall of 1986 and published my first book of poetry in 1997. Over the years, I have written a number of “blog” pieces as well. I was a competitive athlete and enjoy learning about health and diet. I was trained in the sport of weightlifting by the best trainer in Canada and competed in nationals in 1989, 1991 and 1992. In 1991 and 1992, I qualified for the olympic selection in weightlifting. My favourite sport was curling and I joined a couple of teams to play at least once per week for many years. I worked at a lumber company from 1988 to 1990 and played softball on a men’s team that went to the nationals in 1990. During this time, I was also a member of the club and a placekicker for the Regina Roughriders for 3 seasons. I started my own home-based lawn care business in 1989 and operated it until 1997.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am 33 years old. I was born and raised in Melbourne, Australia. I grew up in a family of five. I am the middle child, with a younger sister and an older brother. I also had two step brothers and two step sisters, though they have since moved out of the house. I believe that my family has taught me the most important values in life, and made me the person I am today. I love my family more than anything in the world.

I was born in March 1984, to a father from Scotland, and a mother from Australia. I was raised with both Scottish and Australian culture, and I believe I have a better appreciation of life as a result. I was taught that being happy, loving and having a sense of humour is a great way to be, and these values have guided my life since.

I was fortunate enough to be raised in a two parent household, which I believe has been a key factor in who I am today. My mother was a nurse, and my father was a surgeon. As a result of their professions, I have always had a healthy respect for science, and medical science in particular. My family was a large part of my life, and I spent a lot of time with them. As a result, I believe that I have a strong understanding of family values.

I enjoy reading, hiking, running, and playing video games. I also like spending time with friends and family. I like to keep busy, and don’t like to waste time. When I am not busy with work or school, I like to take advantage of my down time and relax with a book or a game.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I am in my late 30s. I grew up in a large family. My parents were working class and struggled. There was a lot of financial stress. But we were happy because we were close. We played together, laughed together and cried together. When I was a kid, I was very shy. I was afraid to talk to people. It was hard for me to talk about feelings and emotions. I was just a quiet kid. I grew up in an Italian neighborhood, and all my friends were Italian. We were just like family. I spent a lot of time with my grandparents and my cousins. When I was a teenager, I felt a little bit different from everybody else. I liked art, music and books. My friends didn’t really share my interests. I was a little bit of an outsider. I was a little bit lonely. I started to drink alcohol to fit in with my friends. I drank beer and then whiskey. When I was a teenager, my parents got divorced. I had to live with my father. He was an alcoholic and a drug addict. He abused me and my brothers. We had a lot of fights. It was very difficult for me. I couldn’t escape. I couldn’t run away. I didn’t have a lot of money. I didn’t have a car. I didn’t have a driver’s license. I was just a kid. I didn’t know how to get out of the situation. I felt trapped. I was very angry. I didn’t know how to express my anger. I felt like I was being punished. I felt like I was being punished for something that I didn’t do. I felt like I was being punished for something that I didn’t do. I felt like I was being punished for something that I didn’t do.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am from Oklahoma City, Oklahoma. I am 38 years old. I was the oldest of five children; the youngest being my twin sister. I grew up with the dream of becoming a professional baseball player. I wanted to play in the majors for the San Diego Padres. I felt I had that potential until I tried out for the USA Baseball National Team at 16. I then understood how underwhelming my abilities really were and how I had deluded myself in believing I could play in the majors. I learned this truth at an early age and was fully open to understanding myself and my dreams to be more real than they actually were. Still, I don’t ever regret trying to play in the majors because that is where I felt I belonged. I enjoyed baseball. More than anything I enjoyed competing. I loved finding out what I was capable of and pushing that potential as far as possible.

I also always loved reading and writing. Reading allowed me to escape the confines of the town I grew up in and imagine myself being or becoming anyone or anything. Writing allowed me to do the same thing, but on my own terms. I could produce my own work. I could imagine something in my mind and imagine it in physical form on a page. There was something magical about being able to do that.

I enjoyed all sports, but baseball most of all. The first thing I did when I woke up was grab the baseball I had on my bedside table and read the scores from the previous day’s games.

I was probably a bit on the slow side growing up. Not just when it came to getting good grades in school but also in my emotional maturation. My voice didn’t drop until 17. I was probably the last kid in my class to hit 6 feet. My brother still dwarfs me. I probably would have been described as sensitive or even timid when I was young. I still tend to be when I am not performing my social duties in the wake of becoming a business person.

By the time I was 17, I could bench press my weight. This was a huge feat for me and something that made me very proud. It was a confidence booster and got me thinking I was much stronger than I actually was. By the time I was 21, I could max out a few plates per side on a power bar. I was proud of that. Today, I am sure I could not max anything. When it comes to physical strength, I know my weakness.

When it came to academics, I always enjoyed school but never felt I was any good at it. I was not especially smart or athletic. My college transcripts will attest to this. This is also why when I saw how Underachiever Choppers were able to take a person of very low intellectual capacity and assist them to be successful in the field of healthcare administration, I was intrigued.

I did well in public school but was never thought of as gifted. I graduated #32 in my class out of 600 students. When I say I wasn’t gifted, I don’t mean that as an excuse for being mediocre. I mean that I thought of myself as mediocre. I never felt as if I were a genius or that I were more intelligent or talented than anyone else. I’ve always felt that if everyone worked as hard as they could, they could all be good at something. I just never worked hard. I always maintained a C average.

This actually changed after getting out of the military because I had no real guidance in college at the time I was attending. It was boring to me. College felt like another four years I had to put in before I could go out and get a job that would make me as much money as possible. I also came from a very working class family. In order to not have to work another day in my life, I needed a degree that led to a job that would make me enough money to do so. The field of healthcare administration was perfect for this. With a bachelor’s degree in health service administration and no work experience, I could make a minimum of $50k a year in a remote city like Wichita, Kansas. Even better, with a few more classes, I would be able to manage a healthcare facility. This would allow me to travel the world, meet business people and potential friends, and have some of the most exciting life experiences a person could want.

This is why I went to college the first time. I wanted to be successful and make money. I wasn’t passionate about anything else and there were no other fields that fit that criteria. Plus, I knew that healthcare administration involved numbers and numbers are the one subject matter I could think of that I did well in school.

College was not my first choice. I did not want to go to college out of high school. This was not because I was lazy. It was because I had zero guidance or planning for my college experience at that time. In retrospect, I didn’t want to go to college because I didn’t know what I wanted to do for the rest of my life. I also feared failure. I don’t believe I am inherently lazy. Quite the contrary, I work hard and would have worked hard in college had I known how to use my time better.

After about two years of struggling in college, I decided I needed help. I had no idea who to ask so I turned to my buddy who was in the military at the time. I asked him if I could go to the Marine Corps Recruiters office. He told me I could go there if I wanted to, but that I should not go there. I didn’t understand why he said this. He told me if I didn’t make the grade in college or didn’t have a lot of money to go to college that the Marines were a great option. This resonated with me, but I still needed help in college. I knew that.

I enrolled in community college and told the Marine Recruiters that I was thinking of joining and asked them how my grades would have to look in order to be accepted. I had almost no grades to report at the time and they basically told me I could join immediately. I was dumbfounded. I was so overwhelmed at that moment that I couldn’t comprehend the enormity of the decision I was making. I signed up for the next recruit training class that was to leave in 30 days.

I knew this was probably the dumbest decision I would ever make in my life, but I decided to go to college for the sole purpose of getting recruited. They had a way for me to do it. I was working full-time nights at Tyson Foods in the food processing department. I was making meat patties. It was the most mind-numbing, repetitive work I could ever imagine. I was tired all the time and it paid $8 an hour. I had started working there in high school, though, so I was not unaware of how hard a manufacturing job could be. I had put myself in this position and I had every intention of getting myself out.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I’m 41 years old, a 14th generation American. I grew up in the northwest. While growing up, I was encouraged to explore the region and I developed a deep affinity for the outdoors. After high school, I attended a few colleges but wasn’t able to focus. I was often upset over what was going on in the world and was like a wounded animal at times, but through doing things that challenged me and that gave me physical and mental satisfaction, I was able to become more centered. In addition to my academic struggles I had many stressful home experiences. About 15 years ago I met my wife, which helped me to focus. It still took many years and many challenges, but now I am at a point where I understand myself and am at peace. Both my writing and woodworking activities have given me a sense of purpose and accomplishment that helps me cope with life’s difficulties.

It’s been quite a journey for me and perhaps not everyone will believe what I am saying, but I am writing this with all sincerity. If people don’t believe me, that’s ok. Maybe someone out there who is going through similar experiences may find encouragement from my experiences.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I’m a 31 year old single guy who lives in Boston. My mother was born in Pittsburgh and she moved to Worcester when she was three or four years old. My father was born in Argentina and moved to Boston when he was a teenager. The two of them met on a blind date at a bar in Faneuil Hall. I have two younger sisters and we all attended Worcester public schools. My father is a mechanic and my mother is a nurse. I went to Clark University, where I got my B.A. in literature. I spent a year in France on a Fulbright scholarship and have been working in the service industry ever since. I have a cat named Leo, and he’s my best friend. I enjoy dining out, going to the movies, hanging out with my friends, writing, and the beach.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: It’s about 9:00PM and I’m quite hungry. You won’t believe this, but I’m wondering if you make a good tuna sandwich. I could take a nibble out of that right about now. You don’t, do you? Of course, you don’t. You’re a resume, not a pub. Let’s get on with this, shall we? I’m 42. I grew up in Brooklyn, lived in Manhattan for 20 years, and right now I am in Canada where, as part of the “Dependent Visa” program I am finally allowed to shop around at the local market for some tuna. And as for the tuna sandwich, I’m actually eating a salad with hard boiled eggs and sesame seeds. I grew up with my Mom and a younger sister and brother. My father died when I was two. My mother’s name is Ida but my father calls her “Momma”. Momma and I were very close and still are. We used to go see all of her favorite singers together but after I turned six it wasn’t the same. Momma bought a guitar. I tried learning to play when I was 10, but got bored with it. When I was 14 Momma took me to see the Moody Blues in concert. I thought it was amazing! It was just the two of us in the front row of a small concert hall. My brother and sister couldn’t get in because I was underage. When Momma and I got home, she made me a tuna sandwich, but this time I said it was awful. She didn’t believe me. I said it was, though. Then I left to see a Beatles concert and left Momma alone with her sandwich. I came back the next day to find out what was left of the sandwich and discovered she was sick with pneumonia. I realized how much I loved my Momma and how it was possible to love her. I still love her very much. It’s important to me that those I work with see me for who I am, not for what they think of my politics. But I do tend to speak my mind, and that can be dangerous in some settings. However, I know how to behave professionally. I listen to the people around me and do as they ask. My job is to do whatever I can to get things done, not get caught up in my own affairs. If there is anything else you need to know about me, it will take too long to answer, so let’s just forget about it for now and let you and me get on with our relationship. There will be plenty of opportunities for you and I to get to know each other if you give me a chance. Is there anything else you want to know?

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I’m in my early thirties. I grew up in Northern England. One of my parents was emotional and the other was absent. This is a brief summary. At 11 years old I was given crystal meth in school. Even at that age I saw it as a drug. Every young person I knew was high. It would’ve been great to not have that in school. It brought the sickest and worst out of everyone. I’m not sure how to describe that all. I’d say I had the burden of all that suffering. I carried an extra lot of it.

A short time ago I bought a bunch of fireworks at an unknown, small, fireworks-only shop (for obvious reasons). I’ve never fired off a firework before. I could’ve bought one firework and lit it. That’s not really what I had in mind. I went to an area where I could have a bit of space. There were no people around. Some about a quarter of a mile away but on a distant hill. I created a huge boom. My whole body shook. I ran up a small hill. My chest heaved. I noticed something I haven’t noticed in years. I heard a bit of my own voice. A bit of clarity. I felt strong. The coming of the fireworks I just bought couldn’t have felt any better. That was in the back of my head. That day and the whole day after I felt pure. I felt perfect. I was in my truest form and it felt wonderful.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I am 47 years old. I was born in Nagpur, India and I went to school there. I moved to Atlanta, Georgia, in the US at age 12. My father got a job in a pharmaceutical company in India and he later moved to the US. All of my family lives in the US. Because I moved around quite a bit as a child, I always had to learn to adapt. I have lived in a small town in Alabama, a big city in Pennsylvania, a college town in Illinois, a suburban town in Georgia, and a suburb in New York City. I can make friends and fit into any new community. I am a friendly, easy-going person. I think kindness is the most important characteristic for a person to have. I like reading fiction novels, doing Sudoku puzzles, and cooking. I also enjoy teaching college students. I’ve taught since 2000.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I guess I'm like everyone else. I enjoy what I like, don't enjoy what I don't enjoy, have goals and aspirations, some I've already completed and some I'm still pursuing. I'm 31 years old. I've been divorced for about 6 years. I'm not ashamed of my past - I really love who I am now. I don't regret what happened, because I learned some invaluable lessons. In fact, I think if I hadn't been through what I did, I may not be half the person I am now. I've made some amazing friends, who I wouldn't trade for anything. I have a great job, working with some of the best people in the world. I do well for myself and I've even made a couple of good investments that have given me financial stability and independence that I wouldn't have had if not for some specific events that happened to me. As of now, I don't own a home, but in the next couple of years, I want to obtain that by purchasing a home. I'm not financially tied down right now, and I like it that way. In the future, I'd like to have someone to share all of this with, that I could also take care of.

I love physical activities. I'm kind of an adrenaline junkie. So anything adrenaline-producing or at least fast I'm usually all over it. Except biking. I can't ride a bike. There's one of my biggest regrets in life, but I digress. I ride horses in the great state of Arizona, as often as I can. I used to enjoy riding a motorcycle quite often, before the aforementioned accident.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I am 49 years old. I grew up in San Jose, California. My elementary years were in poverty where we weren't even given shoes to wear to school, so I would stuff my shoes with newspaper every day to make it look like I was wearing shoes. My main memory was trying to get to school early enough so that I could wear my friends shoes for the day, because they would be taking them off in class. Many nights we would have to use our one pair of pants for the entire family. I remember once we got our old car fixed and we drove into a small town called San Rafael to a street fair. I remember my parents being so excited because they didn't have to walk; they were so proud of their old beat up car. I had never seen my parents so proud before. I was nine years old. I would have to go into the lunchroom as a high school student with my family every day, sitting on the floor, and I would have to eat my lunch before the high school students got in the lunch line. As a high school student I remember being pulled out of the computer program at the high school I attended and being sent to a special needs class for stupid kids. I had a fantastic teacher named Mrs. Harper. She inspired me to go back to school and she helped me to get my GED diploma. I got my GED at age 17 and then applied for jobs at around 17. I got one job at age 17 at an electronics store, Circuit City, the day after Christmas. The manager who gave me the job made me pick up all of the broken Christmas presents lying on the floor. And he said to me, "You just earned this job, don't screw it up." I stayed at that job for four years until they went bankrupt. I got another electronics store job, CompUSA, at age 21, and I worked there until age 24. I left because they downsized the company. I got another electronics store job at age 25, at Computer City, and I stayed there until age 28. I left Computer City because it downsized again. I went to work for SBC/AT&T at age 28, and stayed there until age 45. I quit after a humiliating affair with a female co-worker at AT&T that left me feeling used and embarrassed and crushed, and I wanted nothing more to do with that job or that company.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I’m 45 years old and was born in an interesting time for Iceland. We were a small, peaceful country with a large amount of freedom and few problems. My childhood was all about discovering and exploring the world around me. I loved building things and that made me curious about how things worked. There is a great opportunity to learn and develop when you are young. I was also lucky to have a supportive family that encouraged me to read and encouraged me to pursue my interests in science.

I was fortunate to get a great education in an inspiring science high school. All my classes were taught in English and the teachers were often friends from the University so their knowledge and focus was excellent.

My classes were also well funded and we were encouraged to attend science competitions around Europe, and even occasionally the USA.

My experience with science competitions resulted in me winning a few and getting to visit places around Europe, as well as representing my country in the World Science Olympiad in Washington DC, USA. It was fun but also got me curious about the world – but why are some countries so rich and others so poor?

After I finished high school I was not yet ready to make the leap into a university degree. I was 17 and being able to take a year off to work and explore the world while learning about new careers was a great way to spend my time. I worked as an assistant chef and learned about being an entrepreneur when I started a car magazine. After one year I had to go back to the University of Iceland. In my first year I got tired of the heavy math and physics and wanted to try out something new – let’s try economics!

The class I chose was all about cities and all I could think was, “YAY I love LEGO and the city! I can relate to this! I like this! I think I might be an urban planner!”

That is how I discovered my passion. I decided to take all I could on urban planning and by my second year I could add planning to my repertoire. What started out as a plan to get my Bachelors degree turned into a plan to continue to a PhD!

I focused my thesis on encouraging planning graduates to work in developing countries. Why do so few planning graduates help build good cities in developing countries? One of the reasons is that developing countries have so few planning institutions and graduates that offer planning services. And planning institutions are afraid that hiring a recent graduate will affect the institution’s brand. My thesis also explored ways to solve these problems and I am so happy to say that my thesis was awarded the scholarship for the best PhD thesis on Transport Planning in Iceland.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I’m in my forties. I grew up on the east coast, including as a young child in Japan, and my family still lives out in the midwest. I have a husband and two daughters. I was educated in the sciences (Ph.D) and the humanities (MFA). I have a lot of varied interests. I’m a visual artist, a writer, and a musician. I love to read. I love to be creative. I have an addictive personality when it comes to music. I’m very involved in environmental and political activism, yet at heart I’m a tree hugger.

I love to read books on the great philosophers. I also love to read children’s books. I love to read psychology textbooks and studies, and neurology books and studies. I love to read science and research related to life’s meaning and the possibility of life after death. I love reading political/activist books and articles, especially if they promote civil rights and saving the planet, and include scientific evidence to back up the theories. I like reading sci-fi novels, but I’m particular, so it’s a hit-or-miss as far as whether or not I like the book. I have a love affair with the movies and I love 50s and 60s old TV shows such as “Leave it to Beaver” and “The Andy Griffith Show”. Yet I also love things like “Breaking Bad” and “Game of Thrones.” I love going to classical music concerts. And I love Indian cuisine. I also love organic cookbooks, since I’m vegetarian. I can’t think of anything I don’t like to do when it comes to activities and hobbies, except I really don’t like to run or jog, for example. My main philosophy in life is this: Try everything at least once. You just never know what you’ll like.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I’m 32. I grew up in the heart of Ohio in a small city called Columbus. I grew up middle class and raised by two wonderful parents. Who gave me unconditional love and support. I learned to read at three and was off and running and never looked back. I have always been an avid reader and it’s my only regret that my father didn’t live long enough to read all the books he wanted to with his son.

I also believe that with very little, you can make something great out of nothing, or at least create the opportunity to do so. You just have to persevere and never give up.

Finally as to what truly matters to me is spending time with my immediate family. Whether it’s going out for dinner, watching a movie, or playing monopoly with my son at night. I wouldn’t trade it for the world.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
My name is Chuck. I am 32 years old and I was born in Southern California. I was raised in a single parent household with a sister who was 5 years older than me. My parents were never married and I never met my father. I do not know anything about him, other than his name and that he lived in California. He died when I was 16 years old.

I grew up in a very poor family. My mother was an alcoholic and we were often homeless. She drank a lot of wine and vodka. She would drink until she passed out in the morning and I would leave for school before she woke up. Often we had no food to eat and no money for bills. I had to learn to cook at an early age because we could not afford to buy food or eat out. The house was filthy. We were always living with friends or at motels.

My mother tried to find work but she was a terrible worker. She did not show up for work and when she did she would fall asleep. She would also call in sick often, but she would never go to the doctor to find out what was wrong with her. I think she was embarrassed that she had no money to buy food or pay for medicine. She was too ashamed to admit that she was an alcoholic. It was very difficult to live with her.

The best years of my life were the ones that I spent with my friends and family. My mother was always drinking and I was always on the street trying to find food or something to do. I would spend my days playing in the park with my friends and going to school. I never liked school. It was a place that I never wanted to go. I hated my teachers and I did not like being in class with them. I would get up in the morning and go to school just so I could be alone. I would sit in my desk all day and do nothing. I was very unhappy at school.

My mother and I did not get along well. She was always drunk and I would not listen to her. I would do my own thing and she would yell at me. I never had a good relationship with her. She would always be angry and yell at me. I did not understand why she was so angry all the time. She was very violent and I would get hurt a lot. I never knew why she would be so angry all the time. She would never let me have a conversation with her.

She was never there for me. I would have to find my own way home and she would not help me. I would have to ask my friends for help. My mother never wanted to spend time with me. She was always busy with her friends and she never wanted to spend time with me. I would always try to find a way to spend time with her but she would never be there for me. She would always be angry and yelling at me. She would never talk to me.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I'm in my early 30s. I was born on a US military base overseas (this won't matter, since I lived mostly in a little suburb that was almost exclusively populated by military families) and moved back to the States when I was one year old. I've always lived in the South until about five years ago when I moved to an Upper Middle Class town in New England. My parents divorced when I was a small child. I don't have much contact with my father, but I have an excellent relationship with my mother. My childhood was very stable and idyllic, and that's the first reason I attribute to my well-balanced upbringing. The second reason is my move to New England, where my family was poorer than those of most of my peers. I had to work for everything I had. If I wanted something, I had to work for it and wait for it. I don't view my family as poor, since my parents were responsible for their decision to move to New England for my father's job. Additionally, we were paid quite well by the military, so we weren't impoverished. Nonetheless, I had to sacrifice a great deal (including access to high-speed internet until I was a teenager) due to the lack of a higher household income. Many of my peers were able to have cell phones and cars paid for by their parents. I had to take public transportation everywhere and didn't have a cell phone until high school. This is the second reason why I contribute a lack of materialism to my success in achieving an academic and work-life balance. I couldn't buy gadgets and clothes like other people. I had to put in effort, and I had to do things differently than most people (for example, I didn't go out for the prom).

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I am currently 31 years old. Born in Baltimore, MD but lived most of my childhood and up to my teenage years in Howard County, Maryland. I love animals, especially dogs, and having been an owner for most of my life. I don't currently have a dog but I've had two Australian Shepherds growing up: an Aussie Aiden (16-17 years old, 2002-2004) and an Aussie Harley (12 years old, 2009-2011). I am a very organized person. I have a very high pain tolerance. After suffering a compound fracture to my right forearm in my sophomore year of high school (2002), I think that tolerance has only increased. Back in college at Saint Francis University (Stillman, PA), I've suffered numerous injuries. However, I consider them all my superhero scars. I once got sick (I do not recall what virus it was) with a sore throat which landed me in the hospital for a day because I could not breathe. I have a binder filled with notes which documents my previous injuries. Recently (in 2019), I have had a pulmonary embolism caused by airbags from a car crash at age 17. I also dislocated my left kneecap while playing soccer during my freshman year in college. By the age of 15, I had had 2 hernia surgeries due to playing sports. I've had multiple concussions as well from playing football and soccer.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I am 39 years old. I am dedicated husband and father. I enjoy comics and watching and reviewing movies on the internet and writing about all things geek. I enjoy hanging out with my daughter and dogs, spending time with my wife and staying home. I am a fitness enthusiast, love all sorts of activities that involve endurance and stamina. I am an outdoors guy. I work out daily. I am an adrenaline junkie. I always take my wife and children on road trips and seek adventure and life learning experiences. I like doing new and exciting things. I am a Jedi in search of the one true way. I work for a multi national IT consulting firm specializing in Project Management and Business Analysis. I am a lifetime learner.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I am 33 years old and was raised in the Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, suburb of Havertown. It is a great place to live if you love talking about yourself. I believe there is a time and place for self-contrived, hollow conversation but it should not dominate our everyday conversations. I come from a big, loving Italian family who taught me how to cook and provided me with a good, Catholic, work ethic. Over the years, I developed an innate curiosity and a love for writing which has greatly influenced my educational path, job journey and personal life. I am passionate about interesting writing. I strive for my writing to be persuasive and captivating, like an essay written by Shakespeare. Naturally, after reading about computers in the paper each morning, I enjoy sitting at my computer and writing. My family, reading and movies matter to me. I get lost in a good book or movie and really engage with the characters. When I was a kid, my father handed me a book to read and I never really looked back. I am grateful for the childhood I had, the education I have and for the kindness and support of the people in my life. I think it is my duty to help others and to give back, and I enjoy doing so whenever possible. I am in the process of volunteering for a local art museum as a guide. And whenever possible, I will be jumping in my car and driving south to get a slice of pizza at Pompei Ristorante in Absecon, New Jersey.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I’m in my early 30s, I am from Western Europe, I love traveling. My early years were great. My parents are still together and were great role models for me. I met so many people from all over the world. When I was young, I never had the impression that I could not do something because of my gender. The world was open to me, so I travelled the world, discovering the world through the most diverse cultures.

Traveling still is a passion of mine. I enjoy making things. I enjoy fixing things and love the DIY approach. I like the idea of building things with my own hands, without using too many tools. I like the challenge. I enjoy traveling on a bicycle, one bag only. I like doing things myself with my hands and that includes repairing, fixing or building things myself.

I know I have excellent IT skills and that I am good at IT. I know people are often impressed by the way I can explain things to them. While English is not my mother tongue, I sometimes am overheard speaking with colleagues and they compliment me on my English. They believe I’m bilingual and sometimes they might suggest I could try out for being a trainer. I’m not exactly sure why people get the impression I could be a trainer, but I must be doing something right.

When I think about the things in life I most enjoy, I realize they are mostly the same as the things that made me grow into a person who I am now. Building things with my hands is a passion. I grew up building things with my father. I have a loving and caring family and a very loving partner. I don’t think there is much more to add to that.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I am 32 years old and grew up in Bergen County, New Jersey, to Irish American parents who are working-class. I was raised to believe in God and had a good childhood, despite the fact that there was a lot of poverty. After this, I left the country and worked with the Irish in Israel as a consultant to the PA, a political assistant to [former Irish Prime Minister] Albert Reynolds, and an advisor to the Government on [Northern] Ireland. After coming back to Ireland, I worked in the US Embassy in Dublin and its Mission in the UN in Geneva as Ireland’s first Political & Human Rights Minister and as Chief Political Advisor. I am currently here in New York at the UN as Head of Mission, which is working to maintain the peace process and to assist the US with their goals, and as of 2021, I will become Consul General to New York.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I am a 34 year old man born in the Philippines and raised in a catholic family. I was born of a poor family and because we didn’t have enough resources I was forced to dropped out in my second year of high school in pursuit of finding a job to support my family. Because I wasn’t able to continue my education in the Philippines I decided to work abroad for a chance to earn money for my family. That decision put me in a state of being far away from my family. As a child I was taught that family is important to me, my family is my first priority and they are the ones I should give my loyalty and devotion. One of the things that matters to me is people, especially the poor, the needy and the downtrodden. A lot of things matter to me which are easily to name, and as for what I enjoy, it’s spending time with my family and sometimes get together with friends, eat out, watch movies, play computer games and of course, it gives me a good time whenever I can drive my vehicles. These are just some of the things I do to escape reality, to enjoy the moment and to put a smile on my face.

I was raised in a family where I get to spend time with my siblings and other relatives who have been a positive influence in my life. For me, one of the major events that made me who I am is my move to Saudi Arabia as a live in helper in 1999. That event made me realize to cherish and appreciate the little things I have in life and not to take anything for granted.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I am 45 years old from the San Francisco Bay area. I was born in the US. I grew up in a place called Hayward, California. I loved living in California, there were many beautiful beaches there. In my spare time I love to play sports and exercise. I am very athletic and I enjoyed playing basketball and running. I also enjoyed surfing. As I got older, I fell in love with the bay area. San Francisco is my favorite city and I hope to visit again soon. I learned a lot about the U.S. by traveling to many states. I visited the Grand Canyon in Arizona, New York City, Las Vegas, and the East Coast in general. These experiences gave me a great view of the U.S. I also traveled abroad and I visited Mexico. I love Mexico and I hope to visit again soon. My favorite thing to do in Mexico was to visit the beaches. The water was beautiful.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I’m 31 and grew up in SC with my parents and three brothers. I was a ballerina for 11 years. I loved exploring the world with my family. My life was very close knit. I loved being a dancer and enjoyed school very much. I went to Ohio State University in Columbus, Ohio where I majored in Criminology and Spanish. I graduated with honors and then earned a masters in Criminal Justice and Peace and Justice. I spent a year volunteering in Costa Rica. I’ve always been drawn to teach and be in the academic setting. I’ve only taught high school for 1.5 years. I thoroughly enjoy my students and consider them my community. I live and eat with them. I give them more than just content in history, I give them a life lesson. I want to build these students into model citizens who are contributing to a better community everyday and I know teaching can help me achieve that. My students come from a wide diversity of backgrounds. They are in public school because their families have extremely low incomes. Most of my students come from single parent households, some do not even have one. My students don’t have mentors at home who can help them navigate what college will look like or what career will be the best for them. A lot of my students have never been outside of the county or traveled at all. Students like these need something to look forward to and teaching is exactly that. I want to inspire them to be greater than they have ever imagined they could be. I want them to know they have other people in this world rooting for them and helping them achieve the things they say they want. I want them to know that the decisions they make today will change their tomorrow. That is why I want to be a teacher.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
What are you, a cop? I’ll give you some answers.

I’m 34 years old, grew up in Michigan, and I recently moved to Boston. I have a degree in Astrophysics and a minor in Astronomy. I speak 4 languages; English, Spanish, French, and Chinese. I lived in Costa Rica, Panama, France, and England for about 3 months each. I have been an elementary school teacher in Panama, an auditor for the United Nations in Brussels and for private companies in California, and a furniture builder in Chongqing. I didn’t have any jobs growing up before all this, but that might be what led to some adventures.

I speak Cantonese fairly well, which has been pretty handy, as I’ve had to help many people in China understand complex things. My Chinese was quite good when I was in Chongqing, but has regressed in Boston, and in San Francisco, since my interview two months ago.

In my spare time, I ride motorcycles, watch dragon boat races (I’ve been to the world championships), travel around, and work on complex computer models. I am interested in environmental and energy issues, but am skeptical of the effectiveness of anything that can’t be accomplished by economics. As a result, I work on such environmental issues a little, trying to understand the problems and solutions, and I write about things that I understand. I got very little into climate science, since it requires its own specialized language, and when I can understand the science (and 98% of all the climate experts can’t explain it to anyone), the costs involved are economically outrageous compared to the few pennies it would take to generate more energy.

To tell you more about me, I think the best thing would be to hear me talk about something you’re interested in, and learn more about my experiences. That would be a better idea than some interview questions.

I think you got my gist though.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I am in my thirties, grew up in [two nearby towns], lived in [nearby city] with my mother from the ages of 14 to 18 while my father worked and started his own [industry]. At this point I was the child of a broken home, and I took it upon myself to care for my family. For the first time in my life, my family was dependent upon my success. I realized that the hard work and right choices would land my father a job and bring my family back together. If I did not succeed, my mother and younger brother would be harmed. My sister had already left for college. My family relied upon me.

I knew that my young life was about to change, and so I made the choices that would propel me to the highest levels of success. Working hard through the school system, I played sports and was on the honor roll. I gave my best effort to make my family proud, which meant helping and caring for others rather than just helping myself. I did not just survive, I excelled.

I was a part of high school debate, which helped to increase my leadership skills and learn to think on my feet. I also graduated from college with my Bachelor’s Degree in Sociology and played with other nationally recognized debaters. I did internships in law school, and I established a private legal practice. My wife and I even have a wonderful, young child. I am constantly working to better myself as a person, leader and father.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I told him I'm a single guy living in Los Angeles. I'm 36. I still live with my mom since my dad has cancer and lives in a nursing home. My stepfather just passed away and I'm looking to get out of that emotional tangle. I grew up in the midwest. I told him some of my hobbies (video games, martial arts, engineering). I told him I'm into a certain branch of Christianity and gave a summary of why I like it (its very contemplative and takes a long-term, ground-level approach to a spiritual life). I told him about my dog. I even told him I enjoy watching anime. Then I drew upon some 7-year-old journal entries (I've been keeping a private journal since I was 7). I did a couple of write-ups on gardening and schoolwork. I put an emphasis on art since I felt he was an artist. Then I decided to go off on a tangent to give him an idea of what kind of person I am since we were sort of personal already and we were getting to the end. I told him about how I feel my life is like a book. I'm like a guy writing a book with all these events from his past (ie experience) making up a storyline and the main character. Then I brought up the idea of a story without an end or an open story. The example I used was Harry Potter. How the end of the final Harry Potter book could easily have been the end of the story, but was not the end because there was another book and there could be another. That is the concept of a story with an open ending.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am a proud American. I am 35 years old. I lived in Toronto, Canada until I was 19, at which point I moved to New York City. My education has always been crucial to me. I received degrees from Princeton and Columbia, and am now about to complete my degree from Yale. I like people. I have excellent communication skills. I have good instincts. I value my country. I respect those who have done good service for it. I love Washington DC. I love my country. I have traveled. I have fought in faraway places. I have risked my life for my country. My wife and my son are precious to me. I enjoy my family. I hope to see my son grow up and become a happy man. (obviously this is way different from the real answer, which I can’t remember, but was just as good. You have to want this job)

In other words, I have a lot of extraneous details but very little content. My interviewer would ask follow-up questions that allowed me to flesh things out (at least somewhat). At the end of 45 minutes, we went over her questions one at a time so I could follow up.

After you go through the interview, take notes. I’ll bet you that they all mean the same thing. I read it through a second time, and my interviewer’s critiques were all the same: You need to make the important aspects of your life salient. I have thought about why that has been so hard, and I think it is because I have a hard time emphasizing the important aspects. I tend to assume it is self-evident. I have to get out of that habit.

I asked: “I am curious as to whether I should make a game out of this, like I have done with My Conquering Emperor.” My interviewer smiled. “Probably not,” she said. “Or you can make a joke out of it, like people say that their family is important to them.” Yeah, no, I don’t think so either. I guess a little humor is a good thing though.

I’ve been pondering certain things lately. In particular, things related to Kēphas “Simon” Petros. I have come up with three themes in which he has told a white lie.

The least important is where he claimed to be virginal, even though he was lying. It is important only because he told people that they were lying if they disagreed with him. He did this one time.

The most important is where he claimed to be pregnant. It was a much bigger lie than the virgin lie. My Emperor once asked if this was the gravest sin he had ever committed. I like to think the more the merrier. Maybe I can do better with my next life. Then I thought, why haven’t I ever denied Christ?

What is the least important one?

The answer is straightforward. He claimed that he is friendly with John the Baptist, even though he is not. Why? He tried to lie his way into Heaven, and an angel almost shot him. I’m going to say that the three lies are related to that.

Consider: How do you lie your way into Heaven? You have to want it, first of all. Beyond that, you have to lie really well, and to the right people. Finally, you have to believe you are worthy.

Why might he lie to Kēphas that he was friends with John the Baptist? As I explained elsewhere, that is because Kēphas loves John the Baptist, even if he is pissed off at Yeshua. Kēphas claimed to be friends with John the Baptist because Kēphas would love to be friends with John the Baptist. I think in the bigger picture, it is related to the virgin lie, that Kēphas is lying to himself, or to God, about who he is.

The last lie occurs in Tractate Athlone. Here, I am amazed to find that Kēphas does not lie to the devil, as Yeshua supposedly did. I get a kick out of this, because this means my Emperor lied to me.

You’re sitting around minding your own business when a guy plops down in the same pew as you and begins sleeping with the light on. This happened in a café in Santa Monica today. When he left, I wrote something for him in my notebook. That was after I explained to my daughter that he was looking for sex. She may have figured it out on her own, but I was happy to supply the confirmation. I wonder if he had sex with one of my church members. One of the notes in my notebook may have been that person, although I couldn’t get any guarantee.

I couldn’t remember the name of the clip on my penis where the pubic hair goes into. I tried looking online, but even though I could find pictures of penises, they weren’t the right kind. I was going to post to Quora asking for such a pic, but someone had asked a similar question already and Google had delivered the answer. I found the answer myself by looking for my shape of penis. That “pinch point” was my ptegion. But it says on Wikipedia that every penis has a ptegion. That makes sense, but I don’t think they are referring to the same thing. Wikipedia’s definition does not describe what the little ridge is, but it is what causes the hairs to be uncomfortable on my penis. I don’t know what you call it but I’m sure it is unique to every penis.

Speaking of unique, I have discussed on Quora before that I have something I think is called a sympathetic erection, that the penis follows the tongue. I would like to have a second opinion on this as well, but I can’t really explain it any better than the quora discussion did. You have to be male, or have watched enough porn, to understand how the penis works. Although I know it is impossible, I wish I could post video from a first person view to show what it looks like. It even happens to me when I am kissing someone on the neck, not just on their mouth.

I have a great memory. When I lived in Santa Monica, I remembered just about everything I experienced there. Now I have the same, or even better, memory in Brooklyn. The worst thing about living in SF is the decrease in memory retention, because it is incredibly noisy there.

We had a meeting at work today. During the meeting, I thought about getting a little coffee at the 7/11 (on Cherry Ave.). They sell a hot pocket brand pizza, although you can’t choose your flavor, so I bought the cheapest one, tomato and something else. When I got back to the office I ate some of the Hot Pocket and wondered why it tasted so bad. I took a bite and spit it out into the trash. I opened it, and it turns out the meat in the Hot Pocket was black. It is packaged, and I can’t figure out how that happens.

I had to throw my computer backpack away when I came back to America because it was considered contraband and I was not allowed to bring it in. I can’t remember the contents of the backpack, but I know I will never see it again.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I am currently 30 years old and live in Fort Mill SC, with my wife and two small children. I grew up in an area of TX where there was a culture shock between church kids and kids who never even heard of a church, it left me feeling alone and isolated. I am still wrestling with this feeling even now and sometimes do not open up to my wife about my thoughts because I do not want to burden her. She is patient with me and she loves me. My parents have never asked to meet her. This is something that keeps me on my toes and wondering if everything will be OK. I constantly wonder if I have enough money to pay the next bill. I am an adult and feel I should know how to handle all the complexities of life. I struggle with this on a daily basis. I like to go on outings with my family, even if we just walk around the local mall or go downtown to the local music venue. I feel very blessed that I have a family that loves me and for what seems like all my friends are Christians. I find great joy in seeing the smile on my children faces when we are making the simplest of memories. It is nice to be around people who love the lord so much, it always gets me in a good mood no matter how rough my day is. My children are a constant reminder that if God can love me this much, I can try harder to be a better person for the ones I love.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I'm Jenn. I'm 31. I grew up in Nashville, TN where my parents still live. While I've been living and working in New York City the last 6 years, I love returning to the South and look forward to bringing my family and the rest of my family up to visit me when I buy the house I'm talking to you about.

I attended Vanderbilt University where I met my future husband, Nathan. We got married shortly after graduation in 1999. We bought our first house in Brooklyn Heights in 2002. We sold our first house in 2004 and moved into a condominium on the Upper West Side. We decided to have children and Nathan moved his 10-year-old son into our apartment. We felt trapped by the constraints of the city. While we thought we could tolerate the limited open space and noisy neighbors, we decided in 2005 to sell and start looking for a house in Nashville, so we rented an apartment in Nashville.

Once in Nashville, we found a house that we loved immediately. We completed an inspection and began renovations right away. However, within days of the inspection, we discovered that some parts of the house needed considerable repairs. Shortly after Nathan, our son and I moved out, we were told that there was toxic mold throughout our house. No tenant should have to live in a house that has harmful mold. Fortunately, we have been able to get a refund from the seller for our security deposit, so we are less impacted by the transaction than many investors. Additionally, the seller reached an agreement with the Realtor and her broker that we will get a refund for our commissions.

My husband and I both decided that while a house would bring us tremendous joy, we needed to be able to focus on our work. So we are currently looking for a short-term rental in another Nashville neighborhood so that we can focus on settling into our new jobs with a clear head, and we are excited about the idea of looking for our permanent home in a few months. We love the location of the house I told you about, and look forward to having a place in the city center and proximity to public transportation.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am 30 years old and I am from Albuquerque, New Mexico. I grew up in a family that valued education and a hard work ethic. I was an excellent student and when it was time to choose a university, I took a stab at CU because it is so highly rated. I came to Boulder in 2008 with my best friend from home and was completely swept off my feet by the mountains, the small town vibe, and the philosophy of this school. My older sister and brother had become the children of liberal politics. CU showed me a new way of thinking and gave me the space to grow and experience all that I could.

Since coming to CU, I have made so many mistakes, but they have taught me so much about myself. The older I got, the more I realized how my family was going to need to change. I tried my best to do the things that would keep my mom alive so that she could see me graduate from CU, but it wasn’t enough. That is when I became fully aware of the fact that she was going to die. I asked myself, “what am I going to do with my life?” So I decided to focus all of my energy on healing.

When I was 24, my mom and I went to a convention for cannabis. We were able to learn more about its medicinal qualities and make a connection with a doctor that would become an important part of my life. My mom did 80% of the work and I did 20% because she was only getting worse. She was always fighting for her life, she was a very strong woman. Even though she was bedridden, she would make sure that I was taken care of. My mom inspired me to help others through my actions and that is how I became a social worker. As a social worker, I want to help others get the healthcare that they need and make a difference in someone’s life.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am 36 years old. I was born and raised in Oklahoma City and went to Putnam City High School. I joined the Army at 20 years old. I trained as a radio operator and as an infantryman. I deployed to Iraq twice and was attached to an infantry company both times. I was a specialist when I deployed the first time and staff sergeant when I deployed the second time. I earned a Purple Heart in 2006 for a TBI (traumatic brain injury) and for shrapnel wounds to my right leg.

I still play a lot of guitar. I was in a punk band back in the day and I am still trying to get my band off the ground. I play the drums some too. I have an email address for my band if you want to check it out. justslyder@hotmail.com

I work as an Administrative Coordinator at the University of Kansas Dental School. I like what I do and I am good at it. I supervise other Administrative Coordinators at the Dental School, as well as the Dental School Secretary.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I am 32 years old. I spent the first 11 years of my life on a farm on the U.P. Michigan, where I helped raise cattle and collect eggs from chickens. When I turned 12 we moved to a more populated area. A year later we bought a dog at an auction. I ended up raising two dogs over 17 years. My parents divorced, and as I got older I moved out with my mom. In the city I studied studio art at a community college in 2006. During this time I also began photographing people. After graduating in 2008 I took a road trip down to Texas and then to California with my mom and our dogs. During this trip I was offered the job of being a photographer’s assistant. I moved to Cali to live with family. I studied Art History at a University for a year. I worked as a visual merchandiser at a department store for a year. I also became close to the M.F.A. program at Cal State Fullerton, where I became a part-time M.F.A. student in 2013. In September 2015, I began my Masters in M.F.A. in Photography at Cal State Fullerton. As a student I’ve gained a lot from visiting places like the Los Angeles Center for Photography and the Pasadena Center for the Photography in this area. I have also had a lot of hands on experience from hanging the M.F.A. shows. After I have finished my thesis project, I intend to return to California to finish my thesis show and become an assistant, working on a portrait project in California.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: Hello. My name is John. I’m 36 and live in New York City with my two daughters, Leslie and Megan. They’re ten and six years old. In my younger years, I was a television reporter. I loved telling others about the interesting things I saw in life. Now, I spend most of my time playing with my kids, and I work part-time for a non-profit organization that supports homeless families. In general, I love my job. It gives me the opportunity to help others in need, and I truly believe in the cause we promote. My interests include fishing, hunting, and playing golf. There is a lot to say about myself, but this question is for 1000 words! What would you like to know about me?

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
That was a long question. I’m about 30 years old now and I was born in Ho Chi Minh City (Saigon) in Vietnam. I was one year old when the country was unified. Both of my parents lived through those difficult times and they taught me how to persevere and to be creative and productive in the face of adversity.

I grew up in a big family, one of eight siblings, and we were so poor that there were times we didn’t have enough to eat. So I learned early on to work hard, to become creative and independent, and to be there for my family. My life was very different in those early years than it is now, but I appreciate that these challenging circumstances made me stronger and more successful today.

I can’t pick just one memory, but one experience I’ve carried with me is this — as a teenager, I was working several part-time jobs to help my family get by. I had a particularly difficult schedule one week — I worked at least 70 hours, and some nights I didn’t get home until after midnight. That was a lot of pressure on an 18 year old.

I remember on that last day, I had just made it home and crawled into bed when the phone rang. It was a friend of mine and he asked me to come to his house, urgently, because he wanted to share something. I knew he meant something bad, and he said there had been a terrible traffic accident and my brother had been involved. I began to cry but my friend told me not to worry, that he would be okay, that he was being treated in a hospital nearby. I called the hospital and my brother was there. He was doing okay.

But that moment was really challenging for me. I started to wonder what I was doing with my life. Why was I wasting so much time on work? Where was I heading? Shouldn’t I have been doing more important things with my time? The lesson for me was to find balance in life and to treat myself and my body well in the face of challenges. This is why I love to make time for the gym and meditate whenever I can. It also reminded me to be there for my friends when they need me.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I am 40 years old. My age is 41 now. I was born in Nevada, USA. I grew up in Las Vegas, Nevada and attended the local schools in that area. I learned to read and write.
