I am a hard working person who takes pride in being supportive, loyal and active.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I am a 41 year old man, I grew up in New York, near Boston, My father is a psychiatrist and a great man, my mother is an amazing, wonderful person who I don’t know if she knew it when I was growing up but we were best friends, and my sisters and I are all best friends to each other as well. I spent the first 30 or so years of my life mostly in Texas, California, Massachusetts, Virginia, etc., moving around the country a lot; before my wife and I settled here in CNY. I got into construction in High School, worked in construction all through college, both as I worked towards my Bachelor’s degree in literature and philosophy and then as I continued afterwards with a graduate degree in theology from Princeton Divinity School. I met my wife while I was studying and continued to work construction. We have five children, I spend much of my time with my kids. We homeschool, and I am active in the world around me politically, with my faith, and in our local community. I’m a white man with straight, thick hair and glasses. What really matters to me are my wife and my children. As far as enjoying goes, I like learning new things, I like engaging in intellectual or philosophical discussions, having fun, sometimes reading philosophy (this does not mean I enjoy reading philosophy, I find it quite challenging and difficult, but I do enjoy discovering things by engaging in philosophical discussions), and being as “good” as I can.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I have a vast life experience. It's hard to condense that into a small time. I'm 41 years old. I grew up in a small town in Ohio. I was born into an old farm family. We were down on our luck at times. During my time living in Ohio I experienced depression, suffering, and famine as well as joy, fun and wealth. In 2001, I graduated 2 years early with a 3.6 GPA. I joined the US Air Force in 2001. In 2004, I graduated top of my class in a group of 350 recruits. In 2005, I joined a command that had never promoted a single Technician in 20 years. Within 3 years, I had promoted 1 Master Sargent, 6 Senior Sargents, 5 Tech Sergeants and 1 Staff Sergeant. I've served 11 years in the USAF. During that time I was deployed 6 times. I've worked as an aerospace engineer, a mission planning officer and an acquisitions officer. I've worked in Flight Test, Aerospace Maintenance, Communications, Command and Control and data analysis. I am a professional-level finance manager. I made a 800 on my SAT math score. I am also a Certified Paralegal (CP) and a Certified Paralegal Manager (CPM). I am a Certified Financial Planner (CFP). I am a Certified Personal Financial Manager (CPFM). I have a bachelor's degree in Aerospace Studies (Leadership) from the University of Maryland University College (UMUC). I am a Golden Key International Honour Society graduate and member of Alpha Phi Sigma (Criminal Justice).

I have volunteered with Boy Scouts of America since 2005. During that time I have worked as an assistant Cub Scout Leader, Merit Badge Counselor, Troop Committee Member, Scoutmaster and Committee Chairman. I currently am in my 8th year as a Scoutmaster. I am also a member of 2 Apple User Groups. I am the owner and administrator of the Wings Over Oakland Club Apple Mac Users Group.

I enjoy photography. I enjoy military aviation and history. I enjoy literature. I have an interest in teaching others. I have a great interest in history, aviation, networking and computers.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I’m 31 and very happy with who I’ve become. I grew up in the silicon valley suburbs of California and grew up to be a midwesterner in my heart of hearts. My fondest memories are of road trips with my parents and siblings. Taking turns driving, the occasional argument over what “certified” meant in the context of orange juice, listening to book on tape and road atlases: were cherished moments in my childhood. It was because of these road trips that I developed a passion for travel and has led to some of the more enriching moments in my life. There were two major events that shaped my outlook on life and how I live it. In high school I got a part time job that I worked 15 hours a week and saved every penny. By the time I was a senior I had saved enough money to travel for 6 months around Europe. I spent time in Britain, France, Spain and Italy. It was a perfect time in my life. I was still young enough to be inexperienced, but old enough to be responsible. I learned to budget and make it on my own, but not so old that I wasn’t easily impressed. It was one of the best experiences of my life. I returned more confident and with a greater appreciation for the good things I had been given. The next big event was getting married. I’ve known my wife since grade school, but we’ve been together for over ten years. It’s so easy to get comfortable in your routine. I took what I’d learned from travel and from life and made our wedding experience a spiritual journey. Our wedding, instead of being a party, was an experience. A communion of the spirits of all who were there and all who had passed. One way that I tried to achieve that was by writing our own vows. We were open and honest about the challenges we faced and how we felt we would deal with them. I had read an article about a woman who lost her father a month before her wedding. In an attempt to bring him into the ceremony, she had written him a letter and placed a seat for him at the ceremony. In that same spirit, I had written a letter to my grandmothers, who both had been a part of my life growing up. After the vows, the officiant read each letter. This brought me to tears, but it also elicited a response from everyone. This reaction was followed by us sealing the letters in little bottles and sealing them into my grandparents’ plot. I know that I will never forget that day and know that the memories of it will grow rich with every passing year. This is why my education and career are important to me. If I can do that for just one more person, then I will have changed that person’s life for the better, and what is better than that?

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am in my late 30s, a native New Yorker, born in Brooklyn to an Anglo-American mother and a Greek father. My brother had cystic fibrosis and a difficult childhood, so we moved often to hospitals for surgeries and so on. I learned sign language at a young age. I guess my natural place in the world had to do with being the big brother and looking out for him and being there for him. Sometimes we got a lot of attention for that but other times we got ignored. I am a loner and a poor learner from my early years at primary school on and I always had a hard time connecting with others emotionally. I had to spend most of the time looking out for my brother and making sure he was OK.

This is also why I hated school, because the environment didn’t offer the conditions necessary for me to learn, and I just could not get along with my teachers. Unfortunately, despite all my efforts I never succeeded in making friends. When I was young, I became addicted to porn and made a lot of money reselling it. The same drive that drove me to social ineptitude drove me to be successful in this area. But I grew out of it and eventually I helped create a company that promotes diversity in the industry. I have maintained my vices: I love internet porn, booze, and sex and do them extensively, which I believe actually contributed to my success in the future.

I am trained in big data and AI and work in financial services. I made some smart investments at the beginning of this decade that put me in a comfortable situation financially (but my brother is no longer with us).

I started doing porn in my early 20s as a one-off project for some extra money, it then developed into a way for me to feel good about myself, a way to experience many different things, and also a way to connect with people. Now I make a living doing porn and I am very happy with it.

I am a very healthy person who loves nature, reading, art, music, outdoor activities, animals, and cooking.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I am a 33-year-old male from Binghamton, New York. My father was a doctor and my mother was a kindergarten teacher. My father was a brilliant and caring person. However, he always worked long hours and very hard. He worked 80 to 100 hours a week. He always told us that my younger brother and I did not need money as much as we needed time. He told us he wanted us to play and not worry about the family bills. My mother always went with us on school trips and field trips.

I always had a good upbringing. I was always surrounded by caring people who taught me how to work hard and be responsible. I have a younger brother who is seven years younger than me. I am very close to him and we have a special relationship. In high school, I joined the wrestling team and football team. I was a hard worker and developed a strong work ethic, which I continue to have to this day.

I never really thought much about why I chose the careers I did. It is only now that I really understand what I was thinking and why I made the decisions I did. My parents were not wealthy. They had to budget every dollar and borrow money at times to buy the things they wanted. One thing that comes from that childhood is the understanding that nothing comes easy or for free. Everything is paid for and has a price. This thought process was always in the back of my mind.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I’m 39, born in Boca Raton, FL, went to college at UCLA and got a degree in Political Science and a minor in International Affairs. I was a state championship winning high school debater who was trained by the same man who taught the Korean high school that was attending UCLA.

I enjoy boxing, crossword puzzles, and conspiracy theories. I’ve worked in construction, restaurants, even once as a gas pump attendant. I’ve been fired from jobs because my boss hated me (they always hated me).

I spent a fair amount of time homeless after college, living on the streets of Los Angeles.

I used to be an alcoholic who drank until blacking out, punching holes in walls. I’m much better now. I’m engaged to a great woman with whom I’ll soon be starting a family.

My family came to America 100 years ago from Eastern Europe. My grandfather was 5 years old. As far as where I came from, I’m really not sure. I haven’t been back there.

I don’t like debates with people who change the rules in the middle.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I am a 32 year old widow, returning to college for the second time. I grew up in southwest Virginia in a very conservative religious community. I was born into this environment and raised to believe this way. We were raised to believe being gay or lesbian was a choice and not the way God made us. Being gay was seen as a sin. I was raised to believe a woman was to be submissive to her husband and that I would one day have children and then it would be my job to tend to the house.

When I was 15, I met a girl. We quickly became best friends and fell in love. I struggled with the feelings I was having and did everything I could to fight them. It was the hardest thing I’ve ever had to do. At 16 I told my mom what I was feeling and she did her best to guide me and help me understand I wasn’t alone. It took me 6 years before I decided that in order to follow what I believe God wanted me to do I had to tell my mom how I felt about her. It was a very hard and painful experience, but I did it and it is one of the best things I ever did. It helped me understand that God loved me for me and that my mom would continue to love me despite my sexual orientation.

It wasn’t until 10 years after that that I was able to accept myself and feel comfortable with who I was. Even though I had been in love with a woman since I was 15, I still was made to feel that what I was doing was wrong.

Due to the fact that I had to spend so much time learning and internalizing what my family believed, I graduated from high school at 20 but never did anything with my diploma. For many years I struggled to feel successful and to find meaning in my life.

A couple years ago I decided I would go back to school. I enrolled at John Tyler Community College in Hopewell, VA in the Fall of 2014 as a full-time student. I continued to live at home with my mom, which was very important to me because I have no siblings, but she was also one of my biggest supporters. This allowed me to focus solely on my studies and to find more time for writing.

I did very well during my first semester, but during the next year, my world began to crumble around me. My mom was diagnosed with stage four lung cancer, which had been caused by heavy smoking for most of her life. During the year I lost my job and our family home. Fortunately, as of the beginning of 2017 I am back in school full-time as well as working part-time. My current grades are great, my work schedule allows for me to have time to write, but my life is far from what I had hoped it would be at this point.

When I sat down to write this I was sure I was going to talk about something else, but as I think back and review where I am today I realize this is the part of my story that mattered the most. I am 33, I live with my mom, I am full-time student, and work in a plant nursery. I am rarely nervous, I have learned to navigate a system that does not believe in me, and I am always surrounded by individuals who do not believe in my success. It took me a long time to learn to not only accept myself and my story, but to also stand on my own and say this is who I am and you either accept me or you do not. I am going to fail and I am going to succeed, but either way my life, my struggles, and my dreams are my own to make and keep.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I'm 36. I grew up in Singapore, but moved to the U.S. in my teenage years. Growing up in Asia, I was fortunate to attend some of the best schools in the country and had access to many resources that I realize now I was extremely lucky to have had. Being a minority gave me insight into a certain way of looking at things. I understood early that individual effort did not really matter and that networks, and who you knew and how they knew you were of higher importance. Once in the U.S. it became very important to make a name for myself and learn to be a person of trust, reliability and someone that could get things done. That meant joining a fraternity in college. It meant being in charge of logistics at school. It meant building my professional network early. It meant hard work and focus early on. I took some chances to learn how I felt about myself - being broke, doing whatever it took for that day to survive. Overall, I would say my upbringing helped me understand the values I had and where I wanted to go. I took responsibility for my own actions - whether that's going on an adventure to remote island in Indonesia with no money in my pocket or staying at home and watching my stocks during the 2008 crash. Decisions such as these helped me to become the person that I am today - someone that is independent, driven, and can be trusted in difficult situations.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I am 39 years old. I was born and raised in the outskirts of Detroit, Michigan, to a loving Italian father and mother of German heritage. Both of my parents had very challenging childhoods. My father was raised by a single mother and grew up during the Depression and during WWII. My mother was raised on a farm and didn't have many opportunities. But both parents took personal responsibility for their life and raised my brother and me to have the same. I have been married to my wonderful wife for 18 years, and we have two wonderful children, ages 17 and 13.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: Hello, my name is Monica. I'm 38 and live in southern New Jersey. I grew up in a lower-middle class household in NJ but spent a lot of my summers visiting my grandparents in Florida and North Carolina and building the person I am today. Growing up I was very shy and never stood up for myself, as I'm told I saw my father as a protective figure when I was younger. I was married young, had 2 wonderful kids and divorced at 31. The divorce was hard on my self esteem and I allowed myself to be mislead to someone who just as I began to realize wasn't good for me emotionally, he passed away. I haven't looked back since I realized that and so far doing quite well. I love to sing and study alternative medicine and am in the process of completing the level II Reiki classes to become certified in Reiki as well as other related classes such as Healing Touch, Guided Imagery, etc. I also have an interest in alternative energy fields that I plan on following through with next year as I'm currently laid off. I also love to spend time with my friends and family. Anything else you would like to know, please feel free to ask. I am a very peaceful person and believe in non-violent ways to solve problems. I believe in making friends not enemies and I would not want to be put in the position of choosing to potentially have to do damage to someone else for any reason.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I come from a long line of Italian-Catholic immigrants, and I am quite proud of that heritage. My great-grandfather came over from Italy with his wife (who I was named after) and his two kids. His daughter is my mother’s mother. I am 34 years old. I lived in Miami until I was 20 and I moved to Orlando, Florida because I had family here. I attended UCF and earned a bachelor’s degree in Secondary Education, with minors in English and Geography. I never ended up teaching due to a lack of opportunities. I started working at McDonald’s when I was 13 years old, during the construction of the Long Island Railroad’s first heavy rail double track east of Queens. I worked there until I was 17 years old, when I started at UCF. Upon leaving UCF, I worked at Office Depot. After several years, and being passed over for advancement, I started working for another telecommunications company for ten years. During that time, I was able to sit my mother’s bedside while she died from cancer. This helped me understand how short and fragile life can be. I try not to take things for granted. I would really like to become a primary school teacher.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
My name is Dr. John Smith. I just turned 42 on my last birthday in June. I am the oldest of three children. My dad is a banker and my mom was a civil servant who had her own business which she recently sold.

Like all children, I was raised in a Christian home. We attended church regularly. But when I was nine, we moved to Southern New Jersey in the US. For the first time in my life I was exposed to people who did not seem to share the same beliefs. I grew up in a predominantly white neighbourhood and in middle school, went to a high school where there were more African Americans than Caucasians. Coming from the culture in Guyana where Christians are in the majority, it was the first time I had encounters with atheists, agnostics and people who openly rejected the Christian religion.

As far back as I can remember, I wanted to be a doctor. But I soon discovered that I also loved talking to people and wanted to study psychology. At the age of 16, I went to a Christian summer camp where a visiting missionary spoke about the atrocities being committed by the Khmer Rouge in Cambodia under Pol Pot and other communist leaders. Many had been killed, their bodies dumped in mass graves in the middle of the forest. Those who survived were summarily executed.

God suddenly became very real to me as I contemplated these atrocities. He was, I discovered, the only solution to my heartbreak and despair. The one thing my parents could not give me was the hope that the world would not end in complete chaos and without God all hope is gone.

It was just what I needed. That moment of clarity, of finding hope in my despair was the genesis of a deep and lasting desire to serve my fellow man, to share the message of hope to others. It has been almost 30 years since that life changing moment, and I have never looked back.

After graduating from high school, I was forced to work for a couple of years in a local restaurant and also did some odd jobs like painting houses to pay the bills.

Then one day it happened. I was going to a job site when I was hit by a drunk driver. He was going 60 mph. My injuries were all internal. When they pulled me from the wreck, I was missing a kidney and I could not feel my legs.

The doctors said I would never walk again. Just when I had given up hope and thought my life had ended, I remember walking back to the hospital that night with my fiancee. It was hard to put one foot in front of the other, but we made it back to the hospital. That was the turning point for me. It was then that I decided I would not just survive, but I would take advantage of all the experience and learning I had.

I returned to college after a one year hiatus and studied psychology. I graduated with distinction from my university. I then went on to medical school at the University of Delaware where I was honoured with the prestigious E. R. Henry Price Award for Academic Excellence in General Surgery.

I am now a renowned head trauma surgeon at the best neurosurgery hospital in the US. I am also a published author with several books which I write on my spare time. I am also a Doctoral Candidate in Psychology.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
Good question. Hmmm… I’m 43. I was born in Santa Monica, California. I grew up mostly in Southern California and spent a year in Amarillo, Texas. In college I traveled a bit (China, Canada, Vietnam, and parts of Europe) and lived briefly in Australia. I was in Europe until 2o11, when I returned to California to work on my Ph.D. for a couple years. I then moved to West Virginia to help run the journalism school at West Virginia University. I took some time off for a few years and traveled on and off with my wife, who is also a journalist.

What matters? A lot. I’m always trying to make the world better. I love learning, which I hope comes through in the books I’ve written, in my new one as well as the ones before it. For me, travel and language learning, and learning about other cultures, have been two of the most transformative parts of my life. There are big ways of changing the world, like learning a new language or starting a company, and small ways, like learning a new way to cook a local dish, or reading a new book or film. I’m constantly trying to learn new skills and to see the world in new ways. This is what the books and the courses at lingq.com are all about.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I’m a 36 year old female raised in a household with both parents. I grew up in rural northern California, the population was about 3,500. There was a lot of farming and logging.

Two major things that shaped who I am are my experience with bullying in school and my travels abroad.

I was bullied in elementary school and part of middle school.

When I was in middle school a boy thought it would be funny to spread word that I was going to get pregnant.

Here’s what I did about it:

I had to shift my schedule and be in different classes than I had been. The entire time I ignored him. I kept smiling and talking to my friends. After a week or so, he stopped bothering me. What I also did was stop allowing my classmates to gossip about what I was doing and whether I was dating other boys, talking to other boys, etc.

I learned to give no fucks.

After he left me alone, I had some respect from my peers. I was no longer a “target”.

In high school I studied abroad with the American Field Service.

My high school and the school I studied abroad at made our relationship official by making it part of the AFS program, so I wasn’t just visiting, I was studying abroad.

My high school applied for a scholarship for a student to go to Japan, and I won.

I never traveled outside of California so I was a virgin.

The same cultural differences I saw on my AFS trip as a teen, I still see today, like heavy gender roles and face saving.

Life changing event as a teen because I realized the world is bigger than my own small town and current bubble

The experience with bullying made me strong and confident

I have visited the below countries:

France

Italy

Japan

Kenya

Kenya was culturally extreme

I also take care of my 80 year old grandparents

I help them take their dog out and I walk their dog. I take my grandmother to dialysis three times a week.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I’m 35. Grew up in small town NY. My parents were very attentive and I was the nerdy guy. I attended TCS with a roommate from JFK. Went on to Long Island University. College was hard with two jobs and a girlfriend. Came out with my AA and BA and met my now wife during my senior year. We began a partnership working two jobs and living in house that needed repairs that we saved our money to restore. We had both sets of parents who had bought houses and were helping us. We had our first child on 6/5/1997. We went back to work the next day until we moved into our new home. We bought our home cash then built a separate cottage which we rented. The tenant moved out and left us with a $7000 build bill. Our parents gave us loans and we fixed it up before selling. The next house we purchased we bought before my new boss/ owner told me I was being paid as a supervisor who doesn’t have a supervisor’s skills or experience. So I quit. Became a school teacher, was laid off at 35, 19 weeks pregnant with my second child due 3/7/2009. I got on unemployment and began looking for work. I found a school, applied to be a assistant, was given an interview, offered a job as the instructor then offered a raise since there was no assistant. I taught for two years until I was laid off and replaced by a friend. At 40 I was hired for my present job as a special agent. I’m now 43. I have a master’s degree in Ed and just completed my teacher’s license. I also have a certification in drug and alcohol counseling and criminal justice. I plan to earn my teacher’s license from the school where I was hired. I teach a very large class. Each year I increase my skills and knowledge and take every course I can. I think I can use my skills to teach in private schools, after school programs and detention centers as an alternative. Teaching is very rewarding for me.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am a fit, creative, and confident 35 year old man. I am 6'2" and weight 215 pounds. I grew up primarily on the southern plains, but I consider Texas to be my home. I was 18 years old when my mom died, and at the time of her passing, I was able to go to Arkansas and live on a ranch with my best friends. I fell in love with the outdoors, and it is still my most cherished time of the year when my best friend runs a ranch for his family in the northern hemisphere.

As I write, I consider my writing skills to be subpar. But it is a skill that I truly wish to polish, because I feel that I have a duty to everyone and to myself to document my philosophy and thoughts in hopes that they may help others.

As a child, I was always interested in thinking and acting with individuality. I also fell in love with self-improvement. I never cared to be athletic or popular, because the beauty of life to me is to take care of myself, and to write, develop businesses, and achieve goals. I have achieved countless goals over the course of my life, and though I have a few that are left, I feel that I have obtained a great deal of wisdom that I wish to share with others.

I believe that selflessness is the most important concept in life. When you are in a position of power or success, and in a position to help others, it is the most fulfilling experience that you will ever have. To live a life that is devoted to helping others and becoming a success is the ultimate combination in life that cannot be replaced or overshadowed by any other kind of fulfillment. But it must be followed up with self-improvement in order to maintain your energy and your attention, and you must not be selfish when it comes to helping others.

As a child, I read books, and found myself very engaged by many of the stories that I read. My all time favorite book was, "The Great Gatsby" by F. Scott Fitzgerald. The book captures the essence of the American dream in a masterful manner, and paints a vivid portrait of what life is like when you first obtain your dream.

I have become, and always have been, an entrepreneur. I was selling t-shirts by the time I was 10 years old, and by the time I was 16 years old, I had made my first major breakthrough by starting two businesses in a row which both were very successful. I then began my entrepreneurial ventures one after another, and I was always able to achieve success. I found this achievement fascinating, and it led me to study philosophy and psychology in an attempt to understand my drive and motivation for being an entrepreneur. I learned so much about the psychology of entrepreneurship, and I feel that I am a master psychologist. My methods for helping others to improve and flourish are unrivaled, and my wisdom is entirely unique to me.

I am often asked, "Why are you an entrepreneur?" I am usually left without words when this question is asked, because I realize that the simple, short, generic answer, "Because I like making my own hours and setting my own goals" is unfulfilling, and it doesn't encapsulate the heart of who I am, and what my desires and motivators are.

The answer, really, is simple, though it is short. I love to create. I love ideas and I love problem solving. I believe in the power of the mind, and in the power of the spirit. And though I can outline many reasons why I believe in being an entrepreneur, there is one answer that I have failed to give in the past, and it is the reason, "Why I am an entrepreneur?"

I am an entrepreneur, because I create. I have created many jobs for myself, and I have created hundreds of jobs for other people. When I was in my early 20s, I bought a foreclosure and renovated it entirely by myself. I then started a second foreclosure project in my early 30s, and my family sold the first house, bought the second house, and we made a great profit.

When I was 21 years old, I bought a currency exchange business. I trained myself on currency exchange, and I hired three key people. We were very successful, and we made great profits for years. Eventually, I was able to open a third business, and we had much success for years, as well.

Entrepreneurship has given me the ability to help people who were looking for jobs. I have always had people come to me and say, "My daughter really needs a job. She is a great worker, and she is going to school. Can you help her?" I have always been able to help, and I have always known that I am doing my part to make this world a better place.

I am told often that I am a perfect example of the American Dream. I was told this because I worked hard when I was a kid and a teenager, and it paid off for me. I also was told this because I am an entrepreneur. I was told this because I love to create, and I was also told this because I love to help others. Because of all of this, I consider myself the perfect example of the American Dream.

Now, I am moving on to the next chapter in my life. I will be a pilot, and a captain. I will become a master of the skies, and I will be able to help people as a pilot. And the airplane will be my canvas, and I will be able to fly across the skies.

The beauty of being an entrepreneur is that you can always learn something new and be even better. That is why I love it, and I am grateful for the many opportunities I have had to create successful businesses.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I am 40 and was born in Wisconsin. I am in the Army and have been married for 10 years now and have four kids. I am the youngest of five children. In high school, I was heavily involved in the marching band. I loved to play golf, listen to music, and ride 4wheelers. As I got older I got more responsible and am the type of person that takes charge when a decision has to be made. However, I am more like my mother in that I love to nurture and care for my children.

I am presently stationed in Arizona and my son is Autistic. We were stationed in Germany last year so he would get an opportunity to get some services. And then he came home during the summer for services there. I chose to come home during the summer to get more services for him and be closer to home. Now that he is stabilized and needs less services, we both were able to come back to our home base. My son has been diagnosed with Autism and he is non verbal. We are in the process of him receiving a diagnosis of ‘severe Autism’ as well. When I signed up for this trip, I wanted to have as much exposure to autism as I could. I wanted to share this experience with my son because this is a great opportunity for him to go somewhere new and meet new people and it is fun for him, as well. Autism is still very rare where we live and is unknown to most people. I want to raise Autism awareness and hope to educate the community about Autism. We spend hours researching Autism online and talking about it with other families.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I am a 43 year old American adult woman who is divorced. I grew up in the suburbs in a pretty nice house. My parents worked hard and took care of me and my sisters. I do not have siblings. I played a lot of sports. When I was 10, I wanted to run track at the Olympics. When I was 12, I wanted to be a singer. My grandmother is a school teacher. She always taught me that if you have a problem, you can always get help. My mother told me to always work hard. I am a very generous person. I have many passions. I love the beauty of nature, the culture of New Orleans and ancient Rome, the history of Jamestown and Yorktown, the art of Salvador Dali, and the literature of Alexander Dumas. I like that I am a big picture thinker and can understand the big picture a lot of the time. This can be empowering but it can also be frustrating when I want to fix a problem and the cause of the problem is too big. I try to keep things in perspective. That doesn’t mean I don’t feel intense emotion or express my emotions. It means I can look at my emotions from a distance and learn and grow from the experience. I have two cats. They are sisters named Eleanor and Penelope. I have a degree in journalism but I now work for a government relations company. I get to write, use my logical analytical skills, and interact with lots of different people on a daily basis.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I am 31. I was born in Troy, New York to Charlie and Laura Dillon. I grew up in Arlington, Texas, which is a Dallas suburb about 15 minutes from the downtown area. I grew up as an only child, I have 1 cousin and 1 aunt and uncle. I grew up fairly poor, but was never aware of my circumstance, my parents always worked hard and taught me and my cousin the difference between want and need. As a kid I was always in to things with heavy motor components, I played football and baseball for a time. But my interest in motor sports and F1 grew exponentially. My father would watch F1 during every grand prix we could find on American television, but a family member brought home a radio recording of the 1993 Belgian Grand Prix at Spa Francorchamps and the rest, as they say, was history. Watching that race sparked a lifelong passion in me to one day race professionally in F1. I would watch the next race on that same radio recording, I believe it was the 1993 Canadian Grand Prix at Montreal. In High School I worked as a mechanic for my Dad's business for a time, but eventually landed an internship at MetLife and was studying economics and business management at the same time. Since graduating from that program, I have worked for Walt Disney World in various finance roles, and then decided to go back to grad school and get my MBA, which I completed in 2019. I am currently pursuing a doctoral degree in global affairs and through that, aim to make some lasting change in the world.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: My name is Bruno. I am 35 years old. I was born in France. But I feel very fortunate to have spent most of my life in many countries around the world such as the UK, Italy, India, Japan, Cambodia, Lebanon. For most of my life I moved every 2 or 3 years or so. Living in so many different countries and cultures allowed me to get exposed and learn about many different perspectives on life. I also think that I am fortunate enough to have lived in different countries and cultures because it was always a good opportunity for me to reflect on my life and what I stood for, which is probably something that I am always grateful for. Some of the major points are: growing up in a society that was mostly focused on success and money and put a high value on certain attributes and certain personality traits that I later realized were quite superficial. This led me to feel like I was not a really good fit for my society and society in general. Feeling like I didn’t fit in, I set off on my own self-improvement journey at a young age to discover the hidden keys to happiness and to self-acceptance. Along the way I became more and more interested in spirituality, in other words finding my unique path in life, and more than anything understanding why we suffer. With many years of studying and self-experimentation I realized I was going through what you could call a life crisis when I was around 20-25. I think that’s when I started developing that particular level of self-awareness. A lot happened in those years until it led me to India where I stayed for 6 years and where I met my guru and a lot of important teachers and my two main life teachers, Mr. Bevan Morris and Mr. Joe Baalack and I would really like to express my gratitude to them because they really had a strong impact on my life and they guided me on my path.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I’m 35 and I grew up in a rural farm community in the Midwest US. The farms have gone out of business due to years of pressure from everything from high energy costs, wages that no longer reflect the cost of living, and even subsidies given to foreign crops. Everything from potatoes to sugar beets to corn to even milk. Over time this has essentially created a farm vacuum.

As I grew up, the people in the community and farms in general went through a mix of political opinions from people believing in individualism to freedom, sovereignty, and self-determination all the way to those who willfully supported the current corrupt system because the system “owed” them.

Other than my father and our store of firearms the only thing that protected my family was the trust of the people in the community and their desire to see everyone else be free and not threatened by the system. We knew who the counterfeiter was, and just how many kids who couldn’t read were being passed along with bribes. We knew about illegals, the crime, and the sheep-like tendencies of those who turned a blind eye to their neighbors, the animals, or the community as a whole.

Overall, this culture taught me to be better and do better. To contribute more than I take. It also gave me a love and appreciation for the agrarian way of life. I ultimately started my own farms on a small scale and engaged with others to learn what happened over the past couple of generations.

This area isn’t completely farmed out, so I hope that my offspring will eventually take it over. They may use it as a hobby or farm on a larger scale.

That’s why it matters to me to raise a family and that my offspring would one day live in an agrarian way. That they live and would continue to live free of coercion, corruption, and tyranny.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am 39 years old, born in Pittsburgh PA, 2 children, older son 8, and younger daughter 5. I have lived in Williamsport Pa with my wife for 6 years. I was born into an average white family, my mother and father were high school sweethearts who later graduated college with bachelors degrees and then married. I lived a relatively average life which I was unaware of. In high school I was the varsity baseball player, class president and played in a high school cover band. After graduating college and getting married I worked at a local iron foundry for 12 years. Until I fell ill to multiple illnesses which forced me to miss work and eventually live in unbearable pain every day for the next 5 years. The years of poor care and inability to access real assistance had me being arrested several times in the span of 5 years until I was properly diagnosed. The system failed me, and it failed my family and caused us to loose everything, we lost all of our savings, everything we owned and for 5 years I lived in a 24 x 10 trailer filled with cardboard boxes. A year ago I was able to obtain the help I needed, and finally things started to come together. Now I have found the diagnosis that was given to me years ago and finally found what was causing me to live in pain. I found my freedom from the pain and work everyday to maintain it. While I have found my freedom from pain, it was not a result of the health care system. Today my life is better than it ever was. I am pain free, my body is much more robust than when I was working at the foundry. I am teaching my kids at home what is important, I have gone from constantly planning my next pain medication dose to actually thinking what is going to be in our next meal. It all sounds very simple and to some might be easy but for me it is an everyday struggle. Every single day is different, today the pain does not show up, but the next I will be living through what feels like torture. The unpredictable aspect of chronic illnesses is the aspect of mental health that makes chronic pain very difficult for the sufferer. Chronic illnesses and pain play a serious role in the mental health of the sufferer.

Some part of me died when I was not taken seriously by doctors. The human part that has a responsibility to take the thoughts and perceptions of another and understand them. I realize how difficult my narrative is, and how difficult it would be to make a judgement or formulate a thought around my illness. It is hard for me to understand it, it is extremely difficult for me to verbalize it. It is hard to have my story documented to sound like a five page essay that is written by someone who has the time to write a five page essay. For me it’s almost impossible. When I write, it feels like I am lost in my own mind, when I speak I feel like I lost my train of thought, or the thoughts don’t come out properly, and when I don’t speak I feel that the person listening did not have the appropriate means to digest what I had said. Having a disability in the mental health arena is much more difficult than physical disability. The disability that does not have a means to be measured, the disability that is on a slow steady decline that is written off as something that you are just looking for attention. It’s very common for people to shut out a person with mental illness, to shut out a person with chronic pain to self medicate, and isolate. Isolation makes the pain much more difficult, the fear of what other people think does not go away. If we could look into each other’s minds, we would all be most interested in each other. The mind is what we can not hide, you can put on make up, you can cut your hair, you can wear designer clothes, you can have money, power and fame, but you can not hide your mind. The mind is the true story and is what makes our story unique. Having a mental disability and a physical disability, the difficulty in finding the right help is very real. All disabilities should be taken serious no matter how that person displays their disability. Isolation is the evil that every person with a disability must fight against every single day. Even though the facade of the mental health disability is what we all see in other people, it is also the one disability that we hide from each other. When that facade begins to crack and show the underlying issue, the other person is not prepared for what is being let into the light. Mental illness is the last illness that can be witnessed and continues to be in our minds the person who we first encountered. It leaves a mark, a scar and a memory that cannot be removed.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I am 40 years old. I was born in Santa Barbara, California and lived there until I was 9 years old. There I went to Marshall Elementary school and made a few friends. My family moved to Edmond, Oklahoma, where I went to school and enjoyed the excellent public school system. I graduated from Edmond High and then enrolled at the University of Oklahoma, where I earned degrees in Computer Science and Mathematics. There I made a lot of great friends who I'm still friends with today. At some point after college I decided to go to Mexico and I really liked living there so much I stayed for 7 years. There I learned Spanish and met my future wife, Uli. After moving back to the USA I worked at various jobs in the San Francisco Bay Area until I was laid off, and then I went back to Mexico. I stayed in Mexico for about another 5 years, during which time I finally learned Spanish. After leaving Mexico, I started staying longer periods of time back in the USA. I eventually moved back to Oklahoma, where I met my current wife, Becky, and the rest is history.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am 39, I was born in Cleveland, Ohio, was raised in Columbus, Ohio, and I currently live in Louisville, KY. I grew up in a devout southern Baptist family (fervent deists), enjoying everything that life has to offer, especially summer, boating, and time spent outside in all manner of adventure sports from water skiing to white water rafting. I studied mathematics, which I found both challenging and rewarding, and where I discovered that my passion for business far exceeded my interest in academic study. I dabbled in some entrepreneurial ventures along the way, but quickly realized that I wanted to live life, not spend it.

Since joining Wall Street in 2004, I have helped my team successfully launch two new business divisions within the trading firm I work at, launched a start-up, and established it as a top Wall Street advisory. Over that time, I’ve lived in Switzerland, Amsterdam, New York City, Hong Kong, Singapore, Boston, Toronto, and Chicago. I moved back to Hong Kong when I met and married my lovely wife. I’ve been a vegetarian for 8 years, and an avid surfer for 14 years. I am a passionate advocate for social entrepreneurship and enjoy educating the public about the nonprofit sector.

In short, I have become a serial practitioner of entrepreneurship, moving to different countries around the world to further my career, and continually challenging myself to work with and learn from the best. I strongly believe that entrepreneurship as a career, and living a life that is fully engaged in the search for one’s purpose are my main driving forces in life.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I’m 33 years old. I grew up in rural North Carolina. Some of my most memorable events occurred in the summer of 1991 after my parents got divorced. I went fishing with my father. Then I went horseback riding. During the course of the summer we went to Silver Dollar City in Branson, Missouri and visited some Indian reservations in Arkansas. It was fun being on our own and away from the adult influence. I wrote songs on my guitar and produced some amateur recording tapes. It was a time of innocence and I didn’t really know what was happening at the time. I started to lose my innocence and for about a decade I made some bad decisions and got into some trouble and derailed myself. I got bored of playing music, had a midlife crisis around 2000, joined the military in 2001, and deployed to Iraq in 2006. I am now a pretty different person than I was in the past and am learning to accept it. I take care of my kids and do a lot of physical work like planting trees and lifting heavy objects. I think it’s a waste of time to use a computer to figure out the social structure when I could be growing corn and other food. It’s frustrating that I’m in prison and have this education that I can’t use to support myself. I’m trying to figure out how to get my education and practice my skills without breaking laws that will get me in trouble. I do enjoy reading about history, political philosophy, theology, and social structure. I feel like I’ve been in the world to see it, so I might as well write about it.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: Good question. I am 34 years old (in US terms, but I’m from Australia originally). I grew up in the beautiful Blue Mountains, not far from Sydney, where I lived until the age of 10, when I moved with my family to Melbourne to live with my mother. As a child I moved around a lot, bouncing from school to school, as my parents were both music teachers, and whenever they changed jobs so did I. I was an average child, good at most things, but with a flare for languages and writing. At 10 I started high school, a place I did not feel I fit in at. I left school when I was 15, and got a job in a publishing house as a typist in the marketing division. I worked in publishing for 10 years (the latter half as an executive editor), before deciding, in 2001, that I wanted a change, and moved to New York, where I have lived since. I came to New York to follow the journey of one of my favorite books, Billy Budd. It’s not a real journey – the book was written in the 1880s – but it is an imaginary one, that takes a young sailor from Australia to America, crossing over from Java, near Indonesia, into the open oceans of the Pacific and the Atlantic. The novel’s events really happened to me in a different form, though. The novel led me to New York, and then to other places around the world. When I first arrived in New York in October 2001, I had just finished another book, which I had been writing for 10 years. It had started out as a piece of non-fiction, but over the course of those years changed into a coming-of-age novel called Sorrows of Sheer Bliss, which I self-published in 2002. My book, which is about a young man coming of age in the 1970s, got me a few good reviews in a few good places, and it was that that attracted the attention of an agent, who got me a book deal with a small indie press in the UK. And from there, well, it snowballed.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
Okay… I am 37 and I was born in the US in the city of Providence, Rhode Island. I grew up in an inner city neighborhood of many ethnicities (see photograph on my ‘Welcome’ page). These were the days when downtown Providence had theaters and nightclubs and family entertainment. Some people at my church also took our family in as ‘hucksters’ during a great time of need for us when we moved into our house on Erie Street (described below).

I mentioned things happening to me that made me who I am. I will spare you the story that I prepared. Instead I will just state that when I was about 15 years old (I am 37 now), I moved into my present location in Southbridge, Massachusetts, where I live to this day.

I am an American citizen. The state to which I owe the allegiance of my vote (and heart) is Massachusetts, the state to which my primary residence is under the guardianship of, the state to which I am registered to vote, and where I possess a voter registration card and where I was born.

At age 30, I opened up a 2nd bank account into which all of my financial support flows, at a financial institution not affiliated with the US government, at a financial institution where the security, privacy, and confidentiality of its customers and their deposits have been legally defined and preserved, and where no federal, state, or local government agency (including the Federal Bureau of Investigation) has the legal power to attach this account or remove money from it.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I’m 30 years old, a first generation American with heritage from Hungary. Born in Dearborn, MI. Raised in

Houston, TX. Which explains a bit about my personality.

For more than 14 years, I’ve lived on the outskirts of Austin, TX and have stayed pretty local when it comes to the work I have done. Though, this isn’t to say I wouldn’t relocate for the right opportunity, I have always lived close to home.

I am a production artist with most of my work experience in direct mail, label, and email marketing. I’ve dealt with different output formats over the years; specifically MS-PowerPoint templates, Windows Metafiles, Photoshop PSDs, Adobe Illustrator AI, Adobe InDesign INDD, Word DOC, and TXT documents. Lately I’ve been more involved in web and email production but the need for a print background has not been lost upon me.

Over the years I have been required to be an expert in both front and back end production. I am constantly working with various versions of Microsoft Office applications for production purposes and for templates that need to be edited and saved back to a format that’s ready to be applied to other documents. Each day there is always a battle between the user (templates) and the content (documents). I am most proud of the fact that I have never been unable to produce a file and none of my files have ever been rejected due to a design and/or production issue.

I am currently a multi-platform, multi-project Art Director for a 20+ year old regional bank that has fewer than 80 branches. I help keep the bank's overall marketing direction grounded and ensure that every message is strong, consistent, and relevant across multiple platforms, which consists of print ads, packaging, and direct mail. My involvement with each piece is extensive and requires attention to detail and an understanding of the big picture. Every piece of work that I am responsible for must be done with efficiency and professionalism.

I have nearly 7 years of experience using Photoshop, CS3 and above. I have been using InDesign CS5, CS5.5 and CS6 for 4+ years. As of late, I have also used Quark XPress 8.5, Dreamweaver CS4 and CS5. My knowledge of HTML & CSS isn’t great but enough to get by. My knowledge of email web production using Litmus is enough for me to keep my job.

I have been working in Adobe Illustrator for over 5 years but my skill is closer to a beginner level. I have used templates more than actually creating graphics from scratch. Nonetheless, my design sense is pretty good and is much stronger in color than on paper.

I have been working with Microsoft Office, specifically PowerPoint since 1998. I can create advanced MS-PowerPoint presentations with ease. It is nothing for me to be stuck in front of a busy track coach as he/she prepares a run-down for an upcoming relay and without having to flinch, ask what he/she is doing. It’s all muscle memory.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I am 31 years old. I was born in the small town of Nesmith, South Carolina, but I currently reside in New Haven, Connecticut. I am a fairly outgoing individual, and I am comfortable in most social situations, which I attribute to my childhood growing up in the South where I was exposed to a variety of people and cultures. I grew up in a very religious household, and it was from there that I developed a passion for the choir, music, traveling, reading, writing and helping others. The one event in my life that had the most impact on who I am today was my decision to enlist in the United States Army. I was deeply affected by the attacks on our homeland on September 11, 2001, and I was compelled to do something to correct this atrocious wrong. As a member of the military, I served my country all over the world.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I will attempt to provide a brief autobiography. I am 40 years old. I grew up in Rocky Mount, NC. From early on I remember the community was impoverished and a lot of high school kids grew up in the street life. For me, I had older siblings and community influence so I was lucky to not be in the street life, though I did sell the occasional weed when I was in high school. Some of my closest high school classmates were in the life. Some of them are still alive, but they are, for the most part, behind bars now. Growing up, I was always into sports. I began to run track in the fifth grade and continued into the 10th grade. I have a deeply ingrained interest in sports, but I did not know if I was talented enough to obtain an athletic scholarship. Therefore, I was academically inclined and wanted to get into a good school to study pre-med to become a pediatrician. I attended a community college, Lenoir Community College, to be close to home where my family could help provide some mentoring during my years there. I only attended a year and a half before transferring to East Carolina University in Greenville, NC. I started out as a pre-med major but my grades dropped too far to the point where I was put on academic probation. I had to take summer school to raise my grades enough for me to be removed from academic probation. I finished with a double major: I received my degree in Biology, but I graduated with honors with a major in Chemistry and a minor in Sports Studies. For my senior research project, I did a team build project by being a referee for a mixed level wheelchair basketball game. It was a great experience.

My interest in how the human body operates led me into physical therapy. However, I wanted to experience working in the physical therapy field prior to going to school, so I applied for an opening that didn’t require me to have a degree. I worked in PT for 3.5 years prior to going to PT school, and I was eventually promoted to Assistant Clinic Manager. I eventually began to want to make a lot of changes in the workplace, but management didn’t want to listen to some of my suggestions. I made some comments to the manager that I did not want to follow orders in violation of state laws for billing certain services. He said if I did not like it then I could “pack my stuff” and leave. I told him he was “undercutting me” and that is when he called me a “traitor” to my race. He implied that I was an Uncle Tom. I went outside to take a few minutes to gather my thoughts so I could reply in a calm and collected manner. It seemed he was looking for an excuse to fire me. When I returned, he said, “Welcome to the unemployment line.” The next day I went to an unemployment hearing to discuss why I did not return to work. I was awarded $400 in unemployment benefits, which ran out after 4 weeks. When the former employer tried to collect the money from unemployment, I told them, “You should sue me because that wasn’t my choice to leave.” I did have some money saved. I also have something that might be considered gold among the unemployed: I have a creative writing portfolio that I had been developing for several years. When my employer said I could go I replied, “Great. I’m off to the ‘Writer’s Club.’ The phrase stuck among my friends as a humorous way to express being released from the corporate world.

I had enough income to pursue my passion of writing. I still had some debts so I sold a few cars, computers, musical instruments, and a drum set I had accumulated over the years, then I proceeded to find a small inexpensive apartment in Atlanta, GA. My intention was to write enough material to obtain a literary agent who would shop it around to publishing houses. However, I didn’t even try to find literary representation. I was a bit burnt out from working in healthcare for 13 years. I knew there were other ways to make a living without selling my life away in a one-sided, hour for an hour, trade of 10% for 90% to someone else who really wasn’t there when I took the 4:30 a.m. alarm clock call on Saturday to take a handicapped patient to a doctor’s appointment. I didn’t understand that the person who bought my 4:30 a.m. Saturday alarm call was on Saturday Night Live making millions of dollars, enjoying their life and family, all on the backs of healthcare workers who never see a piece of the largesse.

Instead of just writing and waiting for someone to find me, I got the idea to self-publish one of my manuscripts as a fiction book that would fit a niche in the marketplace of military books. I knew some military personnel and I had spent time around the military. So, I began writing a fictional account of a modern-day story that took place on a military installation. I wrote and rewrote the same material over and over until I polished it enough to submit to book publishing companies. Within a couple of months I sold my story to a book publisher who would market my book for a percentage of the profits. I still write poetry and intend to eventually publish them under my real name as a collection of poetry and for use in an attempt to self-publish literary books.

I have a great passion for writing that has sustained me for many years. I know of no other way to be in this world than to write in one form or another. Writing has come natural to me, and it was interesting that there were some distinct reasons why I was chosen to do this craft in the world that I live in today.

My mother was not able to complete college. My father started college, but dropped out before graduating. I have two older brothers. One brother was murdered by two teenagers in a drug deal gone bad when my mother was pregnant with me. The other brother, who is 2.5 years older than I, graduated from college, but never was able to find a good job. Eventually, he became a mentally deranged homeless man who often goes through periods of happiness and then sadness. There is much more that could be said about him and I don’t discuss him too much.

I like writing plays and screenplays. I like the technical nature of storytelling and screenwriting. I have an imagination that allows me to visualize things that are not apparent or understood by everyone else. Not everything that we sense or see is fully understood or recognized. For instance, I have often wondered why some songs can make me feel happy. It’s like as soon as I hear the song, I am taken to another place by the music. It is that “other place” I am curious about and the reason some songs have this kind of effect. I would like to write material that can create a feeling that stirs our soul and opens us to a different place. I want to write some material that will cause others to “feel” something in a way they never experienced before.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am 31 years old. I am originally from Windsor but I am currently living in London. I think the most defining moment for me was when I was adopted from an orphanage in Latvia.

When I first found out I was adopted I couldn’t really handle the knowledge. Later on though I came to see it as a blessing. Adoption showed me that I can be loved and provided for no matter what.

They always had my back and I can remember many amazing family events where I felt truly loved and taken care of. It took some time but eventually I would regard my parents as my parents and I still think about what a difference they made in my life.

I think of those moments when I look at the song choices in my set list as a musical director because I try to find and play the songs that remind me of the time I felt most loved or most special.

Music is an amazing thing. When I play it I feel safe, warm, and surrounded by love. I can be happy and sad, euphoric and sad, energetic, and lazy all to the tune of a few well chosen notes.

However music for me isn’t just a way for me to escape or to express myself. Music for me as a musical director is a communication tool. Its how I lead the dancers to dance. It’s how I get them thinking and feeling together in harmony.

Of course music can be used by God too. Music can lift you up when you are feeling lost, alone, and forgotten.

Anytime when you are ready, whether in excitement or in despair just listen and you can always find that you are never really alone.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I am 33. I was born in Columbus Ohio. My mother and father divorced when I was 4 years old. I grew up in western Massachusetts where I had a great childhood. I enjoyed my time in elementary school playing with friends, and participating in baseball and football. Middle school wasn’t as fun for me, I felt like there were a lot of bully’s at that age and some teachers didn’t like me. I was able to play high school football and graduated from Westfield High school. I took a year off before going to college and took a job where I was paid $16.00 an hour to sit at a desk and do filing. I hated that job. I went back to college to study engineering, and was scouted by recruiters from various companies to join their engineering workforce. After talking to various recruiters I decided to join General Motors. I graduated college in 2009 and joined General Motors in 2010. I was able to build hybrid cars for 6 years. I had lots of opportunities to go to Canada, and was offered a position in Milwaukee to work with the engineering group for manufacturing equipment for GM. The bad news was Milwaukee wasn’t a hybrid plant and wouldn’t utilize my current skills as a hybrid lead. I started work in Milwaukee in March of 2016 and unfortunately there were layoffs in November 2016. I was able to transfer to the Lowell hybrid plant where I was promoted to an engineering group lead. I started work at the Lowell plant in 2017 and was able to start a family.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am 45 years old. I grew up in Chicago, Illinois. I had the standard family upbringing. I was a Christian from my earliest memories and still am today. My mom did everything she could to make sure I knew it too. The members of the household that were part of my growth were my parents and one brother. We didn’t have much because my parents had much debt when they married, my mom was a single mom until she remarried when I was 15. I worked throughout most of high school because my parents couldn’t afford me to keep going and I knew that the only way I was going to ever be able to do the things that I wanted to do was to work hard and make money, not ask my parents for it and generally not expect them to have much for me. My folks both had pretty good jobs and lots of friends, but it didn’t leave a lot of money for us kids.

I had a lot of problems in school, but overall I worked my butt off to make sure that I had good grades and was in some activity or another. When I was 16 I had to quit driving and start riding the bus, which was a good experience for me. I learned to deal with the elements better, deal with other people a lot better, and get more exercise (I remember walking to and from school in bad weather from time to time). I went to college and realized quickly that I didn’t want to stay there. I had a second job. The second one had health benefits and I used them. I went to an eye doctor and he prescribed me glasses, but told me that if I wanted to wear them I would have to learn how to tolerate them, that my eyes would naturally adapt to them. This is because they measured my eyes for astigmatism and found that I had none. I never had to wear glasses. But they were the motivation I needed to really get a major part of my life rolling, academic. I liked school, and I had some very good teachers. I saw the world as an opportunity.

Around my senior year of college I accepted Christ, as one of my former high school friends from Oklahoma had done and told me about it. For the next three years I attended Graduate Theological Union in Berkeley, California in an attempt to satisfy some residual curiosity. I pursued a Masters in Theology, and finished all the coursework in the spring of 1996. I had already begun teaching, and at this time I began teaching second language grammar courses at an adult education facility in San Francisco. It was then that I began learning about Classical grammar from an older gentleman who had edited a Chinese university’s grammar.

It was also at this time that I began experiencing negative financial consequences of some of my decisions in life. I was living with a roommate who was in the military but when he got out in 1997 and took a job in Chicago I was faced with finding a roommate to share the rent. I had saved very little, and had loaned several thousands of dollars to people, which did not bode well for my ability to find another roommate. What also was not helpful was the fact that I was a poor fit for just about any place that was willing to rent an apartment. I smoked and had too much stuff to have the kind of “professional look” that apartment complexes wanted. For all intents and purposes I was an object of discrimination and I could feel it. In May 1997 I sold everything except one chair, one lamp, and one suitcase and drove across the country to California to look for a job. I couldn’t afford to finish school because I had had to buy a used car to make the drive. I found a job and finally made my home in California.

Life continued to go along, and I settled into the routine of work, living in a part of San Francisco that I could afford, and doing what I could to have a life in the midst of very busy work. I took an additional course, this one to teach English. I had often heard that the best way to learn a language was to try to teach it. I didn’t realize the applicability of that to other subjects until later. In the meantime I continued to teach my adult language classes. I met my wife before I finished my additional certification. We have been married for 10 years and have two daughters, one 5 and one 1.5.

My original plan in San Francisco was to finish my certification in teaching English and then apply for positions as a “Tutor for English,” which was essentially a glorified English Teacher for ESL classes, giving test prep, academic English tutoring, etc. One thing I was very good at was gaining rapport with students, and I used this to encourage them in their study habits and academic results. I made it my habit to push students past where they thought they could go. Because I was a good teacher I got to a position where I was teaching at all of the district’s schools and served as an employment referral specialist.

It wasn’t until 2004 that the wheels came off. I realized that I would never earn a living wage as an ESL or TESOL instructor and instructor of academic writing (intensive) and that my talents were being wasted. It was time to make a change. I began pursing opportunities in instructional design. This wasn’t too hard because I had been keeping up with web design and development, an area of interest of mine since before the web was wide enough to experience much traffic. Many of the programs I had built and websites I had created had already been built using some of the technologies I was already learning about for my new career. One interview and one lecture on the Delphi Method later and I was an Instructional Designer. I have done this work for nearly 6 years now, and have had many different positions and assignments and was recently rewarded with a title change.

Now I am an Instructional Design Consultant for one of the largest defense contractors in the world. I work with a great team that is very supportive of me and my family. It is an excellent place to be, and though I know that it will not last forever, the tenure here is a welcome improvement over the two previous jobs I held. This job allows me to do the things that I feel I am called to do. I have advanced education to draw on, relevant experience to draw on, and a diverse skill set to draw on as well. I have traveled around the United States doing research on instructional design best practices and learning about education in general. I have researched groups in Switzerland, California, and Italy on how they address the differences in mental abilities in their processes for developing and teaching courses, as well as groups in the UK and in South America and East Asia.

I see that my life experiences have all been part of a learning journey that has led me here. It has been a journey that has seen its fair share of disappointments and failures, but one that has also seen its share of successes and rewards. Overall, I feel that the ups and downs and the many lessons I have been learning have all been beneficial and have contributed to what I know to be an important part of who I am.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I’m 33, born in Japan and raised in East and Southern Africa.  I’ve lived in a number of different countries and cultures and I believe that’s fundamentally shaped who I am, what I believe in, and the way I see the world. A lot of important issues and ideas have been very important to me for a long time.  I was studying in Germany when the 2011 Fukushima disaster happened. I had a Japanese friend at the time who was working as a nurse at a hospital in Tokyo.  She showed me pictures of dead fish, the nuclear reactor still leaking, the radiation, and she told me the epicenter was an hour away from where she grew up.  At the same time, friends were also asking me, “is the nuclear power station in London safe?”  So it kind of felt like this realization hit me, like, we are all on the same small planet.

One of the pivotal experiences in my life was when I was working in an African orphanage for kids with HIV. It was one of the saddest, most difficult things I have ever done, but it was also one of the most amazing, gratifying, and significant things I have ever experienced. It kind of changed my life. Another was when I was part of this very small environmental-focused international NGO that started working at the United Nations to address sustainable development and climate change.  That work was transformative and I gained a lot of experience working in international organizations, large multilateral development banks, including the World Bank, as well as some very inspiring non-governmental organizations focused on sustainable development, gender equality, and climate change.  In the end, those experiences shaped who I am and the way I see the world.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I am 38 years old and was born in the town of Helenwood, TN. I was raised in Oneida, TN and graduated from Oneida High School in 1997. I attended the University of Tennessee at Martin and received a degree in 2001 in Media Studies with a concentration in Digital Media, Game Design, and Instructional Tech. I married my wife in 2002.
