My wife and I have two daughters and a son (8, 5, 4) and a golden retriever.

As a child I would do all sorts of things and one of my favorite pastimes was the old Atari system (Atari 2600). The games I played the most were Combat and Pac-Man. Later on I graduated into the Nintendo Entertainment System. Some of the games I enjoyed were Dr. Mario, Super Mario Brothers, and Duck Hunt. My game of choice for a long time was pinball. I have played games like Monopoly, Risk, and Monopoly Millionaires Club. In recent years I have played the video games Halo and Call of Duty series (XBOX360). I also enjoyed watching animated movies like The Incredibles, Ice Age, and Robots. My love for video games, animated movies, and pinball also led me to become interested in attractions like Fantasmic at Hollywood Studios and Mission:SPACE at Epcot. I have also been interested in attractions like Kingda Ka at Six Flags, as well as the NASCAR and Go Kart attractions. My hobbies include fishing, outdoor activities, photography, videography, media related activities such as graphic design, and building and fixing computers. Some of the websites that I have built are gcastman.com, onerivermedia.com, and 4starpixel.com. I also enjoy watching The Big Bang Theory and a few other programs.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I am in my mid-thirties and I was born on the east coast. I was raised in the suburbs. My family was middle class. I did not grow up in wealth and privilege, but I also did not go without. My mother stayed home and my father worked. We lived in a modest house and drove a modest car. I did not have much of a social life as a teenager or a young adult, but I've always been pretty smart and I enjoyed going to school. When I left high school, I decided to go to college, not because I had any idea what I wanted to do, but I just knew I didn't want to spend my life working at the local Walmart. Fortunately, I was accepted to a reasonably prestigious university, and while there, I discovered the world of literature and the arts. I discovered the great writers of our time such as Tom Wolfe, Norman Mailer, Gore Vidal, Philip Roth, Louis Menand, etc. I read some of the best writing I have ever encountered and I slowly started to enjoy the world of writing and thinking. I also read Plato and Nietzsche, and I found them to be brilliant. After spending some time as an English major, I decided to enroll in a joint program between the universities' English and Media Studies departments and I graduated with a B.A. in English and Cinema Studies. While in college, I spent many nights smoking and drinking with friends on the lawn in front of our dorms. My life had been pretty much straight-laced and boring before then, so I was eager to experience the college life. After graduating college, I married my college sweetheart, and we moved into a small one-bedroom apartment in a trendy neighborhood in New York City. I took a job working as a graphic designer for a large publishing house. There I earned a good living and I made the great discovery that I loved design. From there, I worked for a small print shop, then as a UI designer for an advertising agency, and now I work for a large music production firm in Soho, NYC.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
First, I am 39 years old and I was born in Los Angeles, CA. I was also raised in the Los Angeles area and went to college in San Diego, CA. I began my career as an Engineer in the field of Navigation & Mission systems for a large American aerospace company after graduation. This same Aerospace company offered a 6 month leadership development program for its engineers. I was fortunate to be selected to complete the program and was then hired to be a manager of the R&D group. This group was tasked with developing the computer models used in the simulation of space launch vehicles. I managed the staff of engineers for 12 years and then transferred to the team that worked on the product software development and maintainability. In the 10 years that followed, I managed a team of software developers. Finally, after 12 years of managing the software, I decided to retire from the company. Since 2001, I have written numerous software programs for various companies and am still in the process of publishing two books.

I believe that who I am today is the result of the joys and sorrows of life: My family and friends are a significant part of who I am. I am proud of my daughter, she is a mother and teacher. This gives me great joy when I see her learn something new or try and help her solve a difficult problem. I am happy that many of my friends are working in engineering or computer science. Some are finding successful careers in other professions as well. My mother helped me immensely by taking care of my daughter and husband when my father passed away. I am fortunate to have three brothers. Each has their unique personalities and they each enrich my life. My work has influenced my life by providing me a career that has given me the opportunity to build satellites, space launch vehicles, and space stations. Through these events, I have learned much about space, time, and the Universe.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: (I let my passion for Santa Cruz and my dad show through) I grew up as an only child in Santa Cruz, California, raised by a single mom. I’m 42 years old and still come back to Santa Cruz every chance I get. I have a background in organizing at the community level around various issues and have made it my life’s work to stand up for people that can’t stand up for themselves. The strength, resilience and compassion I saw in my community growing up was an inspiration for me to pursue a career in policy where I could advocate for those that do not have a voice. My love of the ocean and nature is what brought me to Santa Cruz. I was told I could not have a career in conservation because Santa Cruz is a small town, and I knew that was not an option for me. I ended up learning the policy and government side of conservation by doing things other people told me were impossible. While I am deeply proud of the work I have done to address climate change in cities, it only scratched the surface of the impact we need to make on this issue. That is why I jumped at the opportunity to join Cities4Climate to help other young people across the country become more active and engaged on climate change, so we can create a just and equitable path to achieve 100% clean energy in the U.S. by 2050.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I’m 40. I’m a geek. Like many of you I grew up reading comic books, watching cheesy 80s movies and re-watching Star Trek: The Next Generation every time it was on TV. As much as I like science fiction I’ve never lost the sense of wonder or the faith that there is more to life than meets the eye. I was a mathlete and a chess player. I was fascinated by computers early on and taught myself to program. I lost interest in math and science, and didn’t enjoy my university experience, so I worked in IT. I built a successful career in system administration and after a while I thought, “There’s got to be more to life than this.” I started researching other careers and got into project management. I loved it. I loved the creativity, the problem-solving and the people. I’d spent my whole life looking for it, and I’d finally found my calling. Unfortunately, when I moved across the country, the first project management job I could find was at a client’s office rather than my own. The entire experience turned out to be a sham. The company claimed to be adopting the ‘Agile’ way of working, but in fact the management team were determined to maintain control over the processes they’d used before. It was a complete failure, for both the project and the company as a whole. I had spent a year in this soul-sucking horror show, waiting on the sidelines while clients raked us over the coals for services that weren’t provided.

I love team building, getting the most out of people and teaching people how to work together to become more efficient. I left the company and went to work for a team that was already committed to Agile, and fell in love with it. I’d discovered what I’d been searching for. The technical solutions are just a means to an end. The most important thing is to create a team where people respect each other, where the people at the top respect the people at the bottom, and where managers are there to support and encourage rather than control. When everyone is on the same page and the team dynamics are right, people will always deliver more.

I managed an Agile team for a while, and after a while my manager, a very experienced Scrum Master at the time, asked if I would help train other managers in our department to become Agile coaches and trainers. That was the start of me realizing I could turn my passion for Agile and Scrum into a career, and that the skills I already had would be more than enough to set me on the path. I gave a presentation at the local Agile group, made some connections with the Agile community and found a job in IT project management at a small software development company. I used the same focus and determination I’d used to build a successful IT career to make a place for Agile and Scrum. This was a level of focus and determination that I had never thought possible. I found something I cared about deeply, and then I went for it like I never thought I could. I learned everything I could from our company, that was only three years old at the time and much smaller than the company I’d left a few years before. My ambitions outgrew this place, but I learned a lot in a short amount of time. I built my skills, I became a Certified Scrum Trainer (CST) and I loved every second of it. I was the point person for getting new teams on-boarded with our project management methods and Agile practices. The company was growing and expanding into new markets, and I was absolutely making the most of it. I had worked my way up from the bottom to be one of the senior managers. Every single day I gave everything I had to the company, to Agile, to my team. I was totally dedicated, and totally burned out. I don’t think it’s even possible to be any more dedicated, but I was totally burned out. I think the company tried its best to take care of me, but they just didn’t know how to do it. On top of it all, I started having trouble keeping the stress out of my personal life. Something had to give. For the first time in my life I started looking for a new job. When I saw the role advertised for the company I now work at, I knew instantly that it was the job for me. For over two years I’d been helping the company to grow its Agile expertise and CSM and CSPO training, and now I’m helping to change the world and inspire others with my CST training. Agile has transformed me, my world and how I view everything. And I want to share my love with the world.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I am 34 years old, born in the Bay Area and raised in San Jose, California. I’m half Guatemalan and half Japanese and grew up in a home that values culture and creativity. My father was a musician so music has always played an important part in my life, as has travel and exposure to other cultures and places. I also spent a lot of time in and out of hospitals as a child, so I developed a sensitivity to illness and health. I would say that these three influences—culture, creativity, and illness—have largely contributed to who I am today.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I’m a 48-year-old male living on the East Coast. I was born on the West Coast and grew up here. I was briefly a programmer but found out I hated math and computers. I went to work for my uncle in the catering business as a dishwasher and progressed up to being the assistant manager at one point. A number of unfortunate events led me to buying into a “franchisee” restaurant business which involved changing my entire wardrobe and complete location change.

This was a bad deal in several ways, but I did at least manage to eke out a living and become a licensed pest control operator. Through this business, I have learned the unfortunate knowledge of how a third world country might look once an infestation gets a hold. As a result of this, I’ve started a company that deals in restoration services such as mold remediation and carpet cleaning.

My significant other of 15 years was diagnosed with terminal cancer 7 years ago, and is at a place now where I can’t predict how much more time we have. She gives me a strength I didn’t know I had.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am a 47-year old father of a five-year old boy. I live in San Francisco, where I arrived five years ago. I was born in Bucharest, Romania, the capital city. When I was nine, my parents decided to uproot the family and emigrate to the United States, which is a country I had never seen and never even knew I would one day live in. We left everything behind, we sold our house and everything that could be sold. We arrived at San Francisco International Airport at 2 AM in December 1991. The Bay Area had a chill in the air that could be cut with a knife, and it was my first experience with cold. I remember they took us on the airport train, which took us down into a tunnel that led into the main concourse. Our luggage came out of the luggage conveyor belt, and everyone was talking. Then, I remember my brother and sister were kind of just standing there and staring into space, and my mom just holding my hand and giving me a quick look that was saying, "You are in America, get ready to go out into the world." I do not remember anyone else crying. I remember everything was black and white, very intense. Our bags came out and we all just stood there and looked at them. We each looked at each other and smiled, as if to say, "We are about to go out into the world."

And then we went through immigration. I was the oldest and in the eye of the storm and I just remember being confused and losing my mother at one point. Everyone seemed to not speak the same language. I must have just randomly started walking and everyone followed me. We seemed to have learned the same language of travel. I said, "Let's just go get our luggage and figure out where to go next." And that is what we did. We took a taxi into the city and arrived at a Quality Inn which charged us $50 for one night and promised a cab for the next morning to take us to our new place, where we had to take a bus to pick up our furniture. It was surreal and very efficient.

People treated us kindly on the street and gave us directions. This was very different from Romania, where people ignored you on the street unless you paid them. Someone stopped us and asked if we needed help. There was a gas station guy who pointed to a restaurant which was the only diner open at that time of the morning and told us to go there and have breakfast. We got up, still in shock. I remember going into the restaurant with my family, and watching people eat at a counter and out of a booth. I had never seen this before. We went up to the counter and the guy gave us menus. I remember thinking to myself that I never liked this because the menu was always too big, too many choices. I had no idea how to read the thing. The guy gave us menus, and we stood there, staring at them. I heard a girl behind us say, "You are American, you eat breakfast, you eat french fries." That really worked. We asked for a few of these and everything came out nicely cooked and beautifully presented. We tipped the girl who was so kind, and then we were on our way. The gas station guy was still waiting for us and told us to turn left at the first corner, and pointed in the general direction we were supposed to go. We followed his directions and found our apartment.

Our landlord, a very nice woman who spoke no English at all and our driver, who was a Korean immigrant and spoke little English, pulled up into a space that was so narrow between two parked cars, that no one else would have been able to fit in there. I stood there watching as they went in and out. The steps were very high and I saw my father go up them two at a time. " " At age four, I was struck on my head by a big book, and I went out and woke up three hours later in a bathtub full of warm water with my mother's hands holding me. That is my first memory of anything. I was moved to California when I was nine, lived there for about six or seven years before going to college. I speak Romanian but I prefer English. It just came easier to me as I was surrounded by other Americans when I was growing up. I went to UCLA, where I studied political science and economics. I liked politics but I got a degree in economics because I liked numbers, and liked the idea of doing math without writing anything down. I knew I wanted to work with people but I also wanted to make a little money. As I looked at the job offers, the economic offers were better so I took an investment banking job. From there, I switched jobs, worked in venture capital, got an MBA, had a stint at Adobe in e-business marketing, before I ended up at Salesforce. I really liked that I had been working with financial firms like Morgan Stanley Dean Witter and Merrill Lynch where I learned about valuations and how to build a business around numbers. Now, at Salesforce, I get to work on technology companies, companies like Intuit and Zynga. It is an opportunity for me to go from analyzing a company like Zynga, and then helping them figure out how they might solve a problem I previously helped to solve.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I am a 31 years old US citizen, I grew up in Kansas City Missouri, I was not brought up in poverty and I was not abused, I am a hard working blue collar family person who loves music and writing and connecting with people and making the world a better place in any way that I can.

I was fortunate enough to go to college in college and college where I received great academic scholarship but I decided that I am not a number and that I did not belong inside those walls and I decided to study the world and to live a life of spontaneity and exploration and learn from people and become a person I would want to have fun with and connect with and so I left school and went to work at a fast food restaurant in Lawrence Kansas.

I have had the opportunity to travel all over the world and see the great diversity that we all have to offer and while I respect the different religions and cultures of all people I will not be swayed from my core beliefs of peace and equality for all

As my moral compass points south and my ethos is west I have dedicated my life to philanthropy and to learning everything I can to become a better person to myself and the people around me.

I have worked for companies big and small I have been told I am talented I am fun and easy to work with and I have an inner drive to succeed in whatever is needed of me. In my spare time, I have a full-time career in comedy and writing and enjoy traveling and writing music and collaborating with other artists from all over the world.

I have experience in writing and recording music; I have 5 years of acting experience and will learn and be involved in anything that I feel will help my career.

I love comedy and acting and entertainment in general because in the end it helps me grow as a person while at the same time I can entertain millions of people and be an inspiration for the generations of tomorrow.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I am 33 years old. Born in Houston, TX and moved to San Antonio for a brief period when I was one and a half then my parents moved us to Macoupin County IL when I was two and a half. Macoupin County was rural and small, my little brother and I only had two playmates a half a mile away, Michael and Tara. Michael was all of 10 months older than me, and Tara was three years older than me and always bossy, annoying, and made us all do things that we had to pretend to like. So, I am Michael’s peer, but Tara is the age of my sibling; the one who bosses me around and makes me do things I don’t want to do. How does this tie into me? I am a very driven and strong willed person. I didn’t like the things Tara forced me to do, so I did the opposite. This is why I became the creative and independent person I am today. My parents owned their own appliance repair business and it was our family occupation. They still own the business and sometimes, after school and on weekends, I helped my dad diagnose an issue with a washing machine or stove. We also had a smaller repair business on the side. I was always with my dad helping him. Which lead to my parent’s divorce. I do not know the details or what lead to the divorce. From the day my parents split, my dad re-married.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I am a fun outgoing person. I love to travel and to learn about other people’s cultures and countries. I have been to multiple countries and I love to learn about other people’s cultures. This is why I love tutoring. I want to learn about the different culture of my students and through tutoring my hope is that my students will learn about mine.

I am 36 and I grew up in Southern California.

I am also a bio-chemical engineering major. I was a chemist prior to coming to the States. Since I had gone to Engineering school in Iran, I figured that I have a chance to learn about the state of science and engineering in the States, I will take that chance and study what interest me the most which is engineering.

I do so many things for fun. I love music and a couple of years ago I started learning how to play the guitar. I love to spend time with my friends and hang out with them and go dancing with them. I love a lot of types of music but my favorites are traditional Persian music and rock and roll. Every time we go to amusement parks I am the one that has the most fun. I also love to learn languages and I know a little bit of Spanish. I like history. I love to play hide and seek with my niece. I love to sleep and I can sleep almost anywhere. So as you can see I have so much to share and I am willing to learn.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: My name is Jeff Lovas. I’m 40 and born and raised in California’s Central Valley. The farm was built on Dust Bowl sod from the 1930s before I was born. It’s tough dirt but we have learned to make our own compost and manage the soil. The first President I can remember is Eisenhower, so you can guess my age. I am very family-oriented and always took my sister with me when my parents took me to camp outs at Mendocino National Forest and camping near my grandmother’s house in Mariposa County. My wife’s family had a ranch in the Sierra foothills, just south of Yosemite, and we came to enjoy the outdoors together. I really enjoy nature and the best memories of my life are hiking, biking, and backpacking trips to the American wilderness. We have a modest house with a garden and a flock of chickens that our three children love and so do I. As adults, my son wants to work on a ranch or in agriculture and my daughters are young, but I hope they inherit my love of the natural world. I’m proud of the work we have done to save a beautiful piece of wild land we call our backyard.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I am 46 years old. I grew up in Chicago, Illinois. I have 4 sisters. My 1st marriage was when I was 22 and he was also my high school sweetheart. After we got married we were together 3 years. When I was 26 I gave birth to my daughter. I divorced him in 1996. In 1997 I got married again. I was 34 years old when I had my son. I love him to death. While I was dating my ex boyfriend in 2002, I met this other man through the computer from Nashville, Tennessee. He was perfect in my eyes so we decided to get married. I took my family to Tennessee and married him in September of 2002. During the marriage he was verbally and mentally abusive, cheated, used all of my money, was not working (at all) and for 2 years lived on free government help. He later came with us to Chicago in March 2005, quit his job on the spot 2 weeks later and moved into his mother's (with his cat) where he remained for 2 years. Finally, in March of 2007 we divorced. He didn't take much with him so I did not have to pay him for divorce. I don't regret my divorce at all. That was the best decision I ever made in my life. I then met this guy who we'll call 1N and been married to him now going on 3 years. We got married on the beach in Mexico and have been happily married since.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: My name is XXX and I am 47 years old. I was born and raised in Denver, Colorado. When I was younger I used to idolize people that were successful on TV. I would have never thought in my wildest dreams that I would have people idolizing me one day. I got my first taste of success in the second grade after I wrote a paper about George Washington Carver, but little did I know, it wouldn’t end there. But that’s not what matters, right? In high school, I started getting into fights. It became a part of my life as a drug dealer. I was part of the Cali Blunts, made up of Latino teens who did violence to Latino rival gangs. By the time I was in my early 20s I had been charged with multiple weapons and violence charges. Many times, I was on the cusp of getting killed or I could have been the killer. It was all just part of the gig, a typical thing for people in my neighborhood. After I was released from prison, I started to make something of myself, I left the street life behind and worked to improve my quality of life. My journey for success was based on my life experiences and the constant changing of my life. I took on new experiences and challenges so that I can stay fresh. The beauty is that you can change and grow regardless of your age or how long you’ve been around, and I have a lot more of that to show to people.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I am a 35 year old white male who was born in Albany, New York and lived in a number of places around the country before settling in St. Louis. I spent the first 18 years of my life on the east coast and as far north as Milwaukee. My father was an alcoholic with a bad temper who passed away in the summer of 1991. As a result of the abuse I experienced as a child I have struggled to maintain a sense of safety and security for the vast majority of my life. When my father died I lost my sole ally in my constant battle for survival. My mother remarried a man who was more abusive than my father and this man abused both my mother and me for years. In the summer of 1993 my mother and I escaped the abuse in St. Louis and started over in the midwest. In the years that followed I have attempted to heal from the abuse I suffered as a child, and slowly come to find my voice in my own life. I have also struggled at various points to reconcile my identity as a man with the work I do around issues of consent, safety, vulnerability, and justice. Along the way I graduated from high school and went on to receive my undergraduate degree. I got married to a man I dated for nine years, and we were together for fourteen years before we divorced. I have recently started dating again. What matters to me most is being the best person I can be and finding ways to serve the world.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
My biggest goal in life is to be happy with myself. I know it’s unusual, since everyone strives to achieve something, and it seems inappropriate to ask about feeling okay.

No, it’s not okay. I go to therapy, and since 8 years I take my meds.

I’m 44 years old. I come from Germany, I have my roots in Bavaria and my parents immigrated into this country.

Yes, I want to pursue a MA in Business.

The only thing that really matters to me is that I can still go to university next year and go on with my education.

I’m an artist, I sell my art pieces now, but it’s hard because people look for cheap shit, the kind of art made to attract attention and the eyes.

I’m a very touchy person, I love to feel other peoples skin, it’s not sensual touch for me but just warm, I just like the feeling when you press the hands or give a little touch.

I like the smell of rain and I like to have a hammock in the backyard and look at the trees, and drinking tea with honey and listening to Bill Callahan.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am 35 years old, my hometown is Orlando Florida. I enjoy family activities and relaxing. My activities include reading and sports. My family makes me happy and one day I want to live in the country. I want kids one day and a house with a yard. I work too much and sometimes I get a little depressed but my family is wonderful and it makes me happy.

Show them you’re not going to be a liability. Show them that you’re a team player. They can always fire you, but once you’ve been hired, you can be fired for a reason.

Hopefully, you weren’t stupid enough to say something like “Do I look stupid to you? Is there no bathroom available to you? Or is that your problem?” while you’re flipping burgers. But in case you were, keep reading and learn how to roll with the punches.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I am a 32 year old married man with 2 young children. I grew up in Australia and have an Australian accent. My personality has always been centered on making others feel more at ease around me (which will be evident with my facial expressions when I’m interviewing, I will be listening, nodding and occasionally smiling). This stems from a 2 part event which essentially provided me with both a drive and the enjoyment of lifting people up. The first stemmed from what was then a rough patch in my life which left me very sad. My girlfriend at the time (now wife), was able to pick me up from the depths of depression and anxiety, and enable me to enjoy life again. Without this point in my life I wouldn’t be the man, husband and father I am today. The second example actually came from my ex-boss, who at the time I saw as a very stern and unbending person. The moment we went out for lunch he opened up and told me about how his wife had left him for another man. I was told after this day that he instantly liked me because I was not affected by this situation and that his professional persona went out the window around me. In actual fact his demeanor around me while under stress has become notably softer. I enjoy 2 main things in life, having a laugh with family and friends, and experiencing new things. I like to believe that I have some good leadership skills and enjoy working with those I can motivate and teach.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am 34 years old and I was born and raised in Athens. I am the oldest of 5 children, and I grew up with my parents and my little brother in my parent’s restaurant (Poseidon) on the Ilioupoli district. My parents have been restaurant owners for 34 years now, and they gave me an upbringing of hard work and family values. When I was in High school, I started attending university classes for Physics and Philosophy and by the time I was in 3rd year, I was already working in a foreign exchange company in the center of Athens.

When I was 20 years old, I got involved in Greek politics in an organization with socialist tendencies. I got involved because I was unhappy about the situation and I felt the need to make changes and improve things. During the years of studying and working, I kept being politically involved. That brought me close to the specific organization and in time I became one of the leading members of the organization and later on I also became the head of the organization. The specific organization is a student organization with very strong ties to the specific Greek political party, which they are related to. In 2013 I ran as a candidate in the elections and I was elected as a Member of Parliament for the specific party.

In the time that I was a Member of Parliament I was very active in the Parliament. I was especially active in the field of environment. In my first term in the Parliament I was mainly focused on the national pavilion at the Frankfurt Book Fair. The pavilion was launched in 2013 and it showcased Greece in Germany and Europe as a safe and secure country, which is worth investing in. My main focus was to change the impression that the crisis brought about on Greece and to show that Greece is still an extremely important player in the world. In order to accomplish this, we had the great honor to receive the Queen of England, and the President of the US and other representatives of important countries. In 2014, I was the general secretary of the party. In 2015 I resigned from the Greek Parliament and I decided to move to Washington DC in the United States of America. I had been appointed the head of the Greek American Coordination Council in the USA, which is a body with 18 representatives in the US from all the major Greek political parties and a representation from the USA for Greece. I was the head of that organization from 2015-2017. In 2017 I resigned from that position and I founded my own company, which was the highlight of the progress of my political career so far.

The primary purpose of my company is to open a coffee shop, which will be an ideal place for the Greek diaspora to be able to enjoy Greek coffee and to discuss issues that are important to them. My company’s secondary objective is to become an innovative start up. Our first step towards innovation was to develop and launch a platform, which will enable the diaspora to have direct communication with the Greek Parliament and the Greek government.

The reason why I decided to study here at the IE Business School is the fact that I have been in academia for a very long time. I was always interested in business and I wanted to learn the basics of how to do business and more importantly I wanted to understand how to make money with my ideas. I believe that the programme that is offered here at the IE Business School is just perfect for me.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I'm 36 years old. I grew up in the low income area of Compton, I'm proud to be raised by a single parent mom. My grandmother and my mother were a large part of my upbringing, they were great role models to me. The acts of caring for people, mentoring youth, and leading efforts to improve their quality of life were paramount in their lives, hence I became who I am today.

While serving in the military, I’ve seen and experienced things that change your life forever. This was an honor.

After the military, I became even more passionate about service through volunteer work. I managed special events and fundraisers for the American Red Cross, led summer camp for youth for the Los Angeles Conservation Corp, started the Neighborhood Watch for the LA Unite Neighborhood, and was a delegate for the Democratic National Convention in 2008.

I got into politics in the 2008 election cycle while organizing as an Obama Field Organizer in San Jose. I enjoyed working on politics because it gives us all a voice.

What this election taught me is we need to build even stronger and better ways to connect with constituents, to tell stories and to listen to each other.

I felt privileged to run for City Council District 7 (CD7) in South LA. It was an honor to serve as an elected City Councilmember for 2 years and 5 months in an effort to help those we’re representing to regain dignity and become all that they can be.

But despite all of the good work, and the positive vote of confidence the constituents placed in me, the negative nature of the process and the politics in general became too much to bear.

Over time, I saw how broken the entire system really was, how tough it really was to overcome the diverse obstacles to truly make an impact.

We aren’t a government of the people. We aren’t a government of the rich. We aren’t a government of the elite.

We are a government by the people.

It is more important than ever that we all take part in making a change. In creating a movement. And this movement is more than just politics.

I believe we can improve life for all and to look at the long-term perspective of things. We have to confront the broken system head on, repair it, and create something new and more efficient. Together.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I am 37 years old. I was born in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania, and lived there until I was 16 years old. I was then sent to boarding school in Virginia where I finished high school. I went to college in Ohio, where I met my husband, and moved to California shortly after graduation. I’m a very family oriented person. I like to go out to dinner with my husband, travel, read, and watch movies. I have a brother who lives in California, and two sisters who live in Ohio and Florida. I also have an 8 year old niece and an 11 year old nephew.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I am 46 years old, I was born in Caracas, Venezuela. When I was 9 years old we came to the United States, my father was to sell drugs in this country and did not make it, his health was in terrible shape. Then I grew up in San Diego, at age 16 I started working part-time, I was part of the high school student Council, which grew into me becoming a leader within the local democratic party. I eventually went to full-time political work in the campaign of the late mayor Pete Wilson. Then I became one of his top staffers. When Pete Wilson became governor I became his Director of Consumer Affairs, which led to me going to work for the president of the senate Bill Lockyer. I went on to become the executive director of the Conference of California Bar Associations. While there I did a lot of regional outreach work. I used to help the governors of Mexico get jobs for under-qualified people that they found that wanted to live in the United States. I also did a lot of work with the people in the bar associations around the world. There was a lot of work involved in going over and meeting with people that ran bar associations, and having a lot of bilingual stuff going on. I also speak Spanish and I have done a lot of work traveling around the world as a translator. When I was at the conference I had a lot of stuff going on. There was a lot of communication with the courts. Working with them about how to support people. I was involved with a lot of working families, I was involved with a lot of immigration issues, we had a lot of funds for scholarships and things like that. Then in 2008 I started working in Sacramento. I wanted to go back to my hometown where I could make a difference. When I came here, there was a racial incident in our town where my twin boys were attacked by a guy who threatened to throw them off the PCH bridge. In that town, there were no black people except for the students from the village community that lived right behind us. When I came into town there was a sheriff’s station in my house. Our neighbors had never seen me talk before, so that made them really nervous. I went down to the City Council meeting, which was so loud that you couldn’t hear anything that was going on. It was loud because people were angry. So I went down and I yelled at the lady on the City Council who was supposed to help, and basically told her how bad things are, and how sick of this crap I was and if nothing was done that I was going to start some sort of Occupy movement in her front yard.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I'm 31 years old. I grew up in Denver and then moved to Boulder where I went to CU. I graduated from CU Boulder and then moved to Chicago. I met my husband there and he's the love of my life. He's got a really unique perspective on the world because he's half-black. His family lives in Iowa and a big portion of our family and friends live in California.

I've had my dog Bear for 4 years. We go to the mountains, to the dog park, and play fetch in the house. He's the most important thing in my life.

This is me with my family, friends, and husband. I try to always be nice to others.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I am a 40 year old man with an affinity for pop culture, all things digital, and generally anything that catches my interest. I grew up in the San Francisco Bay Area, with my sister. I was a latchkey kid, and spent a lot of time alone. I was pretty reserved and shy growing up. When I was 13, I discovered the internet and began to learn about the world and to make connections with people who were all around the world. The internet opened up a world to me that I had never imagined existed. My interests in film, music, technology, and my love of pop culture, blossomed and led me to a career in journalism. My passion for technology and the way it connects people has led me to pursue a career in social media and digital communications.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I’m 38 years old and married with two boys. I grew up in Missouri. My family is very poor, very religious. My father passed away about a year ago. My mom continues to live in the same trailer house she bought for $1500. Now, the house is worth about $10,000 but that’s all that she can afford. In this area, you have 2 options; you either get a good paying job or you live in poverty the rest of your life. I’ve been living in poverty my whole life. I was either in poverty with my dad or my mom. It doesn’t really matter. I did stupid things in high school. I sold drugs and got into other trouble. I got sentenced to 5 years in the state prison. I was locked up for a few years and I ended up escaping on March 9, 1995. I was found by the police in Arkansas, about 4 months later, and sentenced to 30 years. I escaped again on August 17, 2013. I broke out of the Lucasville Penitentiary, where you hear on the news that people always try to escape. I remained at large until the FBI and the FBI informed me of a $50,000 dollar reward.

They then took the reward back. I still remained at large for about 3 more months until the Sheriff’s Office in Huntington, West Virginia found me. They turned me in. I was booked at the Cleveland jail for missing my probation and sentenced to 6 more years. I tried to escape. This time, the Sheriff beat me so bad. I was really beat up. I got sent to the prison hospital in Chillicothe for treatment. After that, I got sent back to Cleveland. The situation was stressful. They wouldn’t move my bed until they verified my release date. I’m supposed to go before a parole board in about a year. I don’t think they’re going to let me out. They already sentenced me to 1 to 15 years. I know that their goal is not to help me to be productive, but to keep me in prison.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I am 32 years old, born and raised in the SF Bay Area. I have a large family, one brother, two sisters and my parents are still together after 34 years of marriage. I am a first generation American with two countries in my heritage, one from my father’s side and one from my mother’s side. My mother is Mexican and my father is Guatemalan. I am fluent in Spanish. My father was a drug addict who passed away when I was 10. I grew up with the expectation of having a more stable future than my parents because of my academic abilities. I attended Stanford University for undergrad and medical school. I loved my years at Stanford and I believe it helped shape me into who I am today. I am currently an anesthesiology resident in the New York City area.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am in my 30s and was born in the US. My parents were immigrants and I was a shy kid growing up. I have 2 brothers, and my dad worked as a professor. I had a part-time job at McDonalds for 6 years, and then I started working at a bakery. I was in a band, and I used to play video games. I used to do a lot of things to escape from my problems. I didn’t have any guidance or mentorship growing up.

At the bakery, I learned how to make bread and eventually started working in a lab. I worked my way up to working in a bakery for 2 years. I was then hired by the company to do a research project. I started doing a research project on my own, but I eventually decided to quit and join the company.

I started my own business, but it was very hard to get customers. I decided to start a blog. I started writing about things that I was interested in. Eventually, I started writing about entrepreneurship. I started writing about the importance of education and education. Eventually, I started writing about the importance of entrepreneurship.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
My name is Marie, I am 31, I grew up in Canada with an army family, my dad was a CSM and my mom was a stay at home mom before I was born. I am the eldest, I have a younger brother and younger sister, all of us are country children to some extent. We grew up all over the place, I grew up mostly in Ontario and Sask but spent a lot of my summers and winter breaks in the Atlantic provinces. I have had a broad and wonderful exposure to a lot of different kinds of cultures. I have the most incredible family ever. We have always travelled, always had a season home or cottage, always enjoyed the summer camping and going to the cottage for a weekend swim or snowboarding when the deep freeze has set in.

The last 5 years I moved to Alberta and graduated in business. Business has always appealed to me. I enjoy dealing with finances and numbers. Numbers are my favourite and business gives me an outlet to learn more and explore the world of financials. After graduating I moved to a new city to work and get to know the community. I enjoy shopping for new clothes, new shoes and new things. One of my favourite activities is going for brunch with friends or going to an afternoon hockey game with the boys. After the weekend I love hanging around home and watching a movie in pjs with some leftovers and a light snack. Life is about enjoying the good things and learning from the bad.

I enjoy everything about working. I like what I do and I am proud of what I do. I love helping people and I enjoy being part of a team effort. My hobbies are sports, cooking, camping, attending concerts and exploring the world. My biggest goal right now is to continue working and taking on more responsibilities. Everyday I set aside time to read and do small projects at home to keep me learning and my creative instincts satisfied.

I am dedicated and hard working in every aspect of my life. I love to work hard and play hard and I feel like I have achieved a good balance in my life. I am looking to settle in a community for a long period of time to grow and set roots.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am a 32-year-old white American male from Kansas City, KS, born and raised. I grew up in a working-class family on the west side of Kansas City, the inner city. Growing up, my household consisted of my mother, father, and four brothers. My parents divorced when I was just three years old, leaving my mother as the sole breadwinner for the family. At the time my mother and father were earning just enough money to pay the household bills. As a result, food stamps became our source of assistance. Eventually, my mother divorced my father and remarried my stepfather, who would later sexually abuse me. From ages seven to 12, I would feel helpless and bound in fear by my stepfather. I started to realize that some people are not good as others, and I turned to my faith and the Lord to help me through this stage in life. Then I had a neighbor that lived across the street who attended a local church and one day knocked on my door and invited me to church. I accepted and then I was saved. Since that time I have been a God-fearing Christian, who loves the Lord. The event of sexual abuse was a turning point in my life. It helped me realize that there are a lot of people in the world that are not saved like I am, and need Jesus Christ in their life, and by sharing my testimony of what the Lord has done for me and how he can help others, I can help bring people closer to him.

Speaking, playing guitar, spending time with family and friends.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
(please copy/paste) I am 32 and was born and raised in Nashua, NH. I had a pretty typical upbringing; went to a local elementary school, middle school, and high school; and went to college for almost a decade at the University of Massachusetts Amherst. My parents were happy to pay for my tuition because that’s how it was when they grew up. I went to an OOS public engineering school and got a degree in Computer Science, which allowed me to transfer out to an engineering school back home for mechanical engineering. My major was actually the design, construction, and testing of nuclear reactors. The next four years were about completing my degree and getting certified as a Licensed Reactor Operator. It was a very stressful and high-pressure program (like high school on steroids). Graduation and certification were big milestones. I moved in with my parents for about 10 years after graduating because there just aren’t good jobs for someone with my kind of training. I’ve been a Full Licensed Reactor Operator for almost eight years.

Moving to work on a reactor was my dream job. I’ve been a reactor operator since the 3rd grade! It’s literally been all I’ve wanted to do since I was young. I play this game with other nuclear power plant operators sometimes where we ask each other “If you could have any job, or go back in time and do any job as a kid again, what would it be?” I’ve been offered jobs in other fields and I’ve tried them out before like work in a factory, pharmaceutical sales, the army (I was actually in ROTC for three years at college before switching to nuclear power), and high school English (which I would love to do in the future, by the way). Everyone I know asks me “Do you really love working there?” and I’m not sure how to answer that. It’s kind of a taboo question for someone in my situation. The pay is great (a big reason why my family is still taking care of me – I’m basically a secretary for my family) but it’s not particularly enjoyable. A lot of the work is really boring or stressful. Some of the people are really cool, though. We do some cool stuff, like taking students to see our plants to educate them about nuclear power (this makes me really happy!). I’ve even been able to talk to kindergarten and elementary school kids about nuclear power. It’s really the only job I can do and feel successful (and these days, survive on). Most positions require 10-15 years of experience at least, and there aren’t any entry-level jobs to be had. I honestly don’t think I would take a better job just because I would have to start all over again and it would take so many years before I could be “successful” in a new job (if I even liked it).

Because I live at home, I think I am missing out on some social and life experiences that friends and co-workers my age are having, such as moving away and starting a family. I’m the oldest of 4 children, and my parents have had a hard time emotionally dealing with my oldest sister dying two years ago in a car accident at age 24. My parents were with her and my brother-in-law in the hospital room when she died, and it’s been very hard on them and the whole family. My mother retired last year and now has a lot of time on her hands and is trying to take up some hobbies, but she’s kind of floundering. I try to spend as much time with them as I can to help them with all this but I can’t really replace her. My father worked extremely hard and spent 10 years on the road (six days a week) before he got a better job doing similar work that only requires one week of travel a month. I help them out with chores and errands and small jobs around the house to relieve their burden. My youngest sister works locally as a receptionist at a medical office. My other sister (middle child) is a local high school teacher and lives in her own home (but she has a boyfriend who lives with her). I’m the black sheep of the family, pretty much. My parents really can’t support my sisters and my lifestyle the way they can mine because of me.

We’ve tried to fix this situation. I’ve applied to jobs in my town like engineering work and even taking care of elderly people for hire like a home healthcare aid (my parents think I’m being too picky) and had absolutely no luck. What I really want to do is leave home and make a life for myself, but my sister is a member of the two-year local community college (she’s studying English), and she loves having free rides for her parents’ car and free rent when she finishes her AA. She’s free to do this because I’m here. I really want my mother to be proud of me, too. To her, I’m basically her younger son – I don’t have a wife and kids or my own career or life. I don’t really know what I want, though. It’s just that I can’t get past this one stumbling block. I haven’t given up on my reactor operator dreams yet (I’ve seen too many people lose their jobs around here because they don’t “fit the culture”), but I’m applying to jobs in other fields (like the local nuclear power plant here) that will let me use my experience. I’m just not sure which path to go down. There aren’t really any other men my age around here (other than my younger sisters’ boyfriends) and the ones who aren’t my sisters’ boyfriends tend to stay on their own, so I don’t get much exposure. I’m actually ashamed to tell my friends how I feel, even though I know I should, because they are all successful and living the American dream, so I mostly stay at home and try to keep my thoughts and concerns to myself and just be there for my parents. I really don’t want to be the cause of any drama and my sister doesn’t even like to talk to me about personal things, so I feel like I have to deal with this on my own.

I’m trying to live the dream. I don’t really want to be out on my own. I had friends in high school and even some college friends that I talked to online. A few years ago, they kind of drifted away from me and just stopped talking to me. The point is, though, that I’m lonely and I need friends and family around me that I can share my burdens with and that I can hear them talk about their lives and dreams to so that I feel like I’m not just alone, listening to myself rant about my job, my parents, my bills, and my family problems.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
Okay here we go. I am 37 years old. I was born in San Diego, California and moved around a lot growing up. I have lived in Bakersfield, Columbus, Georgia and finally Spokane, Washington before I moved to North Carolina.

The events that helped me become who I am are my parents divorcing, dating a drug addicted man, being bullied, and sexual harassment at work. For a long time, I have been an introverted person. I didn’t do much unless it was required. After those few events, I slowly started to do things on my own like watch anime, video games, and reading books.

Books are still a love that I have to this day. I can read almost anything. If it has fantastical and cool plots, I will read it. If it is nonfiction or young adult, I will read it. There are a few things in life that will keep me going and one of them is books.

I have moved about 20 times since I was born. I have moved four times when I was younger than nine. I have moved about 16 times since I was 9. 12 of those times were on my own. Moving so often has caused me to make many friendships. I have many great friends, one of which I am still talking to almost daily. She has been one of the few constants in my life. I have also met several people along the way.

The people that are in my life are very important to me. I am bad at speaking on the phone, though, so when I do not speak to people often, it is because I am bad at reaching out to people and because I enjoy solitude. So it may be that I am still thinking about you, but am not confident enough to talk about things. I keep friends as long as they want me. If they say it is time, I will go.

I do not know if this counts, but one of the other things that is important to me is food. I am vegan for both ethical and environmental reasons. Though one of my favorite things is a Sonic Blast. They are made with ice cream, syrup, and candy. I LOVE THEM.

Video games are also important to me, especially old-school JRPGs. I prefer games with interesting characters, challenges, and stories. I have a laptop right now, but I use it to watch YouTube and surf the web rather than play games because a laptop is not meant for gaming. I miss being able to game and would really love to play more games that are either JRPGs or classics. Classics can be anything from the NES to PSX.

I have been told that my personality could make me a good teacher and coach, but due to having Asperger Syndrome, I am not good at conflict and have never been able to really work in a professional job. So I have been selling things online as a hobby that also gives me an opportunity to learn about something new.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
(After about a 5 minute pause) I am 30 years old, I was born in Saint Paul, Minnesota. I grew up in Minneapolis, I took classes at Anoka Ramsey College from 2004-2010 and I also attended college at North Central University. I worked full time at a hotel and school part time until graduation, I worked as a DJ, and I worked at a department store too. I've always had busy schedules. I enjoy art and being creative, I enjoy partying with my friends, I enjoy music and designing and organizing things for other people and I enjoy experimenting with different tastes and foods. I am introverted and prefer to spend time alone but I also like people. I take things one step at a time because I have low energy. I'm very well read and I think having a conversation with me is very easy. I think I am open minded and easy to talk to and friendly. I think I am not generally known to be negative and I like to be helpful and I like to go out of my way to assist someone or to make someone's life easier. I do feel like I have been through a lot for a relatively short time and sometimes it feels like a really good idea to share those experiences. I've been to a lot of different cities and I like to see and do new things in different parts of the country but I'm not a huge fan of flying so I haven't done too much traveling. I've lived many different places and met a lot of interesting people and I have many different hobbies.

I then went on to describe my day today and my unique experience talking to George Washington in 1789.

And then: **I don't like washing dishes.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
My legal name is Kris. I am 37 years old. I am white, liberal, goth, interested in videogames and off the wall cult TV shows. I also like anime, professional wrestling, fantasy novels, and music of all varieties (my tastes tend to be older material, classic rock, and weird off-beat folk music). I am also an occasional dabbling in literary criticism, trying to pick out patterns and motives in writing. While I do have some formal training in computer science, psychology and cultural criticism, I am largely a self-taught creative person.

I have two websites that I run, Mephustavep for the Zine Library, and Hypernetica. For me, the act of making something is probably the most important part of creating. I think all creative people experience this; the need to be creative is a natural, human trait. A lot of my energy is spent trying to produce as much material as I can, while still allowing myself time to do the research necessary to make new sites and zines. For me, the high point of my day is when I have created something new, because I know that my work will last longer than me. It’s almost like creating a child, in a sense, and, since I am not a parent (at this point), at the very least I can create a child within the confines of my imagination.

I would probably describe myself as “neurotypical,” though, I can “feel” and experience what I call synesthesia. From what I understand, synesthesia is a rare condition, where the brain links senses together; in my case, I see colored letters when looking at words, and certain words have distinct smells. I also “see” sights when listening to music. While I think that synesthesia can be a useful and interesting trait, I would not call it the “best” or the “only” way to be, and certainly I would never “make a big deal” out of my condition. I have had a lot of problems with doctors, who say, “You don’t have it, that’s something you make up, or that you’ve read about and are trying to portray as something you have,” but, at the same time, if I listen to certain music, I tend to see moving, colorful shapes.

It’s also worth mentioning that I tend to come from a divorced background, and that I have been diagnosed with depression. I tend to avoid large crowds, and any “group” that has a designated “leader,” but I am quite good with one-on-one relationships. I am not sure if I tend to be a good influence on others, but I have done my best to try and encourage others to be creative.

While I don’t take any legal, mind altering substances (and never have), I have very high tolerance for caffeine. I can drink as much as a pot of coffee without feeling any effect, so I sometimes play games like Counterstrike and Halo online for hours on end.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I'm 45 years old, was born and raised in the San Francisco Bay area, California. Came to DC in 1988 and worked as a legislative assistant for a year with a Republican member of Congress from northwestern Illinois. Had to put that work to an end due to my experiences at the Conservative Political Action Conference (CPAC) there in DC. During that meeting I quickly discovered I wasn't a conservative after all but a liberal from the old tradition, not this new tacky cheap Coke Party. Did a variety of political work since then; on or supporting various campaigns or running my own contests. On the other hand, had to work odd jobs; mostly at the time one would call the service industry to make ends meet. I am also a reporter, columnist, talk show host, and public commentator. In addition, I was a registered Republican for a long while, but grew disgusted with the type of politics the Party offered by way of Ed Miller who was in control for a number of years from the mid-90s until recently. Finally I decided to start my own organization with which to communicate my views to the people. That was the inception of The DCArmy. Now we're off and running to help rebuild the conservative principles which I now believe have been abandoned by the new majority within our ranks.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: Whatever you can tell about yourself at this point. Talk about how you are single, maybe living on your own. At my age, I’ve lived through a major war and the Depression. I’ve outlived two wives, both of whom died from a form of leukemia. I was unable to donate bone marrow to save either of them. I have been through several difficult marriages. I was fortunate to have found financial independence in my mid-thirties, which allowed me to pursue my interests and live relatively comfortably, without the need to hold down an ordinary job. I have no children. No matter how hard I tried to have children, nothing happened and I have given up now. I really like to read and to write. I do research on subjects and read a lot of books. I like to go to clubs and see rock concerts. The most exciting thing I have done in recent years was to spend two weeks in Cape Town, South Africa. If I wanted to leave the country, I would like to go to Hong Kong. That is where my favorite flower, the Cymbidium orchid, comes from. It is hard to talk about myself. My whole life has been spent talking about the histories that I wrote, but those histories talk only about my research, my books and my articles.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I’m 33. I grew up in the town of Rutland in central Vermont. My dad was a homebuilder. My mom was a business owner. My dad died when I was 20 and my mom died when I was 25. I’m an only child. My dad’s death made me take a long hard look at my own mortality and made me question what was important in life. I never felt like I fit in anywhere and I always tried to conform to the norm. After he died, I stopped caring about what people thought and began to do whatever I wanted. I felt like I owed him that. It was the hardest thing I’ve ever done but it also changed my life forever. My mom’s death also made me question life but it had the opposite effect. It made me want to conform. I was terrified of not living up to my parents’ expectations and I feared that I wouldn’t be able to fulfill my potential. I was also anorexic and had an unhealthy relationship with food. I finally began to accept who I was after my mom died. It was a painful process but it also freed me. Both deaths were traumatic but both were also life changing. I can’t say that I would be who I am today without them.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
It is difficult to put into words who I am. I could tell you I am 44 years old and a philosopher. But those facts would be without context. My context is the culture that surrounds me in America. America is a melting pot of cultures and ideals. I am white but do not identify with the white culture. I think racism is stupid. I think it is the society that breeds racism because it has a history of slavery, segregation, brutality. But if a black man calls me a racist, then I would agree. However, if it is a white person, then it is an unfair accusation. How many black people believe that white people are racist? Probably many. How many black people are just that? Evil? Probably not that many. Maybe one white person out of a thousand believes that black people are evil. That makes 999 to 1 against. Out of those people, what percent would kill you? Probably 0.0001 percent at best. So my math is that if a black man calls me a racist, the odds of that black man actually being evil is about 1 in a thousand.

Even if a black man called me a racist, it wouldn’t faze me. My answer would be that I think discrimination is stupid. I have a friend who is a preacher. She has a congregation of black people. She wears her hair in locks because it is easier than an afro. She is still black, but I could care less.

For myself, if a black man called me a racist, I would say that I think blacks have some brilliant inventors, doctors, and engineers. So my response to any accusation of racism would be that racism is an abstract concept that doesn’t actually exist. It is just a social construct that can be used to promote self-interest. The only racism that exists is real racism. You know, like the holocaust. I do not have to be Jewish to be a Holocaust denier. I am not Jewish. So why is an accusation of racism any different? If I kill a Jew in the real holocaust, that is real racism. If you accuse me of racism, that is just a social construct to promote self-interest.

I would sum it up by saying that I am a natural born, average, peaceful white American that works for a living and doesn’t care about the color of my skin. Who cares? It is stupid to create an environment of racism. We should treat each other equally. Blacks should do their part to abolish racism because it affects them. It is not ok for them to use racism to promote their interests. My only concern is that they do not allow racism to hold them back.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I am 42 years old. I grew up in Elizabeth, New Jersey. I have a twin brother, my parents, and my two grandmothers who passed in 2013 and 2017. When I was a teenager, my grandmother asked me if I would be home before the day ended. At the time I had no job and my girlfriend of the time was looking for me. I was single and only spending time with my girlfriend and my friends. I told her I would be home, and I was gone for three days. She had passed away on that day and my mother tried to reach me but could not. She told me it would have made her proud if I had stayed in touch. I knew at that moment my relationship with my mother was damaged.

An honorable judge asked me if I would have done the same thing if my grandmother had been a stranger. I told him there is no easy answer. I never spoke to my grandmother after that and I feel truly sorry for that decision. I regret it so much and that will affect me forever. As I grew older, I became accustomed to doing drugs and started spending time with the wrong people. Before my grandmother passed away, I got caught with something that they put on my record. Because I was spending time with the wrong people, I got a 2nd degree felony of larceny from an elderly person. I had to do time on an old felony. It was my wake-up call. I have been drug-free for six months. I want to be someone in life and be treated like everyone else. I have never stolen anything since. I was recently promoted from a diamond drill helper to an RCE at General Shale Masonry and I’m making $21.40 an hour.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I am 33 years old and I was born and raised in the city of Cleveland, Ohio. My family has always been a very close knit group of individuals. As a result of the long hours my parents worked, my brother and I were raised by my grandmother for most of our lives. She was a very hardworking woman who owned her own business. She taught me the value of hard work and the importance of setting goals. My brother is 5 years older than me, so we didn’t really spend a lot of time together. Instead, we spent most of our childhood years playing football, basketball, and baseball with our friends in the neighborhood. Growing up, my neighborhood wasn’t the best, but I made sure I always stayed out of trouble. I would say that my experiences growing up in a poor neighborhood have made me a very disciplined and driven person.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I am 31 years old and I am writing from Tirana, Albania. The first language that I was speaking is Albanian and it is a South Slavic language very close to Greek. The second language I was learning in school and it was Italian, since I have a cousin living in Lecce. Then I was learning English, which is used also in Albania.

After I graduate from high school I went to live in Serbia, so now I was learning Cyrillic letters. I was educated in private, then I went to the university for journalism. And now, when I go to a new place, I have to study the language.

About events that made me who I am, there are a few factors.

First of all, my family. I am being raised by my father who works in hotel business and he has trained me for this profession. He used to take me to job sites, to the field, to learn what hotel is all about. Now, he moved out of the country, so in a new place, I have to learn new things.

Secondly, my childhood. I used to live in a suburb and as a kid I didn’t have any problems. Everything was fine and I lived the type of childhood that I wanted. And now, to a new place, I have to learn how to cope with a new place, a new childhood, of a new type.

But generally, I would say that people who can learn from others are people who will be successful. I see that here in Chicago you have business with US citizens, foreigners, etc. and you really have to learn.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: If it's a very large investment firm, and I think I can benefit from it. I'm here because I'm a concerned citizen. I would say that I'm passionate about my family and friends. As for myself, I'd say that I'm 49 and live in Colorado. I'm in a male dominated field and can take a lot of abuse, so am pretty resilient. I have a strong belief system, I'm not aggressive, I can speak up, but I can also listen and I get stuff done. I'm fun, a little goofy, happy, and I'm hard working. I enjoy my life, and I'm pretty happy.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I am a 35 year old, college educated, middle class woman. I grew up with two hard working parents who valued hard work and education. In middle school I decided I was going to become a doctor after seeing a TV movie about a teenager who had been impaled in the back with a football. The doctors had to remove some of the injured students lung to save her life. When I was in high school a college recruiter visited our school and invited us to our local university's Open House. A group of 15 or so of my friends and I went on the bus ride, not realizing what we were getting into. I saw a lot of stuff that weekend I had never seen before - kids from college walking around in groups at the dorms, old buildings, and tables full of smiling, uniformed, active college students talking about where they would be for spring break, what sports they played, etc. When I got back from that trip I decided that this was what I wanted to do with my life. I decided then that I wanted to become a doctor when I grow up. My first "job" was the cookie stand at my grade school. I worked there all four years I was in school - and we sold hot dogs one time at a fund raiser. The school was a block away from my house, as was the little farm I rode horses on. Both were about the same distance from my friends' houses. All my friends were pretty close to me, within a block to a couple of blocks.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
My 34 years spent on this blue planet haven't meant anything since I got my first dial up AOL account. I was such a nerd that I was a junior sysadmin with a successful small business when I was 12. No wait. I was still a fat little twerp.

I'm a gamer at heart, despite the fact that I'm 5'9", 175 and 41 years old. I've loved computers and games all my life. I do entrepreneurial crap and love working with my friends. I'm a dedicated martial artist. I was an insatiable thief as a youth. I've also been in the Senate of the US. Formerly far right and severely homeless. Now I'd consider myself a left leaning socialist in anarcho-capitalist territory. I'm a travel junkie and absolute enthusiast of random meetings with strangers.
