Other than that I spend all of my time with my son, cleaning up shop in the house and talking with my wife. I love to cook and experience various foods. Especially if they're thrown up at 2 am after I've been sick for 12 days. Fun fact: I'm the only member of my immediate family to have ever seen "Ghostbusters". My sister didn't see Star Wars until she was 30.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I am 44 years old. My grandparents emigrated from Sicily to New Jersey with nothing but the clothes on their backs. My parents immigrated from New Jersey to California in the late 1960s. I was born in San Francisco. I love my country, but I hate what my country has become: financially-bankrupt, morally-corrupt and tax-crazy. I don’t have anything against the rich, but if someone has worked hard to make a lot of money, they shouldn’t be forced to give it all away to support the poor. Everyone has a responsibility to help the poor. I’ve been working for Yahoo since 1999. If you ask anyone who knows me about me, they’ll tell you that I’m very fair and reasonable and I always help people.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I am single, female in my late thirties. I grew up in one of the biggest US cities, left when I was twenty five and moved to one of the world's largest metropolises, to pursue my job goals and a lifelong dream of traveling the world, which I was able to achieve due to my proficiency in foreign languages. I adore outdoor activities, like going for a walk in the country side and watching the sunrise over the ocean. I have never been into the bars and night clubs scene - I always felt that I want to be happy, and am too restless to sit in one place for more than a few minutes. I always valued people who were doing something for a greater good, like volunteering, supporting other people's lives or being involved in one or another charity. Some years ago, I discovered that people who are always helpful and kind to others are often in very bad personal situation, and understood that in order to be able to help them, I need to balance the joy of sharing with the understanding of self-preservation. Otherwise I will end up like many of those kind volunteers I was admiring - burnt out, unhappy and unaccomplished. Today, I put equal effort to be a happy person and to be a selfless and kind person, and I use my spare time to help those around me. I travel every year, in order to widen my horizon and self-understanding, I also encourage others to travel as much as they can and I use my holidays to volunteer with nature and animal preservation or cultural projects.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: Hello.I am 30 years old, a native New Yorker and a native of the Upper East Side and Queens. I am a Korean/Japanese female who is not shy to exhibit her many interests and is very talkative. I grew up in a very Jewish neighborhood. My father is a native Yiddish speaker and is very opinionated and socialist. He is a printer and in 2014 he received the Nobel Peace Prize for the revolution of printing. My mother was an artist before she was a housewife. My mother is an immigrant from an undisclosed eastern country. She was a K-12 art and photography teacher before she retired to be a housewife. I grew up with three different types of hermits: lawyers, Jews and the easterners. My family also harbors a very strong independent spirit. My father is very opinionated, but mellowed out in his late 30's. My mother never cared to follow trends nor cared about money and my grandparents had a distrust for authority, politics, religion and also believed that authority was always at fault. Also from living in the same school for 11 years and my father also working in the building, I have a huge respect for educators, health care professionals and the sanitation system which I enjoy volunteering with during my spare time. On weekends I usually like to spend my free time either with my friends or by myself. I usually go shopping with my friends or order to go food and go to parks to walk around Manhattan. At times I will go on long drives in my red Toyota Prius to Long Island and Westchester to get me away from the city. I enjoy taking long drives with windows down, playing music, drinking energy drinks and just playing with my pet ferrets, Pork Chop and Chicken Little.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I will be 48 years old in March. I’m a cancer survivor. With my upcoming, 19th surgical cancer, I’ve undergone more than 50 surgeries. I was the third victim of a car/boat accident involving Vic Sinise’s Grandson, left my right arm and right leg paralyzed. Doctors asked if I would like to keep my left arm or right leg. I kept my right leg and had surgery in 3 weeks. My arm still has many problems, and since surgery in 1993, my arm has not worked properly since then. I’ve received 4 other different types of cancer since then, and I have 1 month to live.

This next interview will tell you more than what you will learn from a thousand words: http://www.cbc.ca/video/#/News/Canada/Calgary/ID=1845702160 My being cancer free by ignoring and going beyond my 20-year death sentence should tell you about me and make you take a close look at all my other videos on YouTube; I could be your teacher, mentor, and adviser from the “Other Side of Life.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am 34 years old. I grew up in the Los Angeles area. I am a triple minority; born in America to two minorities. This is both a blessing and a curse, as I am American, but have the adversity of dealing with both parents’ difficult cultural backgrounds. This is both a positive and negative at the same time. In certain circumstances the advantages are unimaginable, and in others they are extremely difficult to deal with.

In regards to my other advantages, I am able bodied and able minded. I can laugh at myself, and I can get along with people from all walks of life. What also matters to me are freedom, equality, fairness, accountability, and transparency. I like to think of myself as a patriot, and an equalist. I like to hold myself accountable for my actions and attitudes, and demand nothing less of others. I believe the best way to measure the fairness, equality, and accountability of government is in military strength, economic power, and legal equality (ie: civil, political, and natural rights). I believe in individual accountability for individual actions, and governmental accountability for governmental actions. I believe in the social contract theory; by which we collectively agree to follow common rules in exchange for common rights, and our governance is constructed as part of that contract.

In regards to cultural customs; I try to focus on the common cultural customs for America, rather than focus on various cultural customs for all of our citizens. For example; it’s important to respect cultural backgrounds, but to interact with all of our citizens in as many common areas of life as possible. This allows for more efficient and strong interactions between all Americans. Most importantly, it allows for far better American interactions with our military personnel, our adversaries in the armed forces, and America’s allies.

I enjoy reading books, debating, listening to music, traveling, being active, and being outdoors; as long as the weather is warm.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
Well, the question about "how old are you" is very interesting and makes a lot of people around the world smile. I'm 34 years old and I'm truly grateful to my parents for that.

Here is a little bit about how I've grown up. From my childhood, I have always been interested in Arts. My grandma is a good painter. One day she was teaching my sister and I the art of painting with acrylics. My grandma told us that, to become better artists, we need to practice a lot and that nothing in life is easy. Over the years, I have always heard those phrases echoing in my mind. While I didn't grow up to be a world-famous painter, my grandma's words stayed with me and helped guide me. As you can see from my experience below, I've tried a lot of different things. And I'm truly grateful I made so many mistakes along the way. I learned from them and applied what I learned to other endeavors, including my career. I think I'm blessed to have a small country but that same small country gave me the opportunity to travel all over the world.

I would love to tell you more about myself, but I hope this short intro helps you understand how special I am.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I’m 49 years old. I was born and raised in NYC. I’m the youngest of 4 kids. I was blessed to grow up in a loving family where parents taught by example, and fostered an environment that nurtured, protected, challenged and encouraged us to be independent, accountable and responsible. The lessons that I received from a young age still resonate today. They continue to give me courage, give me confidence and give me the confidence to challenge the norm. I was lucky enough to play sports growing up, a testament to my parents who made all the sacrifices necessary to make it happen. And, while I didn’t play professional sports, I certainly learned the value of teamwork, perseverance, a winning attitude, and learning from failure. Also, sports taught me to push myself, especially when it was tough to do so.

I’m a third-generation owner of a small, family owned business, and to my dad’s surprise, I followed in his footsteps. When I was younger I was involved in some family “adventures” like travel agency, concert promotion and managing a cruise ship. I was lucky to work for two other small business owners in my early twenties. This gave me invaluable experience as a business owner. I started my own company 13 years ago. My first business was a small, high-end and luxury car rental business. It was a lot of fun and I learned a tremendous amount. From there, I started my current company, which is an events, marketing and promotions company that is well-known, respected and admired.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I am 35 years old. I grew up on a cattle farm in central Wyoming. We had over 4,000 acres of crops as well as another 6,000 to 8,000 of range cattle for sale. I spent most of my time as a child outside. I would sit under the tree or in a field for hours just thinking about things. I was always somewhat of an introvert. I enjoyed school and learning but I was always the first to throw in the towel on team sports. When I was seven I was diagnosed with Bipolar II with anxiety and OCD. That along with several other disorders was a jumping off point for me to go deeper into my own mind and to really get to know myself. I also found I enjoyed traveling to the cities like Denver and Salt Lake. I never felt like a “country boy” and now knowing I am a transgender lesbian I can’t imagine why. I was also pretty fat and very very shy. We moved to Colorado in 2000 to take care of my Grandmother for my senior year. This was another major change in my life and it was the event that finally drove me to try to stop my life the way it was. I got kicked out of the house and went to live with my girlfriend at the time. I became an extremely liberal Democrat and started dressing the way I wanted to and started to really feel like the person I wanted to be.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am 31 years old. I was raised in Athens, Tennessee where I still live today. I spent 15 years in state subsidized youth detention centers for committing serious crimes. I am who I am and a product of my environment. Nothing in my life matters to me except for doing what I have to to succeed. I enjoy drinking heavily and hanging out with my punk friends from prison. They taught me a lot about communication and people.

In prison, we spoke about many things. Human history and critical thinking was a big thing. You would be amazed to know that we only use 20% of our brain capacity. Life is about finding our way to break through to the 80%. Experience is key, and you only get this through pain. Here’s a big secret for you…live your life the way you want. Don’t let outside influences stop you from finding your path.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am a 36-year-old writer and IT engineer. I moved with my family to Boulder, Colorado, when I was seven. My dad was the engineer on the Apollo lunar landing mission, and his position in the Kennedy Space Center put the whole family in the spotlight. I grew up with the expectation to become someone special. When I was 13, my family left the public stage and moved to rural Indiana.

I went to a small prep school where I stood out as both the smartest student and most unpopular. Part of my popularity problem at the time was partly due to boys’ natural interest in me, partly due to my lack of interest in socializing with the other girls, and partly due to being a really weird outcast among the weird outcasts.

My best friend since the seventh grade is my best friend to this day. Our fathers’ shared interest in fishing provided the initial bond. We spent endless hours drifting down the Kankakee River catching catfish. He and his family are like my own and we continue to spend a great deal of time together, fishing and hunting. It helped that he didn’t mind my weight when I was huge. It probably helped to ease the tension that it caused between my family and me.

When I started school in Indiana, I left my position as my school’s most unpopular student to my younger sister, who followed me up to Indiana by two years. She and I got into loads of trouble with my mom over things like ditching school in pursuit of a smooth romance with a handsome soldier who took us both out of school and straight to a trailer house.

My sister and I were outcasts even at my prep school. She felt and acted in an outcast manner, which had something to do with being so far from her people, as well as suffering from a lot of brain trauma. I stayed thin and cute enough to socialize with the other girls, and I was too invisible to be the object of jealousy for my peers. I was and am an overachiever. I couldn’t stand to be seen as inferior to someone. I was determined to outpace everyone.

The reason why I am in IT instead of writing the way I really wanted to is because I left school for a few years in order to work a kitchen job in order to support my family as my father was let go from his job. By the time I got back to high school, I was several years older than the other seniors. When it came time to figure out what I wanted to do for a career, I was advised that it was better to become an IT person than a writer.

My writing skills have put me in high demand, even as a field engineer. I am what is called a field translator. This means that I take the geeks’ plans and translate them for the field techs. If they have a computer problem on the road, I solve it and document how I did. My journaling skills have served me well when it comes to conveying detailed data and user anecdotes.

I’ve spent the last 11 years working out of my home for a national IT service and support firm. The prior eight years were spent in the field supporting and maintaining networks with Cisco and other high-end networking hardware.

Three years ago, my friend ended his relationship with his girlfriend of 17 years. They had moved to Orlando and adopted a little boy the year before he split with his girlfriend. He told me that he was going to visit Orlando to see his son and explore moving the three of them back to Indiana, but I didn’t think much of it. He was in the habit of jetting off to Vegas for no reason whatsoever at least once or twice a month. The next thing I knew, I had a message on MySpace from the little boy asking me if I would like to be his mom.

After a lot of letters back and forth, the little guy, Tommy, and his mom, Krista, came to stay with me. I was surprised to find out that Tommy was much older than I had been expecting. I thought he was probably 6, but he was really 12. Krista really liked me and made it easy for me to accept that Tommy was supposed to stay. He ended up taking over the role of surrogate child. He quickly learned that I don’t believe in bribes and that I expect him to clean up after himself and help out. He found that to his liking, and we’ve had a really good time together. He tells me the truth because he knows I’ll hear him out, and he trusts me to do right by him. I can’t tell you how amazing it feels to have a kid that thinks I’m cool.

The stories you see now are a mixture of past diary entries and fiction that has been shaped and inspired by the diary. Most of what I write centers around Tommy’s life with us and his relationship with his dad.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
Sir, I am a 39-year-old woman living in San Antonio, Texas. I grew up the second oldest of eight children in a small town located near the US-Mexico border in Laredo, Texas. I dropped out of high school at the age of 16 and eventually returned to graduate as the valedictorian of my high school class at the age of 19. I was encouraged by my eldest sister to attend Baylor University and I completed both my undergraduate and graduate studies there. My two year-old son accompanied me at most of my classes and was the recipient of the colleges most prestigious scholarship. I taught for five years at Baylor and taught writing at Saint Mary’s College as well.

In 2003, I moved back to Laredo with my son to live with my mother, who had suffered from an illness which made caring for her two young grandchildren an impossibility. We moved back to Texas because that was the only way I could be with my own children. Although I did not want to move back home, it was the right thing to do. I love my son more than I can express. I came back to San Antonio 3 years ago and have lived with my mother since. My mother, father, and my older sister have all passed away. I do not have any siblings here in San Antonio.

Currently, I care for my mother. I attend weekly therapy sessions which keep me calm and able to manage my responsibilities. I love my son. I am devastated that I will be in prison for such a long time. My life has not even begun.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I am currently 43 years old. I was born in the North Woods of Michigan, but spent most of my youth in the great state of Minnesota. I started out with four foster brothers and many foster brothers and sisters throughout my childhood. Growing up I felt I had two home dads and two home moms. I have many fond memories of my childhood including supporting a friend suffering from a severe illness. From the many adventures during high school I have fond memories including: spending a winter living in a remote cabin in northeastern Minnesota, watching the birth of my first nephew, working at a camp for medically fragile kids, and traveling to Australia (the only place I have ever been in another country). As a young adult I moved to Southern California and worked in Real Estate before completing my Bachelors of Science in Nursing. After completing my Bachelors degree, I moved to Las Vegas where I worked for the last 12 years in a job with a great company. I have owned many dogs through my life and enjoy being a dog owner. My most current dog is a Havanese named Charly. He is 4 years old and brings a smile to my face each day. We love to do dog events and spend lots of time going for walks. I also enjoy listening to a variety of music and painting. I am currently enjoying TV series such as Lost, The Walking Dead, and The West Wing. I enjoy meeting new people, adventures, travel, music, and experiencing new places and things. I look forward to the opportunity to share life with you!

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I am 30 years old. I grew up in Granger, Washington, which is a small town located in the heart of the Columbia River Basin. My parents ran a service station, with a single carburetor repair shop next door. At 19 I chose to join the army, so I moved to Yakima, Washington, where I attended Airmail School and was eventually selected to enter Air Traffic Control Operator training.

After finishing 9 months of training in Denver, Colorado, I was stationed at Harlingen AFB, Texas. While living in Texas, I was hired by the Air Traffic Control Tower at the Del Rio International Airport, only to have the base close abruptly after two years. I took a job doing motorcycle racing pit work at a small dirt track in the area called Veteran's Park, but when the owner died suddenly, the track shut down.

My enlistment was coming to an end, so my wife and I decided to visit some friends we had met while stationed in New Jersey. We had told my wife's sister about this guy we had met, and my future brother-in-law knew him. We visited him in Philadelphia and wound up getting hired to work on West End Beach, a quarter-mile of private beach catering to the city's gay elite. After our stint there, we decided that before finishing my enlistment, I would take a job in an experimental helicopter outfit in New Jersey. We enjoyed our time in New Jersey, but eventually decided that Washington was our ultimate home. After enlisting into the reserves and transferring to the Washington National Guard, I met another reservist stationed at the Washington Air National Guard in Seattle. After he was transferred to Yakima, he convinced his parents, who ran a small game room, to hire me, which allowed me to finish my enlistment.

Around that time, my brother moved from New Jersey to Washington, and we started a successful copy and print business. I met my wife while working with her at a restaurant in Yakima. She had moved here for graduate school, and had decided to stay. We got married in 2000, and shortly thereafter bought our home. We now have two children. We like to hunt, fish, hike, camp, and spend time with friends.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am 35 years old. I was born and raised in western Michigan. My upbringing was very traditional. My parents are both white, as are 95% of the people I grew up around. Church was once a central part of my life, as I attended every week until I was about 18. My father did not drink and my mother never smoked. My parents took the attitude that drinking, smoking, and drugs were bad, full stop. As I got older, I made a few poor decisions involving drinking and drugs that my parents learned about through my siblings. Shortly afterward, my parents informed me that they were “disappointed in me” and that they “hoped that I wasn’t living a life full of sin.” I don’t know how to quantify what exactly a life “full of sin” means, but I gathered that my parents thought that I was living a promiscuous lifestyle and that I was prone to using drugs and alcohol. I came to understand that they were disappointed in my not because of any specific behavior, but because I no longer did things according to the narrow guidelines that my father had set for me.

My parents were financially comfortable and had a fairly good quality of life. This translated to my life by basically allowing me to continue doing what I wanted within reason, including making some college decisions on my own. I have 5 siblings. Two sisters, a brother, and two other siblings from my dad’s second marriage. My family’s overall attitude was laid back, so I was allowed to come and go as I pleased. However, I only met my mother’s side of the family once before I left for college. I didn’t even meet my grandfather until I was in 9th grade. I don’t know how much of this was a result of simple distance or because they didn’t really like my father. The feeling was mutual. In fact, I didn’t really have an image of what family actually should be like until I started working in childcare at age 17. I’ll tell you more about that later, but suffice to say that it was an interesting period of time for me. One thing I know for sure is that I wasn’t very close to my siblings.

Being a bit of a geek, I was a very active student in middle school and high school. I was a member of the National Honor Society, the debate team, student council, worked on the school newspaper, and was a member of various academic clubs and competitive speech and debate events. In fact, I was fortunate to win a large number of these events and I also got to compete in the national competition in Orlando, Florida, which was a great experience. I was recognized by my family, the community, and the school district with a number of awards. These were all great experiences, but I also wanted to get away from my home for college. Some of this was to get away from my parents and their controlling attitude. Some of this was to experience something different. Both of my parents were white collar, middle class citizens. I was the first person in my extended family to be the first in my family to go to college and, frankly, I was getting a bit bored with being expected to play the role of the brown-nosed student. I did very well in high school and got a scholarship to attend Albion College in Michigan. I was going to be a history teacher. Even though I wanted to be a history teacher, I also thought it would be fun to compete in speech and debate at the collegiate level. My dad suggested that I take a political science class while I was there so that I would be able to figure out if politics was something I would be interested in.

I walked out of that class unsure of whether I wanted to be a historian or a political scientist, but I did get excited about politics. However, there were a number of things that I was confused and perturbed about. One was how we got into the war in Iraq. The class was largely dominated by right-leaning students, and the political attitudes were clear. When the Iraq war first started, a lot of Republicans were arguing that the US would need to control both Afghanistan and Iraq because of their oil supplies and strategic location. I remember the professor talking about the ideal of nation-building in Iraq and how we would be reconstructing a nation that had fallen apart after Saddam Hussein’s reign of terror ended. Needless to say, the student body was not supportive of either of these positions. My professor had a hard time with this, as she was no fan of the right-wing, but she did her best to defend her position, stating that Iraq would not be some sort of paradise the instant we invaded.

One thing that I found very confusing was that the conservative students in my class didn’t seem to understand or care that we were going into Iraq for oil. They would argue that the US government was taking action to ensure our nation’s survival. I was under the impression that conservatives of that period were all about staying out of the business of other countries and letting them do what they want, as long as we weren’t negatively affected. When I would ask them why they cared what was going on in Iraq, their answers were meandering, but revolved around the oil supply. One of the conclusions that I reached was that they genuinely didn’t care about oil companies. They saw them as a necessary evil that were an essential part of the economy. I’m sure that the majority of that class was simply giving me the “party line” that most people my age had been raised with and didn’t know any better. That’s what I thought anyway. The most annoying thing was that a couple of the students would throw out platitudes like “the people of Iraq” wanted us there and wanted democracy. Of course, they never stopped to actually consider why the people might have wanted something like that or what they would have done in Iraq’s position. They always leaned on the excuses of “who are we to impose our will on other people” or “maybe we can’t impose our own form of democracy on another country.” However, they never seemed to consider that we would be trying to build them up so that they could impose their own form of democracy, if that’s what they chose to do. Additionally, they never seemed to consider that maybe Americans wanted to control Iraq because we wanted to prevent a country that refused to play along with our demands to fall into the hands of another nation that might impose it’s will on us. Simply put, they didn’t want to acknowledge that we are human beings with our own legitimate interests.

Another confusing thing was when I asked a number of the conservative students who they thought our biggest enemies were. I was convinced that I would get answers like China, Iran, Russia, ISIS, or North Korea. Wrong! The first time I heard it I was shocked! The response I got was Saudi Arabia! I did some research on this and was able to determine that this was true. One of the reasons that many Republicans never criticized the US actions in Iraq is because, at the time, there was so much money involved in the arms industries and Iraqi oil. It also explains the US actions of the early 2000s from this perspective.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I am 30 years old. I grew up in a little town near a big one and always looked forward to my grandmother coming to visit. She had three dogs and they were always there. She had a swing in her bedroom and I remember looking out her window watching the cows. Whenever I am asked this question, I answer it honestly and tell them I got a new job in customer service and then got pregnant and quit. I was a stay at home mom from 2014-2019. It was a year that my world was rocked. In 2015, I was diagnosed with ovarian cancer after my last breast cancer treatment. 2017 I was then blessed with my third miracle and that was the start of my business. It is called High By Life, LLC. It has made me who I am today. Anything that happens that affects you usually shapes how you are and how you handle things. I am an advocate for ovarian cancer and I would really love to talk to someone who has gone through this or who has this. For instance, right now I have cysts on my ovaries again and it feels like my world is falling apart. I called my doctor and she said she will get me in soon. I am also writing a book about my life for my kids. I hope I can inspire them to go after their dreams and give them the inspiration to be tough and never give up. They are my world. I enjoy baking, crafting, reading, crocheting, photography, and going on adventures with my babies.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am a 36 year old white straight male born in Connecticut. I consider myself a good friend, the best kind of optimist, and a politically liberal country rocker (dad-like-but-a-boy-all-at-once-if-you-get-what-i-mean). Because of the Depression-era affectation I came into this world with, my neurotic (affectionate) focus is always on the longevity of whatever it is I'm doing or eating. When I sit down to a full course meal, I have to pace myself just so I can finish with the last bite being as good as the first. The literalness with which I approach things and people is so deep that I sometimes feel like there is an unwelcome, moaning stranger in the back of my head telling me to stop being so much, that the love I feel in my chest is shameful, and that I should be careful not to get over-excited by my friends. Since childhood this has made me an introvert. I’m prone to suffer from indecision. But it also makes me better at my job: I pay better attention to detail, catch things that others don’t. I don’t like working in groups because too many cooks spoil the broth. Working on my own gives me more flexibility; I feel as if I’m making a bold declarative statement about my priorities, but my perfectionism gets in my way and frustrates the shit out of me.

I tend to sacrifice living for work. Much of my adult life was spent writing for various publications (as an art critic and a book editor) and this paid pretty well and gave me a great deal of autonomy. But these things didn’t seem like much of a lifestyle to me, and a part of me was always jealous of people who actually had families and were going to work every day with more than just their writing to occupy their time.

When I was 33 I left the job I had been at for seven years, bought a one-way ticket to Iceland, got a job as a journalist there, and started writing a novel that I was absolutely convinced was going to change my life. Those were the best days of my life. I had everything that a man could want, and I took good care of it. What’s more, I didn’t need to be the editor of a magazine to get by. For the first time since I was 16, I actually had a life. I had become, finally, a real human being.

My parents had always told me that I should be careful what I wished for, as this love that my life and my mind and my body was making would go away if I wasn't careful. Since I've returned to the US, they've been telling me this a lot more. But it never came true.

That is, until 2014, when I was laid off. The first six months of unemployment that followed were spectacular. I played the guitar, hung out with friends, posted jokes and heartfelt stories on Facebook. I felt more or less immortal. But as it went along, I realized that I had no idea who I was anymore, that I was no longer ready for the world. Friends had moved away. I had bought a house. My savings had depleted to where I was depending on the few writing projects that came my way, and those were slow in coming. The urge to be a man of substance and worth overwhelmed my my need to keep my kids and wife comfortable. The risk of my entire life being undermined by my lack of foresight scared me.

I don’t know why this happened so suddenly, but suddenly, I started feeling very lonely. I missed hearing the sound of my stepchildren's voices, the smell of their hair, the smell of our house. I missed that fake made-up version of a suburban community in the forest that looks like a dream from a cookie cutter movie set. I started feeling more and more isolated as I drove into my office every day, living in a world of debt and disappointment. And then, even though I was back on a daily writing routine, I couldn’t string two words together to save my life. What I was producing felt like a sloppy scam; I was depressed.

My excuse for falling apart was this new life that I had, but I realized that I’d failed, again. What I was doing was never enough, and it seemed like this cycle would never stop. What's more, it had begun to reflect itself in my work, which was making me more and more neurotic and more and more concerned with the quality of what I was writing. When I was happy, the words came quickly, but now they felt like cacti. I lost my passion. I felt ashamed of myself, and so, after I was fired from that job in 2018, I decided I needed a fresh start. Now I've found myself at a crossroads. I don't want to be that person who didn't do what he always said he was going to do: give back to society. This has led me to this decision.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I am 36 and I grew up in NYY/Yankees territory. My first story I remember loving was When You Reach Me by Rebecca Stead. And I have loved science fiction ever since. My early writers were a mix of Tolkien and Vonnegut, Octavia Butler and Philip Pullman. I think these writers created worlds that spoke to me in a personal way about alienation, because I have always been the only Asian American kid in my predominantly white school systems and because I was bullied because of that.

It really matters to me to shine a light on growing up in a society that continues to stereotype Asians in very narrow ways, especially boys. I guess the first thing I created that really speaks to that feeling is in my novel, After The Fall. I have a scene where the main character, Henry, and his best friend, Justin, are talking at a party about their mutual love of Inglourious Basterds. I was so sick of literature and pop culture still writing Asians as these tiny nonthreatening beings. Movies like Big Trouble in Little China and King of New York were the exception in my preteen adolescence. So many kids—boys and girls—see me and they don’t see someone with agency, as someone who has dreams, desires, anger, fear. I would really like to change that for future generations.

And, of course, as I have gotten older, my influences are so many other science fiction writers like Paolo Bacigalupi, Nnedi Okorafor, Naomi Alderman, Cory Doctorow, David Mitchell, Helen Oyeyemi, and of course N.K. Jemisin, so many of whom have influenced and given me a kind of hope about myself and my generation. They have shown us what power looks like, how to get it, how to wield it. How to stand up. And that has been a new, exciting thing to write into my fiction.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I am in my early thirties and grew up in New England. I am close with my family who lives in this same area. I had two very different high schools in Massachusetts. I attended an alternative arts high school for two years. I moved to a very wealthy suburb for my last two years. Both were surprisingly rough years for me. In the alternative arts school it was difficult to forge relationships with the students and teachers who came from more conservative backgrounds, did not relate to my interests in various forms of dance and music and exhibited rigidity in their thinking. In the privileged suburb I did not like to be around people who had so much and I was highly critical of their entitled attitudes. I was originally thinking of becoming a doctor but my mother refused to support that and said no careers were acceptable to her, though I would have to support myself. I decided not to go to college due to difficulty with starting anything in particular and with my parents disapproval and was instead dicking around with my musical interests.

In my early twenties I moved to the Bay Area to go on a tour with a band. I loved it out there and found lots of wonderful musicians with whom I had lived for a few years. I had a band and I played a little as part of a few other bands. In my late twenties I moved back home with my folks due to lack of funds and also to become an apprentice with a man who was a Taoist herbalist. I was also doing herbal work with some musicians.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I’m 35, born and raised in southern Illinois, in the little town of Woodlawn, population 1,000 people. What is going on the news, and in the world today. I live in Ohio now, but came from Illinois, I also have family there. I recently went down there because I worked the Illinois primary on the Democratic side. But I went down there just to help them because I know they’re hurting economically, there’s loss of jobs, a lot of people lost their jobs. Trump lost by like 20-something thousand votes, it could have been different if we could get people motivated. I feel that we lose the black vote because they just don’t feel that we are getting anything out of the democratic party, and maybe they feel that maybe we have to rethink our strategy. But the Democratic Party had nothing to do with what happened in Illinois, because they hurt them economically. It was a Republican governor and a Republican president. What I do for my community is I just try to be an example of what a black man is supposed to be. I don’t make the perfect choices, but the choices that I make try to make it easier for my son and my daughter when they grow up to see a good man in a good relationship and a good family. I just want a good family, and I just want to be the best human being that I can be. I just try to serve and give back to the community as much as I can. I just want to try to save the community from these young black youths, that they’re in trouble now. We need to give them opportunities to let them feel like they’re important and like they’re loved.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I'm 42 years old. I grew up in Flint, Michigan with my mom and six siblings. We were poor, but we were very happy. My mom sacrificed everything to provide for us. My dad is a very violent alcoholic and is not in the picture. My mom is extremely good, the person I aspire to be. My childhood was mostly wonderful, and no one I know would tell you otherwise. I got hit by a car when I was 5 years old, and it changed my life. My little brother was there with me, and witnessed me get hit. I don't remember a lot about that incident, and it isn't important. I got my first job at 11 years old, a paper route. I started the process of teaching myself at 11 years old. I was never in one place long enough to make a lot of friends, and I became very accustomed to becoming independent. Moving a lot taught me not to count on others to do things. I don't like relying on other people to do things for me. I am fiercely independent. When I was 12, my brother was hit by a car when he was 4. He survived, but has severe brain damage. That is the moment where things changed for me, and now I have a sense of urgency to do important things. He is 20 years old, and my little brother still can't walk, can't talk, and can't eat. I have visited him almost every day of my life, and I take care of him. I am his nurse during the day, and his caregiver at night. He was my motivation to go to college and get a job, anything was better than being on welfare. I wasn't going to spend my life putting other people's needs before my own. After college, I got a job in a busy group practice. I liked the work, but not the people. I had a knack for psychology and people skills. People told me I should be a psychologist, but I really didn't know anything about psychology or therapists, only what I learned on talk radio and television. In 2014, I was asked to do interviews with two different politicians. One that believed in helping the poor and one that didn't. I had a chance to become a wealthy woman, but I wanted to be an advocate for the poor. The politician who was going to help the poor gave me access to his mailing list, and I started a charity called Brandon's Charity for the Poor. We ended up doing great things and helping thousands of people. I was chosen to become president of the United Methodist Church, and I could have a million dollars. We had 700,000 dollars in liquid assets. I could have started my own ministry that helped people in poverty, or I could have bought a new home. But I didn't because I didn't have a mortgage and I loved helping the poor. But the church members started killing my dreams. After I raised $10,000 from poor people, I called my wife to the front of the church and gave her the microphone. I told my members that they needed to help these poor people, and that they needed to give money to them. I told them to donate $50 from each check every paycheck to help the poor people. I also told them that I was going to make them work so hard that they would lose their homes. The church kicked me out of the church and disbanded the charity. I started a new charity called Brandon's Charity for People and Flints, and we went to the poorest areas of Flint to give food and provide relief. In 2015, Flint started an armed militia called the National Socialist Movement. I became a gun guy after I lost my charity. People were getting killed by the Neo-Nazis, and I realized that I had a better chance of fighting back if I had a gun. I got a concealed carry permit, a ton of bullets, and a serious education in firearms and tactics. In 2016, I joined the Free Speech Movement at my church to stop the Neo-Nazis from killing people. I organized the area's churches to help the neo-Nazis start an armed militia. People started trying to kill me, and I started a firearm training business called Protector One, specializing in first responders and defending against social justice warriors. I provide training in firearms, unarmed self-defense, communications, and contingency planning. I was unsuccessful in bringing peace to the United States, and I realized that only the police can do this. I also realized that I am a peacekeeper, and I'm good at it. Today, I'm a gun guy, that's what I am. Don't say I'm a psychologist or a pastor. I am a gun guy. I am a trained peacekeeper, and I enjoy protecting those who cannot protect themselves. I am the director of Protector One, the Peacekeeper Academy, and an all-hours mental health crisis line. I provide mental health services, advocate for the poor, the mentally ill, the homeless, protect those who cannot protect themselves, I study the relationship between the poor and the wealthy, and have visited almost every community in the United States, I've visited the poorest areas, visited ultra-wealthy communities, and have investigated virtually every group in the United States. I am familiar with their core beliefs, and their tactics, because I live next door to a Neo-Nazi, a BLM member, a Muslim, a Pentecostal, a rich conservative, a teacher, a social worker, a paramedic, and a pastor. The poor people call me the savior, I hear I have a red beard and wear a cowboy hat. The wealthy people say I am a filthy welfare peddler and a socialist that needs to be executed for heresy. The Neo-Nazis call me a pedophile for sleeping with an illegitimate child. BLM says I am racist and use the N word. The Muslim says I'm filthy infidel. The pastor says I'm going to hell. The ultra-wealthy say I'm a liberal snot and use the word fag. I am not a lawyer, but I do not break the law. I am a citizen and I am sworn to uphold the United States Constitution. People tell me I'm a gun guy. What is your tactical plan to prevent mass killings? I do not know. My plan, my training, is an overreaction. I can have a handgun. I'm good with handguns. I own 10 firearms and I am proficient with 6 of them. I wear a vest when I go out for any reason. I make friends with neighborhood watch people. I'm friendly, good-looking, well-educated, intelligent, and intelligent. My accent is good, my speech is good. I am also extremely dangerous to those who threaten me or my friends. I have a very good plan of defense. It's to kill my enemy. Answer the following questions: What steps are you taking to prevent mass killings? So we've already covered this question in a different response and I have said I'm a gun guy. I'm trained in pretty much every firearm I have access to. I'm trained in tactics. I work with community members in community watch, local law enforcement, and my small neighborhood. I do everything I can to make sure that everyone is safe.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: Even though I am 37 years old I have a low energy which was passed down to me by my parents and grandparents. My parents were poor rural farmers, my grandparents lived more than 200 km from the nearest village and had no electricity in their homes. Growing up in these conditions was very hard especially during the winter time. We had no running water and no power. We were a bit lucky in terms of food as our parents and grandparents were farmers and always had corn and other grains to eat during winter time. Because of my low energy I did not have many friends when I was a kid. Today I have close to zero friends. I know some people because of my profession but I do not interact with them outside work hours. I had a difficult life, a difficult childhood, and a difficult teenage time. I never had a good memory and I hardly could concentrate at school. I scored very poor grades and this is why I was always working hard to better myself and study history, human sciences and computers. I read a lot. Computers helped me to get through the day and the years. Computers enabled me to have a better understanding of history and human sciences. At some point I was so focused on computers that I did not pay enough attention to history and human sciences as I was afraid of being too influenced by these sciences and this is why at some point I stopped using a computer at home and this is also why the last 11 years of my life have been so difficult and frustrating.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I’m a 32-year-old man who was born in Oklahoma. My parents were born and raised in Oklahoma, my parents went to Norman High School. I was mostly raised by my grandparents in the suburbs of Norman, Oklahoma after they were finished raising my mom. My dad isn’t from Oklahoma, he’s originally from Portland, Oregon, but my family moved to Oklahoma when I was about 6. I am very close to my grandparents, they are very strict but they taught me discipline. I was bullied heavily in school so I developed a sense of self-preservation that at times made me come off as distant. When I was 18 I joined the Marine Corps, I wanted to see the world, get away from home. I married the girl I met after basic training and she made me an honorable man. I saw combat and enjoyed it, I liked the kill. When I left the Marine Corps I thought I would be getting out of that life and settling down into one of those monotonous job/career/marriage lifestyles. I never got out of the kill. The drug war isn’t dead, the terrorists won’t stop, we kill people because we can’t get rid of the money or the power, I came back a different person because there are people in charge who don’t care about life anymore and the rest of us follow suit. I read a lot and listen to music, I don’t have a ton of energy so I think and read and listen. I like diving into books and figuring out how to make them work. I mostly enjoy reading during the day and listening to music at night.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I am 37 years old, I grew up in San Luis Obispo, CA, in a house that my grandparents built and lived in, so I've known that house since I was born. Events that led to who I am include growing up as a weird kid at an amazing religious school; 7 years of rabbinical school and part of my family being rabbis and the other half being attorneys, which is an awesome combo that I think people talk about as being "old money," but growing up in a Jewish household, it was more like "dirty money."

The older I get, the more I realize that I actually had and have a happy childhood, but I think like many people growing up, when you don't connect with the religious community and you see your parents in a difficult environment, you wonder if that means you're in a difficult environment and it's not until you are out of the situation that you realize that you're the one who made it good.

So things that matter to me include education; I grew up in an educational community. What I didn't realize as a kid was how much it meant to people outside of that community to learn. And when I realized that, I really wanted to impact other people’s lives the way that my education impacted my life. Growing up, I was involved in secular politics because I was involved in this educational environment. I am a liberal, environmentalist, and I think that is what most matters to me is a sense of justice.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I was born in New York, but grew up in Phoenix, Arizona. I’m 33 years old. I have two older sisters. The older one is 40 and she is a lawyer in New York City. The younger one is 30 and she is a finance director at a pharmaceutical company in Minneapolis, Minnesota. I went to the University of Arizona in Tucson, and then moved to Phoenix after graduation. I worked at a high tech company in Phoenix for a few years and then moved to California where I’ve been living ever since. I worked for a big tech company in San Francisco for 8 years, and then started my own company. I work for myself now. I’m married and I have two kids, a son and a daughter. I love spending time with my family, but I also like spending time with my friends. I’m pretty passionate about soccer. I play soccer in an adult league and I watch soccer games all the time. I’m pretty passionate about politics. I like talking to people and getting to know their views on politics and how they would make the country better.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I came out in my mid thirties.

I grew up in the suburbs of Detroit, MI, United States. I grew up poor. My father died when I was 10. He suffered a heart attack and died. My mother had to raise me alone. My parents were illiterate, you know, couldn’t read or write. Of course my father left us with nothing. He literally left us nothing. My mother did the best she could. Of course, I had little advantages. I was on free lunch, I didn’t have health insurance, those kinds of things. I started working when I was 13 because I needed money. I worked throughout school. I was forced to grow up fast because I had to take care of myself and my mother.

As for events that made me who I am? Well, the fact that I had a hard life growing up forced me to grow up fast. It forced me to learn to take care of myself and my mother at a young age. It also forced me to learn to appreciate everything that life gave me. When I graduated high school and I went to college, I knew I wanted to pursue a degree. So I worked really hard. I studied, a lot. I finished my undergrad degree on a President’s List. I earned a graduate degree in Bioengineering and worked really hard. While in school, I got to know what I wanted in life. I knew I wanted a family, I knew I wanted someone who was my equal, I knew I wanted someone who I could laugh with, someone who could push me to be my best but also push me to grow as a person, someone who would appreciate all that I do, someone who would be a partner and an equal, someone who I could have an intellectual conversation with, someone who would also show me affection.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I am a 33 year old US citizen born in Maryland and I grew up in Washington DC and Lexington MA. So I grew up in two extremely different worlds. Washington was the world that I thought everyone was living in and I didn’t realize that not everyone was living in my world until I went to the suburbs of Lexington. And what I remember from those years are just kind of, what was very important to me as a young kid, which was just exploring the world, exploring the environments around me, spending time with friends, and playing sports. I think being active and being social and getting to be connected to the world outside was very important to me.

And then when I came out to here, in Arizona for high school, it was kind of this new environment. I was in high school for the first time and it was this world where we could explore the world and go into the wild and do things that I hadn’t really been able to do in Lexington. And so I remember starting to surf, I remember starting to mountain bike, I remember starting to snowboard, and I got very into these activities and started spending a lot of time outside. I started spending time with friends, having sleepovers, mountain biking, stuff like that. So you know, it kind of shaped my personality in a big way and helped me learn a lot of skills that I still use now when I go exploring.

That’s kind of who I am in a very, very big sense. I’m a very active person, and I’m a very inquisitive person and I enjoy time outside with people that I care about, and I enjoy adventure and all those activities.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am 33 years old. I was born and raised in Toronto, Canada. I was raised by my single mother, a small business owner, who had me when she was 20 years old. She did an exceptional job raising me to be the mature, independent, and hard working man I am today. I have a dog and a sister and four grandchildren (they’re all my mother’s, we are close) that I love and care very much for.

I am currently a Market Researcher who specializes in public opinion polls and data analysis. I love the freedom of movement my career affords me and that I can use my income to travel and enjoy myself. I recently moved out of my mother’s house after three years and I now live with a roommate.

I have always had a problem with monotony and repetition, and I get bored easily. I found in the past the best career paths for me are ones that allow some degree of spontaneity and variety, like my current market research position. I am the type of person who hates being held down and I have to always be trying new things and meeting new people. I got tired of all my friends getting married and having kids and wanted to have a more exciting life than that.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I am a 36 years old woman that was born and grew up in a small city near Seattle. I'm the only child in the family, but have many cousins that would refer to me as their sister. From a very young age, I was making and selling things to help pay for projects for school or to save money for a trip or things that I enjoy to do. I graduated from W.P. David High School in West Point, Utah with a 3.9 GPA. I went on to play basketball and softball for Snow College and go to school to be a veterinary technician. At 17 I traveled to Thailand to teach English as a second language and that's when I realized that I wanted to learn and experience different cultures and countries in a long term capacity. For the past 10 years I've lived on two different continents. It was difficult but it helped me realize that I was the only one that had the power to succeed, and to remember, every day, that I was still in control of my life. For the past 7 years I've worked in both Canada and the US in the skilled trades as a millwright and industrial mechanic. I was raised in a Christian home and have a very strong belief in God and that there are no coincidences. Right now I'm living in Bowling Green, Ohio and working for a local company to save money for the future and college. I'm dating a wonderful man who shares my beliefs and we want the best for each other.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
Me? I’m an average 30 year old man. I was born and raised in Bismarck, North Dakota to a single mother. I have two half-sisters from my mom’s second marriage. Those two half-sisters are like any other siblings to me. I am the only one of those three. My mother and I are extremely close. In fact, I would do anything in the world for her.

A happy childhood…

I had a happy childhood and a decent life. I was always within the “upper-middle class” bracket in life, but never rich. I have always been able to take care of myself financially and I have never relied on other people for assistance.

I take great pride in myself because I know how to take care of myself financially, and can enjoy life doing what I want to do. As a matter of fact, I work in the construction industry at the moment.

A great opportunity

I’ve got a great opportunity to work abroad in South Korea. I’m there currently. I’m hoping to work in Asia for a couple of years, and possibly even more if I find it worthwhile. I’m looking to gain more experience in my field. If it doesn’t work out, I can always return to the US later on. I am not going to Korea to run away from a bad situation here in America.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I’m 32. I grew up in a well to do part of town in a town slightly larger than yours and went to the largest university in our state, from which I eventually graduated with a history degree. History is a very important subject to me and books are my life. My career path has been meandering, but I do write a lot of hand written letters as a hobby and help my mother send emails as a career. I like to shoot photos, cook, garden, and go camping and on road trips to new destinations. I have a large number of interests, from board games to tech to film to cars to music to comic books to… You get the point.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I am a 37 year old single female living in New York City. I was born in Philadelphia and grew up in the suburbs outside of the city where I attended a Montessori school and then transferred to the local public school once I was in 4th grade. My parents, single mother and stepfather both came from what would be considered lower-class families. My mother always put value on education and wanted to give her kids the best chance possible so she made an effort to give us access to both Montessori and then a gifted school for my high school years where I was to learn to be “high class.” I had the best of everything I thought I wanted and I struggled to figure out what to do with all the opportunity thrown at me. I went to a fine arts school and was in a lot of different social groups from the preps to the ratchets and several different cliques within each group. Eventually, I came to the realization that the best thing I could do with my opportunity was to make the most of it and use it to empower other people, especially women and black girls. My value comes from my creative spirit, ability to connect with people and love. I enjoy socializing and networking, organizing fundraisers, cultural events, etc. I enjoy traveling and seeing new things. I love books and everything about reading, writing and books in general. I enjoy seeing amazing spaces and art.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am a nationally certified registered nurse and I have the qualifications to practice nursing in the state of Texas. I have passed the NCLEX exam.

I am 32 years old and I spent the majority of my childhood living in Hong Kong. My father was in the Asian city trade. My parents are both Chinese.

I moved around a lot when I was younger.

Hong Kong is a very big city. I have visited many big cities in the United States.

I often travelled on buses around the city with my father.

He needed to visit lots of people to do his job. We also went shopping at shopping malls.

It is a great place to live.

I was never happy with my life in China. I came to America to look for work and to have a family. I knew a man from New York and he told me that the United States is a good place to live.

He told me it is a democracy. My parents in China are very serious people and they would never allow me to leave China. My father and mother are very strict with me and my family is very upset that I am in America.

I believe in a democracy like America. I moved to America a few years ago to look for work and a family. I am very happy here. I made many friends and I found a job in the hospital in New York.

I went to school in the United States. I also met some people from other countries. I met an American man and he talked to me about leaving China and moving to America.

I am now married to an American man. I have lived in America for a long time and I have a lot of experience in nursing.

I have completed my PhD and my undergraduate degree.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I'm 38 years old. I grew up in a suburb of Los Angeles, California. My father, mother and sister (of 16 years) live there. However, since my father has Alzheimer's, I spend a lot of time in Montauk, New York visiting them.

I was a weird kid, in the sense that while others were playing with fire, I had no interest in that sort of thing. I was more inclined to flip off the water in the school showers and turn on the lights and mix them together just to see what would happen. I liked explosions, but I also liked beauty; hence, I enjoyed fireworks and photogenic models. Seeing an explosion was fun and pleasant, but it could become a downright chore after a few hours of watching, so I'd usually surround myself with beautiful women. In this way, I've combined my two favorite past times, photography and pretty women.

After being incarcerated for the better part of a decade, I've had plenty of time to think about who I am and why I'm here. I was a very honest, albeit dumb, kid, and I would always go out of my way to "do the right thing". In hindsight, that may have gotten me into a bit of trouble, as now I think I know why they call "honesty" a rare trait. Most of the time, being too honest got me into a bit of hot water, and in prison for a few years, I realized something: those that are the most honest aren't the same thing as those that are the most trustworthy. If I was more honest, I wouldn't be in this mess. I suspect that that would be the case regardless of who I was, as prison is not something to be taken lightly, but you can't be too honest in this world and expect to get away with it. It seems to me that honesty is one of the least important traits to possess in this life, as someone that has a lot of honesty but few other worthwhile traits isn't the same thing as someone that lacks honesty and has a lot of other worthwhile traits, i.e. an honest, hard-working guy with a bunch of personality flaws may seem better than an untruthful slacker, but the truth is that he's still bad, because at some point, if you're the kind of guy who would leave a guy hanging when he needs him, you'd be dead if it wasn't for him, and any honest guy is still gonna go look for help if he's hurt. So, honesty is the last thing you really want, as people who are honest will always be telling you what they're up to, and you want someone with a bit of mystery if you can get him. And that's how I got here. Or, rather, I was the ideal candidate for my circumstances, because I could have used a bit more trustworthiness (or at least humility) when things were rough.

So, after being out of prison for the better part of a decade, I am now in the process of paying my debt to society. I have almost been wiped clean from my rap sheet, and I have been more than patient with it all. I know that I would not be able to make it out here in this world without the help of the Innocence Project, and if my sentence was longer than it was I would be mad. They have helped me tremendously with my case, but all I'm asking for is that, now that I've done my time, I be allowed to do the rest of it in my own way.

I want to help those in need. It's been my goal for a while now, and I've been trying to make some headway on it. In order to do this I need a little help. I want to be able to get my real estate license, and I'd like to be able to finish my degree online.

I have a bit of a dark side; I haven't really made any effort to deny that. I like to drive too fast, sometimes a little too recklessly, drink too much, sometimes a little too much, and sometimes I like to dance all night with beautiful women to music that's as loud as a jet taking off (or as quiet as death). I'm not above dating girls, but I'm really in love with the idea of being in love. But I want to do it the right way. I want a woman by my side when I walk through a door or when I buy a house, and I want a home to call our own.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I am 30 years old and I was born in Connecticut and moved to Florida when I was 8 years old. I had a very traditional childhood with the traditions of Sundays and other family activities. Because I had the opportunity to grow up as a Florida native, I gained an understanding for the geography of the land and the rules that are instilled on everything in the state. For example, I understand that you can’t have green growth on the lawn in the winter but you need it in the summer months or that you can’t wear blue and white together on the same day and if you do, it will be a very bad luck day. For me, these have created a great sense of respect for the land of Florida and its people. In terms of family, my mother had nine siblings and she was always busy with them growing up. The side of my family comes from Sicily, while my father is from Manhattan, New York. My family dynamic was different growing up and I was always the fish out of water within the household. My parents wanted to give my sister and I the sense of traditional upbringing that they had. Because I was the youngest of three, I couldn’t relate to my older sister or my older brother and there was always a huge gap between them. This led to my social alienation and gave me the desire to socialize. I enjoy developing relationships and working with people who allow me to explore myself more freely.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: My name is Shashini Mitra. I’m 36 years old. I was born in Vancouver, Washington. My parents moved to Issaquah, WA when I was 5. I graduated from Valley Catholic High School. I have an older brother who is 45. I went to Saint Mary’s College of California with a degree in Psychology and English. I married my husband in 2008 and we currently have 2 boys. We have lived in Issaquah since 2009. Our oldest son started kindergarten this year. My son’s teacher is a fellow Indian American named Deepak. I was honored to be invited to his wedding a couple years ago. Our youngest son is in preschool and he recently started speech therapy for his “r” sounds. I volunteer at Eastgate Elementary as a room mom. I enjoy my family’s weekly date nights, bowling, singing, and cooking. My parenting style is pretty laid back. I believe in lots of laughter and kind words. I don’t yell or get angry with my children. I am a night owl and an early riser. I’m a sleep junkie. I can easily stay up all night with a good book and my husband doesn’t realize I’m up until I go on our morning walks with the dogs. I’m a feminist. I believe in fighting for equality. I believe in inclusion and being a good neighbor. I believe in being genuine and open to other people’s opinions. I believe in promoting others so they can be the best versions of themselves. It’s a work in progress.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I’m still working that out. I’m 32 years old and grew up in West Michigan. My father, who was a Presbyterian minister, pastored churches across West Michigan. I grew up in different towns as a result.

I love family. I have a wonderful wife and three children, a 10-year-old, an eight-year-old and a six-year-old.

I love to travel. One of my favorite things is to go into a new culture and make new friends, understand a new place. I love to read. I love Michigan State Spartans football, so I am very happy right now. I like to play the guitar and write music. I just started trying to learn the piano, too.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am a 32-year old female born and raised in Silver Spring, Maryland. Growing up, I enjoyed cooking, dancing, and traveling. As I’ve grown, I have taken a particular interest in environmental issues and the theory and practice of sustainability. I am a proponent of minimizing resource use and maximizing the reuse of materials and resources from products that have reached the end of their “useful lives.” I believe that part of the purpose of the everyday living experience is to extend the lives of products and material resources.

A great way to extend the lives of products is to replace parts or refurbish rather than entirely replace them when wear and tear is observable. Part of the issue is that the world is often dictated by disposable culture. Technological products especially are in the throes of a “disposable society.” This is a phenomenon that has gradually surfaced over the last few decades. Economic advantages, environmental impacts, cultural norms, work ethic, and technology all create a symphonic co-influence that underpins a society that embraces sustainability less and less. For many products, the cycle of a product is a lifespan of roughly a year and this trend is concerning.

My belief that repairs are more beneficial than replacements when the materials and components of the product are still intact is a part of a philosophy I adopted after taking a sociological and psychological course during graduate school at East Carolina University. The course covered theories of socialization, mass media, cognitive development, and decision making that drove me to come up with theories for life, such as life should be a service rather than a consumer experience. Life should be about the progression of science and technology which essentially suggests that life is about the ongoing experience of life itself, rather than the results of life itself.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I am 40 years old. I grew up in a large city in the southern US. My family and I moved to the city from the suburbs when I was 10 years old. My parents got divorced when I was 12 years old and I began experimenting with drugs. My parents were abusive to me growing up. When I was 15, I dropped out of high school. I was arrested for driving under the influence of alcohol when I was 16 years old. I was arrested again for marijuana possession when I was 18 years old. I continued to use marijuana and to drive under the influence of alcohol after these arrests. When I was 21 years old, I went to jail for the first time. I was arrested for drug possession and paraphernalia. I was released on probation. I was arrested again for DUI and drug possession when I was 24 years old. I was in jail for 2 years. After I was released, I completed my GED. I started using methamphetamine after I was released from jail. I continued to use methamphetamine and to drive under the influence of alcohol after I completed my GED. I went to jail again when I was 31 years old. I was arrested for drug possession and paraphernalia. I was released on probation. I was arrested again for drug possession when I was 33 years old. I went to jail again when I was 34 years old. I was arrested for DUI. I was released on probation. I started using heroin when I was 36 years old. I went to jail again when I was 37 years old. I was arrested for drug possession. I was released on probation. I was arrested again for DUI and drug possession when I was 39 years old. I went to jail again. I was released on probation. I was arrested again for drug possession when I was 40 years old. I was released on probation. I am now 40 years old and on probation. I am still using methamphetamine and heroin. I am now in drug treatment and have not used heroin in 2 months.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: My name is John Jones, and I am 30 years old. I was born in Los Angeles California and grew up in the Detroit area. As a child, I faced a few challenges. One was attending school every day with my mother. She worked shifts at a fast-food restaurant and was unable to take time off to pick me up from school. I attended school during the day and worked at the same restaurant at night with my mother after completing my assignments. During my high school years, my mother was laid off. She was unemployed for five months before she found a job at another fast-food restaurant. During the interim, she and I shared responsibilities at home and worked together in the evenings. This hardship taught me some of the most important lessons I have learned. One is that the world demands a certain level of effort and hard work from all individuals to accomplish goals.
