It also taught me that you have to be persistent. Anyone who has an entrepreneurial spirit and refuses to give up will eventually succeed. The other was the value of true friendship and the kind of family that money cannot buy. My mother and I were like that. We always did things together to make ends meet, we cooked and ate meals together, went on family vacations, and got in on-again, off-again quarrels like all families. My best friend was my mother. And now the woman I still call my best friend is my wife.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I grew up on the Long Island, New York in a sleepy suburb of the Hamptons. I had a dream that I wanted to be a famous actor when I grew up, and I am right on track, hooray! I am 37 and about to turn 38, though my head still doesn’t know that. I still think I’m 18, but the rest of me feels 37.

I’m also in love with 19th century French literature, and have been for about 20 years. Recently I’ve taken an interest in other Western and Eastern Philosophies, though 19th century French literature still remains my favorite.

I’m currently teaching high school English at an alternative high school in Santa Barbara. I’m also a yoga teacher, though my career as a teacher is soon coming to an end as I move away for a new one.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am in my thirties. I live in Brooklyn. I grew up in New Jersey, north of Manhattan. My father is a strict Jew from the old country. My mother, who converted, is from an inter-racial family in Pittsburgh. My family life growing up was very stern. We had to daven at least three times a day. I believe my upbringing helped define who I am today. I do not necessarily agree with all of my father’s values but I cherish the many traits he instilled in me. I am a fair, just and principled person because of how he raised me.

My mother, on the other hand, was an artist who encouraged me to think outside the box. I am an original thinker. My mom told me I could be anything I wanted, and ever since I was young I wanted to be a doctor and help others.

I always did well in school. My favorite subjects were math and science. I graduated with a BS in Physiology from the University of Pennsylvania, class of 2004.

After graduation I worked as a medical technician in a lab at Hospital Corporation of America in Plymouth Meeting, PA. There were not a lot of job openings in NYC, so I decided to stay in Philadelphia to get some work experience.

I met my fiancé there. His name is Michael. He runs a start up with his brother. They are building computer systems for pharmacies. His father and brother are in the industry. Michael graduated from the University of Michigan in 2001. He is very tech savvy. He built his own PC when he was 10 and he’s always been working in IT. He is very funny. I love to laugh. We get along well because we have so much in common. We like to watch action movies together. I love sports. He coaches and plays on a softball team. We also like to travel. We are going to Italy for our first honeymoon. When we aren’t hanging out with friends, Michael and I spend a lot of time with our dog, Elvis. He’s a beautiful pit bull. He is very loyal and loving.

Nowadays I am back in school. I am getting my MD from Cornell and will graduate in May 2012. I am a member of the selective Gold Humanism Honor Society and have already received a few awards from my program. I work really hard. I live a relatively simple life.

I can’t really say that I have had any major life experience because I am only in my thirties. I have not been hurt or cheated on. I have no regrets. I haven’t done anything crazy. I just try to work hard and treat people the way I would want to be treated.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I am 43 years old and was born and raised in a small suburb of Dallas. I am the youngest of three children and do not have any children. My parents have been married for over 45 years. I attended schools in the district my entire life. Growing up, I played in the band and orchestra. I did band for 7 years and played saxophone. I also attended the Richland Career Center and was in the TV Production Program. After school, I went to work at a famous amusement park in Garland where I worked the Midway Games. This is where I met Mark. I then graduated from North Dallas High School in 1986. My parents were not very wealthy and it has never been an easy road for me and my family. They always worked hard to support us. I went to work after high school at a few places in restaurants and did not really know what I wanted to do. I then worked at a veterinary hospital which is where I met my wife. After that, I started working for what was then American Airlines. I worked for them for about 6 years and then I got a job with Northwest Airlines. I worked for them for 16 ½ years when I was laid off in 2009. This ended up being one of the best things that has ever happened to me because it enabled me to get the job that I now have. I have worked in the travel business with my wife for over 12 years now and I have never had a job that I have been more happy with in my entire life. I never thought I would like this job as much as I do.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I was born and raised in Brookfield, Wisconsin. I am 35 years old now and I turn 36 in a few weeks. I am the father of a boy and girl, 4 years and 2 years old, respectively. We live in Beaumont, Texas where I am a computational linguist. I have a Bachelor’s degree in Political Science and English from the University of Wisconsin - Stevens Point. My undergraduate studies centered around Philosophy and Analysis of Ideas and Beliefs. I have a Juris Doctor degree from Arizona Summit Law School, I obtained that degree in 2015. I began studies in that field in an attempt to leverage the best and most accessible legal resources in the world to challenge the status quo and make positive changes to the government. My career has included working in a warehouse and security while attending law school on a part time basis. I have worked as a self-employed legal courier and I have managed funds for the broker/dealer firm Ric Investments, LLC. I have passed all three parts of the CPA exam to be a CPA in New Jersey. As a certified accounting professional, I have utilized business licenses to operate on behalf of private parties in Texas. I have several licenses in practice areas that fall within the scope of finance, this process of licensure has now moved into the practice of public and securities law as an extension of my CPA studies, that can be articulated as a comprehensive understanding of those licensure exams which is set forth in the MBA curriculum.

One thing that matters a lot to me and that I enjoy is writing. I have written several scholarly works, a primary text, and I have co-authored legal treatises with legal professionals in the past. My current concentration is on the deployment of software that renders an individual’s participation in the public realm sustainable on a macro level. It can be manifested through such applications as open-source government and a blockchain that assigns immutable properties to digital and physical resources in a representative and secure manner. The latter is demonstrated in the parcel lockers I design and construct with my own hands.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I am 30 and I grew up in a very small farming town in California. I was the first person in my immediate family to ever graduate high school, so going to college was very important to me. After I graduated high school, I was accepted into a college and with the help of financial aid I was able to attend. While attending college I lived on a farm in one of the least expensive parts of the state while making minimum wage as a farm hand to help pay for the cost of books and dorm supplies. One of my favorite experiences from college was a month long trip to study abroad in Paris, France, it was an amazing experience and I really learned a lot about myself while I was there. After graduating from college with my BA in Business and Leadership, I then went on to work in marketing, web development, and sales. I also sold real estate and wanted to make that a full-time career, but unfortunately the economy and the real estate market in the late 2000's made it very difficult for my first few years. With that said, I still was able to learn a lot about the American and world economies, finance, and the various different business practices all over the world. Through hard work, determination, and a lot of support, I was able to keep pushing through, and I was able to help my parents buy a house, and all three of us are living in a nice house right on the coast.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
Well, I am 37 years old. I was born in Kingfisher, Oklahoma. My father was a pioneer home brewer. I got my first kit for my 13th birthday. I am not, and never have been a chemist, I have made the same 5 gallon kit over and over until I had 5 gallons of every conceivable beer there is to make in a standard kit. I have been a home brewer for 24 years and have grown to enjoy cooking in general. I love to cook for others, especially those who never thought they would enjoy homebrewing.

I am active in my community. I am an avid supporter of my local school. I support it through paying taxes, being a member of the local chapter of the Boosters and I support the school financially by donating my time, talents and my homebrew. I spend my time in the brewing community with other homebrewers. I attend homebrew clubs and regional festivals and I lead excursions into Mexico where people do not drink our beer and I get to teach them that they should.

When I was young I was accused by my father to never have a good word to say about anyone. This statement is in direct conflict with the reality of my true personality. However, when I was young I had more teeth knocked out, more lips split, more nose bloodied than I care to remember. I have a collection of teeth, all of which are broken and are slowly sinking to the bottom of my jaw. Now in my ripe old age of 37 I am not as willing to fight as I once was. I still find myself in a fight on occasion, when it’s my wife who has to rip me off of the offending individual. My reputation for being a brawler is not lost on me, although I have learned to lay down and take a beating in exchange for a quiet neighborhood. I am a complainer and complacency has made me into a whiner, but never a crybaby, because I don’t cry often. I am a homebrewer, and I brew because I enjoy making people taste what beer could taste like.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I am 43 years old, married with two children. I grew up as a child of diplomats. What matters to me is my wife and my children. When I was a child, my parents abandoned us and my siblings and I were abandoned to grow up by ourselves. What I was able to learn was my first passion is music, which I’ve played since I was 7 years old. I went to college, in order to support my father as he was dying. He died when I was a Junior in college. At that point, I dropped out of college, as there was no need for a Social Work degree. A few years later, I started working in the Culinary industry. I was always attracted to public safety, but was far too prideful to go to work as a security guard in order to work to my way up. I made other bad decisions, and it took a 6 year relationship and a two year relationship to come to the realization that I was wasting my time trying to compete with what other people have. I would like to settle down and live a modest life, have a secure job, and provide a good life for my family. Public Safety has been something I’ve been more interested in these last couple of years, ever since I started out of high school in security. It is really too bad that I found this out too late. I was planning on attending a police academy, but I never went through with it. I hope I’m not too old to finally do what it is that I have always wanted to do.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I'm 38 years old, born to a Kentucky farm family, (Edmonson County) on a small tobacco and cattle farm between Magnolia and Cave City Kentucky.

We moved to Maricopa Arizona after I was in the second grade when my father and step mother (not the same ones as my previous post) convinced my mother to take my two younger brothers, and me to a more temperate climate.

After finishing high school in 1989, I briefly attended college while working as a Store Manager. I took a job with the city of Phoenix Police Department, wanting to help fight crime. I quit after 5 years, disappointed that my leadership talents and intuition were not getting better than average results. However, people who observed me thought that I was brilliant. But nobody had the courage to manage me more effectively. I suffered in Phoenix, as they seem to enjoy watching people suffer.

I began to see things and draw conclusions that made no sense to me. The prevalent computerized crime databases, bi-lingual hiring, promotion practices, lack of procedural controls and what I felt was a terribly biased and dysfunctional legal system. The federal Justice Department in Phoenix was secretive and aggressive in fighting against true Justice. I knew it was a microcosm of Washington and our entire nation. It was all about who and where the money was.

I returned to Kentucky when I was 27 years old, thinking I could help start a business. It was a total loss, financially. I sought restitution and political help to get justice. Nothing but lies and politics ensued.

I moved to Owensboro, Kentucky in 2003. I stayed for five months, working on a farm. I was unable to stand the mass of humanity in Owensboro and moved into the middle of nowhere with a partner. It was called THE ANSWER . We built a compound and more than doubled our social circle, becoming more interesting and friendly to anyone who would learn about our journey. We started adding acres. There were many visitors. We kept a Bigfoot camera at an active location and finally had it not be needed. Many visitors came. Most of them became very comfortable and very active. We were growing in both numbers and energy, and becoming open minded. I still could see society falling apart, as my double barrel shotgun and pistol were well maintained.

Sylvia and I split in 2010. I moved away to live close to my mother, who was in poor health. She passed away in late 2011. My two younger brothers are still living in Kentucky, working hard and enjoying life. We all still get along.

I returned to my farm in Colorado at the beginning of 2012. I spent all of 2013 repairing, protecting and planning and planting. I entered Colorado the same time the Columbine shooters entered high school. I knew Denver was corrupt, anti-Christian and anti-gun. I knew CO law enforcement was biased, corrupt and out of control. I had rented out my farm to a local marijuana grower. I ran him out and the farm was put to better use. More people now realize that we have every right to produce our own food. It's better for our environment and communities. We had one trespassing intrusion from the Garfield County Sheriff's Office and one from the Northwest Colorado Drug Task Force. But the largest intrusion was from the Colorado Attorney General's Office. Something about clogged drain pipes, failing septic systems and taxation. They did not understand their statutes. We disagreed on this and an opinion from the AG's office was sent, but not received. I ordered the drain pipes and septic system replaced. I had about five TV camera crews visit, and some newspaper articles as well as their camera crews. Not one said, "thank you" or had manners. But the local sheriff's office has responded in kind. There is mutual respect and they ask before they intrude.

March of 2014, I had a visit from the FBI and another inquiry from the Attorney General's Office, concerning my money. They want me to submit to an audit, but only if they can pay for it. It seems very much like their mortgage interest audits.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am 34 years old. I grew up in New York, New York, until I was eight years old and then moved to a small country town in Vermont, right on the Canadian border. I absolutely adored growing up in Vermont. At a very early age I was out running around the woods, no shoes, picking wild flowers, and cutting down trees with a hatchet. I played with frog in the rivers, caught bugs, I read books. Vermont was amazing. My father taught me how to use a hammer and nails to build tree houses. I went to an elementary school where I was home schooled during the summer while the rest of the school was in session. I was socialized well, and from a very early age was very responsible and used to cooking, cleaning, and other simple adult tasks. Needless to say, I grew up different than a lot of other children. From a very young age I was in charge of my own well being. In 1995 I attended a high school that was special in many ways. It is called Vermont Commons School. It is a high school based on the philosophy of Rudolf Steiner. It was a private school, non profit. I believe the tuition at the time was 30,000 dollars a year. At Vermont Commons school there was a little bit of everything, a lot of teacher student ratio and they focused a lot on the students spirituality and respect for nature. This philosophy came from a German philosopher called Rudolf Steiner. This was a good fit for me because I am a firm believer in freedom and liberty, and I always have been. I honestly don’t know if I would be the same person without the experiences of being in Vermont.

The school just recently closed due to economic conditions. To be in Vermont when I was a kid was almost like being in a totally different country.

And I liked it. I liked it a lot.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I'm 30 years old and I'm from the middle of nowhere in the Southern United States. I was raised in a poverty-stricken part of the country, often working three or four jobs in order to make ends meet. When I was eight, my father, who was my best friend, took his own life, and this set off a chain of events which led me to my current situation. Throughout my early teen years, I was bullied to the point where I seriously thought about killing myself, and I eventually acted on this thought when I was sixteen. I killed a classmate, and I have been incarcerated ever since. I really need to get out of here. I think that with all of the time that I have on my hands, I can help your campaign in some significant way.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I’m 34. I was born in Quebec, in 1974. I grew up in small towns, where there’s a high French population. My father is a doctor of French origin, and my mother is Mexican. I’m bilingual in French and Spanish. I’ve been traveling and working since the time I was 14. I’ve worked in many types of jobs and have met people from different cultures and backgrounds.

I love traveling. I have a big Spanish-speaking family.

I love people and learning, about other people’s cultures and backgrounds. I try to learn about them and get to know them on a personal level. I appreciate what they do and how they live.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I am 32 years old, married with two children. I grew up in North Texas in a very rural setting. After High School I worked for a plumbing company and eventually saved enough money to continue my education. I went back to college at Amarillo College, where I earned an associates degree. I was in the middle of trying to decide where to transfer when the economy collapsed. I couldn’t afford the remaining 2 years at a 4 year University with several semesters of tuition, fees, books, and expenses. I knew I would never complete my bachelors degree at that time.

I attempted to work in the field of customer service for a while. A very dear friend, family really, passed away. I went to the funeral, and while everyone I loved was there I didn’t fit in. It was hard to talk to these people, and I was never very confident in the first place. I needed a career that would give me purpose and stability. I enrolled in a one year Registered Nursing program at Amarillo College. I am proud to say I was the only male in the class. It was one of the most difficult and rewarding times of my life.

In the last several years I have become a proven leader in nursing. I am always up front and vocal about what needs to be done and won’t compromise on unsafe patient care. Nurses need to be front and center advocating for their patients. When nurses can do their job and then help manage conflict and overcome challenges, morale improves.

I have been a charge nurse, first assisting in some of the state’s most difficult and rare surgeries, and right now I am working on a case load and precepting new nurses. Nurses are running and we need to be engaged, informed, and prepared to fight for our patients’ rights and safety.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I’m in my mid-thirties. I grew up in the US and overseas due to diplomatic postings (embassies and missions). I completed all of my education overseas, but upon my return to the US, I got a Bachelor of Science from a small liberal arts college in California. I was awarded an honorable discharge after serving in the US military. I went on to pursue advanced degrees in international relations from a famous US university. I was a government contractor for a bit. I traveled, worked, and studied overseas, developing and studying on my own. I worked in various businesses and agencies. I did my thesis on cultural tension in Southeast Asia. I decided to do a commercial advertising campaign for a popular radio station in Southeast Asia (radio network). I tried being an entrepreneur for a bit, failed, and ended up returning to my home country and coming to this agency (name omitted). I spent the following decade working in various positions. I’ve been to about 15 countries so far. The world is my oyster. I’ll settle down here (my home country).

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I am 51 years old, born in Colombo Sri Lanka, lived there until 2000 when I moved to Illinois in the United States to work for a non profit organization helping migrant children and their families gain access to health care. My first job after my arrival was as the Healthcare Advocate for medically complex uninsured children. I have traveled to 30 countries and have lived abroad in Iran, England and Iran. I visited my family at the age of three and continued my visitings every year without fail until I left the island in 2000. I have been a republican for the last 17 years and I have been happy with them. This election however, I voted for Obama because of his advocacy for working class and middle class people. He is a socially conscious leader and not a military man who can be used by corporations, pharmaceuticals and insurance companies to enact destructive legislation like Medicare, Medicare D. It is a known fact that the pharmaceuticals and the insurance companies have gained huge revenue from these bills which were designed to wreck havoc and the lives of elderly and poor people.

I was a health care provider for over 35 years as a registered nurse. I have been working in different capacities for the past five years from social work to healthcare advocacy, children and family advocacy with youth who have been sexually exploited, abused and undocumented. I hold five different degrees including a master’s in public health. I am co-founder of a nonprofit organization providing service to women who have been trafficked. I also do speaking engagements for corporations and universities on leadership, abuse and advocacy. I have published an article on children with complex health care needs at a major university in Illinois.

I am an avid reader and author. I have been working on my first novel for over 8 years and I am close to publishing it. I had started a second novel when I was in the midst of writing my first one. To my surprise, the first one turned out to be nonfiction instead of fiction as planned. I have also written a screenplay and am finishing it now. I am an award winning painter. I have been painting since I was five years old. I am married for 17 years and my husband is a contractor who grew up here in Illinois. He helps me with my writing and our children (a college freshman and a junior in high school) provide constant feedback. I have been raising my two children since the age of five (both boys). I had to go back to work to make ends meet. I am not a stay at home mom for the sake of staying home. I work because we are a two bread winning family. Many of my friends who live in gated communities and have no idea about the plight of working class and middle class people like me have called me an unfit parent for working.

I have been living without medical insurance until the newly elected Obama passed the Bill. My children do not have health insurance and I work hard every day to keep them healthy. I know what it is like to worry about health care when there is no hope in sight.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: My name is Richard James Alexander Schneider. I am 51 years old, married to Julie Ann. I was born in Omaha, Nebraska on January 20, 1957. My parents moved a lot as I was growing up and I lived in Texas, Missouri, Kansas, Mississippi, Minnesota, and Nebraska. When I was 11, we moved to Saudi Arabia where my father worked in his CPA practice. At age 17, when I finished high school, we returned to the US and I attended the University of Nebraska. On my 18th birthday, my mother stood me up in front of everyone, including the witnesses and signed the letter asking the federal government for political asylum. My father was highly disapproving. In 1977, my mother, siblings, and I and our friends all voted in the US presidential election against Ronald Reagan even though all of us were happy that the republicans won. Four years later, when we had been denied asylum we went to the supreme court and lost. In 1983, my father sent me to Germany with a suitcase full of letters from my professors in Nebraska asking for help with finding a job for me. I made it to Frankfurt but then had to fly home to take care of my youngest sister. I have two half-sisters on my father’s side, one from Missouri and one from Arkansas. In 1984, we got a new attorney who sent us to Dallas, Texas. A few days later we boarded a plane back to Saudi Arabia. I came back on the “unscheduled trip” on a backup ticket. It was a much different plane than the one I had taken home so I knew something was up. I found out as I was going through customs, that the kingdom of Saudi Arabia no longer wanted me there and I was not allowed to travel back there, so that was the end of my trip. In 1984, we left Saudi Arabia and arrived in Switzerland where I met John, who became my friend. When the department of home land security (DHS) heard about it, they were upset that I had lived in the states. So I was deported back to Dallas where the department of home land security (DHS) had me sent to Germany for a background check to see if I had overstayed my visa. After that was done I was once again sent back to Dallas where they kept me there for two weeks, waiting for a flight. I was later to learn that the sister of one of my high school teachers had me added to her bicycle seat. There are many days when I wish I had been arrested or had just stayed in Germany. Today, I would like to retire and live in Iceland or Germany. Julie says that since I am already living in Germany I should stay here. Still, I want to move on to other places where it is easier to learn German.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I am 51 years old and have lived most of my life in the Milwaukee area. I was born on August 8th 1962, 9:33 PM at Wauwatosa Hospital. I’m adopted and didn’t learn of that until I was 22. Being a first-born is often the product of a big story. I have a good sense of humor and am easy going. I am usually amused by the absurd, the seemingly senseless and all things metahumorous and incongruent. I appreciate moments that can be shared when enjoyed by all who are present. I like to have fun and share a good time with friends and loved ones. I enjoy being part of a couple; the nurturing of a relationship and taking care of a family. I value above all, honesty and trustworthiness and especially respect, appreciation and for my other half to be truly committed. Family and friends are also very important to me. I love to travel and enjoy all the things that go with traveling like playing tourist, finding the hidden gems of a city, meeting people, learning about cultures, and seeing great natural and man-made wonders. Spending time outside, especially around water, is also important to me. I like to find ways to reduce stress in my life and living clean and healthy contributes to that. I like to read and have an academic bent. I am currently working on a book and two articles that help to explain and expound upon how people experience spiritual, energy and universal consciousness and how that can help one become a better person who can help others have a better life. A man must always be striving to improve himself so that he can share his best self with the people in his life.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am the daughter of a Mormon who was married to a Jew and I was raised Jewish. I’m 50 and was born in the suburbs of Los Angeles. My family moved to West Hollywood when I was eight years old, and I started going to private school. I was one of two Jewish kids in my class of thirty. We started going to Hebrew school when I was eleven. I graduated from there and went on to study theatre and performance at UC Santa Cruz.

As a freshman, I realized that I did not want to go to theatre school. I didn’t want to be an actress. I felt that I was surrounded by a lot of very talented kids who knew exactly what they wanted to do with their lives. They were doing amazing work that I wanted to be a part of, but I did not know exactly what I wanted to do. So I decided to go study engineering and computer science at Stanford and that is what I did.

From my first semester of college, I knew that I did not want to do that for the rest of my life. I wanted to get an MBA. I didn’t know exactly why. I had a great experience at Stanford. I went to theater performances all the time. I loved my freshman improv class and I loved the choir. I realized how much I missed creative experiences and I felt that if I got an MBA, I would have more opportunities to create.

I graduated with my engineering degree and went to work at Apple, a wonderful company to start with. After about a year, I decided that I was going to quit and go to business school. I needed to figure out exactly why I wanted to get an MBA, but I thought that if I had a better understanding of business, I would have more opportunities to do something with my passion for the arts and entertainment.

I got my MBA at Harvard. I moved to Boston with my best friend. We had gone to Stanford together. We planned a big birthday celebration for ourselves on Cape Cod. She got there first and she called me to say that there was this really great dinner party in town and that I needed to come. I had no idea what I was going to do after graduation, so I went to the party.

There was one guy who was sitting by himself in the corner. I did not know what I was going to do after I finished school. I was taking my time, and I didn’t have a good idea of what I was going to do. I was taking my time, and I didn’t have a good idea of what I was going to do.

I graduated from Harvard with my MBA, and I moved to Boston with my best friend. We had gone to Stanford together. We planned a big birthday celebration for ourselves on Cape Cod. She got there first and she called me to say that there was this really great dinner party in town and that I needed to come. I didn’t know what I was going to do after I finished school. I was taking my time, and I didn’t have a good idea of what I was going to do.

The guy sitting in the corner of the party was Paul, a 34-year-old who was getting divorced. He was super hot, and he was a lawyer. I was not interested in him. I was a little bit intimidated, but I thought that I would give him a chance.

I had never been in a relationship with a guy who had a divorce. My dad got divorced when I was about two years old. My mom got divorced when I was five. I was raised by a single mom. I had a great dad and a great relationship with him.

I was very much like my mom, and I thought that was a bad thing. I had a very negative view of divorce. I thought that if a couple divorced, it was bad for everyone involved. I thought that if you were divorced, you were a failure. I had a lot of fear around it.

But as I spent more time with Paul, I realized that my views of divorce were totally skewed. I didn’t realize that the reason why I thought divorce was bad was because I hadn’t been raised in a divorced family. My parents were married, but they were not divorced. I thought that if you were divorced, you were a failure.

My parents were very involved in my upbringing. They always had a lot of input on how I was going to be raised. I didn’t know that I wasn’t being raised in a divorced family. My parents had a lot of money. I had a very sheltered life. I was not going to have a lot of friends. I was going to be in a very small town. I was going to be in a very small school.

My parents never said anything negative about divorce, and they did not make me feel bad for it. They did not make me feel guilty about it. I felt like it was the right thing to do. I felt that if I didn’t get divorced, I would not be a good parent. I felt that if I got divorced, I would not be a good parent.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I am in my 50s. I was born in the UK but spent a lot of my childhood in different countries (Zambia, Kenya, Tanzania, Somalia, Nigeria). My father worked for the British Government for 38 years which meant that we moved a lot. When we lived in Nairobi, I had the opportunity to spend a lot of time with a master bushman. His name was Augee (nee John McKie). He taught me a lot about food plants and we used to eat seeds, bulbs and fruits from various wild fruit trees and bushes. He spoke about how he used to grind seeds and make a sort of gruel that he could add to his daily goat milk and make a nutritious meal. I was fascinated by this idea and came up with this wild variety of grain called teff which grows in Ethiopia, I think it may have actually originated in Eritrea but that’s okay. This seed is the smallest in the world, harvested and ground and it is the only seed that I know of which has 14 of the essential 19 amino acids! The rest of the essential ones can be acquired elsewhere in the diet so the fact that it has the majority of them really does blow me away! I am convinced that this seed also has a wonderful capacity to feed people in long-standing drought regions and regions affected by war and civil strife. I have this wonderful charity I have set up called the African Teff Institute. This is because I think Ethiopia has enough teff to feed most of the developing world from within, if only they would work in harmony to do so. The charity also promotes opportunities for women to be involved in their own development and the development of women in the world who are poor and vulnerable. I was also very drawn to music and loved art, particularly the art of indigenous Africans.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I am now 58 years old. I grew up in Pittsburgh, PA and went to (listed below). I have had a number of experiences since then that have helped me to form the values that are important to me. The most important for me is that I value quality and attention to detail. For example, I like to cook - I feel that if I’m going to spend the time and energy to cook and it’s going to be worth it, then I don’t want to be served some piece of dried-out, tasteless protein (a bad piece of chicken or burnt chicken are two such examples) to eat. I like to make sure that the food is prepared as well as possible in order to achieve the best eating experience possible. This applies to many other aspects of my life as well.

For example, I read books because I like to learn what I can. If I’m going to take the time and mental energy to learn something, then I want to make sure that what I am learning is the best there is. It’s important to me that the information is accurate and up to date, so I need to make sure that who I am reading is a qualified expert in that particular field (or has expertise in writing) and not just someone with a connection to an online database or periodical.

Also, if I’m going to work on my car, I want to make sure that the parts I am using are top quality. I don’t want to invest time and money into trying to repair my car only to end up having the same problem because the parts I replaced were defective or poor quality. I want to make sure that the things that I spend my time and money on are worth it. I believe that this characteristic of mine comes across in the work that I do. I spend a lot of time to make sure that the work that I do for a company is quality work and something that I believe they can be proud of. Because of this characteristic that I have, I can put a lot of passion into the work that I do. That is what I would bring to Microsoft.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I have lived most of my life in Upstate SC, I am 54 years old, born on January 24, 1962. I have lived in Greenville and Anderson, SC and am married to Donna P. Bowman, which I am very blessed to have found. There have been many events that made me who I am and I feel like many of those events are part of who I am. I have been married to Donna P. Bowman since February 14, 2010.

We recently moved to Easley, SC in 2015 from Pendleton, SC. Donna and I enjoy playing LRC, she is competitive and I am learning. I enjoy motorcycling and we do that often. I was an Army reservist from 1981-1987. That was a good experience, but there was no chance of going back to active duty. I was prepared to go but decided against it. In October of 1986 my orders came in to go to the West Coast for in processing and medical tests, etc.

My orders were changed to go to Camp Long. I was to serve as a Royal Thai Citizen in Thailand. As an Army reservist, we were prepared to go active duty at anytime, but I chose not to go. I did volunteer to go overseas but no one was getting deployed, so I couldn’t go. I completed my active reserve on 11/10/1987 at the Air Force Base. The wife and I were married in 2010, so I have two step sons, Aaron and Derek. Aaron lives in Texas with his family, which consists of three boys. Derek is single and is living in Kentucky with my wife’s youngest sister and her family.

We, my wife and I, have our own business, which consists of maintenance and repair work to various types of equipment. We also do trading of other vehicles, motorcycles, etc. We have been together since May 22, 2009. We were married on February 14, 2010. We enjoy working on cars, going to swap meets, playing LRC and working on our motorcycles. When we are not doing things like that we work on the yard and the exterior of the home.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
Oh, boy! What do you really want to know? How about…

I am 54, the same age as the South from Civil War.

I grew up in the South during the civil rights era. The constant police presence was a constant reminder that we were not free. So I learned to dream about the day I could cross the Mason Dixon line and be free. I wanted to be on the other side where black people were celebrated and honored for who they were. I wanted to be free from the constant message that I was only valuable as a domestic worker who should be grateful for the scraps of food on the table that belonged to those who owned me. I wanted to be free from a system that didn’t value black lives.

My parents are age 78 and 70 and I grew up in one of the last large multi generational families in America. While my parents were cleaning white houses I was making sure that my parents took care of themselves, worked hard, and had the power to resist and deny white people’s requests to break the laws of our conscience. While my parents were accepting crumbs, I was insistent that we have the freedom to earn our bread. They decided to give me to another white family, if they chose to do that again today, white children would be up in arms over the injustice. Today white parents would not be so quick to give away a child, they have learned their mistakes. One day I will write a book to help white people heal the trauma of turning their children over to be traumatized by white people. Despite the pain of my upbringing, it is a healing feeling to see the way that people create space, how much they love their children, and how deeply and intensely black people are loved. I didn’t learn about deep love until I was much older, but I understand love and from the bottom of my heart I appreciate that which white families who gave up their children had and never had themselves… a beautiful heart and desire to give their children a loving home.

I went to my first PWI when I was 16. I was asked to work hard and study. I was told to go above and beyond because people would never expect me to be as capable, as able as they are. It was a chance for me to shine. And I did. I had high hopes that one day I would have a full time job, a little cute apartment, a decent car, maybe even a man. Maybe I could have a daughter so I could instill in her the love and power that she would need to rise above those people who make life so difficult. I read the Black American Woman’s Survival Guide. It taught me how to put on my make-up and how to respect the white man’s position. I never saw the stories of white woman who hated their job, cried in the bathroom stalls, begged for help when they didn’t know how to calculate a rate of growth. I never saw the issue of why doesn’t the university restructure or invest in the diversity and inclusion center? Why do they dismiss the consistent need for creating programs for black people that never existed in the first place?

While the black students were friends with white students, there was never a relationship between the university and the local black community. There was never an equal partnership between black people and white people. Nothing is equal and there is constant fear that black people will rise up and seize power. Everything is about a check list. It is up to me to earn their trust by proving to them that I can do just as good as everyone else. It took me a while to get to the place where I could say F my white friends. They had nothing to do with me and they only talked to me to pass a class and to see if I was as socially acceptable as they are. I no longer needed them to like me. They are not God. They could only become God by threatening my life or taking a part of my body, just like white people do to black people every day in America.

Today, I continue to resist and deny white requests to break my conscience. But I have one wish, that my children wouldn’t be expected to leave home when they are 18 years old, work hard to earn a great education, and marry and raise a child who may one day be killed or jailed because of the evil that exists in America. It is time to push the reset button, it is time to build a new world where the innate dignity and human value of all black people is the driving force. Why would anyone want to leave America and go live in China? Where they are not racist, but the belief that blacks are the origin of modern human life undermines centuries of scientific racism?

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I’m 50 years old, although I feel and think like I’m about 23. I guess in a way I had an unusual childhood. From the time I was about 5 until I was 9 my parents were separated. My mother was away most of the time and I was raised mainly by my brother, aunts and uncles, and friends of the family. When I was 7 my mother left home (they later divorced). I’m not going to go into a bunch of details but that’s when some major changes occurred in my life and the true direction of my life was set. Again I could go into this in detail, but I’m not going to. Suffice it to say I’m in a good place right now. I’m a passionate person about everything. It’s just part of who I am. The last seven years or so have been very difficult, I won’t lie about it, but you can’t keep a fighter down. I have things in my life that are very important to me and a few things that I enjoy. As I said, I’m very passionate about everything in my life. I’m at a point where I just want peace. I feel and think like I’m young but sometimes I feel and think like I’m getting old, I have moments of clarity and insight, but I also have moments where I feel like I have no idea what is going on. I believe that I’ve lived some of my life in the past. I also believe that the past or the dead do not stay dead.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I am 51 years old and I was born and raised in New Orleans, Louisiana. I am the only child of a single mother who worked hard at jobs as a clerk at local stores and a custodian in various schools and government buildings. My mom instilled in me the virtue of education and was constantly pushing me to do better in school. Her love of reading and storytelling are traits I’ve inherited, and I also grew up watching my mom and dad perform in church, singing and playing instruments, which developed my love of music. Being a one child, and for better or worse, a single child, I also grew up with an overactive imagination, and as a result I can’t watch scary movies or listen to spooky stories without hiding behind a pillow. There were lots of holidays and summer vacations when my mom would load up the car and we would travel back to Louisiana to visit family. I grew up on an endless stream of stories about the elders in our family and I would listen with fascination. These were the seeds that grew to be Talespinner Theatre.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am an American by birth, a Brit by adoption, living in a Spanish city called Las Palmas de Gran Canaria. I am 50, was born in New York and moved to New Jersey. I had a great childhood spent in a Jewish suburb and even had 10 years in England before I came here. I loved growing up in the US and my parents valued school and other activities besides TV and video games. I went to a performing arts magnet school and then to a great private high school. We spent summers traveling all over the United States and even visited Hawaii and Canada once and got to see Michigan and Alaska. We were to visit Alaska again and the first night in our hotel the heater broke. We had to move into a small hotel in Alaska and the next morning the family next door was dead from carbon monoxide poisoning. We were supposed to go back the next summer but my mom got breast cancer. We’ve since been to Europe together. My mom still has cancer but it is stable and she is now 76.

I was a good student. I did well in junior and senior high school and got straight A’s in college. I went to the University of Michigan because I fell in love with the campus and the city. I considered Columbia because it was going to be free since my dad was a grad student at NYU. He even took us on tours and both schools had guided tours. I almost went to NYU but the reason I got in was to be a TA for my dad. I also decided to try living in another part of the country for college.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I am 56 years old, I grew up in Ramat Gan, Israel, and 20 years ago I moved to Akko (or Acre). I graduated from Tel Aviv University with a degree in engineering and business. I worked as a web programmer and as a marketing consultant. My wife and I have 4 children. I am an avid reader, fan of all things Star Wars, and in the last 20 years I have been collecting masks from different parts of the world. Some of my interests are motorcycle riding, martial arts, and aerial acrobatics. I love movies, music, and books. I enjoy helping people and I like teaching, and in recent years I have been giving online language courses and coaching on writing and interviews for both Israelis and Russians.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I am 51 years old and I was born in an Afro-American neighborhood in the south. I attended school there as a kid, and it was a rough environment. I think I was exposed to a lot of things at an early age that most kids my age weren't. It shaped the way I saw the world and have an interesting perspective on how life is, as compared to other kids from other backgrounds.

During that time, I started experiencing hardships. My family couldn't afford a lot of things and lived modestly. My dad was a janitor, so he made minimum wage. I was one of nine kids, so things were hard for us. That didn't stop me from seeing the outside world, however, and I saw what other people had and wanted it for myself. I wanted better for myself and not to live my life the way my father did, which is why I decided I wanted to be a scientist, and I was pretty good in school.

I think I learned the skill of hard work at an early age. When we got homework, I did it immediately, whereas my other classmates let it pile up. Doing school work wasn't a luxury for me, but a necessity. I got good grades and went to a nice college, where I did a double major in astrophysics and aeronautical engineering.

After that, I went straight to work in a field that I knew would take me places. And that is when I joined Upwork and began to learn how to write books. And you know how that went.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I am a 51 year old male from Michigan who is married and has three children (two daughters and a son). I am a director in the banking industry with over 20 years experience. I grew up on a dairy farm where my chores included feeding cows, helping with harvest, helping my father get ready to work and helping on my uncle’s dairy farm. This shaped who I am and how I relate to others. In high school, I had a variety of jobs including working as a lifeguard, at a pizza shop, at a golf course and at a Dairy Queen. I then attended the University of Michigan where I received my degree in Finance. After graduation, I started working for a company that I am still with today.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I'm a 52 year old American born to American parents in America. I have been in education from a very early age. When I was very young, I'd already entered and won a writing competition for my short stories. I didn't become a teacher until much later in life. Part of this was my mother dying of cancer when I was still very young; part of this was college too expensive for my family for most of my growing years. I went into medicine instead, but that did not work out, so I went into teaching as an alternative. I am still writing, but the invention came after the teaching, so the invention had nothing to do with teaching but having trouble finding a job, as I really loved my students in all areas of my education, from math to language arts to physical fitness. I had always been an early reader and writer and this followed a suit to my work. I'm also involved in videography, which I also was working on long before I became a professional videographer, and my children are both artists and writers, so it's a passion that seems to span from one generation to the next. It's a family trait. I'm also a martial artist in both traditional and competitive fields, and I am involved in several studies, including liturgical studies such as Jewish, Christian, and Muslim traditions and studies in various martial arts systems. I enjoy spending time with my family, especially my children who are young adults in college. I'm a very conventional person in most ways, but I have a very silly side as well. I enjoy a good laugh with good friends.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I’m 50 years old, I was born and raised in Auburn, Alabama, before I was even a toddler I remember my cousins spending every summer with us. My Uncle Mike was a DJ and he introduced me to rock and roll, and from the time I heard The Beatles at the age of five, I wanted to be like them. I was always wanting to perform and entertain from an early age, being fascinated with people. With my Uncle Joe, there was nothing but acronyms and abbreviations in our household. Sunday dinners I would see my aunts singing and my uncles playing music, and Sunday was a fun day. So, when I got older, I got my first drum set, it was a first act drum set, and from that point on, I was hooked. I started playing drums and singing, and I remember going on talent shows, and people would tell me, “Wow, you did good! You were good. But don’t stop singing because you have a voice!” I had no clue I could sing. I was just up there just doing my thing, but I always had that voice in my head saying, “Don’t stop singing.” I remember watching MTV, all kinds of videos and I wanted to be a part of that industry. But what got me really hooked on the music was with my cousins. My cousin Larry Smith got me on the hip-hop early, and I remember standing in our garage and listening to Slick Rick, and I wanted to sound like Slick Rick, LL Cool J, Chris Rock. I started writing lyrics and poems. I wanted to be a part of all that. I then got into the rap and hip-hop phase when I was in middle school and high school. I took that avenue and listened to a lot of rap and hip-hop. But I always carried with me my love of rock and roll and never turned away from it. I remember being an adolescent and having to decide on what genre to really stick with. I was torn between the two, but my love of rock and roll always stuck to me. But rock and roll didn’t just always stick to me; I’ve always loved soul and R&B. There’s this guy named B.T. Express, that was a funk band in the 70s. I was sitting in my Uncle John’s garage just messing around with music, and he always told me, “Don’t stop singing.” I’ll be honest, in college, the business side was not really cool for me. I made a business decision and started doing road construction. I finished college and got a construction management degree, and that was just not my vibe. But I thought it was, and I wanted to make money. I said, “Okay, I’m going to switch lanes,” and became a pharmaceutical rep. I was about forty years old and I said, “You know what? I want to get back into this music thing, but I want to take it seriously.” So, I got a band together, we entered the Memphis contest, and we were the first Southern hip-hop act to advance from Memphis to the regional competition in New Orleans. And it was six hundred acts. We made it all the way to the top twelve in Memphis. I was like, “What’s next for me?” I mean, I had to be at least forty by that time, I’ve got two sons that are about to graduate high school and go to college, I’ve got a wife at home, and I’m thinking, “Man, what’s next for me?” but I remember watching and listening to artists from the 80s and 90s being part of that industry and thinking, “Man, if they can do it, why can’t I do it?” But as a business professional who happened to be a rap artist and a singer, I can easily retire and be an artist full time without having to worry about any kind of stress, but I didn’t see the love in it anymore; I lost that love. So, with music, I loved it. I loved the idea of wanting to be in music, and so it was at that time I didn’t have that energy to write, I didn’t have that juice in me to do this thing. But life was happening, too. Things happen. Life happens. My oldest son got in an accident, and because of that, I was able to focus on wanting to do what I wanted to do, and I was actually able to release that music. And for me, my music is very personal. It takes a lot of hard work and dedication to write a song. It takes a lot of courage to write a song and show the world who you are. My songs come from personal experience, and I really don’t have to do much to write a song. It just comes out of me. When I write my music, I can tell you about every emotion, and that’s the beauty of it. No one else can play my music. Nobody can replicate me. And when you write a good song, it hits everybody on some kind of emotional level, and the song becomes the soundtrack of your life. I do all of my recording and writing at my home. It’s my sanctuary, and I really enjoy the process of getting ready to put my next project together.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I am 57, of Scottish/Irish decent. I was born in Yonkers, NY and grew up on the south shore of Long Island. My parents were in the wholesale seafood business and as a result I spent my childhood fishing the Chesapeake Bay and Long Island Sound. That environment formed my appreciation for the inherent beauty of our marine environment. However, there were other influences that set the stage for my military career and my environmental advocacy later in life. As a youth I felt the profound uneasiness of a possible nuclear holocaust. The chorus of the peace movement was intensely heartwrenching to me and I can still remember hearing the familiar lyrics of “we won’t be fooled again” in my head as I sat on the beach fishing for striped bass at sunrise. I realize now that the song was later used as a theme song of sorts for the chemical lobby in a certain anti-environmental TV ads of the late 80’s and early 90’s. Nevertheless, I vividly remember the sense of foreboding I felt during those years. In my youth I was also cognizant of nature’s balance, a fact that I believe the staggering extinction rates of this era attest to, but which, as a nation, we have failed to take into account. While I cherish the outdoors and despise modern day lifestyle trappings, I have tried not to be against, but more concerned with, and vigilant toward those forces that would dominate our natural spaces as well as our lives, and to this end I believe there is an intimate connection between our personal desires, our lifestyles, and the health of the natural systems we rely on for survival.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I am 52, yes it is old for many, but I do not feel old. I was raised in a small town outside of Fort Worth, TX. I grew up on a farm. Yes, that means I grew up in a log cabin home. Grew up loving the outdoors, hunting, fishing, shooting and spending time with friends and family. Some say I was born into a gold mine, a warm happy home with loving parents and family. I believe my family is the reason I am who I am. And the events in my life do have a huge effect on who I am. I was originally a country kid and have loved the outdoors all my life. I fell in love with hunting and fishing at an early age and would spend endless hours in the woods, fields, creeks and rivers. Both a family tradition and a tradition passed on from a Native American. I lost my dad in 1999 and have been living an entirely different life style. I spend more time alone and have a stronger spiritual walk with the Creator. I feel closer to God and am at a more at peace with life than I have ever been. I am not an outdoorsman in the traditional sense. I have plenty of cousins and friends that are, but not me. I had a job that was convenient for me and where I could raise my family and work close by. Now that I am retired I spend a great deal of time with my hands in the dirt. I do very little hunting now and much more fishing. This is where I am most at peace. I get to spend all the quality time I want with my grandsons and their dad, my son, which I did not get to spend with my dad.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I am about to turn 60 years old. I grew up in a small farming community located in the northern part of Kansas called Hudson. My first memories are of the house we lived in and walking on the dirt roads leading to school. I liked school because of the friendships that I made. However, most of my friends lived on farms and when I was old enough to go with them I spent most of my free time either working on a farm or riding horseback. I can remember working behind combines, milking cows, feeding livestock, and throwing bales of hay. I can also remember riding horseback all day and coming in only when it got too dark to see. We would take a break for supper then in the evening we would clean out the stock tanks and then follow this up by loading two to three tons of hay into the back of a pickup truck. While I enjoyed working at this physical activity that my friends and I did my senior year in high school I developed a health condition that made it impossible to continue this. For the last several years I was out of school and diagnosed as a T-3 Quadriplegic. The physical issues that I developed was from my neck and my doctor says it will take about five or six years before I lose all of my motor skills and this will be compounded by 40-50 years of having diabetes and the beginning of diabetes dementia. However, I did get to see the world and most important get a chance to see the love and care of the children and others that donated to me and/or volunteered.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I am from a small town, Montague, Massachusetts. I am 57 years old. I am a grandmother with two great grandchildren. I enjoy the simple things in life; an afternoon breeze on my face, taking a nice long drive with the top down on my car, watching my dogs play. I enjoy the thunder storms, the fall foliage, the sunsets, my garden, and the serenity of the flowers blooming. I want a comfortable home where I can keep all my animals, and every family gathering I want to know that everyone there is safe and healthy, and as happy as they can be. I want my children to enjoy their home. I want my grandchildren to want to live next door to me. I want them to have a big garden to play in and to learn from, and to be close enough to their grandparents and aunts and uncles that they feel that closeness. And when they are at the house of their parents, my husband and I, they want to feel that safety and love like their parents are receiving. I want people to realize that it isn’t the place they are, but the people and the things that mean the most to them, that love them and they love in return that they always return to, or want to return to. I want to have a huge garden and a huge family, including friends. That makes my heart hurt less when my children and their children are out and about and I’m home. And it makes the joy of spending the holidays with them that much more joyous.

My children and grandchildren are my reason for being. They are my miracle. I wouldn’t trade them for anything. My past is my past. A baggage I carry, but no one else has to know about it. My life is mine to live and mine alone. It’s been a good life, full of hard times and good times, but in the end I got through it all.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I am a 50-year-old veteran who lives in the northwest with my husband and two dogs. I spent the past two years exploring this part of the country. We live in a very remote area, surrounded by wildlife and some very cool people. I have been traveling a lot and have met some pretty wonderful people, some who have been in the same area for nearly 30 years. I have lived in eight states during the past 30 years. I like to hike, read, and spend time outside. I learned to cook in my 20s when my husband and I were camping a lot. We eventually bought an RV and started living in it as we traveled. I grew up in a city and realized very early in my life that I had to make the best of what I had. My mother taught me to be grateful for what I had and to work hard to accomplish my goals. I worked in nursing homes during my first two years of college and later went to nursing school. As a single mother with two children and a full-time job, it took me eight years to get through school. My first nursing job was as a charge nurse on a double-unit ICU in Indianapolis, Indiana. It was a very stressful job, especially when we only had four nurses on duty and a full load of patients. I left that job and got into the health benefits field, where I worked for 14 years. I switched companies about every two years and got to know a lot of people in the healthcare and health benefits industry. I got to travel a lot for work and had a decent income. I quit my last job and got very sick. I spent most of the next two years sick in bed. I eventually sought care and was diagnosed with a chronic illness. I recovered but had some residual effects from the illness and the treatments I had received. I made a decision to take some time off and get well.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I was born in Orange, California on the campus of Western Seminary (a Bible school). A few weeks later, we moved to Southern California. I was baptized in 6th grade. Freshman year of high school, I began to really read my Bible and God’s Word began to take root in my life. College was more of the same. I am now 52 years old. I was the only male child of four. My dad was an engineer and worked on several Hollywood films as a stunt man. He was also a hot rod builder. He showed me how to weld and work on engines. I was a car guy at a young age and I still am. My mom was a stay at home mom who loves the Lord and served in church wherever needed. My older brother and two sisters all ran off to do their own thing. My family matters deeply to me. I have been married to my beautiful wife, Jen, for almost 30 years. I love Jen deeply and she has stood by me in a variety of jobs from owning an auto repair shop, to doing IT work for several non-profits, to working for a major government agency and now finally, as a writer, from home. I love Disney and superheroes. I love a good story.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am 56 years old. I grew up in a small town with its own identity called Laleham. Part of Laleham is Slough, which is famous for being the birthplace of Queen Elizabeth the Second. So, for those of you outside of the UK this might not make any sense. Then again, many of my international readers will find little of what I write that is relatable to them. None of us can write for everyone. Personally, I prefer to write about topics that I am comfortable with. I enjoy being out in nature and also working on my computer.

I will never write to please any group. If I could use one word to describe my self that word would be Misanthrope. I don’t hold with the idea that we should share the planet with other life-forms. I do consider dogs and cats to be our equals. But that’s as far as it goes. I am not a trained biologist. I don’t have any of the answers to the big questions.

Life feels to me like a horrible event. It was better before we were born. When we die, the nightmare will be over. Personally, I am not religious, though I understand that some are. I don’t feel any need to condemn them because they believe what they believe. I do though, feel a very deep concern about their state of affairs. I don’t believe that there is anything beyond what I refer to as ‘the Matrix’ and the better that I understand ‘the Matrix’ the better my chance of winning the game. By winning the game, I am referring to surviving long enough to be able to create sufficient wealth to pay for cryogenic preservation on my death.

This is something that I am deeply concerned about. Each year more people are born than are able to stay within the service sector. This can only mean one thing. They die before they are able to create enough wealth to provide for themselves. My long-term plan is to become the richest person on the planet. This should ensure that I have some chance of being able to afford cryogenic preservation.

The biggest problem for me is that you have to die to become cryonically preserved. So there is no guarantee. I have read about cryonics. The company I am looking at is Alcor. Which is in the USA. Some of the guys from my local casino have had their consciousness frozen there. They paid for it out of their winnings. I also know of a lot of people who have tried CryoEden in England and their families have had to put up their family home as security against a no-refund loan.

There are currently 190 companies offering cryopreservation of human tissue. All of them put up obstacles that force you to take out a loan. You have to get yourself cryopreserved if you want to continue to exist once your body has died. I don’t want to waste my life trying to save for the deposit required by these companies. I much prefer the idea of living in comfort for as long as possible before dying.

I could become a cryonically preserved brain in a jar if I preferred. But I want to enjoy some continuity from death into a cold storage facility. I want my feet to not be stiff and my mind to remain intact as long as possible before entering into a frozen existence.

So, will I find a solution to my problems? I don’t know. I am currently looking for work for the first time in the best part of 30 years. I don’t know where to start. I can’t afford to work for an employer. I am assuming that there must be an equivalent to the gig economy for brain-in-a-jar-businesspeople. I hope to find it soon and that it will be at least as comfortable as the casino that I used to run. I was kicked out for drug abuse by the casino owner. So I have nowhere to go back to. I am sorry, I should have remembered to tell you that I used to be the owner of a casino.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am 55 years old. I grew up in Africa and on the island of Singapore in the far east. It was an extraordinary environment for a child to grow up in. I am the youngest of four girls.

There were two specific things that have had the most profound impact on my life. First, was being a second generation medical student, which meant that there was never any doubt about what I was going to do with my life. I was going to be a doctor. At the time, all I could imagine was being a general practitioner. I had no understanding of the complexity and profundity of the science or of the speciality I ended up specialising in, pathology.

The other experience was the death of a sibling at a very early age. The emotional impact was enormous. It was something that fundamentally changed my understanding of life and death and informed my thinking about things at a very early age. I can’t think of anything that had more profound implications for my approach to life than the death of my sister.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I’m 53 and grew up in the town of Holton on a cattle farm. Holton is an unlikely setting for a young girl but it was a good place to grow up and I loved the place. My dad was born in rural Utah and my mother was from Chicagoland. She met my dad when she was in her junior year of college and headed off to Utah to start her career as a teacher and they courted long distance via letters written in pencil and paper. My parents were married and continued to live on the farm until my mother’s death at 50 in 1994. Her professional obituary is below as it was written by the small local paper in Holton.

She loved people, children, learning and life. As a result she was blessed to spend nearly half her life as a teacher in the field that she loved. At the peak of her teaching career she was teaching kids in kindergarten thru 6th grade in a small school district in a rural setting. She loved the mixture of children and always cared deeply about them and her ability to positively influence their lives. Through all that time she involved herself with the most important human institution in her life, and that was her family. She and my dad loved their children, loved being married, loved traveling to new places and above all loved being with each other. We loved her and will miss her greatly.

We thought of my mom as a modern day Mary Poppins, who was quick to be frivolous and slow to be strict. She made teaching fun, games and arts and crafts and shared her wonder and joy for life and learning with our family. Both as a daughter and as a mother I was blessed to have her as a role model of the kind of life that we could love to live. My mom was murdered in 1994 on a snowy Kansas road and that tragic event changed my life forever. What I’d seen in the funeral home before she was buried, went home and looked in the mirror and realized that I too was like her. I would never have thought that I’d grown up to look like her, but there we were both standing in the mirror looking with the same eyes and sharing the same memories. In a strange way the bond with her was tighter and stronger and more powerful in her death than in her life. She would have loved to laugh about that with me, and we both know now that we will, someday, somehow.

As for my siblings, I have a very intelligent but ADD older brother of whom I am very proud. He works as a vice president for a Denver division of United Airlines and is married with 3 girls, is very creative and talented at everything he does and I am very blessed to have him in my life. We have a younger sister that has always been a good friend and great support to me since my mom’s passing. The three of us will be together this weekend in Chicago to celebrate mom’s birthday, something that we’ve always done since her death. I’m grateful for all three of them.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I’m 55 years old. Born in Salina, Kansas. Grew up in Grand Junction, Colorado. I’m a legal Alien who became a US citizen when I was 46 and a dual-citizen for the last 8 years. I was always treated as an Outcast. Everyone was afraid of my difference. I went to school and worked. I continued studying Buddhism and looking for a scientific-based explanation of Consciousness. I lived a life of quiet, patience, and small rewards. I waited 46 years for what I got from you and my life is in harmony.

I don’t often seek, or find, like-minded people. I’ve been alone a lot. When they find me, I go silent or tell them that it’s taken 46 years for my life to make sense. I know it is the Dharma.
