I’m not perfect but no one else understands this. The Universe is alive with sensing, evolving, living creatures who are made from the same Elements as we are. The key is within, inside you. It’s your desire for truth.

We use a shower gel. I’ll ask her to examine all ingredients. She’ll see that one of them is derived from Lactic Acid produced by Lactobacilli & beneficial bacteria in a Petri dish. That is how most cosmetics and many shampoos, & shower gels are produced. There is a new form of lactobacillus used in cosmetics. It produces the antioxidant acetic acid which was until recently considered impossible by conventional science.

Oil of thyme is naturally antiseptic and produced by a fungus. All 3 of these compounds are derived from Organic Natural acids which protect us from parasitic growths on our skin that we absorb from the earth.

I didn’t choose cosmetics as a showcase for my Buddhist practice. I used them because there was no choice. When you know the ingredients and the process, you realize it doesn’t matter if you buy cosmetics or how much you pay. What matters is your Consciousness and how it’s changing while you’re brushing your teeth or washing your hair, and then your appreciation, respect and gratitude for your Body, Mind, & Consciousness that you take for granted.

The cosmetics industry is clean. They use bacterial growth to produce biochemicals for make-up and scrubs, hand and body creams, and body washes. In each of the 4 products on my vanity, my wife and I tested, 2 of the 4 ingredients were derived from bacteria. The 3rd ingredient in one product was harvested from the ocean and the 4th ingredient was harvested from plants in the ground. So, to be clean, we need to learn how to use it without killing the bacteria that protect us. Our Skin has a different Purpose. Lactic acid is an antibacterial agent used to kill bacteria and in our body it is transported around in the Circulatory System to every part of our body, to every cell to break down sugars. Science does not know why. The Secret to the human Body is in Consciousness. I’ll ask her if she has Good Conscience, Good Karma, and Good energy. If she says no, I’ll tell her, as I did my mother and others, to say thank you to the Universe for our bodies because they are all given to us. We didn’t make them. We didn’t invent them, or can we invent any thing outside the Universe. It is only from the Universe that we can form a sense of Compassion, an energy of Joy, a faith in a working Relationship. We each have a job to do.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am not a former military serviceperson and I am not a Marine. I am a 53 year old bald white guy that grew up in a country town of 1000 people in Kansas where we raise wheat and cattle. I am a university professor that specializes in political science. I was taught to never throw away my old notebooks. I have a few boxes and bags full of old notebooks where I have recorded snippets of ideas or questions that were interesting, but that may not have fit into a book, essay, or article at the time. After the incident at Pine Valley church I started to think about this personal world of mine as a very complicated and messy labyrinth that I had to somehow find my way through. Over the months following the incident I started writing things down about my life and my personal problems and my legal problems and all the other things that were going on at the same time.

I started writing long and detailed journals. I kept up this habit for several months. I then collected these journals into a stack and then into several stacks. I was careful to write completely and accurately. I included the time, date, and place for each event and the exact names and titles of all the people that were involved. This was a normal behavior pattern for me. I was simply following an impulse I had developed over the years. If you do well on a test, write down what you did in some detail so you can repeat it later. Don’t forget. Do better the next time. Be good at what you do. If you make a mistake, what you did right so you will be more likely to do it right next time. I thought that if the police wanted to arrest me for these awful crimes I had committed I would have my own testimony ready for them in writing. I thought it would save them some work.

I then transcribed these journals into a computer text file and printed it out and filed it away. I didn’t read it much over the next few years. There was more important stuff I had to do, more fights to fight, and more trials to run. All kinds of law-suites and legal problems going on all at the same time. Never a dull moment.

Several times I went back and re-read the material as I edited and cleaned it up. I tried to get it into publishable shape. I tried to make it into a book. It did not work out that way. The original material just did not fit together in a way that seemed natural or logical to my mind. I needed some help. I didn’t have any relatives I could talk to about this personal stuff. I didn’t have many friends. I had no psychologist or therapist or counselor or priest or Rabbi or minister or preacher. I talked it over with my wife, but I couldn’t bring myself to talking about it with some of the people I knew. They had too much invested in their own way of thinking and too much hope in their own expectations for me. I had hurt a lot of people with my actions and I was afraid that talking about my actions or my personal problems would only make them angrier, feel worse, and demand that I do things to make them feel better. I am afraid that my actions hurt those people too much. I am afraid I hurt those people too much.

I tried giving my stories to some law professors that taught criminal justice courses. I gave them several chapters of some of my journals and stories. I told them what the files were about and what they meant to me and what I intended to do with them. I asked if they would read them over and give me some feedback. Maybe their students could help me make sense of this. Maybe a professional in that field would know what I could do with this stuff. I offered to give them a free copy of my book if they would give me a fair evaluation of the material. At that time I really did intend to write a book. In fact I still have the same idea in mind. Someday I may get around to it. Anyway, I did not hear back from those professors. I never got my book published.

A few days ago I gave my book to a grad student at the university who was writing a journal article on computers and the law. I thought the article had a very strong point and asked him if he would read some of my stuff in the hopes that he would have some thoughts on how I could use my information. I gave him a copy of my book. He told me yesterday that he read the book and thought it was interesting, but it didn’t fit in with his research goals. I asked him if he thought it would make a good novel or movie and he said no, because you need too much plot to make it that way. He is a successful lawyer now and a good writer. I thought about it all weekend. I thought about what I could do with my material. I came up with a plan.

I don’t know if this will turn out to be right or not. I don’t know what I am doing. All I do know is that I need to do this for myself. I am not writing this book for money, or as a condition of some court order, or for any lawyer to defend me or to convince some judge to do something. I am trying to do this for myself. I don’t know what to call my book yet.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I’m 62, a baby boomer. I was born in Rock Hill, South Carolina. My parents divorced when I was a toddler and I moved with my mom to Sumter, SC. Sumter was an armpit but then it was my home and I loved it. I still love it. My mom left when I was about 10 and remarried and moved to Georgetown, SC.

The schools were ok in Georgetown, my friends were cool, but the humidity was oppressive, I felt miserable and was always cold. I had a great job on the shop floor at a company that made materials for airplane parts, but I was seriously bullied there. I was overweight and not in a good place physically or emotionally. In 9th grade I tried to kill myself.

So, I transferred to a military school, which was the best thing that ever happened to me. My greatest success in school came when I was there. I learned leadership, I got physically fit, I learned responsibility, duty, honor and all those things. I won scholarships to every college in the country. I went to the Citadel for a while and then transferred to Clemson and earned a degree in Accounting.

I’m a patriotic guy with family scattered all over the country and have always had a desire to go to where the fighting is. I learned to love this country and respect the people who defend it. For most of my life I worked in accounting or commercial trucking. After 9/11 I traveled to and from Afghanistan (logistics). But the heart of me is a soldier and I have never stopped pursuing my dream of military service, at a time when they’re too expensive to have unless there’s a serious need.

At some point I’ll have to leave to go back to the civilian world. This is a dream for me and I am prepared to support it. I am humbled and privileged to work with such good men and women.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I'm a 50 year old millennial with a terminal degree (at 39) in a nontraditional nonbusiness field. I've had many jobs in my field but I worked as a personal chef for a long time and, before that, I operated a group home for people with intellectual disability. I started as a counselor-in-training in high school and continued in that role until I was a full-time, salaried worker. I started as a weekend camp counselor in junior high and worked camps every summer up until I was in college. I took a weekend job at a party store that didn't pay enough. My father and stepmother had a pizzeria and, when I was an adolescent, I took over running the kitchen when my stepmother became ill. I went into chef training and worked my way up to Executive Chef. I worked in restaurants from a little dive near my high school, up through a small downtown restaurant to a spot in the historic district. My method of relaxation and self-development is self-teaching. I prefer books, online tutorials, and classes. I am a musician and a multi-instrumentalist. I am very self-sufficient in life. I've worked on everything from plumbing to automotives and general handy-man skills. I'm pretty handy. I'm friendly and I get along with most people. I'm outgoing and I like to entertain. I love to cook. Cooking is the ultimate process of sensual experimentation and discovery. It is, in essence, the pinnacle of self-knowledge.

So that's basically it. I have an inordinately interesting past with as many facets as any human being could have; too many to count, in fact, and even those I've only just begun to identify. My sense of humor is very on-point and I'm a quick-witted person with a perfect sense of timing.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I am 52 years old. I was born and raised in Indonesia in a big family of 9 children. I was born 2nd in the line. My dad was in the military and was sent to other locations throughout Indonesia. I was unable to finish college and decided to move to the US. I did not come to the US with the idea of settling down. I was only here on a visit. But after being here for 3 years, I ended up with a green card. Now I am 52 years old and have been here for 30 years. I worked for the government for 24 years as an accountant and 6 years as a Certified Internal Auditor. Then I became a Business Risk Professional. I have been doing real estate for 30 years and own rental properties and land. I had a 2nd job at night at Safeway when I first came to the US. I have a PhD in Business Administration and speak 3 languages: English, Bahasa Indonesian and Javanese. Now I am retired. I have been in love with America and have been to 47 states (I missed Maine). I have been a United States citizen for 5 years and am a happy camper. I have never been on welfare and I have never taken a penny from the US government. I would consider myself a hard working middle class American. I do however, disapprove of some US Government policies and I do have a few grievances against them.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I’m 52. I grew up a ten minute drive from my current address. I was a latchkey kid. In the 1970’s latchkey kids stayed with adult movie producers on location, or me. My childhood was much like many other kids my age. I enjoy nothing more than taking my dogs for a walk. When I can I walk my dogs for over an hour while I smoke. I also really like cooking. If I’m writing code, I get a snack. If I’m designing a website, I usually order pizza. If I’m talking to a professional customer, I usually go to a nice restaurant.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: My name is Cathryn Gallagher, and I’m thrilled to be at Hofstra. My passion is the teaching of writing, but I also love the publishing and editing work I’ve done in the past. I write poetry, fiction and literary criticism, and am the managing editor of Orbis International, a nonprofit organization that works to improve sight around the world. I’m 51 years old and live in the forest of Vermont, where I raise chickens and grow most of my own food. My work and personal lives are entirely intertwined in my life, but not always as neatly as you might imagine. Working with children in the community is immensely gratifying to me. I currently teach preschool at The Upper School for Integrated Studies in Burlington, where I get to spend four hours every day interacting with a group of children and observing what matters to them.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am 53 years old and was born and raised in a very rough neighborhood in Chicago. I grew up on the south side of Chicago in the Roseland neighborhood. I have enjoyed a successful career in law enforcement as well as the military for the past 34 years. I became a police officer after graduating college in the late 1980’s. I felt there would be more opportunity to advance in the field than other jobs I had considered. It was also close to my family so I would not be so far away from my mother. My father had left us when I was in high school and I did not want to be so far away from my mom. She needed me to help her. We did not have very much money so my stepfather did not allow me to leave to go to college. He would not even sign the paper to let me go. I had to figure out a way to leave home on my own. I was fortunate that I was able to go to college on a basketball scholarship. Even though I was not interested in a career in the military at the time, it was where I started. I was in the military for about six years before I applied to the police department. I joined the Air Force at 18 years old and was in the Air Force for just under six years before I transferred to the National Guard. I found my time in the service enjoyable and very rewarding.

I joined the Air Force at 18 years old and was in the Air Force for just under six years before I transferred to the National Guard. I found my time in the service enjoyable and very rewarding. After I had some experience in the Air Force, I became more interested in military life. I decided that I wanted to give back in some way, especially to other veterans. I was recruited as a police officer by our city’s first black police officer, Mr. Vernon Jones. He hired a lot of us who were in the military. This career change allowed me to not have to worry about being drafted into the military and gave me time to do the other things I wanted to do. It helped me become more familiar with the weapons I carried on the job in my military life. It also allowed me to continue to serve a noble cause. I had a lot of family and other family friends who served in the military. I felt that by putting my life on the line to serve my country, I could help carry on the family legacy. I wanted to be in a situation where I could do a lot of good, help a lot of people and serve a lot of people. Helping someone was one of the greatest feelings in the world. The first time I saw the face of a scared kid or the relieved look on the face of a father whose child was ok, I knew there was nothing better that I wanted to do.

I am a product of my neighborhood and all of my experiences growing up. My environment shaped my life. It was rough and there was always a lot of violence and people who didn’t have any hope. I saw so much violence and there was not a lot of family support. People would always yell at each other and a lot of the time their fears were justified. It was hard to stay away from all the problems, and not get caught up in it. I have memories that still haunt me even today. I also have good memories. I have had a lot of trials and tribulations, but I wouldn’t change anything. It helped shape my outlook on life.

I have had a successful career as a police officer. I have retired once from law enforcement but now I am back at it. I am able to do a lot more work in a more relaxed and secure manner than I did when I was younger. I am able to devote my time to helping other people who are struggling with their lives, and who have not had a chance to get the support they need. I help them get back on their feet. I also help them with any issues that are stopping them from advancing in life. There are so many ways that I can help others, it makes me feel good to do it.

My family has been a big part of my life. I have three children and four grandchildren. My wife and I have been married for 35 years. We have been living in our house for 20 years. We live right next door to each other. We are so close that it is like we are one family. I work in the neighborhood and go over to the house a lot. My kids live with us and we love it. I have a special bond with my grandchildren and they are so special to me. I love spending time with them. They are like my little kids and they call me daddy.

My children and grandchildren are a big part of my life. I love spending time with them and doing things with them. I love spending time with my wife and enjoy going out with her. She has been there for me through all the hard times.

Some of my best memories are when we go to the zoo and when I take my granddaughter to Disneyland. It makes my heart smile. I have always been so proud of her and proud to have her in my life.

I love spending time with my grandchildren and doing things with them.

I always knew I would be able to get back to being the person I used to be. It was like the floodgates had opened. I was ready to move on and have some fun. I am getting there slowly but surely. I have a lot of friends that I would like to see again. There are people that I have not seen since I was 16 years old. That is 30 years. There are people that I have not talked to for 10 years. I love to hear from them again. I want to share my life experiences with them.

I love sharing my life experiences with others. I love to hear their stories. I love to hear about their lives. I love to hear about the people that were in my life. I like to hear their lives and their journeys. I like to hear about where they are going. I like to hear about where they have been. It gives me something to talk about. I can always tell a story about the person. I love to tell their stories and it is usually from my perspective. My friends and family will tell me how their life was before they met me and what they are now. I can usually see the similarities between us and how they felt. I like to hear the stories and the stories of others. The stories are great. It makes me feel better and it gives me something to think about. It is like having someone to share their life experience with. I like that. It makes me feel better and it gives me something to think about. It is like having someone to share their life experience with.

I like sharing my life experiences with others. I like hearing their stories. I like hearing their life experience. I like listening to people talk about their lives and what they have been through. I like hearing the stories of my parents. I like hearing their stories and how they were able to go through their life. I like listening to their lives and their experiences. I like listening to the stories of my friends and family. I like listening to their stories and their lives. I like hearing their stories and their lives.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I’m 54 years old. I grew up in Fort Lauderdale. My daddy was a bank robber, turned sheriff. He eventually ended up in jail, where he died when I was nine years old. My mother died when I was three. I had an older brother and sister, but they were not my parents. My father and my biological mother did drugs. I got passed around to a lot of different relatives. I never saw my grandmother much, but my grandfather and my two uncles, Delmer and Dennis, were like brothers to me. As I got older, they gave me a choice of going to one of their houses or jail. I chose to go to a house, any house.

Anyway, I’m a pretty boring guy. I did the normal things like having a beer with my boys, or going out to have a steak, but I never cared much for it. When I was 18, I decided to do something different, so I enrolled in the Navy, just for a tour. Then my ship was going to head over to the Middle East. Being pretty young and wanting to see the world, I said, “sign me up!” So I spent the next year and half traveling the world and seeing things I thought I’d never see in this lifetime. That really got me thinking about life. I never realized how beautiful the world was. Like anything, it has its pluses and its minuses, but you’ve got to look at the bright side. I’ve tried to live that way for the past 34 years.

The one thing I’ve been very proud of is being good to people. I’ve donated blood and platelets and have helped raise money for cancer. I’ve always tried to help when I could. I know it might seem foolish, or I might even get laughed at, but I can’t stop thinking about how and why this happened to my brother. I could look at it as that’s life, or I could keep working to figure out what happened to my little brother. I’ve tried everything and spoken to everybody, but to no avail. So, I’m here for a couple of reasons. I want to know what happened to my brother. And I want to help bring justice to all the people the killer hurt, the people he left behind.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I am a vibrant 50 year old woman, who loves life and loves helping other people. I’m an only child, who was born in Georgia. I’m the only girl on my mother’s side, although I have several cousins on my father’s side who are girls. The boys have always looked after the girls and continue to look after me.

I enjoy fine wines, dinner with friends, working out at the gym, water sports, trips to the islands, just playing with my dogs, going for long walks, reading, spiritual activities, working in the community, spending time at church, watching movies, football (my favorite team is the Falcons), woodworking, home repair, cooking, and gardening.

The highlights of my life and experiences are being born and being able to spend most of my life in a loving home with a father who worked hard for his family, but did a very poor job of showing it, through the divorce that separated us into 3 family units and a mother who did the best she could to love us all and make sure that we had a wonderful life, to meeting the man of my dreams who I was married to for 14 years, to the day that I had a heart attack and called my husband to be with me, to a time that I wasn’t sure if I would live to see my children grow up and accomplish their goals and dreams. For all the fun times, the sad times, and hard work that I put in to help my children with their goals and dreams, I wouldn’t have changed a thing, because I have 2 loving children who are happy and successful.

Some of my best accomplishments are graduating from High School with honors, completing a year of college, and working hard at multiple jobs for 28 years.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I am 50, my family moved a lot when I was a child to get away from my mother’s many toxic relationships (It didn’t work), so I have lived in a lot of places and have met a lot of interesting people who have made me the person I am. I was raised mostly by my wonderful father, who taught me the value of honesty and who I can count on in a crisis. He also showed me the joy of savoring things, whether it’s a fine cigar, or an oil on canvas painting of a tropical sunset. What matters most to me is to be able to help someone in distress, to alleviate their suffering in whatever way I can. It is a personal failure if I cannot, because I am a confident person with a lot of skills and abilities that should be used for the greater good. This is my obligation to society, to make it a better place. It is my duty to leave the place better than I found it. In the end all we can take with us is the mark we’ve left on this world. What we’ve done to help others or what we’ve done to destroy them, either way we will be remembered for it. Even if it’s for bad things. Be careful about making enemies; you may die before they do. What keeps me going is that one day, I will put my feet up on the coffee table and enjoy a good cigar and think of all the things I did right. I will look back fondly on the years that passed. But until then, I must do what needs to be done in order to ensure a life worth living. This is not a dress rehearsal.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I'm a proud 54-year-old Puerto Rican man. I was born in South Bronx and grew up in the South Bronx with my family. We lived in a two-bedroom home with six of my siblings. My parents emigrated from Puerto Rico to the mainland in 1956. As a child, I was curious and ask lots of questions. I loved to read. I didn't get to enjoy sports when I was young due to my family's situation. However, I did get to enjoy football with my little league team, the Mighty Midgets. I attended elementary school at P.S. 1 in the South Bronx. I loved going to school. I met great people while I was there. I eventually started attending another school and performed well. I moved from elementary to middle school with Ms. Ramos from P.S. 1. She was my math teacher and also the step mom to one of my friends. My older brother, Victor J. Ochoa was killed at the age of 17, just two weeks after he turned 17. Two days after my brother died, we went to the hospital to clean out his belongings. I saw his hospital gown and his blood-soaked sheets. It was the worst and most painful experience of my life. I was completely devastated.

I believe I suffered some sort of Post Traumatic Stress Disorder as a result of the loss of my brother. I was very sad, depressed, and angry for many years. In high school, I tried drugs with my friends. I eventually got kicked out of my high school. I was enrolled at Intermediate School 70 and then attended Catholic Boys High School. I didn't perform well, so I was kicked out. I spent some time at a vocational school where I learned more about construction work.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: As of yesterday I am 58. My father joined the air force in 1940. I spent much of my childhood in England. We moved around with the air force. I like reading, history, jazz, most music, arts, and I have visited about 20 countries. I enjoy working with others to accomplish things. Anything interesting I will do my best at. I have a long history of working with others to accomplish things. I’m devoted to family and friends. I like 50s/60s/70s garage bands and old punk, and old french records. I enjoy operating machines. I don’t like any kind of fighting (including verbal fighting) and I don’t like lies.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I’m a man from New York, an avid Harley rider, bachelor farmer, and loyal friend and member of my country. I’m 52 years old. I’ll admit I don’t have many fond memories growing up in Brooklyn and Brighton Beach. I lived on that waterfront until I was 18. When my parents separated I split my time between them, so I went to school in both Midwood and Ditmas Park, mostly hanging out on the boardwalk with my buddies smoking cigarettes and sneaking beer from my parent’s fridge.

We got along well together, we’d roll through the strip and make as much noise as possible in our vintage motorcycles. A lotta people complained about our noise, but the truth is we brought a lot of people into those businesses. A lot of people know New York because of us. We had a picture in the Times every year. You’ll see us out on the road during holidays. Thousands of bikers all up and down the Strip every holiday. We know how to celebrate the sacrifices and endeavors of our nation. The Strip is proud of us.

As for me, I don’t live on the Strip now. I live and farm here in Natick on acres that I’ve saved up for in the last fifteen years. There’s a lot of upkeep that goes into the farm, but I got it all right where I want it now. I raise goats for cheese, pigs for my wife who enjoys pork roast, and sheep for warm sweaters. I got a small trailer outside my barn where I store my ’94 lowrider which I got by bein’ nice to my probation officer. If I lose my bike then I lose my home.

My neighborhood, my part of Brooklyn, has been going down the shitter since the military base shut down during the last Bush Presidency. People left in droves because we couldn’t afford to live anymore. So the area went gangster and drug-riddled. It’s unsafe to go outside your house alone after dark. I ain’t naïve enough to think this one dude is responsible for what’s been going on over there these last 5 years.

There’s one other thing that makes me who I am. My wife’s pregnant with our first kid, my first child, and I’m a nervous wreck. There’s always been lots of screaming and yelling at my house. I grew up with it, I try to stop it, and I will never let it happen to my children. I want my baby to have a good, safe, and secure upbringing. So I don’t leave for long periods of time. I stay with my wife and go to sleep every night next to the woman I love.

I know women ain’t ladies anymore. Feminism’s a lot stronger than what my parents’ generation had. Sometimes they ain’t even considered women at all. But that ain’t the world I want to grow up in. I want the kids to be able to say ‘I love you’ to their wives or husbands without feeling embarrassed or ashamed about it. When a person says ‘I love you’ to another, it should be the greatest thing in the world. I wanna leave that with my kids. That’s the only thing I care about, and I don’t think that’s too much to ask.

As for what I enjoy, I like to write. I write letters, blogs, stories, journals. I write about my experience in this world. I grew up in a world that taught me, in times of hardship, you gotta just sit down and write about it. You gotta get that shit off your mind. You don’t solve anything by complaining about it all the time, you solve a problem by writing your way through it and that’s what I do. I read too, most anything’s good to me. But I enjoy watching old B-movies with my wife on Friday night and drinking white wine.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I am now 50 years old, my early years were very complicated. My father had a violent temper so I used to run away from home and find refuge in the family of a friend. When I was 16, my father insisted on driving me to a boys’ gym, saying that I was going to be a boxer. That day, I went to class, but then I realized that there is a crosstown rivalry between one of the student gyms and mine. I was in a cul de sac so I decided that this was a good reason to continue boxing. I didn’t know what I was getting into, as it is a dangerous sport and I had very little support. After having worked very hard on my career, with the valuable aid of my coach, I won a bronze medal in the 1984 Olympics in Los Angeles. When I was a beginner in school, the last fight was against another boxer. We both got injured, we had crutches for two weeks and bandages for a month. I was very proud of the bronze medal I won and I decided to use it as my passport to finish school. I don’t like authority so I needed to jump right into the workforce. I worked as a hospital orderly and I was quite successful until I got into an accident that required surgery in my nose. I was told that this would affect my career. I decided to leave work and study to become a personal trainer. I also learned how to be a soccer coach. After a few years, I was asked to give a lecture to young people on how to deal with the pressure to win and the effects of violence in their life. I found it interesting and I was successful in my first lecture. I gave lots of talks in which I talked about self-esteem. I received a lot of thanks from young people and their parents. I also witnessed the poor nutrition of many kids. My dream is to work with kids as a sport nutritionist to provide healthy ways of living. I also want to work as a bodyguard and personal trainer to celebrities.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I’m 52 years old. I grew up in Oklahoma, home of the movie Twister. I did a lot of tractor-riding, rodeo-ing, cowboy-wearing, and horse-riding. (I used to have an equestrian center of my own and trained horses for people. One day I will again.) I have one son who is 17 and a girlfriend. I’m so happy to be in Colorado, and I absolutely love driving the Trappist Road up to Bear Mountain in Woodland Park. I love the drive, and I love the view of the mountains from the summit.

My natural gregariousness serves me in the world of “sales” or offering people services. It has been interesting to explore all the things that go into the idea of “sales” and all the things that come with it, such as uncertainty. Many people are afraid to be “pushy,” but I feel like I’m not that person. To me, selling is about listening to what the person wants. It’s really simple. (What a relief!)

I’m starting to realize I’m somewhat selfish. I actually have a wonderful fear of traveling. It has to do with the thought of not being able to control my environment. And when I’m scared or stressed, I don’t like to try new things because I’m afraid I might not be good at them. I need to work on this. I just have to be who I am, even though it takes work sometimes.

My life has been interesting to say the least. I grew up in a volatile home. My parents were married four times between the two of them, and each of them had three kids. I have one sister who was actually my half-sister, so I was a “foster child.” When I was four years old, my father left, and my mother was left to raise four kids. My mother had a hard time providing for us. She moved from place to place, so we lived in shelters at times, a travel trailer, and even a tent, which was actually kind of cool. My mother worked hard to support us.

I remember she worked at night and slept during the day. She was forced to rely on food stamps to make ends meet. I remember eating tons of “taco-soup” with crackers. The joy of this soup was the toppings—sour cream and lettuce. I still remember how good it tasted.

I remember being in first grade and I looked around at all the kids in the class. I thought, “None of them look like they go to my house.” I had no real friends. It’s hard to make friends when you’re moving around and have no stability. I remember wondering when my mother was going to find a man to be our new father. She dated a few guys, but most were abusive to us.

I started taking jobs at a young age because my mother didn’t have the money to help us. I started out selling the morning newspaper (remember the Saturday Night Post?). I remember standing outside waiting for customers to come to the car window to buy papers. One day I fell asleep because I was so bored. A kid threw a rock and hit me in the head. My head was bleeding, but I didn’t tell anyone. I was still so shy, so I just sat in the car and pretended nothing happened. A month later, a boy named Jeff led me on a tour of the neighborhood, and it was there that he beat me unconscious. I didn’t have anyone to turn to.

In middle school, I got involved in drugs and alcohol. I was on the pill by the age of 14. I remember being passed out in the shower and my mom had to knock me out so she could take my clothes off. Then she had to drive me home because I was too drunk to walk. It was embarrassing and it was sad.

I was raped. I was held down by two boys and raped. One of them ended up in the newspaper for another crime. I wasn’t shocked because I had already had so much tragedy in my life. It wasn’t a surprise to me.

I ran away at the age of 16 to see the world. I thought there was a bigger world out there than the one I was in. I ended up in a mental hospital, and I realized how dysfunctional my home life was. It was there that I started to wake up.

I started seeing a psychiatrist, and he was the first person to tell me that I had depression and anxiety. I got on the medication that helps people with those conditions. I still struggle with this every day, but it is so much better than it was when I was young. I’ve learned that when I’m feeling anxious or depressed, I can deal with it better.

I tried to commit suicide in high school, and I had all sorts of weird ideas to commit suicide, like drinking drain cleaner. It didn’t end up working out, but it was the thoughts of doing it that helped me in my early life. I learned how to cope with these thoughts and not act on them. My parents got help for me. (I have been on medication for over 25 years.)

What I learned is that our families and our upbringings have a huge impact on our lives. I’m very aware of my shortcomings as a parent. But now, I don’t blame my mother for what happened. I just learned how to be a better parent for my son. And I still want to find love and have a life.

What makes me so passionate about yoga is that it’s my outlet. Yoga helps me calm down and helps me relax. (I’m a fighter, so I love to be mad and aggressive. I have a hard time being in touch with my soft, feminine side, but I know it’s there.)

I like to be around other people, and I love to help people. I love to listen to people’s stories. I love to see them accomplish their goals. I love to be a teacher, a healer, a student, and an explorer. That’s what yoga is to me. It’s everything I want and need. And there’s always more to learn.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am 52, born and raised in Portland, OR. My family has owned the floral shop, Stevens & Son Florist, for over 75 years. I learned to work hard early in my life and value the work that I do. My education and upbringing instilled in me that being passionate and committed to the work you do is very important. In fact, my grandpa always told me, “You only do something once in life, so you might as well do it well!”

I am a mother of three beautiful and intelligent daughters and a wife to a very supportive husband. A few of my passions are my kids, eating, drinking wine, learning about the history of Northwest wines, attending the opera, being a little spoiled, and travel.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I live in Metro Atlanta but was originally from the Eastern U.S. No particular reason. I am a 58 year old Navy Veteran who served during the end of the Vietnam War. In my mid teens I went to Church when I felt like it. It did not affect my life enough for me to go every Sunday or any other day. My mother was a Christian who went to church at least twice a week. My father was a non-religious person who did not drink or do drugs. My two sisters did go to church, and we, the 3 of us went to church together every Sunday for several years at around the age of 13 to 16. My mom basically gave us some notions about Jesus and God and we learned a bit in Church, but that is the extent of my Church education. I like history and read a lot about military history. I find it interesting to read about technology and the different kinds of military technology, but I also like reading mysteries and suspense books. I really enjoyed the "Pendergast" series of books by Preston and Child, though they are really fantasy (the main character is an agent for the FBI, but he possesses a genius IQ and is also very athletic and possesses psychic abilities). I learned about camping and the outdoors when I went to the Military. I enjoy activities such as camping, swimming, fishing, hiking, etc. We do not get to do those activities that often now, but I plan to do more of that when I have the means to get outdoors and do some activities on the weekends. I also like to travel. I like going to New York City, New Orleans, and Los Angeles.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I grew up in West Seattle and graduated from West Seattle High in 1986. I'm 52 years old now and I am a 2nd generation Italian. My great grandfather immigrated from Italy in 1904 and settled in West Seattle near Othello Park. I live now in Seward Park and enjoy the parks and the summer sun there. I really enjoy the outdoors. I like walking in the woods, playing in the streams and lakes, swimming in the ocean and just taking my dog for a walk in the neighborhoods near my house. I like to watch movies, especially comedies. I love eating in little Mexican restaurants and fixing dishes with jalapeño peppers, cilantro, onions and limes. I really enjoy being around people. I feel most like myself when I'm with people I love, laugh and relate to. I'm not a great conversationalist, but I'm interested in a lot of things and like to learn new stuff. I grew up in a community that was very accepting, open-minded and embraced equality for all races, religions and sexual orientations, so I guess that's why it was such a shock to me when the marriage ruling came down. I grew up in a loving community where people of different races were always coming together and celebrating each other, but I guess now I'm seeing that there are still some pretty ugly folks out there who have no regard for others. Most of the people in my life are so much better than the ones who want to take away other people's freedoms.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I grew up in Ohio, I’m 51, and as I grew older and our family moved around a lot, I soon realized that in order to understand someone else, you need to understand where they are coming from. It was also the beginning of “managing” people in different cities and different situations. I have seen good leadership, as well as really, really bad leadership. Through my life I have seen peoples true colors show… and to be honest, it wasn’t always pretty, especially when people felt threatened. So as a young lad I saw the inner workings of people and businesses, and realized you can get further in life by learning from the mistakes that others have already made. After college I took a couple of years and worked in a number of different industries and although I gained the ability to analyze problems and find solutions, I found the greatest growth was when I decided to go back to school and got into Engineering. I discovered I loved problem solving and finding the inner workings of systems. It was a short time later when the realization hit me that we are social beings with a lot of different variables and when it comes to problem solving and systems, people are the most difficult variables to work with, and also the most important. So I decided to seek out a career in Human Resources and would soon discover my weakness. I never thought I could manage people or help them understand how to work better together. It became my desire to grow and become a better manager, leader and mentor. Fortunately, I found a very supportive management group that gave me an open door to help others through difficulties and assist them in finding a solution through common sense and empathy. It helped me grow as a person and I hope I can say I became the Leader that I always wanted to be. Through that position I also had the opportunity to be a coach in many sports and the ability to witness the application of good leadership and teamwork and to also see how a lack of it can destroy an organization. So, in short, I learned it doesn’t matter if you have it all figured out, sometimes you have to go and find out what you are going to do with the skills you learn on your journey. Over the years, I took a different turn into the warehousing business and soon grew into 3PL work and founded a company with my partner in 2008, building it from the ground up and have been growing it ever since. I haven’t looked back since.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I’m 55 and I grew up in Buenos Aires, Argentina. My stepfather was a swimming coach and I grew up by the pool. As a kid, I learned how to read at the pool, skip stones, draw, play ping-pong, tango, and write. My teachers used to call me the Renaissance boy. I loved insects, and my grandmother supported my dream of becoming a doctor when I was ten, when we lived in the US for a few years. I became friends with bugs and worked on a patent on how to put tiny pieces of metal on medical instruments as a child.

I also studied musical composition and knew that I wanted to be a film composer when I was fourteen. Every Thursday, I drove my mom’s, Fiat 128 to the movies to watch films by Akira Kurosawa, Fellini, Ozu, Bergman and many others.

During high school, I played piano for movies at the cinema because they had lost their musician. At 15, I was already a veteran and I used to play the movies in various theaters across the city while watching the movies.

What I enjoyed the most then was meeting new people and making them laugh. I was never athletic. Sports were not my thing and besides playing piano, they would have put me in the orchestra instead. In college, I went to several U.S. universities in order to study film composition. The only problem was that I couldn’t fit in and was very depressed. I had worked so hard to be able to travel to the U.S., but eventually left.

When I came back to Argentina, I worked as a book illustrator. I studied 3D animation and worked as a visual designer at a computer animation studio. Then, I went back to Argentina and became a film composer. Still depressed, I worked on getting a Ph.D. in computer animation and later, a Th. D. in law, both of which I finished and still miss being a musician more. I’ve been married for 30 years and have two children. One of the pictures that I have in my study is of my grandmother, celebrating her 100 birthday.

My grandmother was an artist. She specialized in Chinese brush painting and was very proud of it. Her father had been a prisoner of war, and my grandmother lived a life where she faced sorrow and loss. Still, she was deeply interested in the world and had a deep curiosity about life. We had in our home a wall filled with all sorts of curiosities. Everyone who was anybody visited us, from musicians, professors, scientists, artists, etc. She was very playful and worked as a nurse. She was very funny and kind. I loved visiting her. After she died, I kept her gold wire earrings. My grandmother would do the most amazing things with brushes. In the evening, we would say “good night” to her painting. We would all stand before the painting and say good night to it. Then, my father would switch off the lights. Every night, we would say “good night” to the painting. When I was small, my grandmother asked me why she was on death row, and since I was small, I asked her, “Why are you on death row?” She said that she was not, so I asked her what it meant and my grandmother started to explain to me the history of prisons and death row.

She always encouraged me to learn and so, at the age of nine, I had memorized the entire New Testament of the Bible and taught a workshop to all the eight and ninth graders, analyzing the Beatitudes. The fact is that I always worked hard and I enjoyed learning. Sometimes, it was hard to know which study I enjoyed more. I like to think that I am extremely curious about things, and I like to read deeply.

My thesis on music law in the digital era was short in pages, but deep in content. It is also available to all on my website. I don’t believe in holding content away from people. I have spoken at Harvard, Oxford, and Yale among other universities on different topics. I have published a few dozen articles and I published three books for every thesis that I have written.

It is not just what you read but how you read it. We live in a complex world and need to be able to think deeply and respond effectively. It is clear to me that in the past, people read mainly to be informed, while today we need to read to be able to exercise our sense of judgment. What do I enjoy reading? I like to think. This world requires more of that. What I am truly interested in is deep learning. Is it wrong to say that? I suppose that it is if I want to speak like a salesman. I read extensively to be able to know things. Most people don’t know much and are unaware of much and live in a state of blissful ignorance. We have to read to know what is happening. People are often told not to read anything negative. If we do this, we will all go astray and do nothing. The same happened in Europe during the Middle Ages when we burnt all books to avoid knowledge.

What matters to me is really what is going on in the world and what makes a difference. Am I interested in what the Chinese are doing in Africa? Absolutely. When I read about it, I learn. Now, people will probably think that I am paranoid or smart, but I read to know.

I read, for example, all the articles in the science section of “Nature” so I can have a basic understanding of what is happening in the world. I learn a great deal. I have read that knowledge is just information gathered to learn that which is unlearnable. What I learned recently is how to analyze political discourse by analyzing its words. Some words indicate structural polarization and then, it is all very sad when we realize that our democracies are all failing. The bigger danger is not fascism; it is rampant political polarization.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I’m 57 years old, born on my father’s 50th birthday. I lived with my parents until I got married when I was 23 years old. We later divorced when my son was 8 years old. After I got the first job with Verizon, I obtained my degree in communications because I realized I had a passion for teaching and speaking. I believe my past experiences have made me who I am now and my future will make me someone else.

As for my hobbies, I enjoy movies, especially the suspense movies that make you feel things, feel life. I enjoy traveling. So far I have been to places in Europe and Asia. I enjoy speaking to people and listening to their stories. I am attracted to people who have passion in their lives. I feel the inner struggle of people and the sheer drive they have to succeed. I love the challenges I overcome in my life and that passion is what motivates me to learn about life and experience the full spectrum. A specific movie, for example, is one called “El Cid”. It’s an older movie but it does not age with the years. I watch movies to see how life was back in the day, and how it’s still relatively the same today. I watch out of curiosity and because I like a good story. I like to read books that teach me something. Life is beautiful if you don’t take it too seriously. I am passionate about my son and my two grandchildren who are my entire world.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I was born 15 miles north of this building, here in downtown Boston in December. I'm 56 years old. I graduated from Harvard College in 1985. I was an undergraduate here at Harvard. I've spent most of my life in Boston. I've spent about 15 years in Washington, DC. I started off as a street kid and then became a social service provider in the inner city; I worked in a drug rehabilitation program and then in a homeless program. I went to college in the evenings at Northeastern University. I got my degree in 1985. I've worked for a wide range of social service organizations; the Jesuit Volunteer Corps in New Orleans and in Springfield, Illinois; then I worked for a homeless shelter and then as a drug counselor at the Roxbury YMCA. I've worked as a teacher at Inner City, a program in Boston which served high school dropouts. I've worked for Youthbuild, which is a non-profit vocational program in Dorchester and in Dorchester.

In 1991, I was elected to the Boston City Council. Since that time, I've served two full terms there and I'm now serving my third term as a state representative. I've enjoyed my work as a legislator a lot. I'm a rank and file member of the legislature so I don't have a leadership position. I've served on a number of legislative committees and commissions, especially the Massachusetts Energy Facilities Siting Board. I'm the chair of that. I spent six years on the Joint Committee on Children, Families and Persons with Disabilities. I'm now chair of the Public Health Committee, which takes up issues of health care and public health issues. I was also elected in 1998 as the Democratic City Council president for Boston, which was a nice honor for me.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I am a 54 year old man who was born to a single mother in Florida. We moved around a lot and I grew up on lots of different military bases. I decided that I wanted to become a doctor when I was about 5. I could not describe to you why. I knew that people would look up to me and that I could save lives. I finally decided that I was not going to be a medical doctor because I did not like the thought of losing a patient on my first surgery. After seeing my father run from home when I was 8 and his young wife (my mother) I understood that I wanted to pursue a career in law enforcement. I loved history and society so that made it an easy decision to become a cop. I pursued that goal in earnest and completed the physical part of the cop test but I eventually got bored with my role as a dispatcher because there wasn’t a whole lot of physical action and I wanted to be a foot cop. I eventually joined the local police department and worked there for 6 months until I quit and started a mobile patrol business with my mother. I did that for 6 months and sold it to my mother. I was active in it for about 18 months so I was at my peak during the hurricanes of 2004. I learned from that that a true company must be constantly learning and adapting because the definition of a mob is a group of people without a leader. I started the IT companies that I have today and recently exited from a software development business that I started and raised millions of dollars for. That business allowed me to live in beautiful places with amazing women all over the world. I am now finally coming back to the US to make some real money for the first time.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I am 55 years old, I live in the south of Italy in Taranto, on the Adriatic Sea, and I am at peace with who I am and my story. I grew up in the countryside between Venice and Trieste, in the most northern region of Italy, so I have always had the sea and the mountains on my doorstep. I grew up in the countryside and my father was a successful entrepreneur in the building sector. I was a hyperactive child who, after a period of delay in my academic studies (due to an innate desire to experiment and discover), managed to become a medical doctor, specializing in ophthalmology. Later, I understood that the competition and hierarchy of medicine were not for me, and so I studied nutrition and became a nutrition consultant. So far, I have written 5 books and have published more than 200 articles, some of which have been translated into English. I am a mom, which is my most beautiful role.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I am 60 years old. When I was 6 years old I immigrated to Los Angeles. My parents came here with just $25. I grew up very poor, but I've always had an adventurous spirit. I don't like authority figures, and that is what drew me to computers. I've never been good at following rules, and I'm an individualist. I was trained as a mechanic, but when I was a teenager I was befriended by people in the underground computer industry. They gave me jobs cleaning out the trash at computer stores, and showed me how to take computers apart. As soon as I could do so I started stealing parts. Once I was caught trying to take a computer, and the police talked me into letting them search my home. I cooperated, and then my parents helped me run away. I've been on my own since then. Computer parts are easy to steal, and I discovered I could be a professional in a few days just by taking some stuff from the computer store and trying to sell it. To prevent theft from the store it is simply a matter of being smarter and faster than the other people who steal. I can type 100 words per minute, and I'm also trained as a computer programmer. I was tempted to go back to mechanical work, but I got in trouble one night because I threw away a used oil filter. I only did it because I saw a machine on TV that could recycle the oil. It got me thinking that nobody wants to make things anymore. I started to think that maybe my dreams weren't all that unrealistic.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: Well I am 54 years old and born on the Hawaiian islands but not raised there. Hawaii is my birthplace but not my home and the environment was not the most healthy to raise my children. I like to think that I am a music lover, an all-around handyman and also very much love animals. My favorite animal is a horse and has been from the first moment I saw them. I come from a very big family and yes there was lots of turmoil and abuse. My brothers have a lot of things in common, and I learned a lot from them. I am a happy person who wants to have fun but also has a very strong work ethic.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: The question of who I am, can I answer in few simple words? I think not, I am over 50. I was born in the small country of Jamaica, came to America at age 12 and grew up in a section of Brooklyn New York known as East New York. I love music and grew up in a musical family, my dad was a night club owner. I have learned from both my mother and dad. I got my morals from my dad and my principles from my mom. I am a mix of both and if I wanted to be great I had to choose one over the other. I chose my mom. East New York where I grew up was home to heroin and just about anything else one can name. I made a vow to never put drugs in my body and I have never, I became street smart and earned the streets respect at the age of 13. By the age of 19 I ran a small business with over $1.5 million dollars a year. If I do my part in this world and for my country I will live a long and happy life. I enjoy friends, conversation, coffee, the warm embrace of my wife, bike riding with my kids, classical and smooth jazz music and jazz fusion, movies, dinner on the weekends, and my kids will love to hear me say going on a family cruise, family is what really matters to me. I also enjoy traveling on my own and teaching myself all I can about the Bible, I learn something new every day and with that I keep finding myself and enjoy the journey.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am a 50 year-old single woman. I grew up in a rural and small city area of the United States. My mother was a typical traditional southern housewife and my father was a small business owner. My mother was one of five siblings and my father is the eldest of nine siblings. I was not raised religious at all, but am more traditional and conservative. My father died in 1995 and I have been estranged from my mother since 2006.

While I do not believe in labels, I hold traditional conservative values and believe that people should take responsibility for their actions. I believe in “what goes around comes around”, so if you are good to others, the universe will be good to you and you can expect a good life.

My father worked hard in an inefficient business. He had his own stresses, though they differed from mine. I am most proud of my accomplishments and that I have shown the fortitude and perseverance to achieve my goals. While I am not where I thought I would be at this stage of my life, I am grateful for my successes and look forward to more. I have a granddaughter and my mission is to raise her to have self-confidence, be independent, compassionate, and be a force of good in the world. I enjoy reading (various fiction genres), crafting, volunteering in the community, raising a granddaughter, and spending time with my mother’s sisters. I am a perfectionist and an extremist. When I have goals, I am all in and want to do things perfectly.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I am 52. I was born in Gargazı, Turkey. My mother and my aunt share many pictures of me from a young age, exploring the streets of my hometown. When I look at those pictures, I can see Gargazı in the palm of my hand. I can feel its narrow streets, its huge piles of waste, and the thickness of its air. It is quite clear from my clothes that our family was poor, just like most of the people in our town. My mother is a single mother. She has been taking care of my brother and me since my father left us. It wasn't easy for her to bring us up. It was hard to afford groceries. We spent most of our days at home. Because my mother worked in the fields, she would come home after work. When I think about my childhood, I recall the hardship of being poor, but I don't remember it as an experience of suffering. Instead, I have wonderful memories of playing in the courtyard, speaking Turkish with my relatives, and listening to the stories of my grandmother. I wasn't an introverted child. As a little boy, I was always excited to visit my grandmother. She would entertain me with her endless stories. Once a week, I would accompany my mother to the fields. She would collect tomatoes and potatoes. I would lend a hand in the collection. I would also participate in the farming process. When we came home from work, I would prepare food for my mother. My family knew that there would be no rest for me, but they would let me work. They did it so that I could see that there are people in this world who have less than us. It is because of these childhood memories that it is very important to me today to make sure that people who have less than me are also given a chance. My children have been born and raised here in the US. My son was born in North Philadelphia. My daughter was born in Queens. In recent years, I have spent a lot of time helping them with their education. This is why I strive so much to be a good role model for my children. My daughter has already begun to do something good for her community. She started a club in the 4th grade, and it has already earned the merit award from the New York City Mayor. It's all about this age that I was helped by strangers to make the most of my time. I would work hard to study on my own and to help others in my community. I worked on that. I would study in the evenings, I would cook and clean during the weekends, I would do business when I had time, and I would give money when I could. I spent my life knowing that every minute of my day was important. I have dedicated myself to education, to entrepreneurship, to service, and to academia. Throughout my career, I have demonstrated an ability to carry on my education, while maintaining a high level of service. For example, the New York State Board of Education approved my application. I am confident that I am the right person for this position. I feel that I am qualified for this role because I have been in the financial field for many years, and I know how important it is to apply to my current position. I know how to work with multiple personalities. I understand the needs of customers, and I know how to adapt to new situations. I am passionate about customer service, and I see this as an opportunity to serve the community in a meaningful way. I am someone who loves to explore new technologies. It is no surprise to me that I have a long term plan to continue my studies, and that I plan to work on a degree in political science. It is important for me to understand, communicate, and act upon political issues. If I am appointed to this position, it would not be the first time that I would be working as a professor at any other institution. Instead, I would serve the community in a meaningful way. I am excited to be able to serve the community in a meaningful way. I am excited to be able to serve the community in a meaningful way. I am excited to be able to serve the community in a meaningful way.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I’m 58 and I was born in Chicago. What made me who I am is my upbringing by my parents and my childhood in Chicago and my high school years at New Trier and my move to Los Angeles. My move to LA has also been a major part of my life, as has the shows I’ve been involved in, both in Los Angeles and in New York.

I grew up in Hyde Park and was raised by two Jewish parents. I was an only child. My father was born in Chicago and my mother was born in New York. They moved to Chicago in the ‘30s and he worked for his father who was in the textile business. My mother was a hat maker and a milliner and my father was in the textile business. When I was little we lived on Waldron Avenue and it was a pretty diverse street. Most of the families were Jewish, a couple were not. I did not grow up Orthodox, nor Conservative, nor Reform. I was brought up a Cultural Jew. I did not observe the Sabbath, but we were Bar Mitzvahed and we went to synagogue on the High Holy Days.

My parents used to take me to an evening screening of old movies every Saturday night at the Art Theater in Hyde Park. My dad would take me to old movies at the Parkway in Chicago and we would drive down to see Double Indemnity and Laura and all the wonderful old movies. I used to get to see all the great movies because they were $1.25 at that time, a nickel more if you went at night.

My parents owned a corner grocery store and I used to hang out in that store and that is where I got my fascination with movies. I would read the books of the movies that came out in the 30s and 40s. I’d be so intrigued with the titles of the films and what they were about and then I would try to follow them by following the characters in the films and they were usually movie magazines like Photoplay and Movie Life.

We always had a record player and records of Louis Armstrong and Tommy Dorsey and Duke Ellington and Stan Kenton. That’s the kind of music that we heard in my house. We also had classical music. I wasn’t into rock ‘n’ roll until the Beatles came out and then I discovered rock ‘n’ roll, which was kind of a no-no in the Jewish households at that time.

We were not religious, but my parents sent me to Hebrew School and Jewish summer camp and I had a Bar Mitzvah. My father belonged to a Yiddish society. The first movie that I remember seeing that affected me was The Good Earth, which I saw when I was 10 or 11 years old. It was about these Chinese peasants and I just found it fascinating and I was so moved by the poverty in that film. I was so impressed with the great actresses that played the Chinese women.

I saw it at the Drake Theater, a big theater on Roosevelt Road in the city. That was my first big movie experience.

The first adult movie I saw was Sergeant York. I was 13 when I saw that movie and I remember being moved by it. But it took me a while before I really knew that movies could make me cry. That was 1939. It was my first movie in a movie theater. I saw a lot of movies with my father, who liked movies like The Seventh Veil and Mr. Chips and old movies like My Man Godfrey. I used to watch Abbott and Costello and Laurel and Hardy and all the old films that he loved and then we’d see movies like The Wizard of Oz and Gone with the Wind together.

I used to sneak in and watch movies on Chicago TV. There was a channel that showed nothing but Hollywood movies all the time. We’d always get out a script from the library and take turns with the lines and all of that. We did that as kids.

At that time the big stars were Ronald Colman and Bing Crosby and John Wayne and Jane Wyman. Those were the stars that I was interested in. That’s what I was into as a kid.

My mom made all of our clothes in those days, as did all Jewish moms. And she used to cut out all the actresses and I remember she had cut-outs of Jean Simmons and Olivia de Havilland and Ginger Rogers and all those people. I used to cut out all the male stars and we had them all over our bedroom wall. Every Sunday afternoon she’d cut out all the celebrity photos and it was all of those people like Errol Flynn and Dick Powell and all those old people.

My family moved to Northfield, Illinois in 1955. I started high school at New Trier in Winnetka in 1956. I got on the second or third level for the freshman theater group. I used to go to the movies every Friday night. I was going to Chicago to see movies on a regular basis. I saw that first Broadway play, My Fair Lady, when I was a senior in high school and I remember I went to the show on a Saturday night with a date.

I went to Northwestern University and I did a play there and I acted there for a while. I graduated from Northwestern in 1968. I remember the night that the Democratic convention started and all of us were riveted to the TV to see what was going to happen. That was an exciting summer. We went up to Wisconsin for the Badger’s game and my father drove us. We were on the radio with a show about Northwestern sports. That was the summer of 1968. I wanted to get into show business. I did a couple of plays while I was at Northwestern and then I went into show business right away.

I was 21 years old when I graduated from Northwestern in 1968. I moved to Los Angeles in 1970. I moved out here with my girlfriend at the time. She’s a publicist and I came out to show business. I worked in the mail room at a talent agency for about two years. I was going to do comedy. I was writing comedy. I met a lot of people doing comedy in LA. I remember when David Steinberg came out to do a show and he brought with him Barry Levinson and Neil Simon. I knew them both from LA. They were coming into town with this show called The Secret Life of Dave Steinberg.

I started working in comedy and going out and meeting people and then I went into drama. I was into acting. I was going to acting class and I was out there working as an extra on TV. I had no idea that it was going to go the way it did, that I would be in this business all of these years. I moved to New York in 1977. I thought I was going to be a stage actor and I was doing a lot of plays. Then I started doing shows in New York and I started auditioning for TV and film. I moved to New York and then I moved back to LA.

My first TV role was in a little play on “Nurse.” I was the drunk doctor on that show. I don’t remember much about it. My first episode on a TV show was The Love Boat. I was on this one called I Love You, Daddy.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I’m 64, born and raised in Cleveland, Ohio. I worked as a computer professional for over 35 years, have 2 grown children, I love the outdoors (sporting clays), and I enjoy woodworking and traveling with my wife. I love to kayak and this past summer we did a trip of 125 miles from Erie, PA down the Allegheny and French Creek River to Delaware.

I want my guns, I’m prepared to defend them, I am hoping to actually use them before the doors of hell are opened, and that America is not transformed into a Communist country.

I enjoyed your recent responses, the way you handled with grace and dignity so much hate from the anti-gun movement. I can’t believe how delusional some of those people are. However, your comments about the Internet make me wonder if you are aware of your own contradictions. In two posts you repeatedly (and even disingenuously) referred to “illegal” guns used in crimes. You expressed your hatred for assault weapons and stated that “they don’t need them for hunting or for defense” and that you use you weapons for “hunting or defense.” You even described a single year when half of all guns involved in crimes were traced to crimes in the previous year. I’ll try to ignore that you didn’t offer a source for that statistic.

But you did take shots at the NRA, yet made no mention of the country’s leading anti-gun group, the Brady Campaign. The Brady Campaign admits that it has “no evidence that the vast majority of gun owners – responsible, law-abiding people – have had their guns used in crimes or violence” — yet it continues its campaign to ban “assault weapons” and get guns “off the streets.”

You also admit that “Gun controls schemes will have a negative effect on this country. No one needs those things.” Apparently you mean that no one needs a firearm made with certain features that Democrats have recently labeled “assault weapons” — features that have been lawful for many decades and which are irrelevant to the firearm’s functioning.

When has the United States government made laws so that we do not need something? That is not the role of government, unless you are proposing that the U.S. government should own everything and that Americans should simply rent anything we need, provided the government decides we need it.

Last time the anti-gun crowd had its way on “assault weapons,” it banned some models for more than 10 years. It was a resounding success. Between 1994 and 2004, crime skyrocketed. The number of murders committed with “assault weapons” dropped, and only because there were fewer of the guns in circulation as gun owners bought the guns they were worried would be banned and locked them up or gave them away. But, overall, crime continued its frightening ascent.

Contrary to your point, numerous experts have done research on this and found that the armed citizens you deride as dangerous “hillbilly knuckle draggers” (why are they all hillbilly and knuckle-dragging? You do not stereotype your anti-gun friends, do you?) deter crime, have no effect on rates of violent crime and even play a statistically significant role in the prevention of crime. In other words, your categorical statements do not match reality.

It’s also particularly ironic for you to write that Americans can’t “arm themselves in a society where so many Americans either allow their emotions to force them into irrational and unprincipled thought, or are deliberately misinformed.” What rational, informed voter — of which there are many — takes the time to reply to someone like you who often combines the abject irrationality of Sarah Palin with the gross ignorance of someone who has a favorite feature on a firearm while knowing zero about guns.

Of all your admittedly nonsensical comments, “we are preparing and arming ourselves for the hell that opens its doors, and for those who support it” is perhaps the most delusional. That’s not America — yet. It is a nation, however, with a serious crime problem, one that will not go away without law-abiding citizens choosing to protect themselves and others.
