question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I am 50 years old and married. I grew up in the suburbs of a small town on the coast of Texas. We lived in a house that was backed up to the pasture and because my Father owned and worked on the land, I grew up believing that I had access to it. Being “connected to nature” was a foundational belief and this connection is an important part of who I am. I had pet cows, horses, pigs and chickens, but my closest connection was with a cow named Daisy. Daisy was a purebred Guernsey. She was beautiful and she was completely blind. She stepped on her tongue, which made her unable to eat or drink. However, because she was always connected to the pasture, she was able to find her way around without being lead around. My father eventually sold Daisy to a friend who also kept cows. My connection with Daisy developed in me an affection for animals and led me to want to help them have a good life. My adult life has been guided by the realization that there is a profound connection between humans and animals, and that connection can be positive or negative. I realize now that when I left Texas for college, I needed to distance myself from Daisy and the life that I had grown up with so that I could develop independence.

When I went to college, I tried to push away my ties to Daisy and the pasture and focused on studying and the opportunities that studying would give me. Ultimately, my studies led me to a 27-year career in the United States Navy. My first duty station was with a small submarine tender in Newport, Rhode Island. In Newport, I began to understand the connections that exist between human and animals and the importance of food security. Like Daisy, the closer I became to my surroundings, the more grounded I felt and the more I understood the great importance that we all place on our connection to food and nature.

The story of Daisy led me to where I am today, in an exciting career as an Animal Nutrition Advisor to the largest pork producer in the world. To have an impact in the world of animal agriculture, I need to continue to understand the power of nature and the dynamics that link animals to their environment. Even as an adult, I still feel a tie to Daisy and the land around her. My family supports that connection by having a garden and by buying food directly from farmers.

As I progress in my career, I can see that many of the challenges of animal agriculture relate to linking the animals to their environment, whether it is through improving feed efficiency, treating the animals humanely or supporting the growth of local farms. Even with my family’s support, it is clear that there is an inevitable disconnect between what I believe and what is required to be successful in the industrial scale of commercial agriculture. In my current position, it is easy for me to remain committed to my core values, but if I would pursue a more senior management position, the pressures to compromise on my values would become greater. I continue to search for ways to understand how I can have the greatest impact on the problems that plague the pork industry, both locally and globally.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I'm a very funny man and I love laughter and animals. I love to laugh and appreciate life and all that it entails.

I'm a 51 year old part time National Park volunteer, who lives to work in the wilderness with his dog. I like hiking, bicycling, horseback riding, and camping. I'm an adrenaline junkie and skydiver who needs life's activities to keep me alive.

I'm a retail meat cutter who is learning more and more to be vegetarian. I'm a lover of wine, art and I love to cook a variety of international cuisine.

I'm too funny for my own good, a klutz, love children, my 2 dogs, horses, and cats. I laugh loud and laugh hard.

I moved here from Italy to find my passions, to find happiness, and to live freely in God's great nation, to lead a more independent life, and to have a great life, not a good life.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: “Well, I’m 52. Born and raised in Washington, D.C. after my mom and dad separated when I was 3. My mom raised me until I was 12 when she sent me to live with my dad and step mom. As an only child, I felt lonely, left out and that people didn’t like me. They didn’t hang out with me. Maybe because I was too boring, too quiet, too young. I always wanted to be the life of the party, but never knew how. When my stepmom and I were alone together, I’d make her read me stories and tell me about my past before I went to live with my dad. She’d get annoyed with my incessant questions, but she always answered them. Then I was off to the foster care system when I was 17 because my dad got remarried and wouldn’t pay my college tuition, so I got a GED, went to 4 years of technical college and then 2 years of college before I had to join the Navy. I was still young, in my 20s and fresh out of the system so I had to prove that I was strong enough to do the job. All I wanted was to go out there and do something meaningful with my life, serve my country and be proud of who I was. But instead, my ship ended up a patrol boat off the coast of Vietnam. The thing that changed my life the most, though, is that I met my husband after my tour of duty was over. He was the best thing that ever happened to me.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am 51 years old. I was born and raised in the Midwest, with all that means. My mother was one of five children in a very traditional family. All were first generation college graduates with advanced degrees, but following the woman's normal role of marriage, mother, and homemaker. I am the oldest of five girls and my father had four brothers. I attended an all girl Catholic school from pre-school through 12th grade where boys were visitors and non existent, except for the few who came from Brother Dominic's house around the corner. None of us ever thought about not going to college, though as high school graduation approached it became clear to me that my choices of college was more limited. I knew I was definitely going to college, but I had no sense of what a real choice might be. As a result I attended a private women's college in New England.

Leaving home for college in another part of the country in a different time zone, feeling like I was getting ready to start a new and dangerous life, was one of the scariest events in my life to that point. (At the same time, it was also one of the most fun, exciting, and exhilarating events of my life). I thrived in college, taking a major for which I was woefully unprepared (though I didn't really know it at the time), a serious relationship with an unacceptable man, and work in every kind of hospital and clinic I could find. My Catholic undergrad college did not prepare me for the reality of life as a woman, particularly one with a career in a field that was still clearly dominated by men. The women in the program were in a crisis, with the dean who was the woman in charge closing down the program, in some cases kicking students out, several for no reason at all. The other women in the class were outraged at this but all but two of them were of the opinion that if we stayed quiet we would eventually be invited back, and the entire scene would have been lost to history. I eventually left the program entirely and left school instead. I worked in the lab in a variety of jobs and loved the laboratory, but from a practical perspective only. I did not feel confident in making it a career. As a result I used the biology degree I did have to gain entry into the medical technologist program at a local school. I completed the program, passed the certification exam and began my career. I worked in a variety of laboratories, a level of experience I needed to reach a position where I could have a career, that of supervisor. Today I am the chief technologist in a large research institute where I oversee a lab that functions as a high end service lab for scientists. I love my work and in my relatively young career have received a variety of honors. I have run medical emergencies, went through a hurricane, and have lived with an often dangerous commute. But mostly, I have been so blessed as to have enjoyed each step in my career and have never regretted the decisions I have made.

Although the future has never been a certainty, it was pretty clear that I was going to get married (a lot) and have children. What I didn't realize is that with no sisters and only one life long friend to turn to, the messages that I would receive as a woman in training for and eventually pursuing a career, would be so very different than my male counterparts. I would be told to quit or make concessions, while men were never challenged, and never made to feel it wasn't possible to move ahead. The foundation of my development was fractured, with many major parts missing. In the end, being thrust from the shelter of all that I had known into the world alone, and where the people who were at the foundation of the world I grew up in did not support me but instead ridiculed me, is in retrospect, one of the most powerful lessons I learned from my childhood. Its power went completely unnoticed until I was in crisis as an adult, and even then it was as if I was reciting the alphabet. I knew I would have more children, and as a mother I would be different from my mother, who was really a very good mother. I had no idea, however, as to what that meant to me. I would know later that it also meant that my relationship with my children would become a double-edged sword. What I gave them, for all the right reasons, would cause us all, but most of all myself, a lot of pain.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am in my early fifties, as is most of the editorial staff of the Boring via email. My youth and upbringing will be covered elsewhere and I don’t know how much it matters; I’m an American like all of us. Here is where I suppose we could have a discussion about what I love and enjoy. I love the New England Patriots. They are my team. I root for the Atlanta Falcons, a team my family has not followed for 44 years. The New York Yankees are my favorite baseball team. The Boston Red Sox are my second favorite. Both are Boston teams. I love my coffee. It is one of my favorite smells in the world, the kind of smell that makes you want to curl your nose and breath it in deeply. Don’t get me started on the color yellow. You’d think I enjoy words by how much I use them in this answer, but I’m running out of things to say, which is why I went for a third paragraph. When it comes to sports, I also enjoy football, particularly the NFL (yes, even New England). Hockey is not my favorite, but I don’t mind it. Neither is soccer, unless it’s in the Olympics. On the collegiate level, football is always enjoyable as long as the players and teams aren’t cheating. In the Olympics, it’s everything except ice dancing. As an aside, even though I’m a new reader, thanks to my lovely wife and her wise as a serpent conversation, my understanding is that Best Gore does not condone these kinds of sports.

Who I am matters to me only because I am pro-life. My favorite music artist would be Def Leppard. I like science fiction and Doctor Who. I love my family and friends. I despise the socialist crap that passes for compassion. I work with groups of people on a daily basis, which I enjoy, and like to be actively involved with and contribute to projects, the kind that only make a difference if someone gets on them. And I’m a fan of dogs.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I'm very proud to say that I'm an American. I had the fortune of growing up in the Pacific Northwest - the beautiful state of Oregon. I have loving parents - an airline pilot father and elementary school teacher mother. My parents separated when I was 5 years old, so I was raised mostly by my wonderful mother. She's not doing well these days - she has a degenerative disease called PSP. She has 3 or 4 good years left, and I'm grateful to have these remaining years with her. I'm 50 years old, and most recently, I was single, a director at a PR firm, and I helped to create a new video game business unit.

Life has always mattered to me, but lately, I've been much more intensely focused on enjoying life and making a difference in the lives of others. As a result of the disease my mother suffers from, she has become dependent on others. I don't mind that at all, and I'm doing my best to use my words to make an impact.

I run a lot - I live in the temperate Pacific Northwest, and I started running 10 years ago. I know I run because I feel free while I'm running, and because I value the time alone in nature. I run for the pleasure of it - I don't compete. I get a lot of time alone with my thoughts, and I'm working on a video game idea that will be inspired by my mother.

I want to make a difference in a world where people are starving, where people live in desperate, abusive situations, and where life has no value. I want to create something where life has meaning, where people are connected, where we can leave a positive mark on the world.

I enjoy anything that allows me to create, build, and problem-solve.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I am a 60-year-old retired senior in moderate health. I was born and raised in the Midwest and moved to the South at age 21. I was single until my late 30s and married once after 9 years. I have 1 adult child. I have been an independent consultant for 15 years. Prior to that I held leadership positions at several well-known companies. I grew up very poor, so saving money has always mattered a lot to me. I have always been a big reader. Over the years I have become an increasingly passionate Democrat, starting when Bush won in 2000. I love good music, especially blues and classic rock. I look forward to using my advanced skill set to make an impact in the IT workforce.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am 63 years old, as of this publication. I grew up in Rochester, NY, where I did my undergraduate work in English and journalism at the University of Rochester. That is where I developed a lifelong love of English grammar, which later led me to my pursuit of professional work in the copyediting/proofreading fields. I also found my passion for volunteering for nonprofit organizations in my college years, most of which were at the Volunteer Bureau of Rochester and Monroe County, working as a volunteer coordinator. After I graduated, my volunteering continued in what was to become a lifelong commitment to doing good in my community in order to improve the lot of those who cannot easily improve their own situations.

After college, I married my high school sweetheart, raised two sons, and enjoyed working various jobs in the business world for several years. As part of that experience, I dabbled in freelance work, writing articles and providing editing/proofreading services. Then I decided to go to the University of Dayton, where I earned a master’s degree in community development. While at the University of Dayton, I had the great privilege of participating in the Center for Social Concern’s Brazil Summer service-learning program for three weeks in June, 1979, and I also had the honor of acting as an instructor in the program’s Brazil Speakers’ Bureau in the following academic year. During that fall semester, I worked as a volunteer for the Greater Cincinnati Community Action Agency, testing English language proficiency among refugees resettled in southwest Ohio. I was hired part time by the agency the following year to do volunteer management, and, in 1983, after applying for a full-time administrative position with the agency, I was promoted to position of administrative director of the agency, a post I held for 24 years, retiring in December, 2007.

You can read the full story of how I came to found Elifinite by clicking here.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I am 52 years old. I grew up in Montgomery County Maryland. My parents were born and raised in Italy. I grew up speaking English and Italian at home and that has provided me with a strong understanding of the challenges that non-native English speakers face in a country that focuses almost exclusively on English. Learning about Italian politics and culture as a youngster gave me a perspective on how government impacts people’s lives. As a young man, I volunteered for Walter Mondale’s presidential campaign in Virginia. In his presidential campaign, I traveled throughout Virginia on several different airplanes and stayed in some very sketchy places. But I gained invaluable political experience. That experience gave me an opportunity to interact with the American public in a very personal way.

During the 1980’s, I was a manager at a Computer Sciences Corporation. During that time, I earned my B.A. in History and was chosen by my peers to be an APO international advisor for my college. After that, I got a Master’s degree in History and moved to a division of the federal government.

As a young man, I loved both American and foreign sports and became the president of a country club as well as the baseball manager for several years. I love music of every type and have been in many bands over the years. I enjoy cheering for Washington DC sports teams and am a big Redskins fan. I spend time with friends at Luray Caverns, film festivals, and plays in both New York and DC and I listen to classical music. Finally, I like to read legal and political blogs.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I am 54 years old, and I grew up in Oceanport, New Jersey. I am a lifelong fan of the New York Yankees, the Buffalo Bills, and the NY Rangers. I have been a music fan for as long as I can remember. My musical interests are vast but the music I gravitate toward the most is the classic rock bands from the late ’60s and early ’70s. This is just my age and what I gravitate to. I also love Blues, Jazz, Soul and R&B. I have been involved in music in many aspects of it from my teenage years to now. I was the lead singer of a band in the early ’80s and I’ve been in the marketing department at SiriusXM for 15 years. I’ve also spent the last 15 years coaching Little League baseball and traveling sports with my boys. I enjoy spending time with my friends and family, going to dinner and watching movies. I really don’t have much of a life outside of my family. It’s just the way I like it. There are times when it drives me nuts, but I wouldn’t trade it for anything.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I was born in New York, in Brooklyn, in 1969. I am a proud first-generation American. My parents are both of Cuban descent. My father was a Marine, so I moved a lot, and lived in California, North Carolina, and Florida before finally settling in Puerto Rico. I am fluent in Spanish. My parents were teachers and instilled in me a love for learning. I am the first in my family to graduate from college. I am 50 years old.

My life experiences have helped to form my values. I value honesty and loyalty. I have a very strong work ethic. I am very compassionate and caring, especially for children and the elderly. I am an animal lover. I love to read, and spend time with my family.

I like to travel. I enjoy hiking and nature walks. I like to go to museums and go sightseeing.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: Well, I am 50 years old and was born in New York City and I am a first generation American. My Father was a port captain and we moved quite a lot as a kid. But I was raised in Virginia and Washington DC. My Dad wanted to make sure I got a good education and was encouraged to pursue higher education. He and I both were into WW2 history and he helped guide me through college and Navy ROTC. I ended up graduating from the Virginia Military Institute and then I went to Basic school to be an infantry officer. During my military career, I was deployed to a couple of different spots but fortunately I did not have to go to Iraq or Afghanistan. I really found a passion for hunting big game animals in Alaska and Africa and at the time I decided to pursue a medical career. I got into forensic pathology and started training under some great mentors that allowed me to do everything from gunshot wounds to homicides to drug investigations. It took me quite a while to pass my exams and finally I passed and got my board certifications. I am now just waiting for my Boards to get back to me regarding my board certifications. I want to know that I am board certified before I respond to specific crime scenes so I can give an answer to why that person died and how I arrived at that diagnosis. Medical examiners really don’t have a lot of authority when it comes to why and how the person died. So, I’d like to prove that I know what I am talking about so I can write a report.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: My name is XXXXX XXXXX I am 56. I graduated from Delta College, a community college in Michigan. I grew up in a modest house with working class parents. I did not have a happy childhood, and to this day I am affected by the experiences I had. I was the youngest of four children and remember waking up on Saturday mornings to bright sunshine and a cold hard lump. My mom would cry and scream at me in my sleep and I would wake up to pain in my eyes. This happened over a period of about a year and left a terrible impression on my young mind. I remember my dad yelling at me, called me a fool, liar and a little bastard. What a beautiful way to start the day. These horrible experiences in childhood left me always suspicious and anxious about dealing with others and not being able to trust people. I grew up knowing my first instinct about others was to expect to get lied to and treated badly. The first part of my life I was always finding the worst in people and running from them. It was only in the last ten years or so that I learned to trust others and have become far more positive. I believe in God and attend church several times a week. I am an avid collector of model trains and have been for 46 years. This past year I found my mom through a DNA match and have had a chance to meet her and my family. I have not yet told them my history and I’m not sure how it will go.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I am in my early 50s. I grew up in Philadelphia. I had a great childhood. I had loving parents. I have fond memories of time spent with my siblings and cousins. I loved going on vacation with my family. As an adult, I have seen some of the happiest times in my life in going on vacation with my family. Now I have my own family. I live with my wonderful wife and my two wonderful children. I also live with my parents who are both in their 90s. For many years my parents were in the process of moving from the big house into a much smaller condo and then my father suffered a stroke. I have been living with them for the past 6 years.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am 54 years old (at the time of this writing) and was born and raised in the suburbs of Philadelphia, PA. I had the great fortune of attending the very best public school in the area, and to have parents that supported me in every endeavor in life. I attended and graduated the University of Delaware in Newark, DE and acquired my degree in Speech Communications.

Of course, in the “olden days”, we didn’t have email or cell phones, so a lot of important events were missed. This allowed me to watch Penn State football games, which created a lifetime of love and passion for the school! I loved sports as a kid – basketball, football, baseball – you name it. My favorite toy (as a kid) was a plastic football helmet, which I took everywhere.

As an adult, I have worked my entire career in Philadelphia, except for a very short stint in New York City during the early ’90s. In Philadelphia, I have worked in print and radio advertising, owned a vending company and worked as a computer technician. In 2003, I completed my MBA in executive management, to fulfill a lifelong dream.

As I said, sports is my passion. I love to see people come together for a good cause. Raising money for a good cause, something that will change people’s lives for the better. Sports is also a great way to meet new people. I am very active on social media and enjoy sharing thoughts, likes, etc. with like-minded people. Over the years, there have been many great athletes and actors that have made me smile, laugh and made me proud to be an American.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I’m 50 and I’m an Illinois native. After living in Missouri for almost 20 years, my family and I have recently moved back to the Illinois side of St. Louis.

It’s been a great experience to re-integrate with some of the friends and associates I’ve had for a long time and also make new connections with interesting people here. I’ve found that as much as I hated the forced settling in an unfamiliar environment, it was an important part of my life to gain the self-confidence I needed to do what I really want to do.

Most of the questions I get have to do with coaching and working with families. I answer those questions in my post “Helping parents help their kids.” Most of the readers I have are other trainers, nutritionists, and folks in the horse industry. If they’re not professionals, most of them want to help someone they know who needs assistance.

One group I would like to reach more often are the folks who like the Western horse show circuit. It seems like it’s very difficult for them to find a trainer who will be honest about the way their horse is riding. When I hear that someone’s horse isn’t picking up its leads, I tell them to check their own riding habits. Most of the time, it turns out they’re asking their horses to make changes but not giving them the support they need to do it. By the way, we do that too. We try to make changes with our own horses and it turns out that it’s because we’re trying to do too much.

People often make assumptions about you as a trainer based on the way you train. But I have a whole lot of different horses in the barn. Some are great and some are lame. I get a horse with a problem and I get a horse that will blow your socks off.

I remember I trained a horse a few years ago who was really slow. A trainer came to watch me work with him and she told me to take him back home. But I like doing the rehab work. That’s a big part of my job.

As a side note: When I was in my late teens, I lived in Puerto Rico and I worked with thoroughbreds there. At that time, Puerto Rico didn’t require the horses to have their paperwork in order. There was a ship from the Dominican Republic that was scheduled to dock in Puerto Rico that had thousands of horses on it. It was a big deal for the trainers and the people in the thoroughbred industry. The captain said that the horses had all been vaccinated. I remember going through a kennel and seeing all these horses with blankets on. They were like statues, lying down and staring ahead. We rounded up all these horses and put them in stalls so that we could make sure they didn’t have any problems. They turned out to be just fine. But that’s not what I do now.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I am a 56 year old single White woman with 3 grown children. I was married and raised my kids on a horse farm in Annapolis, Maryland. My family was super wealthy and I was able to travel and do all the fun things we should be able to do. Yes, that was not long ago. Life is much better for many Black, Hispanic, Asian etc. – even rich people can live in gated communities so not much has changed for me. But since my husband died 10 years ago, I have really wanted to be free to do the things I wanted without having to listen to some man and answer to him. I have been home alone in an old boat that was given to me by my parents. I grew up thinking that is what I always wanted and I was so relieved when my husband died that I was able to really embrace life.

Everything is expensive and I often take the bus places because it is not that expensive. I also enjoy working at McDonalds in California (which is really hard to get in and out of). The company pays me $20/hour. Yes. You heard me. I love America and I hate that people want to destroy us. I love America and I am not for destroying it like you are. That is how I became a Trump supporter. That is how I became a Republican. My parents taught me about the Constitution and what life was like in the old days when people valued hard work and loved their country. That is why I love Donald Trump. That is why I love the idea of Ivanka becoming the President some day. There are so many things I want to protect from the over reaching, entitled, ignorant, arrogant left.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am 50 years old. I was born in Northbrook, IL. My parents moved to Crystal Lake, IL, a bedroom community in the 1960s. I went to public school. My senior year, in a single classroom, I had every subject taught from third grade through high school. After my parents divorced, I felt very disconnected and was bitter about being poor, but I managed to get into Northwestern University.

So in summary, I am a geek who was not terribly popular and I believe I am intelligent. In college, I was less angry and I began to realize I might have something to offer others. I studied the sciences and math and then I had a spiritual awakening, though I don’t use that term. I went to seminary and became an ordained Jewish rabbi. I met my wife, the woman who would become my life partner, and we moved to New Haven, CT to be near Quinnipiac University. She got a Ph.D. in speech pathology and I went to Yale and got an M.Div. and a doctorate in education. From there we moved to the Chicago area to teach at Illinois Benedictine College, a Lutheran College. My kids were born here, my parents are here, this is our home. At the same time, I felt the rabbinate could be challenging for a gay Jew. At another turning point in my life, I came out of the closet in the Jewish world. I decided to leave the Jewish world and dedicate myself to public service in the secular world.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am proud to say that I am currently 63 years old. I grew up in an academic and political environment. My father was a parliamentarian, a Member of Parliament, in the 1970s, and later in life he became a Senator for National Defense for the Royal Dutch Shell Foundation for Iraq. My mother, Mrs. Nassira K. de Buck, a Palestinian Jew from the Bezekoy area of East Jerusalem, was a geneticist and also worked for the Royal Dutch Shell Foundation in the Genetics, Stem Cell, and Animal Genetics department. I graduated from AUB, American University of Beirut, with a B.Sc. in Pharmacy in 1978.

My first job was the first pharmacist and chemist in the history of the New Hospital in Erbil, a rebel village at the time when the Ba’ath took control in the early 1970s. Afterwards, I was recruited to a newly established Pharmaceutical Research Company in Kuwait. I worked as a medicinal chemist. It was one of the first “research and development” companies in Kuwait, and it’s still in existence as a major pharmaceutical manufacturing and distribution company. Afterwards, I was appointed Professor of Pharmacology at Lebanese University, University of Beirut, and also the Lebanese University in Tyre. During that period I worked on data and information management and systemization. I presented several of my findings as papers at National and International Conferences in the UK and USA. I then moved back to Kuwait and was appointed at a newly established research center, the Kuwait Cancer Research Center, as the Executive Manager responsible for data and information management and statistical analysis. Afterwards, I was appointed as the Manager of the First Arabic Dictionary for Pharmaceutical and Chemical Nomenclature at the Drugs Department in Kuwait, and later as an Assistant Professor at the University of Kuwait and the University of Tunis, where I specialized in Medical Informatics and Statistics. During that period, in 1995, a fellow former researcher in the pharmaceutical research company in Kuwait, Dr. Shadi Basim, then a doctorate student in Pharmacology at UPenn, asked me to work on a research project he had with a biotechnology company in the USA. I moved to Philadelphia and was appointed at the Rockefeller Foundation as a Senior Research Biologist, specialized in cancer research and development. During that period, I published an article in New Scientist about one of the FDA drug approvals in the treatment of hypertension. After that I moved back to Kuwait and was appointed to build and manage the largest drug research and development facility in Kuwait. I also supervised the biggest Center of Excellence in the Arabian Peninsula at that time. I finished my book, “Drug Development Practice and Prospects”, which was published in 2006 in Kuwait. My book was published in Arabic by the Office of the Mufti in Kuwait.

After 23 years in Kuwait, I worked for the Dalla Lana School of Public Health at the University of Toronto, where I had the opportunity to publish three manuscripts in an international peer-reviewed journal. A article was published on the first intervention model for any Gulf and Middle Eastern country. In that article, we tackled the problem of lung cancer smoking in Kuwait, which is now considered the cancer capital of the Gulf region. Three manuscripts were published in an international peer reviewed journal related to advanced statistics and software use in medical informatics. I started a website in 2010, www.drugdevelopmentandstatistics.com, which was dedicated to teaching people about drug development, and later was transferred to my consulting company, called EnvMed / Consulting.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
When I was a little boy I was beat by my father. My mother was a good person but my father was a violent criminal who was also addicted to alcohol. I came to the USA from Kyrgyzstan at the age of five because it was an opportunity for us. At that age I didn’t realize the obstacles my mother had to overcome. She was young, and an immigrant, alone, and couldn’t speak English. She was trying to escape the violence, but there were other forms of violence and many obstacles she had to overcome. Sometimes it’s only now, being in my fifties, that I understand the difficulties of my mother’s life and I can empathize with her and understand her pain. She was a great mom and tried her best to protect me.

I want my dad to be proud of me. If I can do something to be a good son for him I will do that even though it’s too late for him and he’s not here. The love I have for him has no bounds. I also remember his violence. My best memory is when he and I watched a soccer game together and we enjoyed the whole thing. We laughed. He passed away in May 2010.

I have three sisters and a brother. When I was in kindergarten, my mother got pregnant and had my brother. I was so happy to have a little brother. I was obsessed with him as a little kid. We used to fight a lot until my parents moved to the USA. He was later diagnosed with brain cancer and he passed away in 2004. That was difficult for all of us. He was our best brother, our protector, and someone who only cared about the well being of everyone he met.

I have two sons: a 13-year-old and a 7-year-old. We are very close and I feel like I am a good father. I always listen to their ideas and respect them. I also think my children are very smart and very talented. They are the most important people in my life. My love for them has no bounds. I try my best to be a good father for them.

From a professional perspective, I have done a lot of events management. I also enjoy using the computer and my other talents include studying, cooking, music, and traveling.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I was born on the East Coast in Philadelphia in a neighborhood that was mainly white working class Irish, Italian, Jewish, black, and Asian-American. I'm 58. The two major influences that made me who I am today are my parents and my Chinese-American heritage.

My father was a physicist and my mother was a teacher and both were independent thinkers who encouraged me to question everything. They were idealists, democratic socialists, anti-racists, environmentalists, and culturally cosmopolitan, seeing the beauty and value in every tradition.

When I was 5, my father began a project to build a house. He made almost every component himself: built all the interior walls, laid the foundation, did carpentry, laid tile, electric wiring, everything except the plumbing. He worked so much he was home for breakfast and dinner but just the beginning and end of the day. By the time I was 15 we had spent the time to get the house built and paid for. The most important lesson I learned from him was: if you work hard enough and make long-term investments, you will be successful. My father's hands smelled of sawdust and charcoal, but there were always two sizes too big clothes for him in the closet. He was a giant, 6'6" tall and very strong. I'm grateful to have been raised by him and to have inherited his values.

My mother came to the US as a child. Her father was an engineer and fought in WWI. She was an artist and when I was young she painted Chinese landscapes and symbols on the walls of my room, showing me the qualities of Chinese landscape paintings that I would draw on later in my life: making everything beautiful, including the boring mundane details, the beauty of simplicity, tranquility, the harmony of opposites, of earthy and spiritual, conflict and stability, masculine and feminine, and impermanence and eternity. The energy of the brush is another important element of Chinese painting.

We have Chinese relatives. My Chinese aunt was in a concentration camp in WWII and had to wear the same dress for weeks. Some of her family members were murdered. My grandfather was murdered by Nazis. His name was Emile Eichelbaum. Many of his family members also died in the Holocaust. All told, my great grandparents, grandparents, aunts, uncles, and father were all of Jewish heritage. My grandmother's maiden name was Goldsmith. In this way I'm part of a broader diaspora that includes the other displaced and dispossessed groups throughout the world like the African-Americans who were abducted and put to work on my ancestors' farms, the indentured Europeans who were brought to America as slaves, the Roma people in Europe, the Druze people in the Middle East and many other groups who were forced to emigrate by "progressive" governments and ethnic tyrannies.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: My name is Alan Laiacono (hey, thanks, I can count on my own fingers, no more school!). I grew up in northeastern Pennsylvania near Scranton. My family was poor, and I resented them for it – especially my dad – because I saw all the other kids having much better lives than I did. I became a little drug addict in high school and a drug dealer. I grew up resenting my dad for everything. I was an angry kid and I grew up to be an angry man. Now I’m 50. I don’t do drugs any more and I don’t beat my wife any more either (ask her). Thanks to hard work and a degree in History, I am doing great in life (not great great, but fairly wealthy and stable and in a nice town with a nice wife and a nice kid). Now I have put the pieces of my past together and realize that I’m still kinda angry.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am in my late fifties and can honestly say that I like myself. I grew up in St. Louis, Missouri. It wasn’t the best place in the world and there were times when I didn’t like myself. As I grew and matured, I learned that I only have control over me and how I allow others to treat me. It has always been a challenge to set boundaries and stand up for myself but I learned and did. I have also lost a few of my closest people to me. Unfortunately, in the line of work I am in now, death is a very common occurrence.

I am not sure how to answer about what events have made me who I am. I have always been kind and compassionate, even when the person was mean or hateful, I was always kind in return. I became more aware of discrimination when I worked for my bank. For a very long time, I only had a couple gay friends. I can’t really say that I am gay-friendly. I have gay friends and treat them like I treat everyone else. For me to say that I am gay-friendly would be the same as asking someone who has spent most of their life dating and marrying the opposite sex, if they were straight friendly. I have not been touched by the disease that is HIV and until that happens I can’t say that I am an HIV friendly.

I have lost a couple very good friends to the disease. It made me angry for a while. I still have to be careful not to expose myself to blood, bleach, and bleach water. It was a wake up call for me to not take things for granted. I took my health for granted. After my friend died, I went for a physical. I had high blood pressure, bad teeth, bad hearing, and a dirty colon. I had to remind myself to be grateful for having access to a doctor who cared enough to find out what was wrong with me.

Currently, I would have to say that what matters to me is respect and to be treated fairly. It may sound cliché but life is to short and I don’t have time to deal with bullies or people who feel that they deserve something more than someone else. I am also fond of chalk art, needlework, and singing. Singing is probably my favorite thing to do. It is something that I do solely for me.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am a 55 year old veteran (9 years) that moved to the South to be near his mother. We have a close relationship as he has become very ill. I grew up in rural Illinois. I was exposed to the violent white supremacist Timothy McVeigh in my community and then witnessed it in person in New Orleans during Katrina. A Father Figure and my sister, Patsy, lost their life due to alcoholism in the early 80s. The fact that most deaths are alcohol related is to blame. I tried saving them, but ultimately you cannot save someone that doesn't want it. I experienced many lessons before becoming an adult. As a young teenager, I saw the empty glasses they left behind and realized it meant they were dead. I felt like I was just there to be a living host to her when I saw her like that. I was sad, angry, and annoyed because their drinking had made me have to deal with this horror. Her death was partially an accident, but I believe there was a heavy dose of neglect. I was a fucking child that didn't know anything about finances and how to pay bills. I did what I could and do not regret helping. They didn't take responsibility. You know why? Because we wouldn't let them. Alcoholism is a disease that requires support, but not if you are going to abuse it. Being close to her was like living with a bomb. I always think people should know exactly what they are doing and how their actions affect other people and the universe. If they abuse drugs or alcohol (legal or not), there is a price to pay. In my world, you don't really get to choose to drink, smoke, or pop pills. You choose to have a heart attack, stroke, or a fubar'd liver. That being said, everything happens for a reason and there's always a lesson involved.

My childhood problems and adulthood problems were only made worse because of my experiences with violent white supremacy. Living in the South, I am close to neo-nazis that have murdered people. They are alcoholics and I have seen their results. My family members died. It was a bittersweet moment to realize that many people are now gone because they drank themselves to death. They pushed for tolerance because they lost their lives, but who cares? The more respect we give idiots, the more shitty things they do. Just like when you meet a co-worker and they want to gossip about another employee. A wise man advised me that it is better to be nice to everyone, but to stand your ground. He believed you could do both. Someone with a drinking problem doesn't want to stand their ground, they want to fall down.

Obviously, the terrorists and non-terrorists like who perpetrated the April 2007 Fort Hood, Texas attack on soldiers, mentally ill people, and civilians that killed 32 people and wounded another 23 - 29 of the wounded being soldiers because it happened on a military base is related to drinking. It is related to greed, too. People like the murderer like and apply racism and patriotism where there is none. The execution at Fort Hood had nothing to do with country, pride, or patriotism. His family and friends who knew him best said he was the last person they would have expected to do this. They admitted the Islam connection, but claimed they weren't extremists. He may not have been an extremist, but he was a fucknut with an addiction to white privilege.

I would like to state that my grandfather was an alcoholic. My father was an alcoholic. My father's father was an alcoholic. My sister was an alcoholic. Other family members have experienced or exhibited signs of alcoholism. To be sure, many of the perps at Fort Hood were related to alcoholics in some way. Fort Hood is a case study. Some of the perpetrators, even when visiting their family, were found passed out drunk. I will say that as soldiers, they were violent even if they weren't drunk. I am sure they were a little mentally impaired (alcohol is a depressant). But they were capable of right and wrong.

The murderer at Fort Hood is the same as the James Holmes, Sandy Hook shooter, etc. He is no less guilty than the person from my neighborhood or any small town USA. I believe in innocent until proven guilty. I take it to a point that would drive other people nuts. My head still clicks and I start calculating. During jury selection at my trial, the judge asked me if I believed in capital punishment. Without even hesitating, I yelled "OF COURSE" and I have never wavered in that belief. Capital punishment MUST be the norm for anyone convicted of murder. That's it. The murderer of Trayvon Martin, George Zimmerman, received Capital Punishment by way of our cowardly and incompetent, Romney-Ryan Supreme Court. He was tried and convicted in a 100% voter fraud guilty verdict because they thought he was black. If Zimmerman would have turned in paperwork indicating he was Hispanic, instead of black, they wouldn't have given him the death penalty because the court doesn't consider Hispanics to be minorities like blacks are.

If we focused on prevention instead of talking about gun control, we would see that white supremacy, being racist, and being a white supremacist are all related to the kinds of guns people use, money, alcoholism, and selfishness. James Holmes used those same kinds of guns. The shooters at Sandy Hook Elementary, Zebra killers, etc. all were white supremacy. We see the battle wages with the leaders of Russia and China becoming more capitalist than the U.S. The Neo-Nazis like the KKK and other groups were purposely developed in the 60s because the banks thought they were a great asset. I think they always underestimated our intelligence. The banksters plan is moving along quite nicely and they are shocked to discover people want the power back. They must have decided to avoid their mistakes the last time. But that didn't matter to them - they are selfish little monsters. They are associated with degenerate bankers. They are associated with criminal bankers. They are associated with slave-owning, murdering, and rapists. They are associated with criminals like the shooters at Fort Hood. They are associated with animal abusers. They are associated with rapists. They are associated with all kinds of white supremacist atrocities. The last thing they are associated with is hard workers and inventors. The only difference between their white nationalism and someone else's is the italics and we shouldn't be giving it any legitimacy at all. White trash is exactly that and if you believe it is good to have or use gun, my advice is to get out of this country - go live someplace where guns are controlled and where terrorists are treated like the criminals they are. You can't stop my family, my friends, or my people from fighting for our country's freedom like our Founding Fathers did. And make no mistake. Your 7.62 mm bullets won't hold up against us and those criminals that assaulted communities for profit are responsible for those 7.62 bullets. Same with any and all dangerous chemicals that are produced. They are responsible for their overuse, abuse, and waste.

They may try to stop us and they are trying to stop every American from their basic constitutional rights. They are evil and violent.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am 63 years old and I retired from my job with The US Department of Housing and Urban Development over a year ago. I was in the program management office and worked in Rent and Management Solutions that dealt with a software system called Voucher and its modules. My family is from the Upper West Side of Manhattan, New York. My dad was a park worker who only finished 10th grade but was a social reformer and helped many kids stay out of trouble. My mother worked for Bloomingdales and then did volunteer work.

In 2000 my husband and I moved from New York to Key West and we have lived here ever since. I am one of the original Mayor’s Fest in the Spring visitors to Key West. It is great to have a long weekend without the drive and I get to see my friends. We have festivals all year round so there is never a reason not to visit here and it’s always great fun. I love to visit the Keys and Key West is the “Last” one with the most style and personality. We are filled with creative minds and I am filled with energy with much to do in Key West. I look forward to every morning and I am looking forward to achieving my goals and seeing all of you in the Fall and creating new ones in 2019. Lastly, my mother passed away in April of this year. She was 94 years old. She loved the FFF so much.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I’m 60 and I grew up in Mississippi. I enjoyed hunting and fishing. My daddy worked as a welder and would hunt and fish on the weekends. Then when I got older I worked as a frycook when I was in high school. The only thing my mama made me do was go to church on Sunday. I married my high school sweetheart right after I graduated and have 3 boys and 2 girls. When I was in my 30s, I was an electrician and my Daddy asked me one day what I was doing. He said you were born to be a jackleg. I asked, “what’s a jackleg? He said, “babe, you can work where you don’t need no license. Work on your own terms.” I kept thinking about what he said and so I started thinking about how I would want to set my own schedule. I want to go to work when I want to and take the day off whenever I want. So what I did was start doing research. On my lunch breaks and after work, I would research the CNA and cook’s certification courses that are online. I could take the CNA certification course because my daddy worked with my hands all his life and passed those skills on to me. I needed to know anatomy and physiology. I would take that test and if I passed I would go through the cooking course. If I passed that too, then I will have learned those skills in no time. So all my down time and weekends I studied those courses online. So now I’m ready to go to work.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I’m 51 years old, Father of three wonderful children (15, 20, and 24), raised in Texas, raised in a “faith based” home. I never thought about that at the time, but in talking to friends in my area, I realize that I was blessed and have more to be thankful for.

I was raised to believe that you will get what you work for, and do your best to make the world a better place to live in. As a kid I was always able to work things out with people, and I used those skills to get along with everyone. That ability helped me to do just about anything that I needed to. I played on many sports teams and excelled at track and football. Running is what I was better at, and I had a great relationship with my track coach, and that’s where I learned many of my life lessons.

As a teenager, I learned some great work ethics by working in my Dad’s steel production shop, where I learned to weld, and use a lathe, and mill, etc., as well as general shop duties and preventative maintenance. I then went on to work in my first production job at the age of 17 at that company, and moved on from there.

I’ve traveled the world, and spent two and a half years living in England working with people of all nations as a troubleshooter for a manufacturing company. I did a lot of traveling over there and really enjoyed the people and the culture. I wish I could go back, and wouldn’t mind being a Merchant Marine, I’d rather go by ship! That work really taught me how much I enjoy working with people, and doing my best to make their lives better as well as having fun with them.

After returning home, and after the first 9/11, I realized that I needed to do something that mattered. I wanted to use my knowledge and experience to make things better for other people. And, I wanted to make the world a better place to live in. So I started out by becoming a police officer. After 7 years I realized that police work was great, but not the best fit for me. I wanted to make things better, not just stop problems and maintain the status quo. That led to more schooling and working with troubled youth.

It was a great job for a while, and we were able to help these kids with great programs like No Excuses Weekend camps, which taught them life skills and gave them one on one mentoring. However, government took that away from me and now I work for them in my current job.

Today I’m working a 9/5 job, and while it’s not exactly what I want to be doing, I think that I’m making a difference. Not a grand, world changing difference, but at least I’m helping. And, I’m working for a company that is all about helping people. I couldn’t be in a better place right now. But, I’m not in a place I want to stay. For the last five years I’ve been dreaming of something better and looking forward to doing what I can to make it happen. I haven’t figured out what that is yet, but it sure would be nice to be able to say the words, “To Not Go Back to Work”. Especially since the one thing we were taught as kids was the golden rule; “Work with your hands as much as possible.”

Along the way I’ve become a student of literature, history, and philosophy, and really enjoy sharing my knowledge with others. Reading, thinking, and talking about things like that are my passions. Another thing that I enjoy is teaching, whether that’s at the office or in a classroom. I’ve been fortunate to be able to travel all over the country and teach at several different companies for several years. That was fun and I enjoyed meeting and helping new people. It also gave me a chance to meet with people that I probably wouldn’t have met with otherwise, and that’s always nice. Also, I’ve learned to love the water by taking up sailing and rafting. I’m not a very good sailor yet, but I do love to be on the water. That includes the ocean, lakes, rivers, and even my own backyard pool.

All that being said, I’d like to be able to spend more time reading and learning new things that will help me learn how to change the world for the better. I’d also like to learn how to eat better, and work toward getting fit again. I could add so much more, but for now, that’s it.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am part Cherokee Indian, and my blood runs red as that of any other human being. I’m in my early 50s and grew up in Paris, Texas. Paris is a small town (people tell me), but I wouldn’t trade it for the world and after I found Jesus I wouldn’t trade it for any job. I enjoyed playing sports. It was always fun playing basketball and baseball even though I was not good. One time a friend had me to wear his loosing softball team’s uniforms to a game. He told me we would get a good laugh at the end of the game. The look on his face made me angry but I never said a word. After the game while the team was showering I had the equipment bag and laid it across his car’s open trunk and watered the bag with the hose then acted like I had no idea who did it. They had some fun with me, but I got it back on them at my own church when a football team went in to shower. It wasn’t water though, but tar. I enjoyed church and school because I had the freedom to do what I wanted there.

I had many struggles in school and had a hard time behaving. The older I got the harder it got for me to feel good enough to be called one. Many times I struggled to feel like I was enough to be their friend. It only seemed to get worse in the 8th grade. When I was in the 7th grade I started acting out more and getting into trouble. The reason was I had not yet found the Lord and I was feeling desperate because I was so lonely and no one understood my position.

I came into high school and found who I thought was my best friend in high school and we did everything together. We even put on a carnival for a fundraiser to get our band uniforms. I was still trying to get her to understand where I came from and why I was the way I was. I was hungry to be loved by her and others. I invited her to my church to show her she could be accepted anywhere by God. She said that I could be accepted, but she never came. We became very good friends and she helped me do some things that gave me a bad reputation and made it much worse in school and at home. I was starting to feel like an outcast. I was at the point of getting my own apartment when a church member asked me if I would preach at a funeral for a young lady who had killed herself. I was shocked because I had just called her a friend and when I called her at work I had gotten nothing but an answering machine. I must have called 20 times. Finally after about 2 weeks I got a hold of someone who told me the funeral was Friday night at her home church and I needed to be there. I was getting really nervous and was wondering what would happen. When I got to the funeral I started to tell myself she wasn’t really dead. She was just on vacation. I was trying to be stoic and seemed to be doing well. I got up to the front to give the eulogy and when I got to the part that I cried out to Jesus to give me a way to get there now, my whole life flashed before my eyes and I broke down and lost it. I ended up preaching the sermon, the eulogy, and the prayer. People were crying. At the end of the funeral a preacher stood up and asked who could stay and pray for her, I told her I could and so could he. Her parents were grateful to me for getting there but they didn’t know why. When I came to the end of that I had decided what I was going to do in life. I was going to leave for Oklahoma City and serve Jesus.

I left for Oklahoma City and got here in August of 1979. My sister wrote that she had found Jesus and had accepted him into her heart. I always asked her why she had waited so long because I was so lonely and needed someone to call on, someone who would understand me. Since I was a good student, I was able to get a scholarship to a Christian college. The football coach wanted me to walk on the football team, but I did not want to risk the one year he would allow me to keep my scholarship. I liked to play softball in high school, but I was not very good at it. I let the coach of the Christian school softball team know that I could play. She was glad and asked if I wanted to play or coach. I said I wanted to play, but coach because it pays better. I went to college and it was great. This time I did not get into trouble. I started to get into God’s word and fellowship with the other students there. When the good times came I just partied with the girls. The more I stayed out of trouble, the more they came to me for the truth about God. They wanted someone to show them the way to God. I am a shy person by nature, but I felt this was the way to help people. I wanted to help people out of their situations. I knew what it was like to be lonely and not understood. I found out how to pray, that I had to go to God’s word to find out what he expected from me. I have been struggling to do that ever since I got married because it took all my time. It also keeps me away from some things I shouldn’t be doing. I wish I could spend more time there and I will be doing that when I leave here. My life has been great since I found God. I understand what he wants me to do and how he has used me and people he has used to help me. I cannot wait to get back to Paris to start making a difference in the church. I am glad for the things I went through because without them I would not have met as many people as I have. I have received so many blessings by helping people. I would not trade it for anything. I thank God every day for what he has done in my life. It is not perfect and I wish it was, but I also wouldn’t know how to love others or accept others the way that I do if I didn’t have this background. Jesus made me what I am and I accept that completely.

Have you ever taken the time to research just how far a lie can travel? The majority of the information on the Internet is the result of someone’s opinion. Just because someone says something or quotes something without a name as to who it came from doesn’t necessarily make it true. Example: I was accused a while back of being a racist. I did not know the person that made that comment. I investigated and found that someone else had made the accusation and even though he had been caught and fired for making racist comments, that did not change the accusation against me. Why? Because this person could not find out where the quote originated. In fact, he couldn’t even find the original author. I did not have a computer or a facebook account at that time and so I had no way of the charge being proven. I had to deal with the false accusation for the next few months. But, I now have a computer and facebook page and that charge has been totally disproved.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I’m 52, I’m born in Calcutta, India. (Quiet and emotional) After 11 years, I did my MBA in Chemical Engineering in Calcutta, had married when I was 22, came to US in 1986 on F1, my husband sponsored my study, I worked as a manager in several large corporations from 1990, left engineering in 1998 and started R&D for bioinformatics, thinking of going back to school to change into life sciences (Like academics in Pharmacy, Bacteriology, etc.). Did not end up doing so. I changed jobs 3 times in US: working at Mosaid Inc, Neose Technologies (senior chemist, moved to Biotechnology) and Perkin Elmer (as a Marketing Associate, in Biomarkers) for 5 years each, then worked for my own consulting firm (Anish Consultancy) for two years. During that time, I decided to go for a MPhil in biochemistry in India and work in India. So, I did a MPhil in Biochemistry (Nov 2010-2011), finished it 6 months after I’d started it, my dissertation is in Microbiology (I do not know why I switched to microbiology; it was not my choice; in fact I had wanted to study in the US). I spent six months in a lab, I want to give others the time to do the research work (and that left me no time to publish); I could not get published because my dissertation was not interesting enough, but I did apply to a research assistant post at Tezpur University in Assam. (Emotional) I waited for five months for an answer, but there was no answer, so I returned to Hyderabad on Sept 2011 and, until today, I’ve not been able to find employment. (Sad). I looked for job at PG level at several places, like NIMS, SN Medical College in Uttar Pradesh, but they all told me that my skill set is inadequate (I did not know where to start, I found only a few jobs posted online (they are not teaching jobs) and when I appeared for them, I did not fare well because they required very specific knowledge, so I went on a search of State universities and Indian universities –private and public, to no avail. Last year, on the grounds that I had been out of work for a year, my H1-B visa was converted from H1-B to H4. My major skills are management and communication. A few years ago, I did MBA in Marketing & Chemical Engineering from the Indian Institute of Chemical Engineering (late 1990’s).

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am 62 years old. I grew up in southern New Jersey. After 50 years in this area, I believe I have a handle on the personality traits of people in southern New Jersey. My first job was as a lifeguard. I was always involved in some activity related to sports.

Most of my coworkers were blacks, although they were almost all men. That had a profound effect on me. For the first time in my life, I learned that black people were just people, and not the stereotypes people had implanted in my head. I’m amazed sometimes how naïve and ignorant I was about people in the 1960’s.

This is important because one issue, my father’s alcoholism, had the greatest effect on my personality. Alcoholism is an illness, not a moral failing. In my opinion, everyone should know that alcoholism is an illness, but because of our culture, that’s not what happens. I’ve seen people die of other illnesses, but not the way I saw my father with alcoholism. The slow death of an alcoholic destroys people.

When I was in junior high school, alcoholism became a problem for my father. His personality changed. He was abusive, both physically and mentally. He couldn’t hold a job. We were poor. We had no television, and we never went on vacation. In short, we were an unhappy family.

This went on for several years. He became a ward of the state for a time, but we always found a way to take him back because he was all we had. Eventually, I convinced my mother that if he went to jail, we would be better off, so in 1959, at the age of 12, I reported him to the police. The harassment and social embarrassment lasted for years afterward. He was unable to find steady employment, so the abuse continued. And because he was always angry, there were long periods of time when we could never be in the same room with him.

The incident that changed our relationship happened in 1968. We were talking on the phone, and he was angry at my mother and me. He said some really nasty things and he tried to get me to fight him. He got me riled up, and I threatened him with violence, so he said he’d come right over to my house to fight me. It was near the time I had to go to work, so I had to tell him goodbye. That’s what started it. I was carrying a bag in my hand and got very angry at him, so I dumped everything out of the bag and took a short temper tantrum. I raged for about 30 minutes. Afterward, I was exhausted.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I am in my 50s. I was born in New Jersey, and moved to Virginia with my mother, stepfather, and younger sister. We were not allowed to have pets. So, when I was 10, I took my first job as a babysitter and bought a hamster from a local pet store. When I was 12, I saved enough money to buy a beagle named Buster, a dog I still have to this day. But when my mother found out, I was sent away to live with my grandmother. And that made me who I am. I spent the next seven years running away from my grandmother and my “good” uncle, a good cop who sexually abused me until I was 19. Then I was raped, which led to three unwanted pregnancies. I got pregnant three times while trying to save a marriage with an emotionally, sexually, and verbally abusive husband. I had an abortion, and I still think it was wrong. But I became stronger and independent because of my experiences. I work at Friends of the Homeless now, which means I am an advocate for my residents. The things I enjoy are relaxing with a good book, learning new things, and the time I spend with my son.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I’m 51, which means I’ve been around long enough to have my own opinions, even if I am often wrong. I am a lifelong Catholic who had a rather difficult childhood. I worked as a welder before becoming a journalist, and I went to school at night. I have written for the Boston Globe, Boston Herald, New York Newsday, and the Philadelphia Daily News. I was briefly hired by the Boston Globe at the same time Anthony St. Amand, Joseph Connors, and many others were let go, a ploy that the Globe still uses to intimidate its employees. For many years, I taught full-time and operated a small arts collective called Utopian Revolution. I discovered political blogging in 2004, and my first site was Painful Truth. Later, I had a website called Dumb Society, which was nearly a clone of OxBlog. I live in Texas now. I was forced out of my job due to a combination of discrimination and the fact that you cannot protest free trade without automatically being labeled a Communist. In retirement, I spend far too much time on internet forums and podcasting about the evils of globalization.

While the “intent to perform work” test is clearly possible to fail, the burden of proof is on employers. Or rather, in the Great Socialist Utopia that is Canada, the state. Moreover, you may not actually “knowingly” intend to violate a regulation. You have to be able to convince your employer that your violation wasn’t the result of your own actions. Thus, the likelihood of an employee being deported under false pretenses is extremely low.

You can see where this is heading. After Homeland Security takes all these people from their families and jails them, they will need to give them a hefty legal defense. The government will be fighting with their lawyers, trying to prove that they “intended” to harm the country. Since prosecutors have a bias that favors guilty convictions, this will likely be a losing battle, at least in the court of public opinion.

Normally, people are detained as a matter of national security, but there is no legitimate security threat posed by these employees. It’s far more likely that the labor shortage that Trump is trying to deny will become even more acute, or that computer error will cause further chaos for people who are already being treated as criminals.

I hope that Jonathan Stone is right in his belief that we will be done with Trump by November. One hopes that common sense and decency will once again prevail.

Still, don’t get too comfortable, Jonathan. One bad president is capable of electing a second in just four short years.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I’m 60 years old. I’m from Oklahoma City. I was born and raised there. My father was in the military, so I also lived in Vietnam, and a few other states and various countries growing up. And I’m a Marine Corps veteran. I attended school in Oklahoma, and then I attended college in Virginia. I’ve lived in Virginia my entire adult life. I was blessed to have been selected to the Virginia Air National Guard in 2000 and attended basic training at Lackland Air Force Base in San Antonio, Texas.

What I’m most proud of is that in 2004, I deployed to Afghanistan and worked with the national security team at the U.S. Embassy there. That was a very interesting and powerful experience for me. I really learned a lot about life and myself. My military career has provided me with leadership opportunities, which were very rewarding. While the transition from military to civilian was quite an experience, I have been very blessed. It has been and still is a very interesting, rewarding and an awesome journey. I really enjoyed my time in the military, and I still enjoy being a part of the military community. My spiritual journey was a strong influence on me, and it still is. I am grateful for the experiences that life has afforded me.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I am a single, 56 year old woman living in New England. I was born in a poor New York City neighborhood, raised by a single mother who had lost her own mother to cancer at the same age I was at the time, 11. My father was a kind and supportive man, but he’d left my mother when I was a baby. My mother was independent and strong, and she taught me that life was made up of difficult choices. My upbringing was colorless, and I can’t honestly say that I remember being hungry for long, but I did learn that I had to be a good student and find a good job, because we would not be living with anyone else and I would have to be a working woman. My academic and artistic ability was my ticket to go away for college and that was all I focused on. I went to St. John’s University in Queens where I lived at home and tried to convince everyone that I was an artist in residence, but I have to admit I had no talent at all, so I worked very hard instead. I graduated at the top of my class, and really admired my campus intellectuals and their activist idealism. I was chosen to get the President’s award for community service and took an unpaid job in a Brooklyn elementary school. The Mayor of New York came to give out the awards. It was a very long day and I was cold and bored as I waited for hours and did a lot of bowing and scraping and being told what a good person I was, but I never forgot being cold and waiting for long hours in that cold hall. My school teachers were always very critical of my writing ability, and I still feel very insecure about it because of the way they would hand me back papers I had written. So when I looked at my next opportunity it was to become a social worker in public welfare. I remember thinking, I’ll really be needed there. They need teachers and social workers and I can have a big impact on people’s lives. I was assigned to Manhattan community service office and was sent to the Welfare Department for training. I thought it would be great. I was going to be helping poor people who needed help. But the training was not as I’d expected. I was assigned to Manhattan First when the lawsuits had been won to end segregative practices in the shelter system and we were in the process of making sure that they were integrated. Manhattan First was the bus depot and at the end of the day the waiting crowd that needed to stay in shelters were in dire need of housing. A complex system was being developed to keep everyone in the dorms for several days, allowing them to see their old friends, make new friends and socialize a little, do some shopping, and then send them where they needed to go. First of all, we were overwhelmed. We did not have enough beds. Secondly, we had to set priorities. Welfare was for girls and women. They would get the next available bed. But the working men would sit right on the front steps of the dorm all day. When the doors opened they were immediately let in so that they could shower and rest before heading out the door. Homeless girls and women were at the bottom of the list and often had to wait to sleep in shelters for a long time, and the courts finally forced the end of segregative practices.
