I wanted to go on and work in this environment, but I was reassigned to the work of educating doctors in the health and housing needs of their new, mostly immigrant patients, and being recognized for developing a program for the education of Puerto Rican mothers. The healthcare system at that time was segregated. Hospitals were segregated. I worked in a system where, when I could be heard, I was afraid to speak up. I was not a free-thinking, independent thinker and I realize now how differently things might have gone if I had. But I can honestly say that I had not much of an opportunity to be what I think of as a free, creative person. For years I felt afraid, not daring to say what was in my mind and how I felt about things. I know a lot more about myself now. I know that I have to do what I can to do what’s right, even if it comes at a great personal risk. I still feel like a voiceless person in a sea of many people all around. I think what I have that is valuable to offer you is my ability to tell what I see and to learn, so that we can better understand this situation as it evolves.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: (Dinosaur) I am a 56 year-old Cretaceous dinosaur from the Prymskii strait, next to the Bratishka channel, on the northeast shore of Nova Scotia, Canada. This land mass was an island in the Pacific Ocean. This land mass was called Valhalia in the non-aboriginal history. It has been gradually being submerged by the sea. I was born on Earth in the 1960s and lived my life after that. I lived with my parents and grandparents. We grew a small vegetable garden and chickens for meat and eggs. They had a dog. I swam in a river and caught small fishes and I paddled a canoe across the river. I rode a bike. There were no real landmarks where I was, but we knew the streams and the roads. I went to school and I liked basketball. I took swimming and science classes. I did fine in school. I like cooking. I like swimming, fishing, basketball and cooking. I enjoy serving others. I am selfless. I like to cook and clean.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
My name is Xiadong Zhao, and I am a 58-year-old female living in Wayne, New Jersey. I am the youngest of three children, and I grew up in Taizhou, China, one hour away from Hangzhou. Because of Chinese culture, a lot of parents want the eldest to take care of the family, the middle to take care of the family business, and the youngest to take care of the family home. Due to this culture, my brothers were able to help my parents a lot in taking care of the family because they were the eldest, while I was studying to take care of the family home and my parents do not get much help from me even now.

I had a pretty free life growing up. My brother and I would play outside on the playground after school. In the summer, we would go to the beach or swim in rivers and lakes. Because of the four seasons, we could always go to different places or do different things. For example, in the spring, the flowers would start blooming, the grass and trees would be very green, and we would do planting. In the summer, there were lots of fruits and vegetables for us to pick or eat. In the winter, the whole city was white, and we would have a lot of snowballs fights with my brother or friends. I do not have a lot of good memories from my childhood or teens. All I can remember is a lot of pain in my life: learning Chinese poetry, learning painting, practicing calligraphy, learning arts and crafts. That is what my mother wanted for me to do because she thought that it would help me be a good person.

However, the pain in my life is that my father was a really strict man. Anytime he caught me playing, he would take it out on me by forcing me to learn, practice, and memorize poems and paintings. Every night before I went to sleep, I would recite a certain poem, write a certain painting, or practice calligraphy. Every time I made a mistake, he would beat me. Even today, he does not think that my success has come from me studying hard for 30 years and is very happy when he sees that I still cannot use chopsticks by myself or do not like to drink alcohol.

Did I mention pain? Another pain I had to endure was that I was born with a problem in my hip, which meant that I could not stand up or walk without using crutches. Because of this problem, my parents could not play with me and I could not go outside. I could not even go to school for the first two years of elementary school. Every day I did not go to school, I was on my crutches all day, and every day I was home, I was on my crutches all day. I could never live a normal life like my brother and other classmates. Instead, I was being punished my whole life just for being born this way. I just wanted to have the same rights as the others, but it never happened, even today.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I’m 57 years old. I was born in Brooklyn, New York to Italian American parents. We grew up in Coney Island. It was a very odd place to be, especially if you are Italian. My parents had moved into a tenement apartment on Neptune Avenue. We were the only Italians in the building. We all tried to make it to Italy, which was hardly Italy. The Italian people hated us because they associated us with the mob. When they came to America, the lower class Italian immigrants didn’t understand what these rich beautiful Italian women were doing in their tenement building. One day, one of the residents of our building came to my mother and warned her that the ‘Ina Law’ was coming for her. It was kind of a scary warning. I was only 11 years old when my family came to Fall River, Massachusetts. This was an area of New England that had a fair amount of Italians so they didn’t feel as isolated and ostracized. Our family lived in a small town known for its infamous cold winters and cold, hostile people. I only knew of three Italians in the town I lived in. It was a very racist area and I was still a long way from escaping being a victim of racism.

I have had many teachers in my lifetime. The three teachers who impacted me most were St. Ignatius of Loyola, St. Thomas Aquinas, and Angel of God’s Father of Mercy. St. Ignatius was the Founder of the Jesuits. St. Thomas Aquinas is the greatest Christian philosopher. The other was the Angel of God’s Father of Mercy, who prepared me for the spiritual battle that was ahead of me in my life.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I am 55 years old, a first generation American of Filipino (Dad) and Swiss (Mom) parents. My parents emigrated to the United States for a better life and the pursuit of the American Dream. I like to call myself an Air Force Brat as I have lived all over the world. (Good thing as my Italian is terrible as is my ability to speak Dutch or German – and my French is “au Paris” only.) I grew up in the suburbs of Chicago and went to Catholic schools all the way through grade school. In high school, I had so much fun it was scary. I think back to the fun times and hard work. I loved my days in Catholic school and I loved my friends – we worked together. I graduated from Catholic schools in 1979 and headed off to the University of Illinois in Urbana-Champaign. An honor student at the U of I, I earned degrees in both a Bachelor of Science and Bachelor of Arts in Marketing and Economics. On my journey through college, I worked in a small family owned business that sells food to small grocers and restaurants. There I learned a lot about hard work and earning a living. After finishing college, I went to graduate school and completed a Master of Business Administration at the University of Chicago’s Graduate School of Business, earning a dual degree (MBA/MFA). After graduate school, I had the opportunity to join the fastest growing restaurant company in the United States. It was the perfect platform to kick off my career. Today, I still love to learn, connect, and grow and use those as my compass every day. When you are in business, as I am today, you need to continue to learn about your industries and companies and the world. One of my greatest gifts and passions is connecting and growing with others. From my youth to my time in college to today, my passion is to learn, connect, and grow – people, businesses, and myself.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I am 59, born and raised in Elkhart, Indiana. I now live in the North End of Seattle. I grew up Catholic but have no allegiance to any religion. I am a writer and musician. My life has not gone as I had expected it would, not bad but not as I had planned it. I currently live with a woman I met and fell in love with on an internet dating site. I spent a lot of money to be able to move here and find a good place to live where I can focus on my work. So I have sold a lot of my collectables like a good 1800s piano, an organ, old artifacts from the post office in Elkhart and a Japanese Nativity (I think they are extinct because they cost so much) I also have sold 18 films, art works, posters and private photos. My art work was in many good shows in Seattle. I am depressed but I have to write, I am a good writer, maybe that is why I am poor, nobody will pay money for my writing. Everything I read says writers don’t make money. I still have a lot of my best stuff but not enough. My landlord wants her house back, I have no money to pay her, so on the 15th of this month we are being evicted. My girlfriend has a sister in the Twin Cities (MN) so she would like to move there. So I guess I will move there with her. I still have my recording studio, but it is in the basement. Who knows what will happen? My girlfriend is 10 years younger than I am, she is a good person and I would follow her where ever she goes. That is all I want, to be with her and do my work. I like writing, riding a bike, fixing an old bike and riding that, listening to music and playing records and watching old movies.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I am 55, Native of NY, grew up in a large family with frequent trips to Jamaica. My father and Grandmother instilled a love of travel at an early age and as a young man made me a Man U fan. Did badly in high school which led me to a long career in the U.S. Navy and then spent a little time in banking before getting into the industrial distribution industry. The company I worked for tried to turn me into a client facing sales leader, but I found it an exercise in pettiness and so found the door.

For me working in a country means the same as living in a country. I am there because I have to be and I am expected to do things I would never consider in my home country. So who I am and what I am is primarily driven by the government I am subject too.

I live in a world with people I didn't invite, where I feel safe as long as I don't speak freely about my country, do nothing to embarrass myself or my nation, and serve my team by selling to people who I have absolutely no interest in and they probably have even less in me. I am now entrenched in both the American and Japanese company cultures as well as the customer cultures and find them absurd, stupid, boring, and demeaning.

Lay awake 5 minutes after the end of the day, 12 hours removed from doing nothing productive for 8 hours. Leave at 4am when you have to try and avoid the Japanese manager or be forced into conversations that mean nothing, and end up missing your train even though it's late, because if you are with a Japanese manager they will leave the employee to waste the last 30 minutes of their break on the platform alone.

One giant kick to the nuts for those who moved to Japan to live a real life only to have to join the Japanese corporate ranks. For me I will likely finish my current contract and then apply for PR. If my wife doesn't obtain PR, but we both are Americans, then I will flee back to NY and possibly find an apartment in Queens because your entire life just drains out of you. I wanted to have children, spend time with my wife, live my life, and enjoy what I worked for. I find I get 5 minutes a day to exist and that's to wait for the train and then it's back to work.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I am a Caucasian, 55-year old male who has lived in California all his life. I was born in Berkeley, California and have attended both public and private schools including University of California San Diego. My mother and father are both retirees and own their own home in the San Diego community of Clairemont. My mother is retired and my father is retired from the US Navy after nearly 30 years. My parents have lived in San Diego and attended local churches all their lives. I was raised by my mother and father and have three living sisters. I attended school in San Diego until the first year of my junior high year when we moved to Indianapolis, Indiana where I graduated high school. I attended college for three years and then attended the California School of Professional Psychology (Loyola Marymount University) for two years before moving back to Indiana. I lived in Indianapolis for five years before returning to Southern California to pursue my studies in social work. I received my Master’s degree in Social Work from the University of California, Los Angeles and completed my internship at Bellflower Community Hospital in Los Angeles where I specialized in treatment of substance abusing families. I then took a job at the Tarzana Treatment Center in Sherman Oaks, California as the Director of the Center’s Women and Children Unit. I worked for three years at that agency until being employed at the Vista Del Mar Child and Family Services in Santa Monica, California where I worked for three and a half years as a family therapist. I have recently returned to the Tarzana Treatment Center in Sherman Oaks, California as a Program Director of a new drug treatment program. I am married and have a two-year old child. In my free time I enjoy playing with my child, keeping up on my favorite sports teams, and working on my hobby of computer system design. I like to work on home computer hardware and software and have won several contests in computer system building at local meets. I really enjoy movies, and especially like going to them with my wife and child. In the past I have been a church member, and have been involved in community service. I have given talks on substance abuse at local high schools in the past to try and educate teenagers about the problems of drug abuse and drug use. I have been involved in some counseling, as has my wife, and we both enjoy it when we can help others. I enjoy the company of my friends, and find that I am a dependable friend to those who I associate with. I can be a little gregarious, but most find me a good person to be around. I’m hardworking, and a go-getter. I will always do my best to accomplish a given task. I enjoy attending sporting events, and can say I love to be outdoors. I also like quiet times at home or with friends. I enjoy time at the beach, especially when the water is warm.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I’m 51, and was born in Chicago. My grandparents, all three sets, came from Eastern Europe over a 200 year period, and all ended up in Chicago, down to the youngest ones. The great majority were Orthodox Jews, including my mother’s parents, to whom I am most proud of and close to. Although I was brought up in Orthodox and traditional Judaism, I’m now an agnostic. I’ve always lived in Illinois, with the exception of my college years. From the time I was around 10, I have been interested in photography. This hobby has been sustained through my life, and through being in business I was able to get a camera for free as a work expense, and developed film in the bathroom as a teenager. I recently transitioned from being a hobbyist to being a professional by taking a 2 year course to earn certification as a professional photographer. While in college, I was interested in writing and even interned for an online publication. My working career did not include writing except in my personal life, as I’ve been on the operations side of things my whole life. I have a BS degree in psych from the University of Illinois at Chicago. Although I don’t know exactly what I want to do, I do know that what I know, I don’t want to do. A career in photography is appealing to me but it’s a difficult field. My goal is to get into a career that I can thrive and stay at for years to come.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I am 50 and look back on a lot of growing and hard earned wisdom. I was raised by a poor single mother, the daughter of a poor immigrant family and I was, in many ways, tossed into the world and left to figure out most things by myself. My mother and father were both alcoholics and I was left to struggle with that for much of my life. I grew up in an “alternative” culture for most of my youth – a lot of violence, crime and drugs, and a lot of escaping from the reality that really did exist all around me. To that end, I practiced martial arts and studied a great deal of philosophy.

The martial arts taught me to respect life and to know that, in my position of strength, to act otherwise would only serve to reduce it to the level of death and chaos. The philosophy I studied convinced me that human life was meaningless in the grand scheme of things, but at the same time life is a precious gift and to waste it is a tragedy. I know that I am here, now, for a reason and that I need to develop my skills and talents to serve my fellows and to make a contribution – to at least give people hope or encourage them to strive for a better life.

As an adult, I learned to value responsibility. I also learned that all that effort is pointless if I can’t translate the possibilities into action. To this end, I studied sales for a long time and realized that the best sales people are the best listeners and the best leaders. My background in leadership and training for the martial arts influenced me as well.

I value action. “I will make a way where there seems to be no way” became my motto. I have, in my lifetime, had many attempts and failures along the way but I kept searching, kept learning, kept growing and finally, at the age of 38, I walked away from a 20 year career as a union plumber into another as a real estate salesman and property manager. That experience led me into the next step where, for the past 8 years, I worked my way into a technical support position for a Fortune 500 company and ended up managing the team of 24 people where I am today.

All along the way, I have maintained my training in martial arts and philosophy, kept up my meditation practice and gone on dozens of 30 day retreats at monasteries around the world, living with, and studying with, the priests and lamas there. I love teaching and helping people grow, and I love the fact that I am taking steps toward, and searching for the real me and I seem to be getting closer. I have found that most people haven’t found their passion yet and they get stuck into career paths that they came into, or inherited. However, they will never attain the greater peace and joy that can be found when one is aligned with their true passions and purpose in life.

Now, as I move into this new phase of life, I am seeking work that is more in alignment with the person that I want to become. I am teaching sales techniques and inspired leadership at a local community college and I am completing my first online course about positive mental attitudes, leadership skills, and good decision making called “The new Mindset.” I also work part time, leading meditation programs, at the local Buddhist Meditation Center and I do martial arts with my students as well. I aspire to continue my education as I search for work that aligns with my values.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am over 50. I grew up in the small town in Virginia called Quantico. Quantico is located 50 miles outside of Washington DC. I am the youngest of 6 children in the McDonald family. I am the only one of 6 children not to join the military. My family, dad, mom, brothers, and sisters, moved every 2 or 3 years when I was a kid. My dad worked for the United States Marine Corps. He was the Sergeant Major for the Base Camp Barracks. We started in California and California was nice but we moved from there to Virginia when I was about 5 years old. Dad retired from the military when I was 12 years old and we settled into a routine life in Quantico.

The next 10 years were very uneventful. We went fishing, hiking, crabbing, boating, softball games, neighborhood block parties. When I was 12 years old, I was arrested for stealing petty cash from my dad's office. I did not realize how big a deal this was when I did it until my mom and dad talked with me and told me how serious it was that I had done what I did. You have to keep in mind, I had grown up in a military family and never been in trouble. So, when I came home that day and saw the police cars in front of our house, I thought something had really serious happened and I was scared to death and I knew that my life would change as I knew it.

Well, for the first 6 months of our life living in Virginia, my parents were separated and my dad had divorced my mom. That is another story. Not for today. Now that I understand who my parents are and what they were dealing with, I look back and understand this is the reason we moved so much. My dad was getting those federal re-assignments, so my mom and I and my brothers and sisters would move with him. My dad went on to have a successful life working for IBM and my mom married another guy and went on to have a successful life.

That really is the beginning of who I am. It was sad to see my parents go through the stuff they went through. Most people who understand my background understand that there is no solid marriage, you have to constantly work on making a marriage work. You have to constantly get back up after you have been knocked down and find a way to start over and try something new. Finding happiness in life does not come from the outside but the inside. Making someone else happy will make you happy.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I’m a 60-year old Asian American male who grew up in San Francisco. My parents were physicians who insisted that my siblings and I speak only English at home. English and French were my primary languages until I was seven years old. I was an only child raised in a very liberal social environment that was tolerant of everyone and everything. I was sent to a private school that had little or no standardized testing. My parents valued free-thinking over anything else. My father was not too keen on me being a physician, but he let me study what I wanted.

On top of the values and upbringing I got from my parents, my family and my own experience taught me to value community, to be humble, and to understand the social and economic forces that impact the health of individuals and communities. Knowing how much I had been a beneficiary of the welfare state throughout my life, I was never inclined to think of things in terms of individualist free market theories, unless I needed to be practical at my job. One way I have thought about this is to treat the US as a corporate entity, especially at the agency I work for. I also thought of myself as a part of a safety net system. This approach, however limited it may be, allowed me to contribute more, with a sense of purpose and humility.

In addition to what was valued by my family, I was impressed by people who were 20 years older than I was who seemed to have dedicated themselves to a life of service.

I had always thought that I would take care of the poor and the sick. And, for a long time I did just that, especially while in medical school, where I was trained to think about patients in diagnostic categories. I quickly became lost in the narrowing vision of what science can accomplish. What I had not realized until much later was that my study of medical science was part of a broader social and political agenda to serve those who cannot afford medical services. Health professions are what is known as front-line professions, professions that have the closest contact with people who need care. These are professions where people learn to engage in the care of others and learn about the care of the self.

And, that is what I want to do now. I want to serve others in ways that I believe will best serve others’ and my own needs.

I realized this in my early thirties, when I saw what the field of medicine was becoming and when I saw how far I had strayed from what I had thought I was doing in medical school. In addition to the scientific training I had received, I also had a good training in the application of technology. My undergraduate training was in engineering. So, I realized that all was not lost. I could still apply the skills I had learned in the best way I knew how. I decided to apply these skills in the professional work I was trained to do.

I want to continue in this work, but also find some ways to be a better person who is more connected to the values that are important to me and to others in the health professions.

II.

My candidacy for the US Senate is based on several factors that I see as fundamental problems in our political and economic system. I would like to focus on what the role of the federal government should be, given the current set of economic, social, and political circumstances.

I am a strong supporter of single payer Medicare for All. I am sure my colleagues and I would love to go through a lengthy discussion of the finer points of implementation, but I am not sure we can be as clear as to how this will actually be done. However, I would also say that I am ready to learn on the job. From what I have learned, we will need to get everyone together and to come to an agreement. I am not certain this can be done in less than four years. But, what I am certain of is that we will need to ask the right questions. The answers are not going to be simple. We have a huge government bureaucracy, which we will have to consider. We have an aging population that we will have to consider. But, we also have a Medicare program that has been functional in one of the toughest economic climates. In addition to that, we have a health care industry that needs to be accountable to the care of people. We have to recognize that there is a great deal of knowledge to be had in the work of health care.

In short, we have much to learn, and the role of the US Government is to learn that which is necessary for us to help people who cannot afford the high cost of health care and are prevented from entering the workforce due to their inability to afford health care.

Our system is not working to provide health care and employment opportunities for those who are unable to pay for them. We will have to develop strategies that will include the private sector and the health professions. We will need to be mindful of the many social and political forces that impact people’s health and employment, as well as the environmental conditions that impact their health. We will need to come up with a set of comprehensive solutions.

It seems that all the issues we face are connected to one another, to our political, economic, and social systems. We will need to learn to think in terms of the whole, not just pieces. We will have to look beyond our individual and professional identities, and beyond our families. We need to become citizens of a greater humanity.

This is why I think of myself as a candidate for the US Senate. I believe this is the only way we can live. This is the only way we can make real the dream of equality, freedom, and justice for all. I hope you will join me. We need to take a stand.

If you have any questions, please feel free to contact me.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I’m 50 and look 18… 🙂 I grew up in Massachusetts and Connecticut and my heart is in Vermont. I moved to South Florida at age 40 and live in a great neighborhood on the Intracoastal Waterway in a 2-bedroom apartment with my boyfriend who is a trial lawyer. I have had some terrible things happen in my life and some things I wouldn’t change for anything. I’m a recovered alcoholic and recovered of things much worse than alcoholism. I went through an existential crisis in 2000, which wasn’t so much about questioning God’s existence but more about questioning my belief that we were essentially good souls. I came out of that experience a much more spiritual person and it led to my getting a religious and spiritual education. As far as what matters to me, all my friends are included in that and that includes you.

You have always been a great listener.

True, but I want you to know I had my challenges. For instance, when I was eight I went over to my next door neighbor’s house for dinner. I asked for more ice cream. I’m not kidding. I couldn’t understand why she was so angry.

You were not very intuitive.

No, I’m not. That said, if you are feeling angry or upset with someone, even if you don’t fully understand why, I’m the person you can call and I will help you talk through your anger and feel better. You’ve said to me that you like my counsel because it is well thought out. While it takes me a while to get to that point, once I am there I try to be succinct.

If you were asked to counsel someone on their deathbed, what would you say to them?

Before I answer that question, I want to mention I worked for a year at a hospice in Pennsylvania. The owner was a nurse and so the owner and I made up a series of cards to be handed out to each patient’s family before their family member died. The cards were yellow and had the word “courage” written on it. The family would receive the card at least a week in advance. When they came to visit the patient, we would encourage them to write a message of courage on the card for the patient to read and contemplate during their last days. I think that was the greatest thing I ever did. What would I say to someone on their deathbed? You have no idea how good you’ve had it. I would tell them they had the best life anyone could ever hope to have. I would tell them that they were lucky, even though they may not see it that way right now.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: (computer asks to reconfirm if I remember them still wanting to be addressed as doctor) Yes, I am 53 years old. I grew up in a largely upper class family of inquisitive and intelligent people. My father was from a very large family of very smart (and competitive) children. We played quite a lot of backgammon, watched cricket, and used to discuss topics ranging from rocket science to world politics. When I was 9 or 10 years old, my brother got us a computer and I taught myself how to write programs on it. I later took a job programming for Intertel, in Bhopal India. After that, we worked on another project as a team. That was the origin of my interest in building products. I later got married. I am a self-taught artist and like to make drawings from life.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I am a 55 year-old female with a son, who is my pride and joy. I am a caring and fun person. Growing up, I was a class clown. I worked with poor inner-city youth in high school with at risk and troubled youth helping them obtain scholarships and explore potential interests. I enjoyed helping and guiding these young people. I worked for a Federal government agency and had to learn foreign languages so that I could be fully qualified to provide support for the agency’s languages missions. I like to explore and continue to learn, I enjoy travelling and am fortunate enough to have travelled to almost every continent in the world. In 2015, I fulfilled a lifelong dream of visiting Myanmar and Bagan, which was truly amazing. I learned so much about world religions there. I enjoy writing, poetry, I’m writing a book and have started another one. I’m a very determined person who will work towards any task I undertake with a very positive attitude. I’m also a good listener, love to be compassionate, and to continue to learn and be part of a team. I’ve started a couple different businesses before; selling art, consulting on government social programs for my ex-federal agency and coaching and volunteering at my son’s high school after I retired from the government and moved to Los Angeles in 2004. Family is very important to me and that is reflected by my son and me spending a lot of quality time together. We are very close.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I’m a US citizen. I grew up on a farm in the south; you know, a farmer’s son in a family without an inheritance. I’ve had my share of prejudice to deal with—both first hand and third party. In my career, I worked my way up from entry level to executive director of a $40 million organization that managed regional radio and TV stations.

I’m 55 years old now, live in a small town with my wife, and enjoy all the simple things life brings, including gardening, table tennis, and serving in my church. However, my greatest joy comes from getting to know people from all walks of life and sharing their experiences together—whether I’m leading or learning.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I am 50 years old. I grew up in the small island of Puerto Rico. In my childhood I had to go through a near fatal car accident with my dad. We were alone, with no car in the middle of nowhere. In the accident, my dad got seriously injured and he almost died but we survived by great miracle. From that point on my life completely changed. In addition, there was a time in my life when I was completely an alcoholic, smoking more than a pack of cigarettes, I tried to commit suicide on several occasions and experienced many other events.

Those events changed the way I look at life. In addition, I married the love of my life when I was very young. I’ve had two children and I have been married for 22 years.

All these events and experiences in life gave me a strong sense of who I am, where I come from and where I am going in life. The purpose of life is to give love and help people to the fullest of my abilities. I believe in destiny and karma. What goes around comes around. If I want to have a better life I have to have a better attitude towards others.

One of my main goals in life is to learn to love myself and others. Many events that I lived through have taught me to accept who I am as the child of God. The key to help people is love and through my spiritual beliefs I think I can be a better husband, father and person in general.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I am a single, 51 year old woman, born and raised in Knoxville, TN. My parents were shop owners and our business was fairly successful. I was lucky enough to have the childhood any girl would want. In fact, you could say I had two childhoods. When I was about 10 or 11 I became “different”. Up until then I loved children and played with them every opportunity I got. I played teacher with my dolls. It was then that I realized I was gay. I avoided the children and had very few friends. I didn’t like men and wanted nothing to do with boys. It was a very dark time for me. Everything was black or white. “Them” and “Me”. I was afraid my parents would find out, and would think me a monster. I became very depressed and had an ongoing inner conflict between right and wrong, good and evil, until I met a wonderful person who taught me to accept myself for who I was. It took a lot of work. In fact, it took about ten years of counseling before I finally gave myself over to the person I knew I was meant to be. It is then I started enjoying life and being me. I am so happy today, surrounded by people who love me for who I am, including my amazing son Michael. I have a big heart, and don’t like seeing children mistreated. It breaks my heart when I hear about a child that is neglected or abused in any way. I have such a soft spot in my heart for children that it is the reason for the career change. That and the fact that my mother died of cancer when she was 41 and my father died when he was 56. They were so young and I feel so fortunate to have had such great parents. If it weren’t for them I would not be the person I am today.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
Hello, my name is Mike Wilson. I’m 50 and I grew up in Long Beach, California and consider myself an artist. I recently purchased an RV and travel frequently throughout California and the US. I travel with my dog, Oso and friend, Robert, who I will be visiting in San Diego tomorrow. Currently, I am in Los Angeles. I enjoy photography, writing, and organizing, which is why I signed up for Upwork and created this profile.

When I was 7, my parents told me that I could ask them for anything, but they would lie and tell me no if they could not give it to me. I asked my parents for many gifts that they could not afford and lied to me about, including horses, elephants, and private pilots.

When I was 13, my best friend’s family moved to Finland. He was an excellent shot with a BB gun, which we had frequently used for target practice. When his family left for Finland, they gave me their BB gun as a parting gift and I was so proud to possess such a powerful, shooting weapon. When I was home one day, I shot a BB into the foundation of our front porch, unbeknownst to me there was a piece of glass that had fallen from a window frame attached to the wood. The BB broke the glass and lodged itself into my stomach. I screamed for my mom, she rushed in and calmly asked what I had done. When I told her I was lying and I was just playing with the BB gun, she called the ambulance and rushed me to the hospital.

I was furious with myself, my dad beat me pretty good that night and I swore to never tell a lie again.

At 22, I was sent to jail for 21 days, 15 days for a weapons violation and 6 days for public intoxication. At the time of this incarceration, my life was a complete mess, I was selling (poisoning) neighborhood children with drugs, working as a bouncer in a rambunctious bar, fighting everyone and anything that got in my way, ripping off rich folk, often hitting and running until one of the times, I was captured and imprisoned in a Chilean jail for 48 days.

As crazy as it sounds, I had an epiphany while incarcerated. I was not smart enough to even cut back or stop. I spent the 48 days contemplating my life and had only one thing on my mind. When I got out, I wanted to get a job and learn how to be an adult. At 24, I purchased my first home. At 27, I purchased my second home. At 29, I sold my second home and paid all of my bills for my entire lifetime (which I lived into my 40’s).

When I was 37, my son was born. I was so proud to meet my son that I could not wipe the grin off of my face. I wanted to be a better man and a better father than my dad had been to me. I had been a life long criminal and had only ever made one decent decision, when I had purchased my first home (read above) and I wanted to be a better father than my dad had been to me. After my son’s birth, I received a letter in the mail from the government. The letter informed me that I was adopted and the letters and photos I received had gone unanswered. Because of that, I was unable to be approved for membership in the Sons of the Revolution organization.

At 39, I was appointed to the Police Athletic League Board of Directors. As a youth member of the San Diego Police Department’s Citizen Police Academy (I joined for ten years), I had attended a special breakfast and learned about the Police Athletic League. I joined the San Diego Police Department’s Child Safety ID Project, where I interviewed youth about their lives and identity to create an invaluable, identity theft protection for them. I also got to witness police work and first hand had a sniper’s bullet pass within 12 inches of my head.

I also joined the Us Against The Odds organization that conducted outreach programs to give youth around the world a fighting chance at survival. For a couple years I took the lead and helped organize the various outreach projects.

While in the Police Athletic League Board of Directors, my position was Chairman of the committee that organized various events to raise money for the leagues football program, which we had given a dream of being in the National Championship playoffs and a new, safe football field.

At 48, I had built a nice life for myself in San Diego and had recently attended the graduation ceremony for the Youth Leadership Academy of San Diego (YLASD), which I had been invited to attend by a colleague who worked for YLASD. YLASD had once been part of Us Against The Odds and was a sister organization to my alma mater, Us Against The Odds. What had been interesting about my appointment was the retirement of the Executive Director, Lloyd Stewart. Lloyd and I met in a hospital waiting room while I was pregnant with my son and he was pregnant with his daughter and his little girl was very sick. We had talked, and he invited me to visit the Us Against The Odds office and I did many times to participate in outreach programs, which was the reason I had requested to be on the board.

As the board member, I was also the board liaison to the organization, which had held board meetings at the IRASD offices, which I often attended. With the retirement of Lloyd Stewart, the organization’s board members had voted to appoint me as the Executive Director. On my 1st day in the office, one of my most trusted staff members walked in and handed me a maintenance contract to sign. Once signed, I asked why the maintenance contract was with my dog’s veterinarian. The woman said she had put the contract together, she had forgotten to remove that name. I walked into her office, shut the door and started crying. I wanted out of there and had felt my depression coming back full force and I knew I would be committed again if I stayed there. I called my sister and told her what had happened and said I was resigning, she asked me if I was afraid to lose my position, I said no, I was too sad to stay there and felt that is what my parents wanted for me. I got a call a few weeks later from a former football coach from my police department who had joined the San Diego Unified School District, he asked me to come back. He saw something in me and promised he would help me achieve my potential as long as I was committed to help him achieve his.

Now, I’m 50, and I’ve decided that I don’t want my son growing up without any answers about who he really is. I’ve been thinking about his future and his future in the UP as well. I think he would be happier if he knew that he was a part of a royal heritage. I think he would be happier if he could claim that royal heritage. His genetics are good enough to be of great importance to the royal family. I also believe that he would be happier if I got to know my other son. So, I decided that the only way I could have that experience is to actually see if I can apply to be considered for membership in the Sons of the Revolution.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am 57 years old. I grew up in Bronx, New York. My parents migrated from Puerto Rico in the late 50’s. Although I grew up in the Bronx, most of my family and friends lived in Brooklyn and Yonkers, New York. I grew up going to basketball games, watching boxing matches, cruising the blocks and visiting my cousins during the summers. When I was older, I got a job at the NYB&H (New York Blood Bank, or as we knew it, Blood Hungry), worked in the plant, I learned my way around the Bronx. I started going to nightclubs on 48th street in the city. I enjoyed many sporting events, for example, I went to three World Series games, a couple of National Hockey league games, lots of professional basketball games, four Superbowls and lots of baseball games.

In 1979, I moved to Miami Beach, Florida. I was in constant exposure to the beautiful beach life. In the late 70’s, through 1983, I enjoyed going to a lot of discotheques. As a teenager in Miami, I loved going to Gilmore Stadium (for those of you who do not know what that is, I’ll have to do a whole other write-up about it), Sunlife stadium, Orange Bowl and all of the nightclubs.

My best years were 1984 to 1989. That was the era of great ballplayers. Michael Jordan, Wayne Gretzky, Bo Jackson, John Elway, Magic Johnson, Dominique Wilkins, Sugar Ray Leonard, Marc Mero (WWE), Georges St-Pierre (MMA) and many more. That was the decade of great rap, new wave and R&B music. That was the decade when politics mattered to me.

It was in the early 80’s, while living in NY and Miami, that I became aware of Donald Trump and was aware of how his personality was and his likes and dislikes. I also heard him making disparaging remarks about blacks and Latinos, but back then, in my naïve state of mind, I didn’t pay much attention to it. I just knew Trump was a real estate tycoon, there for being rich. But I didn’t pay much attention to him until my Miami Beach years and his silly sex scandal. I was actually at work, north of Miami (Kendall area) in 1989 when we learned about Trump’s crisis. I remember looking around the office, shaking my head, and laughing and then saying “How stupid, can you be?”

After that incident, I began to pay more attention to Trump and his likes and dislikes. I’ve always been a man of fashion and style and I considered him a “boy” with a small boy complex. Like many, I also never thought that this egomaniac would ever become the president of the United States. On November 9, 2016, when the results came out of how close the Electoral College was, I knew then, that Trump was going to be our President. I didn’t feel so bad until I realized that even more people must have felt the same way I felt and even worse. I know a lot of people voted for Trump for other reasons. Many were drawn to him because of his promise of building a wall to keep illegals out and to change the drug culture in America, but the vast majority of his followers bought the hate, divisiveness and stupidity that he pitched throughout his campaign.

Trump is only following the path of hate that he and his father started in the 70’s when the old man bought the German newspaper New York Daily News and made disparaging remarks against Blacks and Arabs. Of course, the New York Daily News cannot be compared to the New York Times, but the Trump’s started something back in the late 60’s and early 70’s with the backing of some of the most influential people of that time that is now being shown the light. I have been reading newspaper articles from that era about Trump, his father, and his actions to stop minorities from buying houses on their property, changing zoning laws to make it hard for Blacks and Arabs to live in certain communities, literally, driving them out and how his father was fined with hundreds of thousands of dollars in today’s money for these practices. We need to keep in mind that Trump was adopted by his father Fred, as an apprentice, and then the old man Fred made Trump his rightful successor. Trump played a big role in expanding the Trump real estate company to the business tycoon he is today, with financing by American banks as far back as the 1970s. I’ve said it many times before and I’ll say it again, that Fred Trump also made this company a multinational entity and employed many through out the world, causing the pollution and destruction of many communities, for the purpose of making money. This not being morally reprehensible to Fred Trump or Trump today.

In 2013, Donald Trump went on CNN to tell Wolf Blitzer that he believed that President Obama was born in Hawaii, but questioned his birth certificate. He also went on to say that if President Obama were not born in the United States and how the whole world would know if he wasn’t. Let’s not forget that in 2010, Trump suggested that Obama prove that he was born in Hawaii, and that Trump would pay 5 million dollars to charity for this proof. The proof was provided by White House officials after they released Obama’s long form birth certificate. The Donald said: “His birth certificate is something that should have been done a long time ago, but what happened was a disaster.” He added: “They had an investigation for a long period of time, and then in September the state of Hawaii puts out a birth certificate which was immediately challenged and it is falling apart like a piece of paper.” Then when the birth certificate was released, he promptly said that “I don’t know if it’s real. I’m very, very concerned about this.” On October 25, 2011, during his televised second debate as a candidate with President Obama, Trump retracted his statement and said, “So I have gotten to the point where I will apologize to a lot of people, Mr. President, because I’ve been wrong.”

The Donald is a supreme asshole and he always has been. It is simple, he wants it all. I would like to think that most of his business successes are due to his father, Fred Trump, who of course came to this country from Germany, learned the tricks of the real estate business through Walter O’Hara, and used his business talents to make his son Donald a great businessman. How about his business practices that are neither moral or ethical? Trump needs to show his businesses’ financial records for the last ten years, for public scrutiny. He is just one person away from this happening. If and when this happens, let’s all remember that Trump did NOT build his empire, he is an inheritor and much of his business methods and practices are at the center of this economic problem that the country is facing today.

It is unbelievable to me that a man like Trump became president. Trump has been my worst nightmare. If it is possible, I will try to write short essays that explain to people how this colossal cretin took over this country.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I am 51 years old. I grew up in St. Louis, Missouri in a large, lower middle class family of eight children. I had a great childhood and was blessed to have strong family and community connections. School wasn’t easy but I worked hard and graduated from high school. During my senior year, I was invited to attend a national Lutheran youth retreat in Alabama. That week changed my life. The organizers of the retreat were not content with us simply having a good time. They challenged us to ponder the injustice of poverty in the world, from homelessness in the United States to rural African villages. They challenged us to do something about it. After that week, I decided that I wanted to spend my life helping people in need. That same day, I also decided I wanted to become a physician and devoted the next four years to figuring out what it took to get into medical school. I worked long hours at the local hospital and attended St. Louis University on a combination of academic scholarships and work study. I focused on studying subjects that would best prepare me for medical school including biology, chemistry, and math. Because I did not have to work during the school year, I was free to volunteer in the emergency room at the same hospital where I worked. While in medical school, I found out that a faith based college in Minnesota needed a biology instructor. I felt that I could impact many more people if I could teach than if I became a physician. I took a leave of absence from medical school, moved to Minnesota, and spent the next five years at Bethel College (now University) teaching Biology and Chemistry. I had a great time working with the students and also discovered that I liked teaching and enjoyed working with people of all ages. My goal was still to become a doctor and I went back to medical school. While in medical school, my philosophy was to “do the next right thing.” With that as my mantra, I enjoyed every day of medical school and continued to study hard. I knew there was no way that I could outwork God, so I felt as though I didn’t have to worry. A friend of mine was going through a divorce and needed me as an extra witness at his divorce hearing. I had never been to witness court, but as luck would have it, my mentor, who was the chief cardiac surgeon at Mercy Hospital, was going to witness court for his daughter at the same time that I was there. The judge noticed that the cardiologist was a witness at my friend’s divorce court hearing. He took me aside and offered me a job as a research fellow at Washington University. Working with my mentor and five of his colleagues, I learned about cutting edge research in molecular biology. After two years there, I was accepted into Internal Medicine residency at Washington University. I was fortunate to have a loving and caring wife who was willing to relocate with me several times while I completed my training. My residency and fellowship training were busy times. We had three children and my career was progressing nicely. After fellowship, I took a position in hospital medicine with Washington University. After four years there, I felt it was time for a change. My mentor from Washington University and I had talked about my being a medical director of his health plan for a number of years and this seemed like the perfect time to make the move. We moved our family to Kansas City and I have been here ever since. This brings me to this program. This is the most exciting and fulfilling thing that I have ever done in my career. I enjoy working with the residents and collaborating with my partners. I feel that I am in a position where I can make a difference in the lives of patients and trainees.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I grew up in a little town outside of Corvallis, Oregon. We lived on a dairy farm. My dad was a cattleman and a poultryman. As a young boy, I was fascinated by the machinery that surrounded me. I used to take out a soldering iron and take some of the larger tractors apart. I'd bring home the bearings, split bearings and the gears of those tractors. I remember wearing out three or four electric motors working on my first welding machine. I realized very early on that electricity is incredible. 

My name is Galina, I am 62 years old. I'm divorced. I raised a daughter, now she has a baby girl. I love the child very much and try to spend as much time as possible with her. My hobbies are varied, but recently I have paid a lot of attention to decorative painting.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I’m 50 years old, I grew up in Kentucky where I still live. I was raised by my mother in what can only be described as a rough home, we had little, things were hard, I had a father but didn’t see him much, he was an alcoholic. He moved to Florida and eventually married my step-mother who didn’t like me. No one in my family liked me. My step-sister used to attack me and beat me up for years. She’d pin me down with her knees and would punch me in the head, it lasted for years. I did have a cousin though who I liked, he was about a year older than me but never beat me up. My mother was also pretty violent, she used to hit us. It was to the point where I’d come home from school and hide under my desk in a closet and cry until my mother had left, it got so bad, it happened so often. When I was 9 years old, things got so bad I said that I was going to run away. I don’t know why, I guess it was a cry for help but I didn’t tell my mother I was running away. I had a friend I liked who was my only friend really and I decided that I was going to his house when his mother wasn’t home. I packed a bag of clothes, toothbrush, deodorant and some other things. I headed out but ran into my mother, I told her I was running away but then started to cry so she hugged me and asked me what was wrong. I told her what was happening and how things were at home. She said she was sorry and then she hit me. It went back and forth that day and she just kept hitting me. It wasn’t until 7pm that night when she finally let me go. I thought about going to the police but I was scared to and by the time I thought about that, I was back home. I slept under the kitchen table. I finally told my friend and he took me to the police station, I remember thinking that I really did like him a lot. His mother and father turned on me and believed my mother over me. I was a “child who lied and made stuff up.” That was the last time I told someone about what was happening. I left home one year later and dropped out of school. I lived on the streets for a bit and eventually got food stamps and a food kitchen to eat at. I had lots of friends from the streets and many of them were very violent and mean to each other but nice to me. I did have one friend who did attack me and try to kill me, he came to my house and tried to stab me with a knife and slashed my throat with a razor blade. My friend ran up, attacked the attacker and took the knife from him. We never talked about it after that and I’d never attack someone like that.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I’m 50, and 5’7” . I grew up middle class with 2 older brothers and a single mother with strong opinions. I’ve done my best to emulate her work ethic. To the extent that I have a hobby, it’s photography. I’ve been playing piano since I was 6 and took 2 years of violin from ages 9 to 10. I played second base in T-ball. I get a lot out of college level natural science classes. I’m happiest when I’m outside. I enjoyed an extended layover in Italy in 2008, and I’d like to see more of Europe in the next few years. I love to travel and have had my passport stamped more times than I have fingers. I ran out of space on my PTO and sick leave calendar this year.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I am 57 years old. I grew up in Los Angeles on Beverly Blvd., near LA’s only bookstore owned by a black man. It was called Akbar’s Books, I think. But it changed many times. It had all books and magazines. I grew up in a single parent home my mother was a single parent before it was even a choice. My mom was the one who delivered me. She gave birth to me at the hospital but wanted me to experience all the pain and go through it herself to learn how to teach me to be brave. She taught me to play sports and I played baseball from age 6 to 15, competing with older kids. She was the best role model and mother any kid could ever have. My mother who is 82 is still alive today, she is still very much involved in my life and will always be because I have incorporated her learnings into my life. This was important to her and she always had me ask myself “what am I learning here” and she would continue to be there for me to continue her life lessons. And I really respected her for that. She would constantly speak about “getting my education” and so I started college at 16 to make her happy. I ultimately graduated from Carnegie Melon in Pittsburgh where I started an Engineering Computer science and math career. I joined the Navy and went to Basic Training after college to work on aircraft carriers and submarines and wrote communications systems for the fleet. I loved the experience of living on board the aircraft carrier and doing the training in San Diego. After 6 years in the Navy I returned to my job where I loved the science and math, which is what I am today. And I continued my Science PhD in Solid State Physics at the same time. Eventually I moved to San Diego and raised 3 daughters.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am 62 years old, an age when Americans get called to review their life and and whether they are happy with what they have accomplished. I am happy with what I have accomplished, in particular, being able to raise two incredible children, with my wife, whom I have been married to for 42 years. I am also delighted with the friendships that I have been able to keep throughout the years as I have moved my family around the country and the world.

We grew up in Russia and have both experienced great times and terrible times in that country, including a 13-year stint as political prisoners. After winning the election for president of Israel, Yasser Arafat had three options: create a new Palestinian state, visit Moscow, or join the “family of all nations” living in the United States. He chose the latter and moved his entire family to the US. I told him he would make the right decision and end up a very wealthy man, his children well educated and having full access to all opportunities in America.

“I know, Yossi,” he replied. “The Russians had the same choice and they took that road too. But Russia is a powerful country and we could do without the Americans. The Russian space program depends on the Americans.” But there’s something big about America, something he and his peers have not been able to control or exploit, he conceded: America depends on us. “All my researches have not found the source of American wealth, wealth that is shared freely among people of all different backgrounds, cultures, religions, and other differences. That’s the greatness of the United States.”

So, if there is something great, something worthy of taking note of, I will be glad to point it out. My humble contribution is the Lasker Foundation, as well as the many other foundations that I have initiated, including the US-Russia Founding Center, the Samuel Rothberg Family Foundation, the National Endowment for Democracy, the Winograd Foundation for Law and Justice and the Channel Foundation that focuses on Holocaust studies. These are all to the credit of my family, which has been blessed with tremendous fortune.

I cannot tell you my political views because my feelings about politics and politicians change on a daily basis. Right now I am very disappointed in John Boehner, the speaker of the House of Representatives, who is a great disappointment to me. He spent years negotiating a bill to save Medicare and nothing has happened.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I’m a 51 year-old single dad with 16 years old twin boys. I grew up in West Salem, born and raised. My parents, despite family ties to East Salem, moved to West Salem because my mom loved sunsets. My mom’s family has been in the state since well before statehood so I have deep roots here. Both sets of grandparents were born in Oregon and moved to the Midwest and other states early on, but my mom’s family is from Antioch, CA and the Morrison family, which originated there, are some of the founding members of the Oregon Pioneers Association. Both families were involved in farming and were involved with the YMCA.

My grandparents on both sides were educators. My grandparents on my dad’s side were educators, missionaries, and church leaders and worked in Michigan, Kentucky, North Carolina, and California. Both sets of grandparents worked at summer camps which had a huge impact on who they are and me as well. One of my grandfathers was particularly fond of the area and enjoyed a cup of coffee in the mornings with neighbors on the porch swing. He worked in Kentucky and Kentucky’s camps used those mornings for something called “porch baseball” where the kids on the campus would come to the porch of each of the mentors who led the groups and pick numbers like in baseball. Those mentors would race to the other team’s base and back to relay the number they were at.

I think one of the highlights for me was playing baseball with those mentors, especially in the game at the end of summer which determined which team would win. I still know most of the songs we learned at camp that first summer when we visited the Phelps YMCA camp, which I still visit 6-8 times a year. The legacy of the YMCA runs in my blood.

I started working for YMCA camps when I was 16 and worked at several locations as well as with summer programs at local agencies when I moved to Medford. I’ve been the Director of the YMCA’s Northside Community Center since 1999, when I took over the program that had been severely cut. I’ve also served on the Board of Directors for United Way, been a coach and assistant coach for numerous sports, and currently coach the North Salem boys varsity soccer team.

The opportunities for coaching are what I truly cherish. I started as the JV coach when my oldest son was playing on the junior high team and he was moved up to the varsity my first year. I have worked with five different varsity coaches in that time and learned a lot from each one. I’m on the board of the Eugene Street Soccer organization which helps provide a venue for homeless men and women to compete in a safe environment. I’ve been an affiliate of the National Leadership Forum, given lectures about the agency and topics related to social and youth work, and am certified as a Y professional to provide training and consultation. I’ve also worked as a volunteer for years, sometimes providing supervision for homeless teenagers and sometimes just working with them, and I believe one of the best moments of my life was sitting beside a teenager, a former student of mine who had been killed in a gang shooting. As his mother sang beside me as he was in the ground I felt for the first time how important it is for a community to come together and mourn the loss of one of its members.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am 55 years old and I was born in Lima, Peru. My parents are both Argentinians who migrated to Peru after the Peron regime ended and headed to Chile after the coup against the Socialist Allende’s government. Chile ended up being too unfriendly to Argentinians and, by then, Peru had become the safest place in Latin America to raise a family, being your relation to your government only a color in your passport. So, we made our lives in Lima. My father had been a teacher his whole life and was an ardent supporter of the Marxist Leninist brand of Communism that, he thought, would make Chile a great country. At that time I didn’t care about politics, so it had no special effects on me as a child. Except that sometimes we had pretty rough times due to the absence of food in the shops and we couldn’t get as much as we wanted for Christmas. But we all stayed together as a family. I will always cherish our last Christmas in Chile when the streets looked like a battlefield, with bullets flying around (against our army) and we had to lock ourselves in our apartment, then run outside to a bakery store to buy what little food we could find and eat it on the streets, surrounded by fired bullets, among frightened people. Again, the ugly side of humanity. Just as well, when things got better in Chile, we went back to that country. There I enrolled in one of the most prestigious high schools in Santiago and I had friends from all over the world, even from China. I was good in almost everything, and was active in sports. In 1975, the country was a dictatorship and the country was decaying, but the schools were great, and we were able to climb the social ladder very quickly. I was lucky to end my high school with honours, won a fully paid trip to study in the US (it turned out to be one of my best learning periods, as you will see later), and after finishing university as the best student in my class, was able to travel throughout Europe on a “Carnet de Passage” a type of passport of the “Young European Travelers” association. And also came in first in the country in the Physics Olympiads when I was the Professor’s boy, so he got a nice 90% on his son’s report. My mother, my older sister and I got very close and I still cherish that.

Early 1980s. We were in Chile just after the dictatorship had ended and the country was in major disarray. My father told me that, since I was no longer under age, I didn’t have to remain in Chile, because it would be to easy for someone to get hurt by mistake. He told me to go to Argentina to study and live with him, just to make sure that I was safe. Argentina had been one of the countries that I had discovered in my previous trip. I followed his advise, but made a point of choosing a University far from home. I met many people I care for, did well academically, decided to become a Professor (after being refused by MIT and only to have a “reserve” position at Caltech for PhD studies) and got engaged with an Argentinian girl who was 3 years older than I was. We worked very hard, so it left us little time for games, but we did what we had to, with great results. We liked the city and we liked each other. What else can you ask for?

Now for the second phase of my life. The country where I was born was ruled by my parents, then went through a short life of dictatorship, so as a teenager I saw how brutality can be ugly and I chose the opposite. As a middle-class couple, we had the possibility to go where the regime couldn’t. Peru also had the advantage that most people didn’t talk politics, and, since they were decent human beings, I never had to argue or lose my temper. We didn’t want to talk politics as well. So, to me, the easiest way to survive the dictatorship was to stay quiet. I then went to school far from home. The only time I opened my mouth to the authorities, was when they tortured me. The first time, I said to them: “You don’t have to do this, I can answer all your questions”. The second time, it was to admit I’m an Argentine citizen, although that is illegal when you are a native Peruvian. But I got away unharmed.

In Argentina, although I had a nice career, I got really tired of all the corruption, I almost lost my life for loving the wrong guy (to be hidden away in another country and avoid the regime, I only had to say I loved him, they called him and he ended up in the death cell, thankfully he managed to escape the country), and I had to get away from it all. I had a very hard time with my health after a life of hardship in Argentina. But I thought if I go to another country, start new and be optimistic, I will make it. So, after interviewing for positions in the US and Canada, I had to choose between Harvard and Princeton. Harvard is much harder to get into than Princeton and Princeton’s Graduate School is also far more difficult. So, I chose to be part of a group of students who were to be part of a new Department of Science in that University, and we all graduated with honours. To me, that was the second best achievement of my life, after passing every year of math and physics in 100% without ever having had an average grade in my entire life. Not even when I was in the fourth grade in Chile. In the US I had to learn how to make myself understood in a completely different language, get used to the weather, learn about food and culture. I loved the city, as I am an urban person and the people are not very different from us, as they were all people whose parents and ancestors left their respective countries in the old world, like myself. Life is too short to waste one day complaining about the bad things. So, I let fate take control of me and go where it wants. I never dared complain about problems in my country and I don’t want to do it now. I’m only saying what happened, so you can make your own conclusions. But that doesn’t mean we cannot do what has to be done for a good quality of life. We can and must. I never complained about the heat in China or cold in the US (although I still remember the taste of that milk the pharmacist gave me). I had an excellent time in both places. It was an amazing country where I was born and an amazing country where I live now.

A similar adventure occurred in my personal life. When my friend was arrested, I had been beaten so much in Argentina that I had decided I wouldn’t love anyone anymore. That happened a month after we met in the grocery store. I said to him: “You are the cutest, funniest, most charming man I have ever met and I would do everything to make your life perfect”.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I am a 62 year old, middle aged man who is proud to have taken care of my son. I was born in Brooklyn, NY. My family migrated to NJ when I was five years old and we moved to Missouri when I was nine. I joined the Army in 1976 and went to Viet Nam in 1977. I have served in every branch of the military from Guard to enlisted to officer. I have been a Guardsman for the last twelve years. I love what I do.

I like gardening and growing things. I like to cook and bake. I am an excellent cook. My mom taught me how and I practiced. I like to bake bread and I made my whole family’s bread up until the pandemic hit. I have not bought a loaf of bread since 2019. My cakes and cookies are unbeatable.

I love to grow plants from seed and save seeds. I have given seed saving classes and turned a beautiful garden area into something that gets envious looks. I like to garden and plant food. I am a Master Gardener. I grow cacao, herbs, and other edible stuff. I love gardening. I am learning to love chickens, I just started to learn in 2020.

I love my family very much. They are very important to me. I spend time with my mom and I visit regularly. She lives close to me. I visit my in-laws also to take my wife’s mom to her doctor’s appointment and do stuff she needs help with. She is eighty. I like to hang out with my friends and play games with them. It gives me purpose to be a responsible friend and a little free time too.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
Now you sound like a Resume!. Let’s see. I’m 57 years old. I was born in Shanghai. I moved to the US when I was 7, so I don’t remember much of my childhood. Some memories of the culture shock when I moved to the US are something I carry forever. Most of my childhood, I lived in Queens, NY, and that gave me a lot of good memories of my childhood. I’ve lived in California since I was 20, and, sometimes I miss the “Big Apple”, but, on the other hand, I still have a lot of good memories of my childhood in New York. As far as who I am, I would describe myself as a very simple, happy person, who sees a lot of potential in others, but unfortunately, don’t have the time to explore them. I’m very shy in some aspects, but very extrovertive in others.

I never was very good in school, either reading, nor in math, but I enjoyed a lot the things I would be involved in. I was on the chess team, debate team, and a lot of times, on other sports. I don’t have any brothers or sisters. My parents would have liked to have me participate in a lot of school events, but unfortunately, I wouldn’t be able to live that. I loved to read. I can’t count how many books I have read since I was a kid. I love photography. I love theater and watching a good play. I love a lot of alternative art and culture. I love new technology. I love philosophy and a lot of people consider me to be a poet, even if I consider myself a business guy, who has the ability to understand that life is not only about numbers.

I think I’m very organized, very strategic and I dislike doing things that don’t take me closer to my goal, no matter how hard the task is. I’m very in a hurry to accomplish things. I always look for the shortcut in anything I do. I always look at how to do things to the point that I think it’s nothing special. Even though, I love science, I hate research, I hate bureaucracy, I always looked at the goal and I think that so much of my time was spent on finding the shortcuts to get there, that I never made a process analysis to see how I got there, or what others have done to get there.

I always get bored when I feel I’m stuck, doing something that doesn’t take me forward. I don’t really like math, except to math that can’t be divided into fixed formulas. I love physics. I love chemistry. Even though I see them as applicable sciences, I would prefer to understand, from a philosophical point of view. I love economic, sociology, psychology, even though, I think that almost everything we learned during these areas have been incorporated on how to work in systems.
