question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I am a 55 year old woman who has experienced great joy and great sadness. I was born in the US in Southern California in a small town called Inglewood, just outside of Los Angeles. As a child, my parents split up and I became a foster child for a short time. I was finally adopted and taken into a home with my new brother and sisters. I remember them being somewhat violent and very hard on me. I was a compliant child. I would do anything to avoid being a target and I was quiet and sweet. I didn’t cause problems but it didn’t take long for them to reject me. There was nothing I could do right. I ran away at 16 and lived on the streets. Eventually, I moved in with a friend who had a mother who told me she would take care of me. That was short lived as her and her two sisters became more abusive. I was afraid to speak up so I ran away. I slept in the park, begged, stole, sold drugs and started stripping to get money for a room. I met a man that wanted to take care of me. He was 15 years older. We eventually got married. A few years later, my husband died in a motorcycle accident. I felt like a failure. I had no identity. No family. No job. I became pregnant and moved to another city and started stripping to make ends meet. My child was born but I had nowhere to turn and no support. I took him to my mothers and she asked me not to come back. She told me that my baby was mine to care for and if I couldn’t do that, she would take him. I went back to stripping and managed to pay off my truck loan. Then I got into a relationship with an older woman and she supported me and my son. We stayed together for 8 years but eventually we separated. I moved to another town and found a job. Within a couple of months I was pregnant again. I had given up on being a good mother. I drank a lot and slept around. I could not sustain the relationship with my daughter. Her father was unstable and abusive to both of us. My daughter would constantly go to her grandmothers. She was eventually taken from me and adopted out. I found my birth mother but she was mentally ill and abused me horribly. I lived in a shelter for abused women. Eventually, I got clean and sober and obtained a better job and then I met a man that was a great father and we began to live together. I had more children and at one point my oldest daughter came back to me. She went to live with my boyfriend and then we got pregnant and married. I have 4 children now. My youngest daughter is now an adult and has her own life. I was taken care of my dying mother and I moved into a place of my own. I developed a neurological illness called “Restless Leg Syndrome” and although it is a manageable condition I still need assistance from family. I worked as a teen advocate for 3 years but I had to take medical leave and take care of my children full time. I struggle to take care of my children. I do my best. I am still in pain but I am still going to try to have a better life for myself and my children.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I am a native of Annapolis. I am going on 55 years old. I grew up at the Naval Academy. I knew nothing else until I went to college. When I was growing up, I lived in an upper middle class environment. I was surrounded by Naval Officers. Many of my teachers were USN in uniform. I am the second oldest of 5 children. My mother and father divorced in 1975. I went through several step-fathers. I am especially proud of the fact that the friends I made in kindergarten are the friends I have today. I made some poor decisions in my life. Alcohol became a serious problem for me in my 30’s. I am 14 years sober today. It is a very difficult thing to ask people to understand. I don’t do it because it is an attractive thing. I have been married for 25 years. I have two sons. I had a dog named Lady who died on 8 April, 2003. She was very special to me. Life has been pretty good to me, but I have learned that one day at a time is all you get. Now I have the opportunity to help someone. I believe that is where my life’s purpose has emerged. I feel honored to do that.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I think those are the questions that make up you. I don’t have a prescribed answer. I’m a little bit older now, I’m 60 years old. I have to admit there are many great things and many terrible things that have happened to me in my life. I am who I am because of my life and the people who were in it. In a way I don’t really want to disclose too much because that’s a lot of the mystery. The whole intrigue is there’s something there that makes you want to know me. There’s something there that maybe is my reason for being. What is it? That’s why I never really want to give away too much about myself.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I am a 54 year old retired Director of Public Health Services who still maintains an interest in public health matters. As far as my childhood is concerned, my father was a professional soldier in the Royal Army Ordinance Corps which left us moving around, so that I was born in Warrington, Cheshire and then grew up in Newcastle upon Tyne. I went to boarding school when I was 8 years old and then to university at 16 and I have basically lived away from home since then. My father met my mother while on a posting in Jamaica, so I am also a British citizen by right of birth in the UK. As an adult I have lived in England, Wales, Jamaica, Kenya, Egypt, China and of course Belize.

My professional career focused on public health and environmental health, covering a very broad area from health & safety at work, occupational health, to working with children and families with issues of child abuse and domestic violence, and developing public health strategies, especially related to communicable diseases and food safety. I am highly specialized in infectious disease and blood borne pathogens (HIV & HCV). I have also had a lot of experience in health legislation and the review of health policies. I am highly regarded for my skills in the development of training materials and the design and delivery of training programs. I set up a Preventive Health Services for the Ministry of Health in Belize and the World Health Organization.

My primary interest is birding and animal welfare. I also have a huge love of nature and many of my vacation trips revolve around nature experiences, birding and wildlife. I currently sit as a Trustee for the Belize Wildlife Referral Clinic and I recently founded and now run Sanctuary Wildlife Centre, a non-government organization focused on education and the prevention of animal cruelty and the conservation of wildlife. I also do yoga and meditation and have a belief in healthy eating and healthy living.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I am 56 years old. I was born in Milwaukee Wisconsin, the son of an electrical engineer and a civil servant. I was raised in the Milwaukee area, except for two years spent in Brooklyn, New York while my father was employed at John Jay College of Criminal Justice at City University of New York. I received a bachelor of arts degree from the University of Wisconsin in Madison in 1980, and a masters of arts in psychology, also from the University of Wisconsin in Madison, in 1983. Over the years I have worked as a bus driver, a retail clerk, an inner city youth counselor, a banquet bartender, a dishwasher, and a paralegal. Currently I am teaching English as a second language to refugees. What matters to me are the Constitution, equality, sustainability, and freedom from coercion. The things that interest me most are history, politics, philosophy, and religion. I enjoy reading, writing, and watching history-related documentaries and movies. I enjoy role-playing video games and most action movies. I am currently in the second stage of divorce, in which I have legal custody of my son and child support payments provide his main source of support. I have taken out a predatory mortgage and have found it very difficult to get legal assistance in attempting to correct the many errors that were made by various financial institutions in the creation of the loan and title documents and in transfer of the documents between various financial institutions. I am not currently employed by any political party.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
That is a loaded question, isn’t it? (laughs) Well, I’m 62 years old. I grew up in upstate NY. I come from a divorced family. My Mom and I lived with her parents in the Catskill Mountains. It was the 50s and we had a 5’ round tube tv and the children’s programs were either Sesame Street or Mr. Rogers. Neither, I was too cool for that. I grew up on Disney’s ‘Darkwing Duck’. I started to love music with Elvis Presley.

The Diva was the only girl at her preschool that played the drums. That’s a BFD for me. I was playing the old Ludwig my uncle gave me. And most of all, she became addicted to the stage. She had her first bar gig at 8 years old. Played drums. She was born to play the drums.

Diva has been playing drums for over 40 years now. When she was in high school she was following bands from Europe. Yes, 1981! Then I started to follow Joey Ramone, Adam Ant, and all the British new wave bands. That’s when I knew what I wanted to do when I was in my teens, to get up on stage in front of people. Music, and meeting people! It didn’t happen right away but I did go to college for music. I was majoring in music theory and vocal and had a band on the side.

In my 30s, I started working in the kitchen of a bar. There I met so many interesting people. I worked with bartenders that used to be in bands like Vince Neil (Motley Crue), Dee Dee Ramone (Ramones) and people who knew those bands very well. They taught me a lot about the music world, and my love for music grew, even more. They gave me great advice that I could follow. Some day, just follow my dream.

My first full time paying job in music was in 1994. I got a gig playing drums for Jerry Lee Lewis. I travelled the USA with him as one of his backing musicians. It was one of the best experiences of my life. We had a TV show on A&E that is now posted on YouTube. I met people in that band and also met my future wife too.

I love to play all types of music. I have played rock, blues, country, jazz, and worship. I love to play and I love to play music with other musicians. Our goal at DRUMS ON FIRE! is to help musicians get their passion for music burning with the fires of discipline, determination, and desire.

It took me nearly 30 years to follow my dream. Yes, it took me 30 years to really follow what my heart was telling me to do. I didn’t give up though. My answer for that question is a look into my soul.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I’m almost 54, live in Boulder, Colorado, was born and raised in Southern California and still carry my badge of a SoCal surfer kid around with me. My father’s story and influence in his life as a Polish immigrant are central to my upbringing and who I am today. I was in elementary school when the Polish ship turned away from the coast of Newfoundland in 1942 and my dad was left behind. When he was 15 years old, my dad escaped through the country (over 200 miles) of Austria, Spain, and France to get to America. I always had the privilege of hearing his story first hand, and the result was I was very young before I heard all about the war, I was very worldly before most of my friends, and I always had a deep respect for my country and the American Dream that I was so fortunate to have.

My father is 94 years old now and still goes to the beach 4 to 5 times a week to surf, walk and skateboard. He is one of my greatest role models and I have always admired his great qualities of hard work, determination, fearlessness, and living a life full of grit and grittiness. Grittiness is a big part of my story as I had to overcome a lot as a kid, grew up with a big chip on my shoulder, had a rough time in college (at a pretty prestigious university) and finally went through a career change from Electrical Engineering to MBA.

The important events that made me who I am today are:

My parents splitting when I was very young (my dad stayed in California and my mom moved to Arizona). I found a sense of independence and individualism early on in my life. I found my own way in life and it was very clear that the decisions and the path I took were my own.

Leaving my home town in California and going to the University of Arizona. It’s amazing how these times of change in your life put so much in perspective. I was one of the youngest in my class and remember spending a lot of time studying and still playing football. When you are one of the youngest in your class, I think it’s a valuable lesson to work hard and play to your strengths.

After graduation and 3 years of marriage, my first wife left me and in the process we had a baby girl, and started our custody battles. This was the worst time in my life and a very difficult experience, but I have to thank this for really helping shape the person I am today. At a young age, my first wife and I had already divorced, I had a baby, lost my college sweetheart, and felt like a total loser. Losing my wife and daughter, I had to get my shit together and start again and this really gave me a different point of view of the world.

Losing my job in 1991 was also a life-changing event for me. Not only was I living in a studio apartment with my 3 year old daughter, but it was the first time in my life I had to find a job and it was an eye-opening experience to be honest. I made a lot of mistakes, interviewed poorly, and in the end landed a job with Motorola in their Research and Development Lab in 1992.

Circumstances brought me to Boulder in 1993, and I was engaged in a program of Master’s degree in Computer Engineering at the University of Colorado in Boulder while working full time at Motorola. I went to school at night with 5 years of hard work while my daughter was in school all day. This period of my life was more difficult and taxing than I could ever have imagined. The focus and determination it took to have a full-time job and go to school at night was monumental. In the end, it was completely worth it as I made the Dean’s List twice, was inducted into the National Society of Collegiate Scholars, and graduated magna cum laude in 1998.

There were no real defining moments in my life until my wife and I started having kids and started our family. Once we started our family, that’s when the pace and the joy of life really took off. In 2003, my wife and I had our first daughter and my second daughter was born in 2005. My daughters make me so happy and their antics and lives are all very meaningful to me. Every night, I read the girls a book and go through the nightly routine, it’s one of my favorite times of the day. My girls are now teenagers and their friends are over a lot. I’ve noticed that their friends always gravitate to our house and hangout at our place because they enjoy the fun of my house and the atmosphere of our home. We never say no to kids hanging out at our house and we love having the kids at our house.

Two years ago, my wife and I welcomed our first grandchild. To my wife and me, my first grandson is the greatest thing in the world, and I look forward to spending time with him every chance we get.

The things that I enjoy most in life are:

Playing tennis at the Boulder Creek Tennis Club, cooking up a good meal, spending time with my family, traveling, boating, and water skiing. Also, playing with my grandson, sharing time with my best friends, and my close group of friends, coaching and coaching my daughters basketball team, watching football on Sundays, and playing golf.

Most importantly, being a good father, husband, and grandfather. My main goal in life is to be a good person and I have found that the closer you get to your true north and the better person you are, the happier your life becomes. The things that truly matter in life are love, happiness, being a good person, and making memories. A happy life comes from treating people the way you want to be treated, being kind to others, and by always being there for your family.

What are some defining experiences you’ve had in your life that have led you to your current career?

Growing up in California, I surfed and played water polo in high school. My favorite athlete of all time is Andy Irons, who unfortunately passed away in 2010. I got to know Andy as he got older and had kids. At a surf event in San Clemente, I met Andy and he came over to me and said, “Is that you, Greg? I love surfing with you”. When I hear that, it’s hard not to get choked up, and as you can imagine, I get choked up right now as I type this. Andy’s story was a great lesson in his grittiness. Andy was one of the world’s greatest surfers ever, had many surgeries, fought a lot of demons, but he had an amazing love for his family, his friends and the ocean. He had a way of winning over anyone he met and was a dear friend to my wife, kids and me.

His death in 2010 still leaves me with a heavy heart, but I remember when I got his story out in the world, he was very happy and I’m happy to say that his story went around the world and touched a lot of lives. He has inspired people all over the world in a way that has been really cool. I am very proud of the story we produced.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
My name is John W. Gerner. I am 58 years old. I was born in Puerto Rico. However, I grew up in New York City. I have lived in Greenville, South Carolina since 1988. My mother was a foreign service officer. When I was eight years old, she was reassigned to Seoul, South Korea. I am the last of four brothers. I graduated from Cornell University with a degree in Communication. I began my first job in advertising in 1983 at GSD&M Advertising in Austin, Texas. In the 1980’s I was recruited by Ted Turkel Advertising in Washington, D.C., where I was an account executive selling radio and print advertising for J.G. Ballard and BUD’s Eye View. From there, I was recruited by Mullen Advertising in Boston, where I was an account executive responsible for account management services and national account development. In 1989, I entered the hospitality industry as Marketing Manager at the Hyatt Corporation in Athens, Greece. Over the next twenty years I held several managerial positions in sales and marketing and conference management, working as International Conference Director at the Palais des Nations, United Nations Office at Geneva; with numerous UN institutions, including the International Labour Organization, the World Health Organization, and the International Telecommunications Union; and with several intergovernmental organizations, including the Organization for Security and Cooperation in Europe, the International Organization for Migration, and the African Union. My main accomplishment while at the UN was a series of conferences in Africa, Europe, and Latin America on genetically engineered foods.

In 2010, I made a dramatic lifestyle change, which included relocating to South Africa to assist my partner of over ten years, Dr. Jaime Tachiquin, to create a new company, Tachiquin Group Properties.

The pandemic altered that plan, and because I have not resumed living in the USA as of this writing, I have continued my work with Tachiquin Group from my office in South Africa.

During my career, I have undertaken multiple graduate-level courses and workshops in sales and marketing, international relations, management, and project management, serving as a leader, facilitator, mediator, negotiator, speaker, trainer, and consultant. I have the confidence to take initiative, think on my feet, make decisions, evaluate risks, and respond quickly and effectively in a crisis, as demonstrated by my record of achievement in media and advertising, events management and venue consultancy, and conference planning and organization in international and domestic settings, including Southern Africa. I have a strong network of professional contacts.

I am also a seasoned traveler. While I was a partner in Tachiquin Group, I spent most of my time in southern Africa, as well as frequently traveling to South Asia and the Middle East, while continuing to maintain a residence in South Carolina.

I strive to lead a healthy, active lifestyle, practicing yoga and practicing t’ai chi and qigong. I also enjoy hiking, gardening, and cooking.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I’m 61 years old. I was born and raised in a big city on the Northeast coast of the United States. Although I moved away when I joined the Navy, I’ve spent my entire life as an urban dweller. In terms of events that have shaped my life, the most significant has been a life-long struggle with depression and agoraphobia. If anything, this has made me the empathetic person that I am today, as well as the introverted person that I am, although I sometimes try to hide that fact. However, one of the most important events in my life occurred when I met a very special person online who asked me to accompany him to a party; we met online in 2003 and continue to be together as a couple today.

A close second would be when I left the military in 1980 to attend university and earn a degree. During that time, I realized that I couldn’t be a writer without actually being a writer, so I began writing short fiction and, since 2004, have published 71 short stories under my own byline in both hard copy and ebooks. The last two years have been an awesome experience as a writer, both in terms of rewards and sales of my short fiction.

Regarding my interests, the first activity that I enjoy is reading, both fiction and non-fiction, mostly those two genres that I like to write. The other two activities are working in the yard and long walks.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I was raised in Birmingham, England, UK. I’m 52 years old. I’ve traveled extensively. I love nature, wildlife, exploring other cultures, talking about history and culture, religious observances and beliefs, morality and human suffering, love and the emotions it generates, optimism and the wonder of our universe, and human psychology. I enjoy deep, inspiring, and meaningful conversations with people from all walks of life. Although this is not something I have done a lot of, I’m most inspired by conversations with those who are living everyday with their own experiences and perspectives in relationships, in work, in service of others, and how they relate to nature and the human condition. I grew up in what most would call a middle-class family. My mother worked in an office, and my father had his own business for many years. We lived in a nice suburb of Birmingham and were comfortably secure. I never wanted for anything in the way of physical needs. I had food, shelter, and clothing. As I got older, I was able to participate in many social activities, and enjoyed traveling, camping, and exploring nature. The only times we seemed to have any money worries were during the 3 periods of unemployment that my father went through over the 25 years that I lived at home.

I love nature and have a deep respect for everything that I experience in it. I love the creatures that live in it, and the sheer beauty of the scenery I’ve experienced all over the world. I love the simple things in life that touch my heart, which mostly are things that a lot of people would just walk by without giving a second thought. There is a mindfulness that I have developed by simply being aware of my surroundings that most people just don’t notice. I enjoy reading, writing, and learning about history, culture, and religion. I have a wide variety of interests, which are mostly things that I have a passion for, such as environmentalism, politics, humanitarian efforts, spirituality, psychology, and philosophy, and in modern society, a passion for simplifying and changing the way in which we live on this planet, especially regarding technology and science. We are at the beginning of a new type of science, one that is based on the physics of electromagnetism, as opposed to the more widely accepted physics of Newtonian mechanics and electromagnetic induction. We still think in the same way that Newton and Faraday did, and are making the same mistakes and using the same models when we really need a whole new way of thinking in order to move forward. I like to be up to date on the latest developments, as well as the science of the past. Knowledge is useful and inspiring, and I try to convey that to others whenever possible.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am 52. I grew up in San Antonio. I don’t like telling people I grew up in San Antonio because it sounds like a lot of bad things happened to me there. I lived in San Antonio during the assassination of JFK and a series of horrific child murders that were never solved. During that same time there was the Dr. Albert Osbourn case where a woman accused her husband of shooting her. She was found shot to death in the living room with a bullet in her head. He was convicted of first-degree murder and was sentenced to death. He was found dead in his cell at the age of 48, and he was kept alive until he was dead because of the electric chair that was in use in Texas at that time. It was a terrible time. Of course I didn’t really live that life, but the aura that was left behind certainly shaped me and every aspect of my life.

There was another huge drama that was going on in my world while this was happening. I grew up with my grandparents while my mother traveled around the world as a flight attendant. My grandfather was an absolute dreamer. I remember he wanted to build a tower in our backyard to bring the weather. You know like the one at Punta Gorda in Italy. A few years later he had become so obsessed with it, he started to turn the entire backyard into this sky tower. My grandmother made him put it in the front yard. My grandfather had a lot of other projects. There was a second job of my mother’s where she was bringing a bus from Paris to Barcelona with a whole bunch of kids. While she was gone my grandfather said, “I’ll cook some dinners and we can have some nice people over.” He started collecting furniture from the stores at the airport in a big trailer and putting it in the backyard. My grandmother said, “No you can’t do that. The inspector is coming in a week.” He didn’t care. This went on for months. My grandparents had a party and while they were talking to their friends, I went to one of the bedrooms to explore and found a secret passage behind the door. I used to call this a secret passage because it had a small window that opened out to the front yard. I thought that was so great. I felt like I was in a spy movie. Then I felt a bit of a breeze coming from behind me. I turned around and it was this wall and I pushed it open. There was a bookcase there and it opened into the bedroom. My grandmother and my mother came to look for me in the bedroom because I was supposed to be watching television. They saw me sitting there on the floor reading a book. “How did you get here? This is the bedroom and this is where your grandfather hides money and jewels.” That’s what they said. I said, “I found a secret passage.” I got so embarrassed and they said, “Why would you look for something like that? Your grandfather would be so ashamed of you.” It made me feel so bad. I felt like I had done something bad, but I was so excited. I felt like I had found a secret place. That is what happened to me. Then I kept looking for another secret passage. I never found another one. I used to go to the movies and watch them find a secret passage and I would always look to see if there was one there. There wasn’t. That’s when I started writing.

In my early years I had been always looking for new experiences. I did a lot of theater, writing, directing. I got into politics in high school and was the mayor of my junior class. We put a lot of work into that campaign and in the end we lost. It was very hurtful to lose but I put my whole self into it. It was a great learning experience.

The best advice I ever received was from my grandfather, “The worst feeling in the world is regret.” I have always remembered that and I try never to regret anything. I still feel like I have to do everything and I haven’t really lived life because I haven’t really had time to take a break.

I want to be more self aware of everything. Sometimes I feel a little blind. I feel I should stop every once in a while and just look at my life and be more present and more aware of where I am and where I’m going. I don’t do that.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am 57 years old. I grew up in Indonesia. My parents are Christians, but didn’t take me to church because they didn’t want me to be indoctrinated. I took one religion class in high school and got 100%. When I was in 4th grade, I started wanting to read the bible for myself. My father and I began arguing over what the bible said. In the end, he told me I didn’t need to read the bible. So I read it on my own and taught myself. About the history of the church, and about various doctrines and doctrines in the bible. Christianity, Judaism, and Islam. I read the Qur’an too. My life experience so far has shown that I should turn my life over to God, not have confidence in myself. I struggle with all the usual sins, but a good thing to my life experience is it has shown me how to pray. My spiritual life is definitely my highest interest. I feel that if I can pray for things and they actually come to pass, it would be a good reason to pray for a specific job.

I got an A in a biology class in university. That gave me a general sense of how to read technical writing. As for technical writing, this was from books and articles that I found in internet forums and blogs. I would read them over and over, and when I had a question, I would ask other people who had more experience than me. In those forums and blogs, people didn’t usually identify themselves with their real name and job title, they used aliases. So you got a good understanding of what they could and couldn’t do, because if the person spoke authoritative, that was what they actually knew. Then I asked myself if I thought they knew what they were talking about, I asked other people, and if they said they knew, I just did what they told me to do. If I found out it didn’t work, I tried something else. If it worked, I would keep doing it. I discovered that technical writing is all about trial and error, learning from other people. I haven’t had any problems doing technical work. I learn things very quickly and I’m willing to spend long hours learning things.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
My birth name is Peter Robert Jones. I am 50 years old. I am single, and I live by myself, in a house that I own. I was raised in Penrith NSW in a very ordinary blue-collar family with little money and no cultural interests or aspirations other than to live as comfortably as possible.

My primary interest in life from the earliest time that I can remember, has always been in sex. I am predominantly heterosexual, but like many people, I have always had strong fantasies about the opposite sex. I also have had strong fantasies about the same sex, but this is only because, I discovered several years ago, that in order to have sex with a woman, I have to imagine her as a man, a process called autosuggestion.

So, in my fantasies, I am mostly a heterosexual, but also a bisexual. My sex fantasies are quite ordinary, given that I don’t have any medical problems and have always had a healthy appetite for sexual pleasure. I have never been a rapist. To restate: although I am sex mad, in real life I have never kidnapped anyone. Nor have I murdered anyone. Nor do I have any propensity to be violent. In the deep dark secrets of my sex fantasies I have often fantasised about sadistic sex, such as having sex with a prisoner, and having sex with someone who is not consenting, but in no way have I any attraction to inflicting pain on others.

Amongst my sexual fantasies is the fantasy that I have of having sexual intercourse with the Queen. Although I am a very ordinary Australian citizen, I have always thought of myself as being royal, and in many of my sexual fantasies I fantasise that I am actually related to the royal family. In many of my sexual fantasies I not only have sex with the Queen, I marry her, have children with her, and live as a royal family member, leading a royal life, sitting on the throne, wearing a crown, becoming Prince of Australia and Prince of New Zealand and Prince of Fiji and Prince of New Caledonia.

I have very strong opinions about sexual morality, and I believe that most people, although they are not honest about this, are similarly motivated. Most people, if you discuss sex with them in a casual manner, will admit that they have strong sexual fantasies. The difference is that while most people think of sex as private, I don’t.

During the course of my life I have talked to many people about sex, mostly men, but also some women. No one has yet been able to stop me from making casual comments about sex, and when I do so, the responses that I get are a mixture of: curiosity, belief that I am mentally ill, or amusement, but no one has said anything negative.

I realise that not everyone likes to talk about sex. Some people find this subject offensive, and don’t want to even entertain the thought of sex. In my opinion, this is sexual repression. Perhaps, there is something about my personality that makes people feel comfortable when I discuss sex. I am not sure what the reason is, but I would be very surprised if anyone who has ever met me doesn’t realise that I am a sex maniac. And I am particularly surprised if someone thinks that sex is an inappropriate subject for casual conversation. Because, sex is a very natural and normal subject to talk about. All people talk about sex, because sex is what makes life worth living. In my view, if you don’t talk about sex, you won’t live a life worth living.

Although my personality may explain why my sex fantasies tend to be an appropriate subject for casual conversation, it is still surprising that other people can’t see my point of view, and are not as normal as me. People who are closeted in this respect are afflicted with a form of sexphobia. Sexphobia is manifested in society in forms such as conservatism. Because I don’t personally get excited about conservative values, it is clear that they are in error. I reject political correctness.

The activities in life that provide me with most satisfaction and enjoyment are playing computer games, watching movies and television shows, reading books, surfing the net and having sex, sometimes all of these things at the same time.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I am 60 years old and was raised in Morrisville, Vermont, a beautiful town of farms and friendly people. This is a part of the country that some people call “New England.” Some people here have big farms with cows and crops. Other people might have big gardens to grow their food. All families have apple and pear trees, raspberry and blackberry patches, strawberries and, of course, maple syrup. I could sit on a bench in my little garden and look at the mountains and talk to God all day long. I could listen to the crickets or the blue birds or the chickadees sing and I could smell the maple syrup or the fresh cut grass. I love walking in the woods.

I want to give you an example of what a house in Vermont is like. My parents built our house themselves. It is in the country. The porch is at the front of the house. There are two bedrooms on the second floor. In the cellar there are two more rooms, a family room and a bathroom. On the first floor, there is a kitchen and a bedroom with one twin bed, and a living room with three couches. The fourth room has a couch and a TV.

One winter our home flooded. It was because our basement had a lot of water that needed to go through the pipes into the sewer. The water would back up into our basement.

I have three brothers, but my family is a small family. One brother is a nun. He taught in a school. Another brother is an alcoholic and I don’t talk to him.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am a 50-year-old female, single, living alone in an apartment, working 4 days a week in the Adult Emergency Department in an inner-city hospital. I am a mother of one son, 21 years old. I was born and raised in a large city in Northern America, but migrated here 10 years ago. The city where I was born gave me a love for music and art, both of which I enjoy spending a great deal of time pursuing. I am a sensitive person and felt as if my community was at risk for a lot of violence. I feel that we were plagued with some pretty violent crimes during the time I grew up. I am also a highly creative person. I love to draw and paint. I love music and dance. I am passionate about the Catholic faith and the great power it has to transform one’s life. And finally, I strongly believe that anyone and everyone has the potential to become the best version of themselves possible. The #1 most important thing to me in life is my faith. I love Jesus and I love to live my life for the sake of serving others and walking in true love.

So basically, what I just told you is more than you will probably ever need to know about who I am. In an interview setting, I would be more focused and concise in my answers, but this is just a free write. I will also tell you that I have written about myself for quite some time and found that no matter what, I could use the same answers repeatedly. But I prefer to tell the truth about who I am. I do not have to sell myself in this way. So if you want me to write some monstrosity about myself, I would advise you to stop reading. I know what is true of me, and I am not shy about admitting it, but I also never tell a lie.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I’m 56. I grew up in Detroit, Michigan. In the early 70s my parents divorced. I am still struggling with the abandonment and the emotional turmoil I went through during the divorce. As a young adult I experienced the prejudice and learned the lesson that what I saw was just one side of the story. I was born with a “glass is half full” outlook on life. I have always been active in social causes and taken an active role in the community. Since 9/11 I have made it my mission to learn as much as I can about Islam, which has led me to community outreach, writing my book “What Every American Needs To Know About The Qur’an”, and speaking out. It is a heavy burden that I am glad to bear.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am a 52 year old man, I was born in Texas and grew up in Arizona. During my summers as a youth we hiked, built forts, and camped. Being physically fit was important to me. This occurred before technology was commonplace, and we couldn’t communicate with each other electronically. I’m a 4th degree black belt in the Korean Martial Art of Taekwondo. What I have learned in this, and other martial arts, to be focused and assertive. I take life seriously and try to be respectful to everyone. My father was a plumber, and there were times when my family was struggling to make ends meet. I worked with my dad during the summers to help make ends meet. One time I stole from my boss at his construction site. I was so embarrassed. He was like a father figure to me, and this was the first time I had ever stole, much less from a family member. Although he and my father forgave me, it was embarrassing and it taught me to never take advantage of family. They love me and have never let me down.

I have a loving wife of 29 years, 3 sons, 6 grand kids. I work as a Driver Trainer for my company. Most of my time is spent on the road, either in my truck or in a car with a student. We work together to ensure our safety and the safety of other drivers on the highway. I eat good healthy foods and believe in preventing myself from getting sick rather than trying to get better once I’m sick. So, I try to sleep regularly, exercise, and eat healthy foods.

When I’m not driving I might be at home with family or I enjoy working on cars and trucks. I love cruising in my 1964 Mustang convertible in the summer, especially on a weekend during the day. As long as it’s not windy I can’t get enough of the convertible, and I enjoy the attention.

I enjoy making dinner from scratch at home, although I don’t do it often. I enjoy learning about historic and prehistoric peoples. My wife and I were married in a tribe in Arizona, and we were blessed by two elders. I’m curious about them, and I love watching the nature channel and learning about animals, not just those we eat. My wife is a vegetarian and doesn’t eat meat. I do eat meat, but I don’t kill the animals. I’m an animal lover, and I care for all animals.

I was in a bad wreck one time in my 64 Mustang convertible, but it was the off season and it wasn’t my fault. There were no injuries, and no one was hurt. I was traveling about 90 mph and it was nighttime. This was a long time ago, but I still remember the gravel road, which threw my car into the ditch. After this incident I decided that I shouldn’t be driving so fast and that driving like a maniac wasn’t worth my life. So, I learned the lesson. The trees and the dirt weren’t the only things flying by my car. I slowed down, but occasionally I will take a fast, twisty road and have some fun with my friends.

In the military I made sure to go home every weekend. My Mom and Dad were always worried and on the phone. I promised them I would come home every weekend.

The past is the past and I am who I am. I love my family and I’m proud of who I am. I don’t make myself happy, I make them happy.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I’m 59 years old. I grew up in Long Island. (I’m originally from Manhattan). I was born in 1953 and consider myself an offspring of the baby boom. I entered first grade the year Ladybird Johnson started the war on poverty. As a boy I was the only white kid living on a side street in a poor Italian neighborhood where kids were taught to hate Jews and the Jews (who had schools and were better off financially) hated the Italians. As a result, I learned to play with kids who were smarter and cuter than me. I had to use my brain. I had to get better at arithmetic and some other subjects I was lousy at. In time, I developed the hard shell that keeps me going to this day. It sounds clichéd, but life isn’t about the destination. It’s the journey. The road you take doesn’t matter. You can get somewhere by following a straight path, you can get somewhere by taking a detour, you can get somewhere through a series of left turns and u turns and round a bouts and I you can just turn in a circle and stumble around for a while. Then you have your life. That’s what matters. My life. Of course I traveled, and I’m grateful for the experience. But I’ve done well in the areas where it counts.

I lived in a military town and I moved there because it was close to the airport. I became an aviation freak because I saw planes all the time as a kid. I was also close to an airport that flew DC3s in and out of Kennedy Airport every day. The pilots were rich (they were airline captains and first officers). The plane was a hot chick who flew in at lunchtime and flew out at 4:00. I used to watch them play pool together in the airport lobby. I wanted to be in the army, but then we had an 18 year old terrorist (I didn’t know what a terrorist was at the time). The army needed experienced troops, so I was sent to Europe to help clean up after the French and British armies. Then I went to Vietnam and got shot in the butt and the arm. I don’t know if that qualifies as “event” that shaped my life, but I do know it defined the rest of my life. It meant that I was wounded, and I’ve gone through many years of pain with it. Sometimes when I get up at night to take a leak I can’t walk without moving slowly because I need to feel my way along. There are a couple times a month when my back locks up and the pain shoots down my leg. I don’t want to be one of those guys who complains about his back. I’m lucky to have one and it makes me realize that it can always be worse. I came back alive and that means so much to me that I don’t even mind the pain that I’ve dealt with for almost 50 years. It’s my trophy. I guess I feel like an athlete, and I’m still surprised I wasn’t killed. All those times I was shot at and shot myself through the intestine, back, leg and arm, and I didn’t die. Now I’m a normal middle aged guy who’s still frustrated about not being able to get into the army. I want to become a captain in the army. I deserve it because of all I went through in Vietnam. I want to take care of my men the way they took care of me.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
(I took a deep breath.) Hello, my name is Steve Chapman. I'm 57 years old and live in Gainesville, Florida. I was born in Aurora, Illinois, near Chicago. My parents lived in Aurora until I was three years old when they moved to Belleville, Illinois, outside of St. Louis. My father worked for the US Post Office. My mother was a housewife. She was, and still is, a devout, fundamentalist Christian. They had no great aspirations, neither were they lazy. I was their only son. They had two daughters. I loved playing with the girls when I was little and still do today. However, I preferred being with my father. I learned many things from him that I couldn't learn from my mother. He believed that it is vitally important to have respect for life. Everyone deserves the right to live in freedom without fear. Respect for the law was another thing I learned from my father. I learned to respect both others and myself.

My favorite part of the day was dinner. This is the time we sat down at the table and had dinner together. My father and I would talk about any number of things. I came to love these times together.

I grew up to be a man who was a husband and a father for 30 years. I have six children, one daughter and five sons. I was fortunate to have a husband-wife working relationship in raising them. I enjoy reading and writing. I always have. I spend time with my children and enjoy them, especially the older ones who I can discuss and debate ideas with. I am proud of them and they are of me.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I’m 56 years old. I was born and grew up in Washington DC. I am the first-born of a mother who sacrificed so much for my success. She passed away when I was a sophomore in college. My father married two other women after my mom passed away and abandoned me to their care. I didn’t get to choose a university. I attended the school where my dad had an apartment. I graduated with a degree in English, after taking 19 hours a semester as a transfer student. In 1985, I was married to a man I met in college. We got married before he went to the military and when he finished his enlistment he graduated with a degree in electrical engineering. After we married, he got a job at a government contractor. After ten years, his employers got all the contracts from the same organization we met at and he was one of three people assigned to train the new hires. We were financially secure and were paying close attention to what was going on in our government and feeling ever more distant from the leaders of this great nation. In 1995, he brought his childhood friend to the US who had graduated from the same law school and he secured a job as a paralegal. We discovered that even though his childhood friend was a law graduate with fluency in 5 languages, he couldn’t get hired in his profession in the US. He was so frustrated he eventually returned to his native country with his wife and two children. We were starting to feel guilty as we helped them for ten years before his friend left.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I’m 50. My parents grew up on the Northside of Chicago in Rogers Park and Wrigleyville. They married and moved to Evanston in the late 60’s where they gave birth to two boys, me being the older. After college they moved to the North Shore in Winnetka where they remain to this day. One brother who lives in San Jose, CA and the other who lives in Middleburg Heights, Ohio. I have a different Dad then them. My folks divorced after my mom’s brother, my Uncle Paul was killed in Viet Nam in 1967. After that my mom took a job with Catholic Charities and we travelled the Chicagoland area for the next five years before settling in Evanston for good when I was 11. After High School my life took a major turn. I moved out west to Portland, Oregon where I lived for five years. One year in Eugene at the U of O and the rest in Portland. Portland is where I came of age and developed my taste for a simpler life. Also in Portland, I learned that I was gifted with superior interpersonal skills and natural leadership qualities. My boss at the last job I had in Portland urged me to take that dream and make it a reality. As a result I moved back to Chicago and attended Saint Mary’s College in Winona, Minnesota for a semester before heading back to Portland to develop my vision.

Portland is where I got into trouble, my old ways caught up with me and I ended up in jail. That’s also where I discovered sobriety and that changed my life forever. Following sobriety I attended and graduated from St John’s Bible College in Winona, Minnesota. At the same time my wife and I (an ex-heroin addict herself) have been raising our beautiful children and working. I have earned a Master’s Degree in Human Development from Walden University in Minneapolis. I have worked in the clean-up industry and the human resources industry for the past 30 years and I’m currently looking to get in with a regulatory body or something of that nature. I have always had an entrepreneurial spirit and every business I started I pushed to profitably 6 months in. I feel that to be the true test of a successful business and I feel my confidence in that category has allowed me to weather adversity. With a mindset of tough it out, make it happen and overall ingenuity I have never been and will never be brought down.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I am 52 years old. I grew up in Albuquerque NM. I attended elementary, junior high, and high school here in Albuquerque. I have fond memories of going to Manzano High and playing baseball there on the school's team. I remember going to Barney Smith's bowling alley after school on Fridays to play with friends. I remember sneaking in to the top of the Sandia Peak Tramway on a quiet afternoon with friends to drink beer and admire the city. I remember a night when I was out late with friends and didn't make it home in time to get my mother's permission to stay out, so I was grounded for a week. That was a hard week. I remember shopping for the New Mexico Demons with some friends and the managers telling us that we couldn't be seen with them (they were a rock band and thought it was too anti-social). And I remember that I got caught having a party at my parent's house when they were out of town and was grounded for 6 months, during which time I had to pay for the fireworks that the police used to break up the party (this was at the height of the Rodney King riots). I remember that my father had a '79 'Merc' which I bought as my first car after I turned 16. I remember walking around town at night as a teenager with friends from school, looking for trouble, which we usually found. I remember my parents selling the house we grew up in and buying one out on the west side of town. I remember when we finally moved into that house. I remember my car getting stolen and not finding it for weeks until the police called saying they had found it. I remember sitting down on the curb at the spot on Central Avenue where my car had been stolen and crying, I was so upset. I remember going to my new '83 Chevette and driving it around town. I remember my first jobs, working at Bel Air Market. I remember wearing out an old blue 4x4 Bronco that I bought from my grandfather. I remember crashing that Bronco when I was with a friend on Monte Vista NE and we tried to drive into the alley at night and got stuck. I remember going to UNM and living on campus in Martin Hall. I remember going through grades 1 through 8 at Taylor Junior High and being looked up to by other students, despite getting into a lot of trouble for acting out. I remember being in the high school drill team in my junior and senior year at Manzano. I remember having a long term crush on my 9th grade English teacher. I remember when my grandfather died and the sadness that was felt in our family. I remember when my dad died and the tearful closeness that we had during that time. I remember when my sister-in-law died when my brother was gone on duty in the Korean War. I remember her funeral and her impact on our family. I remember one day when my brother tried to drive over my friend and I when we were walking in front of his truck. I remember the roughness of the Albuquerque Metro Court parking lot at 5pm every weekday. I remember my younger years of New Mexico softball. I remember the West Mesa High School football stadium and the gravel stands I sat in when the game played. I remember going to a friend's house to play Diplomacy and getting up the following morning to learn that he had moved to Oregon with his family. I remember sitting through various business meetings at LAFCO trying to help get our neighborhood ranchos into the city. I remember hanging out on Isleta when the Tomahawk came in for poker night. I remember several light saber battles with various friends as the Saturday afternoon Star Wars triology came out. I remember the art show at UNM when my cousin and I were beaten with nightsticks during a student demonstration. I remember being at the funeral of one of my dad's female police officer friends. I remember the best feeling of achievement when I beat Manzano's undefeated wrestilng team in my first year in high school, helping our team beat theirs. I remember going to The Correct Song in Nob Hill every weekend during the mid 70s and going to the 10th Street Drive-In and the Rio Grande Drive-In. I remember telling my parents about how good the drive-ins were and inviting my friends to come over to the house to use the landline to call their parents to pick them up. And, of course, I remember my future wife all those years ago at Our Lady of the Annunciation Catholic school in northeast Albuquerque.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I’m a 51-year-old man from Arkansas. My childhood was pretty poor and sad. I was molested by my biological father. I didn’t really have friends as a kid. It was only a very close group of cousins that I had. We were always close and from age 6 until 18, they all lived next door to me. I was very close to both of my parents. My mother was never a “smart” person and often bragged about going back to high school at age 40. My father was an extreme drunk and she controlled him and he rarely drank. I’ve lived in Arkansas all of my life. My two children are grown and live on the other side of the state. So, right now, I would really say that I’m doing well, but often lonely. When my father died I lost my best friend in the world. I was really depressed at that point and only the love of my children and my close cousin brought me out of it. I’m not really sure what matters to me, right now. I would really like a woman. Someone to love and take care of. To have someone to love me back and take care of me.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I’m 54 years old and grew up in Kitchener-Waterloo, Ontario. Although I grew up in a secular Jewish home, we belonged to an Orthodox synagogue, and I attended religious school. I went on my first Birthright trip to Israel when I was 19. While in university, I did a student exchange to Japan for a year. During my post-university travels, I visited many countries in Europe and the Middle East, including Turkey, Israel, Jordan, Egypt, Morocco, and Tunisia. I always had a strong interest in politics and Middle Eastern affairs, and in 1998 I went to Moscow to study Russian and do human rights work with the Committee for the Defense of the Victims of Political Repression.

What matters to me is that I have a positive impact on the world. I strive to treat people with respect, and I believe strongly in individual human rights and civil liberties. My life has been a collection of experiences that have shaped me, but all these experiences – good and bad – have brought me to where I am today. I love to learn about the world and my place in it, and my curiosity about the world has inspired me to write this book.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I am 6 foot tall, 200 pounds, 59 years old, born and raised in the city of Los Angeles. I have no college degree but I am well educated on the streets and in the job markets. I am a Christian who has had several progressive careers. I did not graduate high school because I dropped out of school at age 17 because my girlfriend became pregnant. I left her father to the fate of rearing a child with his junkie girlfriend so I could enter the Army.

In 1976 I enlisted in the United States Army. I loved it so much that I stayed in the United States Army and re-enlisted in the Army Reserves and the Army National Guard before joining the United States Army Reserve Command. Once again I re-enlisted in the Army Reserve and the Army National Guard. Finally I joined Active Guard and Reserve in 1982.

I obtained my first civilian job in October 1976. Since then I have worked in many fields of civilian employment and I have obtained many skills I now use. I have worked in the farm field, lawn maintenance, auto mechanic, with the United States Postal Service, Security Guard, Loan Processor, Mover, Deputy Sheriff, Warehouse Manager, and over a dozen other occupations. On my way to obtaining a second Masters degree I was also working with the Department of Homeland Security as a contractor. I possess so many skills that I hope to have the American people looking for some type of employment when they read about all of my skills in the next post.

Today I work for the Los Angeles World Airports as a Driver.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I do not tell stories about my life, including all my problems. I have never told my problems to anyone.

My present situation is, I left my high paying job as a software engineer and turned to be a professional casino gambler. I play blackjack and craps. I am in the US, but my residence is outside US. I am 63 years old, tall, never married, physically healthy and mentally sound. I always earn 20K+ per month. I never lost money, except for my initial deposits that I don't consider as losses. Therefore, there is no chance of me committing suicide for a financial problem. However, I play with such a large amount of money because I don't like limit my gambling only to a small amount, it hurts me. Therefore, there is a chance for me losing my money.

Personally I hate US, the most racist country in the world. It is not what I think. All my life I have been called "Polish guy". Many Americans don't even know where Poland is on the map. Since 2001, I have been called many times "terrorist" and few times "Bin Laden". My mothers were decendant of Polish slave workers brought to the US in the 1900s, and were considered white people in Poland, but are considered as non-white people in US. I am called "Polish guy" simply because of my name (different spelling of "Polish guy").

Personally I hate US, the country with the most inequality of wealth. I know, I am not qualified to talk about wealth because I have done very well in life. However, I like to comment. In life, I have worked with both rich and poor. The poor people are being treated unfairly. Poor people make this country work (they do menial job that no rich person wants). Poor people suffer much more than rich people because rich people don't know what it means to suffer. I don't see many poor people accepting their situations. There are many poor people that believe they are in the top 5% of the world. That is not an exaggeration, I have seen people who did not have a decent life, wear $300 sport shoes. Most poor people have this fantasy.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I am 54 years old and was raised in Milwaukee Wisconsin. A mother, grandfather and two uncles were influential in shaping me into who I am today. First, my mom, growing up I witnessed her caring for the mentally ill, frail, disabled, and aged. I know she gave her all to these people no matter what. Although she didn’t often mention it, I saw and know she wasn’t always appreciated. Today, it’s one of the few things that’s obvious she was proud of. However, I guess this is why we don’t talk about the things we are proud of (at least publicly) or to strangers I suppose. She raised me with the message that whatever you decide to do in life, give it your all. It still amazes me, so many years later how something like that just sticks with you. For that I’m most grateful. And for her, at age 83 years old she continues to work tirelessly for the elderly. I believe she always will.

My grandfather who was a veteran of WWII told me stories about his service in the Army as a young man during WW2. I remember him telling me of these life lessons…a main one being, “Never give up and always try to do what’s right”. He was a living walking testament to that. Though he never spoke of it much, he had also been wounded and a prisoner of war on the side of the Germans. Years later I saw a report on the History channel about a ceremony he had attended that recognized his and his fellow POW’s heroic efforts to escape the Germans after one of their vehicles broke down. I learned he was a sharpshooter and some of the things he had gone through. He may not have said much, but he still made it very clear, giving up and surrender wasn’t an option. Those two passed on words of wisdom will forever be with me and, without a doubt, the foundation I live my life by today.

Lastly, I had two uncles who were gay and adopted my brother and I. Their sense of humor is legendary in our family. They are also the reason I believe no matter who you are, where you came from, how you think or how you live, if you can be true to yourself, life is good, very good! These are my mentors. These men taught me to be accepting of others and love life no matter what. That was a lesson not only taught, but it was also lived day in and day out. I’m honored to be an uncle and be able to continue that lineage of teaching and mentoring.

These are some of the lessons I’ve learned that make me who I am. Love, honesty, integrity, persistence, and self-reliance, responsibility and character. Whether I’m an advocate, a friend, a father, an uncle, a son, or even a guardian, these are the values I live by. They are the cornerstone of my beliefs.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I’m 55 years old and currently reside in Austin, Texas. 50 years ago I was born in Houston, Texas. I was raised in a 3 bedroom single family house in the humble but nice neighborhood of Shenandoah, Texas by my Mom and Dad. I was born in 1959 so it was a different time. My Dad made more money than the typical earner of that era. Our family was almost upper middle class which made it possible for me to go to the same college preparatory school as the upper class kids. My Dad’s financial success came from pioneering an industry called water hauling. He was the first one in a formerly oil only industry to haul drinking water and to refine used drinking water for people with water systems on their properties instead of using city water. My Mom made twice the money of the average woman of her age as a local news anchor in Houston. I got the best of both worlds. I can remember a time in my life when I wanted to be a white person. My life experiences make me who I am. They make me a real person. I will say what a normal white person would say in almost every situation. When I write of a black person I am not speaking on their behalf. I am speaking on behalf of what a normal black person would be thinking or feeling. Things have changed for the better. My neighborhood is now mixed races but until about 2003 my neighborhood was 100% white. Everyone I met at college was from a different racial background. It was heaven. All of the experiences and hardships and joys and triumphs I have experienced made me who I am. In 1988 I went to Japan on a budget trip to teach English. This was back before Japan started flooding the world with made in Japan garbage. It was one of the best trips I have ever taken in my life. I was able to experience life in a different culture and to see how much my life in America was from my own unconscious point of view. It was an incredible time of life. In my travels around the world I found that I was respected for being American and for that even being from a middle class family. I began to realize that the exposure I had from my childhood was extraordinary. Some of my childhood memories are floating around on youtube videos. Being able to view the world from a different point of view is valuable. For example when I travel to Mexico or Japan I look around for signs of American culture so I can begin to understand both cultures better. It was in 2002 I went on my first trip to Japan and married my Japanese wife. It was in 2006 that we immigrated to Japan. It was in late 2007 we bought and began building our house in Minamizawa, Yamada, Iwate, Japan. In 2014 we moved to our present house in Higashimatsushima, Miyagi, Japan. I see a 360 degree view of the world. I am able to look at the issues in America with the perspective of a foreigner. Many of the issues are of little to no concern to a Japanese or Mexican. As you can tell by my company website inari-tai.com and my youtube videos I have become a tour guide. It is a unique thing to be a middle aged white man tour guide in Japan but this is the career I chose when I felt like there were few options open to me in Japan. My main hobbies are getting to know Japanese people and helping people from other countries to appreciate life in Japan. I am totally bilingual in Japanese and English. I’ve spent many years of my life learning Japanese and maintaining my English. I am able to communicate with about 100,000,000 Americans and with about 126,000,000 Japanese people. The Japanese tend to be quiet people and do not speak English well but it is possible to make friends with the quiet Japanese. For example I was invited into the home of a family who wanted to practice their English. They were sincere and they stuck to it. If you stick to Japanese people they will begin to trust you but if you act like a gaijin then they will hate you or ignore you. That is the way it is here. I am your ordinary typical white male. I am not a black man and I am not a Jew. I also have the privilege of being a foreigner. Japan is a country filled with rewards but also filled with frustrations. Now that I am 55 years old I have ample life experience to write a book. I began teaching myself how to write books at age 55. People ask me all the time how long my book will take and I tell them around 6 months. This is my aim.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I’m nearly 60 years old, a retired advertising executive, and a visual arts fanatic. I grew up in the Bronx, a middle-class kid in a working class family. I moved to Southern California in 1980, though I grew up spending summers at my Grandmother’s house in New Jersey, an old house with a big back yard on the edge of a farmer’s field. My Grandmother was a talented and generous person, well loved by her family and friends. She died before I was born, but her memory lives on in the wonderful people she left behind. I remember seeing her long, flat nails after her manicure and thinking how old she looked; years later I would still cut mine like hers. Like my Grandmother, my mother loved flowers and gardening. We grew most of our vegetables, including beans, tomatoes, carrots, peppers, and leeks. I also grew roses, dahlias, pansies, and zinnias. I also had a grape arbor, an apple tree, and a peach tree. I went to high school in Ridgewood, New Jersey, an odd little place in Bergen County. It’s a nice town, but I had some unpleasant experiences in school that colored my life. We didn’t go to church every Sunday like my father wanted, but the local Greek Orthodox church had a festival once a year with Greek music, dancing, delicious Greek food, and the best Greek yogurt on the East Coast. My father also loved going to Macy’s in New York City every winter to see the windows, the animated displays, and the snowflakes. I’m a bit of an old man trapped in the body of a young man.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am a transgender woman, 55 years old, grew up in a beautiful beach community in Orange County, California, to an Upper Class, religious family. (I don’t like saying “upper middle class”, as it makes people think I mean “middle class”, and there is no such thing as a “middle class”. As in every other country, we either have Upper, Middle, or Lower Class. Not one word I am about to say is a complaint or whining. Everything I have to say is a complaint about “Americantians”.) I have lived through the memory destroying fog of the 9/11, Military, Police, and Government Industrial Complex propaganda of the last 18 years, and I can only be amazed at what hard work is put into lying to every person in every life in the United States.

I left the work force in the 1990’s, and am fortunate enough to be able to live a quiet life on the grounds of my group home for people with “Diagnosed Mental Health Disorders”(Say that five times fast!). Never been happier with my lot. I get to change outfits 10 times a day, wear 6 different underwear, plus 2.5 different bras. I am able to live as I would in my own apartment, but never feel alone.

I work out at the gym a couple hours a day, spend as much time as I can outside, be it jogging, or shooting (My only hobby, and my only target 3-4 days a week), or walking. I am huge on using as much public transportation, and walk as much as I can, but I wouldn’t change the weather I live in, for the world. As I walk, I watch people, walk with people, sit in bars and watch people. If you think you have never been “Gawked” or “Stared at”, not even once, then you would be wrong, and have absolutely no idea what it is to grow up as a trans* person in the 60’s through 90’s.

I understand that most people can’t even comprehend what it is to not want to even be noticed. Even though I love being looked at, and even loved by my many admirers, I can’t imagine a life without the deep seated desire to become invisible in a crowd. Or at least to be noticed, and treated as, “Just one of the guys”.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
Please don’t ask me questions that will make me uncomfortable. But I am retired, own a condo and have lived in Florida for 20 years. I am 60 years old. I grew up in Boston, the only child of my widowed mother, with her sisters and an assortment of Jewish grandparents and other relatives scattered around the Boston area. Raised by intelligent, 21st century feminists with good liberal values, I was lucky enough to receive a great public education in the reasonably well off town where I grew up, and the fact that my mother struggled to keep us afloat while she did so taught me very early on that poverty and lack of basic needs is no way to live. I had to walk everywhere because I had no money for a car, bus fare, or even so much as a bike to ride to school. That meant being in the peak physical condition I was in then.

I also learned to appreciate my privilege as a white, heterosexual, cisgender woman from a solid middle class background. No one in my family had ever had the benefit of even 8th grade schooling, much less anything beyond that. And some of my mother’s relatives had never even been out of Boston. We were in a completely different world from theirs, even though we were very close. I don’t know what my future might have held in store for me had my parents’ premature deaths been the end of the story. My mom wasn’t even 40, and my father was younger than that. As it was, I was 17 when I graduated from high school and found myself able to continue my education on a scholarship I had earned the year before.

I’ve always enjoyed reading and writing, so the path to college seemed a clear one for me, and I am forever grateful for my education. The experience I gained from living in a city also taught me to question everything and think for myself, two habits that have served me well all my life. As I told my interviewers, “I have no idea where I’m going, and as long as it’s here, in the USA, I’m going to be happy with whatever happens next.” That turned out to be true. Living in New Orleans from 1991-2006 was an eye-opening experience, as it was to discover what life was really like for so many ordinary, non-privileged people. It was also instructive to find out how wrongheaded people could be on so many issues, but I was also discovering just how gullible and dysfunctional I had been about my own situation as a girl. This shift in perspective would not have been possible without this exposure to so much more in my world. After I arrived in New Orleans, my vision expanded rapidly to take in all sorts of new concepts and ideas, and to experience the world as much as possible through my own eyes and from my own point of view. What a relief it was to suddenly find myself not just looking at others, but feeling like I belonged in the larger world! When I moved back to Florida, I was not yet willing to succumb to the bland, suburban culture here, so I really wanted to find a way to live my life where I still felt like I was part of the world instead of part of an individualist rat race for a better, and ultimately, different life.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I am a 50 year old young woman. I was born in Chicago, Illinois to second generation American born parents. I grew up in the suburbs and attended private catholic school through high school. At the age of 17, I went away to college in central New York state and I have lived in New York ever since, moving in and out of the city, and the State. I have two children, a boy and a girl, now age 11 and 12. I love them more than anything, and though they drive me crazy and piss me off from time to time, they keep me sane, honest and true to myself. My family has been my anchor. They are the ground upon which I was raised, and I wouldn’t be anywhere, if not for them.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I am 50 years old and married with two children. I moved to the US at age 26, but have spent most of the last 20 years living abroad. My father was an international executive and I grew up in Iran and India. Although the last 20 years in the US have been wonderful, I very much miss the constant exposure to different languages, cuisines, and cultures that my childhood provided.
