I think this experience as an “international” child shaped my perspective on the world and fostered a deep interest in the various facets of human development. A practical nature prompted me to become a medical doctor and over the past 25 years I have had the privilege of practicing medicine in an array of locations: a surgical ICU in a state prison in the 1990’s; an ER in the British Isles, a Kibbutz in Israel; and currently a private outpatient practice in the Philadelphia area. In addition to raising two wonderful kids, my main passion is family genealogy, and I have been able to trace our family tree back 3,000 years to the Bronze age near Biblical Jericho. Today, the Smiths are scattered across the globe, but one can often find us camping, hiking, or sailing whenever possible. I spent five years on the Board of Directors of the Pennsylvania Distance Learning Association, and recently completed a four-year term as the Board Chair of Araminta Freedom Initiative, an NGO in Northeast Philadelphia. Two recently turned 11-year-old twin girls and I live in North Philadelphia.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
Hello, I’m Tom. I’m 53. Born in Buffalo, NY. Father went into the Navy, moved us to Long Beach, California and Long Beach, Mississippi, which is what a sailor wife does. In eighth grade, we moved back to upstate NY, living there until college, where I went for four years and dropped out. Because I dropped out, I spent the next 25 years living in San Diego, mostly. People who say that San Diego is the San Andreas Fault, have never lived in New York. New York is a plutocracy. You can’t go anywhere in life without someone saying, well, you just don’t have the right connections. There are things you don’t know that you need to know. San Diego is the opposite of that, though I think a lot of people who moved there out of privilege (like Arnold, and his Swedish wife) think of the city like it was still Hawaii, or it’s actually an island (like I used to), and the rest of California doesn’t exist, or doesn’t matter.

Long Beach, California is where I learned how to be in the world. I was around black people for the first time, and I watched how they treated people, I watched how the “rich kids” of “The 10” and “The Peninsula” behaved. There I saw the essential mystery of how the world works. People don’t “live” in a hierarchical society, they “inhabit” it. It has its own physics, its own laws, that can’t be bent by will or power or money or your fervent prayers or your desires. What matters and does not matter can change at a whim, and nothing you can do about it. People who think they have the power to change it, and demand that they be allowed to change it are simply wrong. The way I learned this was through three different schools: the School of Integration, the School of Youth, and the School of Surveillance and Indenture. I look back on these experiences and understand that’s all it was. A lot of it was in middle school, around 13-14.

The School of Integration opened my mind as to the way the world truly works. I was around black people for the first time. In San Diego, I mostly just hung around with my white friends, and never really knew any black kids. That was the basic geometry of everything, white boys hanging around white girls. That’s not to say I didn’t see a black kid in San Diego, it just meant, in the final analysis, they didn’t mean anything. In California, black kids made white kids seem normal, while white kids (the kids from the 10 and the Peninsula) made black kids seem normal. In New York, the kids from the 10 and the Peninsula seemed normal, but when you saw a white kid in middle school, you’d be amazed. They weren’t normal. In California, there were a bunch of different versions of normal.

In California, everything was brand new to me. The way people behaved, their coolness or their lack of it, the language they spoke, the sex they had. (The sexuality I never even thought about in San Diego. It was absent, just like women in the 1970s. I had no interaction with them, at all. I mean, once I saw a woman naked, as she was standing on a chair changing the light bulb in her apartment, that’s it.) In California, I was put in with a bunch of 13 year old boys and we were forced to take showers together and being naked in front of each other was the rule. I’d never done that in my life, never seen 13 year old boys naked, with all their stuff hanging out. And these were white boys, kids from the east coast, most of them Jewish boys, and they were the most cocky and shameless little kids you’d ever seen, these kids from the beach and these black kids, and it totally confused me. I had never seen anything like it before. It was a combination of these three worlds. Before California, I was white enough to fit in, just by being white. Here I was, among all these people, these other skin tones, and I was expected to look at myself and do something about it. And the guys in the shower? I would sit and watch, usually with my hands in my pocket, wondering what would happen if I said something. Most of the time they were more concerned about getting lice than taking their willy in their hands and whacking it off in front of everyone. But I had to watch, because I wasn’t sure what they’d do. It was basically this, or fight them. I never really fought them, and they seemed happy to be naked. And there was one skinny Jewish kid from Long Island, his name was Mike Ratner, and he was cooler than all of us. He had a massive collection of thick, leather-bound gay sex novels, with graphic pictures of people sucking each other off, people jerking each other off, and I’m a 14 year old kid with this character in my life, and he’s the one who told me that it was okay to have feelings about what I’m seeing. He was a teenage boy who liked to write down every sensation he ever had about his feelings about that. Anyway, I started to realize this was a normal thing for white boys in California and that’s how I got through that. California brought me the School of Integration and the School of Youth. California is the most wild and cool place on the planet.

But in middle school, it was terrible for me, because I got in trouble. A group of kids, who were having a fight, pulled me into the fight and called me a nigger. I was not to understand that they were white, although I knew what a nigger was. I’d never heard it, except for in movies. In that little moment, it was like a firework exploding in my chest, a firework that changed the entire world. I got beat up for it, and tried to explain to a teacher and the mother of the kid who beat me up that I had a Jewish friend and a black friend and I knew what “the n-word” meant. They didn’t want to hear that, but I had to fight to make them hear it. So, when I said that, I had to say it all, because I was supposed to know what the n-word meant, and it was a warning to them that if they were going to play that card, they’d be playing it against me. I learned to fight. The kids who beat me up thought they won, but the kids who said I was a nigger won. It also left me a gaping hole in my heart that nothing was going to fill.

School of Youth happened in high school, when I went to Marin Catholic High School, in the San Francisco Bay Area. I had to be expelled from Long Beach High School, in order to avoid the Vietnam draft. I don’t know what would have happened if I had stayed there, but I’m sure it would have ended badly. They would have torn my head off, while putting a bullet in my heart.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I’m 56. I was born in a refugee camp in Thailand. I grew up as a kid of the State. California Board of Education assigns foster parents and foster family agencies place kids with families. Some kids were relatives, most were not. I spent most of my early childhood in foster homes, group homes, and a boarding school. I attended regular schools in my community. When I graduated, I was placed in the high school and the Jr. High School in my neighborhood. I was enrolled in a high school for learning disabled kids and I graduated from there, with a high school diploma. I attended community college for 2 years. I took Early Childhood Development classes. I was part of a group of kids that worked through the State Youth Advisory Committee and helped develop the text books that are still being used to teach adults to care for preschool children. The information we put in the first book covered infant and toddler care. When the original group met to develop the next book, a couple of the people who were in the first group went on to write the first toddler book. At that same meeting, we met another group that was also working on a book on infants and toddlers. Later, when I did evaluations for families, I would look in the text books for the latest research. It would be information we’d put in the books. So I graduated from a high school for learning disabled kids. My experience is important to me, but I know it’s been written about in a book. It’s not a household name, but if you’re interested, you can look up “K-12 California Resources for Learning Disabilities”. My parents moved to the middle of nowhere in Montana where my Dad got a job. I got my GED and went back to California, but this time in an apprenticeship for TV and video. Then I moved to a small town in Central Washington where I worked at the local TV station. I worked as the chief engineer for the state association of electric cooperatives and the TV Station in Central Washington where I became staff. I then went to a university. This will sound impossibly strange. But hear me out. I went to college on a full tuition waiver as an English major. For 3 years, I worked full time and attended classes full time. I worked as an usher in the student union, I worked at KUGS, the student radio station. I worked at the school catalog office. The book on “The History of the World” we all learned about in high school, was in development at my university. I met the editor and got to read the book before it was published. I then moved to the UK. First, to volunteer at a 150 bed, war damaged, children’s hospital in Liverpool. I did that for two years and met a lot of famous people and I was a scab for a union that was in a strike when a government grants program ended the money that had paid the hospital for the past 120 years. After 2 years, I moved to London. There, I became the staff librarian for a club for Russian emigres in exile, so I did a lot of research into the history of the Bolshevik revolution. I taught myself Russian, French, and Spanish. I did a lot of translating of all different kinds. I read a lot. I worked for a television studio that did programs for Italian immigrant communities in various locations and I traveled with them to shoot programs. I did 30 minute prime time programs for about 20 different immigrant communities. I got tired of working for the entertainment business, went back to school and got a degree in Education. When I got that degree, I moved to LA and worked for my friend, who was the manager of the largest movie studio in town. I found a school district to hire me in their rehab program. I ran a classroom that helped students with physical handicaps gain an education and it was also their primary employment center. Students had disabilities, they were pregnant teens. We went to schools and taught parents, teachers, and students about how to support the disabled. It was very exciting and I was married for that. After about 4 years, we had had enough of the whole fiasco and left. We went to New York and I was a visiting professor at Columbia. After that, I went back to CA and got a position doing “Literacy Buddies”, which was teaching parents and guardians about how to become Literate. I became a program manager for a number of different program areas. I then moved to Hawaii and started my own business, then met my current husband and we decided to move back to the mainland. I’ve been working for a home health care company as a social worker for a long time and I’m doing a lot of training for the Medicare program. I’m now in the midst of making revisions to my job description to try and prevent a decrease in the dollars for our seniors, by using “Comprehensive Care Plans”. And that’s a really condensed version of my life story. I could spend all day just answering the questions about my life.

Two things that have stuck with me for decades, regarding the refugee camp in Thailand. And this is just me and my life story. I met the first person who had been in the camp as an infant when I was there. I met the first person born there, as well. And I met the last person to have left the camp, so far.

The United States is a nation of immigrants. We still are. And my grandparents were immigrants. They had to meet all kinds of restrictions to get in. But they were the lucky ones. The first Chinese to arrive in America, without treaty rights, were 150 years after people from China were kicked out of the first nation, so they lived over 400 years as nomads in the mountains, on and off the coast, in the mountains, on and off the coast. There was a constant war between the 2 nations. They were nomads living off the land, until they were chased off the coast. Then, it was mountains. Then, on, off. Mountains, coast. It was a practice done to push them in the direction of the continent, so we’d become the land of plenty they could get to. It was a constant push to get them there, so they got there after 150 years.

The Vietnamese boat people fleeing Cambodia were so tired. They had nothing, they were broke. And yet, they kept going. To feed their families. They waited until they had enough money to get to a boat and then they still had to arrange passage on the boat and finally, they ended up here. They were so tired, people said, because they were forced to work in agriculture their whole lives. Working so hard they fell asleep as they worked. People were asked why we weren’t more like them, because they had the hopes for the futures of their families, they kept looking forward, they had no possessions to tie them down, they kept moving.

You can’t help but wonder what the outcome was, what the outcome will be, for the 1.5 million Rohingya, driven by pure torture from Myanmar, so much that they’re fleeing in spite of the risk of drowning, to Malaysia, where their next generation will live on this planet, having left behind their whole lives and families.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I am 56, born and raised in Chicago, moved to Nashville for a year in 2010, but came home (i.e. back to Nashville in 2011). I was a military brat, but lived most of my life in Chicago. I am a divorced mother of four daughters, three of whom have left the nest for various reasons. I have three grand children, two boys and one girl. I love my family (immediate and extended). What is important to me: Friends, Family, Church, Work. I’m a little difficult when it comes to relationships. I tend to be close mouthed. It takes some time for me to relax, trust, and open up to others. I’m loyal, faithful and willing to compromise. I enjoy writing, playing poker (not very well), reading books, watching movies and games (Spurs fan), water activities (pool, lake, ocean), traveling (which I’ve done very little of), trying new things and trying to experience new places. I can be a homebody as well as an out going gregarious person. I enjoy my own company and appreciate my alone time as well. I can be domestic, silly and sometimes funny, but I can also be quiet and serious.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: To the best of my knowledge, I believe I am 59 years old. I was born in Kings Lynn, England. I do not have much education in the traditional sense. When I was twelve years old, my mother died of cancer, and my father began to drink. He had a very bad heart, and I believe the drinking hastened his death. He died when I was thirteen. My father’s death had the most impact on me. Being forced to raise myself and care for my siblings became a daily struggle. I lived with a man when I was fifteen who became my first spouse. He convinced me to fake my own death to get away from my family. I do not feel that either family saw me as someone to love. They never missed me or asked me where I had gone. It is as if they never had or wanted children. My first spouse had little regard for me either, and I left him after two years of marriage. I began to consider myself a nomad since my movements were largely unfettered by others. I have traveled the world, working in taverns as I meet new people, staying as long as it pleases me before moving on. I have enjoyed fine wine and strong ale, and I have even enjoyed the company of prostitutes when they pleased me. I have tried my hand at the Battle Wizard Class, but I find it to be restrictive to my needs as a nomad. I am more suited as a Battle Mage, where I can kill enemies and call upon the dead. I enjoy history and often hear tales from the people I meet about the things that are important to them. I also enjoy visiting theatres and being entertained by song and dance. I plan to live my life to the fullest and seek new adventures.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I am 55 years old. I was born in Boston, Massachusetts, as was my father. My father was 3rd generation American and his father a coal miner in the UK. My mother was an American whose father fled the Hashemite Kingdom to come to America. The Saxe-Coburg and Gotha family really don't want to talk about that right now, but both sides trace their genealogy back to the Germanic speaking people of England and France.

My parents divorced when I was 7 and we moved with my father to Natick, Massachusetts. I went to Natick public schools until I graduated from Natick High School where I took a lot of college level courses. That led to my getting a full scholarship to NYU (New York University), where I graduated with a Bachelor of Science degree in Business Administration in 1982. I got my first job right out of college in New York City where I worked as a photographer's assistant, which is a very good entry level position. I then worked in high end fashion photography. I also acted in off-Broadway plays and some very well know commercials, as well as New York films.

I worked with many famous directors including Martin Scorsese and Woody Allen.

In 1996 my wife accepted a position in California and we moved out here. I started working in film and television commercials. A few years later I switched to architectural photography. Then about 8 years ago I returned to school and have studied marketing, psychology, sociology and philosophy. I also have a number of professional designations from the building industry.

In my spare time I work as a print model and act as a pretend character in an interactive game and therapy program for Autistic children. My wife is a classroom teacher and co-owns a successful tutoring business in LA. We have two sons, one who is a recently graduated Bachelor of Science and the other who just completed high school.

I read voraciously in the fields of philosophy, psycholoty, and sociology. At NYU I majored in the history of philosophy, including epistemology, metaphysics, social and political philosophy and Eastern philosphy. I have studied science from a molecular level to an astronomical one. For fun I read about sports, science fiction, books about the male/female relationship, and biographies. My library is well over 500 books, primarily science, philosophy, and history. I have played chess at a national level, listen to and make music, play piano and guitar, write music, write, play intramural soccer, am a closet stand up comedian, and work out regularly to stave off my father's fate as an early onset diabetic and sufferer of high blood pressure. I do not smoke or drink and I don't do any drugs.

My interests and work have taken me around the world. I have acted and directed in commercials in France, Holland, New Zealand, Australia and Thailand. I have been to Greece, Italy, France, Austria, Belgium, Holland, Germany, Czech Republic, South Africa, Hong Kong, Singapore, Malaysia, the Philippines, Mexico and the Dominican Republic.

In 1996 I became a US citizen. My wife, mother and brother are US citizens also, and my children are US citizens by birth.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
(Sigh.) I’m 50. I grew up in Indiana and moved to the Dallas area in 1998. My wife and I decided to retire there, despite having to live on a bridge in Arlington and eating Altoids, until we could get social security.

This event called us into a cooking contest. We won. And after that came on the radio. Like many other cockroaches, I was sucking up the poison to try to survive and ended up living in some wretched shit state where I’m still trying to get out.

Then came the whole Nazi thing where somehow Trump f****** with Obama made the vile bag of [expletive deleted] think that Obama might have been born in Kenya and therefore black so it should have been okay to criticize him for something that he didn’t even do (because a record exists in which he was born in Hawaii and has had a totally successful presidency). And the sheer stupidity (cognitive and otherwise) of a large swath of Republicans (and people, in general) is incredible to me. The values held by these people were so misplaced, too many died in the process, I don’t know where to start on that mess.

I don’t know, why can’t everyone just accept who they are and be confident about it? Isn’t that what Christianity is trying to say, and secularism is trying to do? It all seems so simple to me, and yet everybody just goes ape [crackers].

Then for fun, we have metapuzzle.org on most of our free time and try to be as good as we can. I have an irrational, irrational love of algebraic solution to linear and non-linear problems involving addition, subtraction, multiplication and division, and I’ve loved solving tough puzzles since I was a kid, maybe why I loved mathematics so much, and then, I’m a totally deaf man who spent most of his life unable to take part in listening to music.

So that’s enough. Ask more or less any question and you’ll get at least a minimal reply, at least around this topic area.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I am 51 years old. I grew up in a small town in Ohio with my mother, father, and older brother. We weren’t very religious although my parents did teach me some bible stories. My brother went off to college before I was old enough for high school. I’m the oldest of three children in the family and my father died when I was 14 years old. I was terrified. I had a lot of questions, and I didn’t get many answers. I got involved in swimming and was a dedicated high school student. In 1974, at the age of 17, I applied to the University of Michigan to study animal behavior. I always loved watching animals and I wanted to understand them. I was rejected by the University of Michigan and would have to wait a year before I could reapply. In the meantime, I got a job at a veterinarian’s office and fell in love with animals even more. I really looked forward to my work. Since I couldn’t go to Michigan, I re-applied to the University of Cincinnati in 1975 and was accepted. I majored in science and spent hours in labs before classes and after classes. I wanted to succeed. Then in 1981, at age 23, I met my husband Michael when he moved into the apartment next to mine. Michael has a blue sapphire wedding band that I bought for him many years ago. He’s a geologist and I went to law school to support him and our family.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am a 52-year-old Caucasian female who grew up in Dallas, Texas. I am a mother of three children who live with their father in Colorado. My father left when I was 3 years old and died when I was 14. My mother died when I was 45 from a long-term illness (but not AIDS). She was a Baptist minister.

I like to write because I like words and the way they look on the page. I’m good at it. I have a way with words and I’ve been told I am a good writer. I love reading essays, short stories, articles, even books (when I have the time). I love music. I love to dance. I like rap and hip-hop, some alternative and old school (Disco to Mid-80’s), but I love any kind of music that is soothing to the soul. I love cooking (mostly baking). I love taking long, hot baths. I don’t like to swim, get my hair wet or get wet. I don’t like to get my hair cut, either.

I love my children. I am a great mother and I love my kids very much. Their father is their father, and I never tell my kids what to think or do. They don’t have to listen to me. They know I love them and I want only the best for them.

I love flowers. I have always loved flowers. They make me happy. I love the way they smell and look and the colors they come in. Flowers just make me feel good.

I like to see movies and watch TV. I am a movie buff. I love the little silver box, as my mom would call it, better than the big silver box with the knobs you have to turn to change the channel. I love movies, sitcoms, all kinds of TV shows. I watch more TV than I should, but I just love it. I love soap operas like Days of Our Lives, Passions, and The Young and the Restless. I also watch Oprah, Sally, J. Moore, Ellen, Jerry Springer and Maury Povich sometimes. Sometimes I watch the WE channel. Sometimes I like to watch movies when I can because there is nothing on TV. I like to watch the soap operas on the soap channel. I like to watch the Disney channel for my kids.

I’ve always been a good person. I did some bad things as a teenager, but everybody does, at least I think everybody does. When I got pregnant with my first child, I quit running around with the wrong crowd and got my life straight.

I have been raped, but my attackers were never caught. I was molested by my mother’s boyfriend when I was 13, but they didn’t catch him either. He was only fined and lost his job with the airline. I lost my virginity when I was 14 years old. My mother found out and she tried to make me keep it a secret, but I told my friends anyway. She had an affair with another man when I was 18 and it broke my heart. She died when I was 45 and that broke my heart, too.

I love my dog, Fancie. I can’t go anywhere unless I have her with me. She goes to work with me everyday. I love her and she loves me. She’s always there for me and she is my best friend. I love her more than life itself. She’s almost 3 years old and I’ve had her since she was a little puppy.

I was in an abusive relationship from when I was 27 to when I was 35. He left me with scars and emotional damage I am trying to get over. He was cheating on me and lying to me and making me jealous, but he didn’t hurt me physically. I told him when we broke up he was going to end up in jail or dead, but it didn’t happen that way. Instead, I cut all ties and moved to Denver, Colorado with my children. There, I have a good job and I have not had any contact with him for almost 17 years.

Anyway, that’s me in a nutshell. So if you want to know me better, you’ll just have to get to know me. It’s about time you started talking. You can start by telling me about your history, if you haven’t done that already. It may not seem important right now, but if you live through this experience, I’m sure you’ll think you’ve lived more than one life. By the way, my name is Stephanie.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I'm 52 years old. Grew up in Michigan with 3 brothers and 1 sister. My dad was a school principal and my mother was a stay-at-home mom. Great parents, very old-fashioned. Attended St Joe’s Catholic grade school and high school, before attending SIU in Carbondale IL. I graduated in 1985 and have been selling insurance and other financial products since 1991. As a successful business owner for over 25 years, I have a strong track record of creating business solutions, cutting costs and consistently increasing the performance and productivity of the department and individuals. I've never been afraid to ask questions and do the hard work necessary to get the job done. I believe if you truly give it your all, it makes the reward that much sweeter. I take pride in the relationships that I build. I can call myself a better person because of my faith and actions in life.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I’m 55 years old and I was born in Calgary, Alberta, Canada. I was the 5th of 7 kids. My mother worked at an orphanage (as a house parent) for many years and because of that, my brothers and sisters and I were a part of some charity organizations where we did things like ‘snowshoe’ for food. I didn’t get into public speaking until my brother gave me the manuscript to what was my first book and asked me to read it. I asked myself, “What’s stopping me from writing a book too?” Because I had an opinion about something that was troubling me, I found that I was able to write my first book in 30 days. When I was in high school, I was one of 10 kids in my graduating class. I was the class president and I was the youngest and I was in the highest grades. That set me on a pathway to not only enjoy being in front of people but to become a high achiever as well.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am 51. I grew up in Spokane, Washington and went to Davenport High School. I started my professional career in IT with AT&T (contract work through Avion Engineering) at Fort Lewis, Washington. I was an Avion contractor on the S-1 project (commercial DOT) from 1994-96 and an Avion systems engineer from 1996-2001. At Avion, I worked closely with Ken Dozier to refine his new concept of managing the large enterprise. Ken was working with a number of small entrepreneurial companies on these same challenges.

In 2001, I left Avion to move to Atlanta to help a startup - Platform Logic Technologies. It was a SaaS subscription company that tried to address a different customer need than on demand from a service standpoint. I also served as Vice President of Marketing.

After P-L became insolvent in 2002-03, I founded Point Logic Technologies with my friend, Ethan Dreilinger. During this time, Point Logic spun out of P-L Technologies and the name changed to LogicTree. I worked on the product from development to launch, with Ken, on the consulting side. I am close personal friends with Ken and have consulted with him for decades. He is someone I can trust with my life.

I led the effort to get LogicTree into Influx from 2004 - 2005. LogicTree was developed to leverage the market position of Influx. LogicTree was instrumental in bringing the first Influx hosted companies live. In September 2005, our first hosted customer, The Recreation Center of Highland Park (managed by HFFA) was put on the books.

In 2006, Sandler Partners and Influx expanded the managed services partnership. Influx wanted to have more channels for services while LogicTree wanted to be able to deliver services in the cloud. LogicTree wanted to augment its products and services with best in class offerings. We re-launched the service in November 2006 as LogicCloud.

LogicTree was instrumental in moving Influx from a small company serving many of the existing customers to a highly profitable company that provided services to the largest, highest-credit quality companies in the country. Today, the number of concurrent users is over 40,000 and increasing by approximately 30% each year.

LogicTree was established with Ken as the president. Ken has complete control over LogicTree operations. I serve in an advisory capacity. It's a great arrangement.

I am most proud of what Ken has accomplished. The rapid rate of growth, the loyalty of the shareholders, the speed of service, the corporate culture, the people and quality of the products are the reason I continue to support Ken. I believe in Ken.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I am a 52 year old US citizen. I moved to the UK from Long Island, NY (about 2 hours outside New York City) when I was 15. I attended London University and did not graduate, instead I went on to open a Pizza and Pasta restaurant and later a second with a friend. I left the restaurant business in 1991 and concentrated on IT. I have been in IT for 23 years. I married in the UK and have 2 adult children. My wife is from England and I live in a very small village in North Northumberland. When we are not in our village, we are in the US in Indiana, driving to Rocky Mountain National Park, riding the trains of USA Rail Pass or wandering around the lakes of the Upper Peninsula of Michigan.

I have attended college in London and have a degree equivalent in IT. I have certifications in business analysis and analysis of IT requirements. I served in the British Army in the UK Infantry (8th King's Own Scottish Borderers). I served in Northern Ireland, Germany and the Falklands. I have lived in Greece, Germany and Holland and worked in Russia.

I have spent a career in IT, managing and directing projects and accounts. For the past three years, I have held the position of Business Analysis and Analysis of IT Requirements, working with large multi-nationals to improve business efficiency through the use of IT solutions. I am now a member of a team working to help a client solve their IT problems. When I am not working, I am a keen golfer, usually playing about 50 - 60 times a year. I would love to play more, but the courses are busy. When I am not golfing I am either researching US history or working on my house, which is filled with museum quality US and Northumberland/Anglo American history artifacts, such as an 1800's typewriter collection. It is a passion of mine to collect history and share it with others.

I am honest and hardworking. I believe that life is full of opportunities and it is what you do with those opportunities that determines what sort of person you are. I treat everyone that I meet with kindness, courtesy and respect, regardless of who or what they are. I have not met a stranger since I came to the UK.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am 50 years old, I am from Hayward, CA and went to school in Sacramento, CA.

The experience of taking the SAT tests was something that I would never forget. I was nervous and excited, which led me to forget some important things I needed to be successful in life. It all started when I went to San Francisco, CA to take the SAT test.

As soon as I started taking the test I knew I was in trouble. I completely blanked out and my mind went blank. I couldn’t remember my name and what my purpose was. I left the test thinking I did horrible, so I prayed to the Lord that I wouldn’t fail the test. I prayed that I would pass the test so I could continue on to my goals and dreams. The whole way home I was nervous and in fear that I was going to fail the test.

When I got home I told my father that I had a bad day. I told him about my problems, which led him to ask me what I did on the test. I told him I couldn’t answer any questions. He then told me he was going to pray for me. He prayed that I wouldn’t fail the test. I remember thinking to myself, if I fail the test I won’t be able to go to college and I won’t be able to follow my dreams.

The next morning I went to the letterbox and I was surprised to see a letter from my high school. It was my SAT results. I went back home to take the test results to my parents. As soon as I walked in, I saw my mother looking at my results. My mother turned and looked at me and told me I got a 1210 and that I made it. I walked into my bedroom and jumped on my bed and thanked the Lord for answering my prayers. Now I know that I can accomplish my goals and dreams and I can continue to follow my dreams. That is how the Lord has impacted my life.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am in my early 50s. I grew up in New York City, after spending the first few years of my life in Japan where my mother came from. My parents were native New Yorkers and they met when my father had moved back to New York to go to the performing arts high school called La Guardia. While he was there, he met my mother, who was also attending that school, and they fell in love. I was conceived in Japan and born in a military hospital in Yokohama. So, to answer your first question, I have dual citizenship with Japan and the USA.

Then we moved to Staten Island when I was a year old, and I grew up and learned English, went to all kinds of different public schools in the city, and had the best high school experience at Fiorello H. LaGuardia High School of Music and Art and Performing Arts, which is generally known as simply LaGuardia. We were an exceptionally talented class, and we all had very different backgrounds. We knew, whether we said so or not, that the school was coveted and that the kids who got in came from all over, and this is before there were zip codes for New York City schools.

We had to perform really well and audition to get in, so everyone expected to be successful in high school, which we all were. All our kids were accepted into colleges and universities across the country, and many of us went on to do really interesting things with our lives after graduating. Some went into music, some into the performing arts, and most were interested in science, medicine and technology. We were so exposed to the arts and creativity that our experiences of arts and science, etc. were the same thing for us. This inspired our creativity and I also learned a lot of mathematics. We met a lot of important people like poets and musicians, as well as politicians and scientists.

I learned a lot about equality and fairness. And I felt empowered by my achievements, and all I knew is that I had to have a career. I had to be a part of the United Nations. I had to make a difference in the world. Since my parents and my upbringing had emphasized doing well academically, studying, excelling and being responsible, I felt quite competitive. I also had to learn how to take my personal values and make them part of my work, as I learned to use the skills to make the world a better place. This is because my parents were both immigrants, who were considered second class citizens until President Truman made it possible for both my mother and father to serve in the US Army. They were both very proud of their country, even though they were second class citizens.

For my father, this was also about overcoming his own fears about himself, which I believe come from the humiliation of growing up with almost no access to music as a child and to other people like himself, who loved it. At the age of ten, he was brought to the United States by his mother, and he went to school here. Because he was such a good singer, people wanted him to join the choir. This took place in an all-white school with black students in the Bronx, and he was singled out by the members of the white choir to sing at school events.

My father told me that as a black student, he had to pass a house in order to get to his own school. When the white teacher who oversaw the choir would order him to sing in front of black kids at school events, this was so traumatizing for him that he would spit on the piano when he came home. He wanted to rebel so badly but did not know how, because he was just a child, and he knew it would mean a beating and maybe even his mother hitting him, which she would. So he just took it. The story he told me was that one day he was so furious that he walked into the choir room, and he smashed the piano. He told me that he did not do this from anger, but from fear.

Then he heard the music coming from the radio of his friend Mr. Adams’ house, and he ran away. His mother went to look for him and found him there, wearing a borrowed coat. He wanted to make her proud so badly because, as an immigrant, she had hope for him, but she did not have the courage to advocate for him, so she told my father that she would be back, and she was taking him with her. It was then that his music teacher begged his mother to leave him at her house. If she had not done this, my father would have not been able to go to LaGuardia. My father told me this story many times, and I know that he believed it was not possible for him to find his place. But music was an outlet that offered him hope. In the end, he saved the best of himself to achieve something he never would have been able to achieve without it. He was also devastated when he lost it. I believe that all of this got into my father, and the fact that he had it in the first place is what motivated me to try my best and do my very best so that nothing could hold me back from my dreams. I was a fighter in and out of school. I was very competitive.

Another important event that influenced me was that, when I was 15, I was abducted by a serial killer. He was a white man named Robert Ben Rhoades. I was saved. I was very active in sports, and he had taken a group of girls who were doing that from one place to another. He had blindfolded us, and we walked a long distance in the snow. I was so happy that I was with other people, because I was afraid of being alone with him.

I remember that I was in the car with him. We were driving for about two hours when I started to hear the car crash. We were in the snow. I was thirsty and saw snow, and wanted to taste it, so I took a small sip of it, and Robert was furious at me. He told me I had not been listening to him, and that I had failed to understand, but I did understand. It was all an illusion. Robert always told me not to do something if I did not know why he wanted me to, and I felt that I should fight for my life, so I did not listen.

I do not know why he stopped the car in the snow, I just know that we were in the snow and that he threw something at me, and I passed out. I woke up in my house, with my brother and sister, and my parents. I heard people talking about me in the kitchen. I thought someone must have abducted my brother or my sister, but it was me. When I looked at myself in the mirror, I thought that the person I saw was totally different. I felt like a stranger to myself, but I did not have to deal with anything. I had the support of my family and my friends, and I have been able to rebuild myself since then.

What are your biggest weaknesses and how do you deal with them?

I believe that everything in life is under my control. I have made many mistakes. However, I believe that everything is under my control and that I am in control of my life.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: My name is Marcia Leonard, I'm 53, and I grew up in Staten Island, NY. I am an adopted child, and was abused until my foster mother saved me at age 12. The pain, fear and struggle of survival in my family forced me to grow up fast. I did well in school, however; my grades were lowered in my senior year due to my absenteeism. I graduated from William Paterson University with a BA in Communications and Public Relations. I started my career working in various TV studios in New York City. While living in Manhattan, I had an affair with a married man. I was conflicted and I hurt my young daughters. I broke up his marriage, ruined his career and we had two children together. I was involved in a disastrous marriage to a very ill man. I struggled with him for over 12 years and raised four children, all while we both battled our demons. I left my husband and moved to South Carolina, where I met my third husband. I have been with him for the past 12 years. I have written about my life experiences, both personal and professional, on my blogs and in my books.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am female. I am 57 years old. I was born in Pennsylvania, but grew up in California. During high school, I took college classes. After graduating high school, I continued to take college classes and earned a certificate in drafting. I began working as an Architectural Drafter. I took some additional classes in Architectural Drafting and Computer Aided Drafting. I fell in love with computers and I transferred to Oregon State University. During school, I took a variety of classes, but was especially interested in Anthropology. I believe it was the teacher because he loved Anthropology and really brought it to life. It gave me a broader perspective of the world and made me realize we are all part of the human family.

I went to work at a large architectural firm for a couple of years, but decided I wanted to be more creative. I went to work for a small firm where I could really learn a lot. I learned the basics of working in construction and working with architects. After about four years, I left and worked for a home builder. During my employment, I bought a home, got married, got divorced, and gave birth to a daughter. I eventually got remarried, my husband passed away, and I raised my two daughters, who are now adults and both married.

I have worked for three different home builders; one was the corporate headquarters of a large home builder and the others were locally owned and operated. It was a very successful endeavor for me. I was able to design homes, oversee pre-construction, marketing, budgets, scheduling, customer service and the installation of equipment. I was also employed as a Superintendent, where I worked on a variety of projects. I worked on renovations, additions and entire new homes. The scope was extensive and it gave me a broad knowledge of building systems and managing all of the teams. After about 11 years, I wanted to try something different, so I began working for a restoration contractor. He needed an estimator and a project manager. I managed several large projects. Some of the projects were repairs after water damage and others were after wind damage. After about five years, I went back to work for a home builder, once again, overseeing the pre-construction and the production process.

Having decided to make this transition about 3 years ago, I have done my best to prepare myself for this transition and have experienced opportunities that are not always available to other professionals. I have had the privilege to work on diverse projects that have ranged from consumer and builder permits, actual construction and then the final inspection. I have worked with a large variety of departments, which include customer service, scheduling, scheduling contractors, building departments, insurance agents, etc.

I understand there are difficult times and it is hard for companies to pay for outside help. I want to be the resource that other companies rely on when they need support and help outside of their resources. I am a versatile professional and I understand the various levels of interaction necessary for success. I was able to work successfully with difficult, unhappy, frustrated customers. I also worked successfully with team members who were new to the industry or their position. I have always been able to ease the concerns of many difficult people; it is something that I am passionate about and this is a talent I want to cultivate. I really want to be that resource that allows others to focus on their immediate tasks and allows them to feel confident in my abilities. I understand the importance of exceeding expectations and strive to achieve that goal consistently.

My strong communication skills are very important to me. I believe that communication is the key to understanding. I enjoy helping others to achieve their goals and I am a firm believer that teamwork is the best approach to accomplish projects. I am a creative problem solver and I am constantly striving to improve processes and introduce productivity and efficiency. I enjoy building trust and bonds with others that last beyond the completion of a project and ensure continued success.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I was born in New York City to middle class parents who were self made. I am 54 years old and grew up in a home where I was valued, but not necessarily loved. I was fortunate to go to college, and married a fellow Marine at age 23. In 2005 my husband and I divorced amicably. I have a single sister who was diagnosed with schizophenia at age 17 and was placed in a mental hospital. She began her journey into mental illness with hallucinations, delusions, and a hatred of women that developed into OCD behaviors (touching water and other surfaces) and paranoia. I became a counselor on campus because I wanted to help teenagers get through their social interactions safely. I became a marine because I wanted to get out of my moldy town and back into the city. The military gave me the money to move to the city and continue my college degree. I graduated, and was awarded a fellowship, and met my mentor who became the single most influential person in my life. I became an attorney and partner in a law firm in Manhattan. I hated the profession but was successful. Today, I am free of that toxic time. I am married to the love of my life. I have two loving, beautiful children and they make my life so rewarding. The background that I have provides me with a very vivid, clear picture of what psychiatric drugging does to individuals and society. I have spent my adult life educating myself on this topic and I am determined to fight for myself and my children and all the people I have come to know who have been harmed by mental health drugs and diagnoses.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am 60 years old. I was raised Catholic and attended religious schools for 18 years until my first year in college. I now practice Christian humanism. The words of both Jesus and Buddha had a profound effect on me. I believe we must be the change we wish to see in the world.

I’m a management consultant and am a Fellow of the Institute of Management Consultants. As a single father raising two daughters I was the driving force of the day to day duties of home and career, while also balancing the demands of work and family.

I love to write. I have more than 30 years’ experience and have written at least 20 books, magazine articles, op-ed columns, and regular online column.

Over the years I became a believer in Liberal values. At least until the last presidential election. Then I fell down and I haven’t gotten up since.

I am on the road to recovery but you can read the story about how I lost my mind here.

I live in sunny San Diego, California with my devoted wife Donna, our beloved Tibetan Terrier Xena, and our two cats, Roxie and Ariel. If you visit I’ll cook you dinner.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I am a 53 year old man and I was born and raised in the State of California. I grew up going to neighborhood schools, play on the streets, and working on our family vineyard. My mother was a homemaker and my father was an entrepreneur and founded our family business. Dad would tell us stories about moving to California in a horse drawn wagon and surviving on fruit crops in the great depression. We would go on long walks into the forest on our family property and Dad would tell us stories from his childhood. I would spend time in our garden and break off a hunk of fresh blackberry and eat it right on the vine. I would often be found in our neighboring groves in search of walnut trees (my favorite) or Citrus trees. I enjoyed helping people. I learned to take on extra work and share the spoils. I would always find another job to do when there was not much to do on the vineyard. I learned from Dad that if I made the effort and did things before they were asked of me then I would be rewarded. I learned to always do more than asked, to show genuine interest in people and what they do, to ask people how they are doing and how I can help them. I don’t remember any specific things that made me who I am, but it was more of a collective life experience. I learned early on how to play for keeps. What matters to me is integrity, honesty, respect, diligence, responsibility, commitment, and effort. I have experienced many joys and sorrows. I find joy in my family and friends and in helping others. Sorrow can be avoided by being diligent and staying true to what I value and what is important in life.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I’m 54. I was born in NYC to a pediatrician and a mother who cared for autistic children.

When I was 5 years old, my mother had enough money to buy a house in the suburbs. I started going to a country day school. At school, I was always the youngest kid. The school was filled with spoiled, rich white kids who were always looking to belittle anyone who came from a lower class family. I was not a good student but I tried my best.

I lost my father when I was 11. He was shot and killed in front of me because he owed money. His cousin came up from the city and killed him. I still have post traumatic stress disorder to this day.

When I was 13, I moved to my moms home state of Alabama. I moved in with my grandmother because my mom moved to California with my dad’s insurance money. My grandmother lived on a plantation. I didn’t know this at first. There were black people working for my grandmother. My grandma told them they could live in the old slave quarters as long as they would cook for me and work for her. My grandmother had a son 20 years earlier. She had given him away to a family to be raised as their own. He came to stay with her when I moved in.

My grandma had a cook who was German. She was a bitch and I was told she was the reason why she wouldn’t become President of the United States. She was always telling the black people what to do and would come up to me and tell me what to do. My grandmother was a leader of a white supremacist group. She tried to recruit me but I was too scared to be around her. My grandma didn’t like me dating her cook’s granddaughter but we did it anyways. Her daughter was the prettiest girl in school but she wasn’t allowed to date anyone because her mother was a Nazi. She tried to kill herself when she turned 18 but my grandmother stopped her. She would cut herself with her nail clippers.

When I was 16, I had a racist girlfriend and a black girlfriend. They would fight with each other and fight with me. My white girlfriend would tell me that black people were bad and I had to make a choice. I chose to break up with both of them. But I didn’t give them up to my grandmother. I took them to their homes. I told them we couldn’t date and then I beat them up. My grandma was furious. When I was 18, I started dealing drugs because I needed the money to go to college. I was making enough money to pay for school. My girlfriend was pregnant at the time. I had no idea where she came from. My grandma kicked her out of the house when she was two. I didn’t want her to leave, but she didn’t have a choice.

I went to college for 3 years. I met my wife at a party. She is a black woman from the south. I went to college in the northern states and she went to a southern school. We didn’t have anything in common at first. She was from a poor family, I was from a rich family. I was a loser with a history of drug addiction. She was a nice girl who was in a car accident in high school and became deaf. She can’t speak and can barely hear anymore. Her parents left her in a car, she lost her hearing, she became mute. I didn’t know she was deaf until after I married her. She doesn’t know sign language, we can’t communicate. She is my everything. I love her and I don’t want to be away from her for a day.

When I was 21, I was in a motorcycle accident. I died and I was paralyzed. I had to be in a wheelchair for a few years. It was hell. I’m very lucky I didn’t lose my life. After a year, I started walking again. It took me 2 more years to get back to normal. I couldn’t work or drive or go out. My wife took care of me. She got me back on my feet and back to normal. My grandma came to visit and she told me I wasn’t strong enough to take care of myself. I said, “I have everything I need in the world. I’m in a wheelchair. I don’t have legs. How am I supposed to walk around? I can’t even go to work. I’m not strong enough to walk. I’m a cripple.”

She said to me, “If you don’t get on your feet, I will have you put in an institution.” I said, “You won’t have to worry about that, I’m getting out of this house.”

So I had to leave. I had to figure out how to live on my own. It was hard. My wife had to live on her own. She didn’t work. She didn’t have friends. She had no one. She was alone. She lived in the city. I lived out in the country. She didn’t know what to do.

I didn’t know what to do either. I had no money, no friends, no support system. My wife and I didn’t talk to each other for a while. We were both sad.

I went to a friend’s house one day. His wife was pregnant. They were having a baby. His wife was an immigrant from El Salvador. She told me that when they got married, they had a gun. They were going to use it on their family. She said that she didn’t want a baby because they were going to kill her parents. She was afraid of her parents. My friend said that he wasn’t a bad guy, but he didn’t want to hurt his wife. My friend told me that he loved her and he was going to take care of her. He said he would take care of her like he would take care of me. He said he wanted to marry her. He loved her, he wanted to take care of her, he wanted to make sure she was safe.

I said, “Ok.” We became friends. We got married. My wife gave birth to a beautiful baby boy.

My wife and I started to become friends again. My grandma came to visit and she told me that I should go to school and get a job. She said that she would help me. I didn’t know what to do. I was in the house. I didn’t know how to take care of myself. My wife took care of me and I loved her for it.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I'm 50 years old. Born in Harbin, China. Grew up in Shenyang, China. I've always had a passion for photography and all things design. At 18 years old I studied design in a little place called Portugal and then I went on to travel for 5 years as a photographer, travelling the world. I eventually went to NY in 1997 to try and build a portfolio to get a job with a big photo agency. I could never get a job with the big agency but was able to find work with a freelance agency and have worked in the States ever since. I currently work at The Whitehouse (for the USA). I also teach and work as an artist.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I am a 60-year-old white man who was born and raised in Ohio. In spite of the fact that I have been a social worker for 35 years, the biggest influence on my life were those 4 years I spent in the Marine Corps. It turned out to be a great thing for me, having been drafted. I learned a lot of life lessons there that have served me well over the years. The people there who were supposed to be my elders were only a few years older than I was at the time and I gained a lot of respect for them. I guess in some ways I am still trying to prove to them that I am worthy. This is why I can’t stand to see kids disrespect their elders. I have a motto that’s stuck with me for all these years: you never know what somebody else has been through. I came to that realization when I was in the Marine Corps and then later, when I was a police officer. You don’t know what a person has been through, so you never want to make assumptions about what someone is capable of. That’s always guided me in how I treat people and I’ve tried to apply that to my professional life as well. I got my BSW from Ohio State in 1981 and my MSW from the University of Chicago in 1984. Then I went to Chicago, to work at the Cook County Public Guardian. This was the first time I had been away from my wife and kids and I was terrified to be so far from them, but I had to prove to myself that I was strong enough to leave. At first, the distance was too great and I was miserable. There was a huge Midwest winter storm and I missed my flight back home and had to stay in Chicago overnight. So I got a job with the Salvation Army doing psychosocial evaluations and I enjoyed that work. After about 9 months, I returned home and was hired by the Lake County Health Department as a child welfare case worker. I was there for 7 years before I went back to graduate school to get my LSW. I became a CASAC (Certified Addictions Specialist) and worked in juvenile correctional facilities for about a year before I got a job with an agency that provided support services to kids in the Chicago Housing Authority. That was where I met my wife, Lottie and she and I got married in 2002. We had our children 2 months apart in 2004 and 2005, and have been raising them ever since. Our son, Braylon, is 15 and our daughter, Blaize, is 13. So, we are at the beginning of the teen years and that is a challenge. They are both typical teens and there have been times when it has been hard, but we have our strategies to deal with their behavior. Our son is a “participatory leader” and our daughter is an “expressive leader.” It has helped that we are doing this together, because then we can each step in when it is needed. As I was raising my kids, I thought about what I had learned when I was raising my kids and decided to get my PHD. This was a goal that I had set when I was a teenager. Then, in 2010, my dad had a stroke. I have been helping him to recover and then in 2016, my mom fell ill. My brother moved back in with her to take care of her, and when my dad was discharged from the hospital, my brother moved in with him to take care of him and take care of his own family. My parents are both in their late 70s and in early 2020, my brother had to take a step back from this role. So I went back to Cleveland, to my family home to be the one to help out and so that my kids would not be away from the rest of the family. I love helping my parents. We have also grown very close since then. In the time that I have been helping my parents, I have also been going back and forth to Cleveland to get my PHD. I recently had a paper published on collaborative leadership. I have always loved the Marine Corps and we have a lot in common. I believe in creating community and not leaving anyone behind. I believe in justice for all, not justice for some. I also love politics and I follow politics very closely. I don’t see myself as a typical liberal, because I am not in favor of the government overstepping their boundaries and interfering in people’s lives. I believe in free will and the pursuit of happiness, so I am not in favor of big government programs, but I do believe in helping the disenfranchised. I am not sure if that makes me a libertarian or an independent. In my current work, I am helping to start a new department at the county level, that works with people with mental illness and the opioid epidemic. We are working to empower the people that we are working with to get the help that they need. In the program that I am developing, we will be working with the people who have mental illness and substance abuse issues and helping them to change their life. It is a 12-month program and it is voluntary. In order to participate, the people need to be working and getting benefits. We help them get into treatment and help them get into a life that is healthy and safe and sober and one that allows them to prosper. This is a big deal for us because the county is becoming one of the highest counties for overdose deaths and we need to make a difference. That is why I am working on this.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I am the youngest child, both in terms of chronological age, as well as born into only the second marriage (of my mother). When I was six years old my father died from cancer and thereafter my mother worked tirelessly to support us. I have attended public schools for my entire educational career. I am 50 years old. I work a fulltime job at a mental health organization where I am paid very little. I am divorced. I have suffered from alcoholism (and have been sober for about 14 years). I was once married to a wonderful woman, she has subsequently become mentally ill, over the years, and we have ultimately become estranged. For many years I also suffered from depression. Recently, I spent two years treating my mother’s terminal illness and cared for her fulltime until she died. I started working out around a year ago and have lost 100 lbs. I am a caring and helpful person.

As a child I loved to play sports: most of my friends in elementary school were either in baseball, basketball or football clubs. I loved music. I played clarinet in the school band. And I was exposed to music at home, by listening to the radio, and tapes and CDs by The Beatles, The Monkees, Olivia Newton-John, Foreigner and Amy Grant. I loved reading as well, but it was often difficult to concentrate with my varying degree of concentration issues throughout school. Like my brother, I enjoyed skateboarding and fell off the pavement numerous times, which led to the high volume of surgical repairs in my shoulders, arms, and knees. I did manage to obtain a driver’s license. I owned a junk automobile, took on parttime jobs and held numerous (mostly dead-end) jobs along the way. (I later lost my ability to drive, but I have access to public transportation). I enjoy cooking and creating art. I am an extreme introvert and I am not much of a “people person.” I still struggle with identity issues and I have self-esteem problems. I strive to be an asset to humanity and society.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I was born in Austin, Texas and grew up between Austin and Midland. My mom passed away when I was 12. My brother and I had to grow up quickly and be more responsible than most of our friends. At a very early age, I became independent, entrepreneurial, and financially savvy. I also wanted to make a lot of money. My family, friends, and faith also played a key role in shaping who I am today.

I am 52 and single. In 1992, I started a corporation called Independent Liquified Petroleum Gas Association, and Independent Delivery Contractor Association. After 9/11, I decided to transform both associations into Truckers News Group, a trucking magazine, website, and eNewsletter. After almost 20 years of running the groups, I sold them to CTB, Inc in 2013. Today, I consult with a few owner-operators and help with the marketing of a book called, “Beyond the GPS” by Ron Sexton, which is about using the power of the United States Legal System to operate a trucking company more efficiently and successfully.

After selling the groups, I started a website and podcast dedicated to the trucking industry called, “Road Dog Radio”. I have interviewed everyone from William Shatner and Kevin Dillon from Entourage, to Coach Tony Dungy, FBI Hostage Negotiator Chris Voss, and celebrities from the movie “Con-Air”.

I also run a program called IRhythm 365 which helps entrepreneurs achieve balance, peace and harmony in their life. The first step is doing a “heart check”. This is where we determine if you have abnormal heart rhythms, also known as arrhythmias. I started doing this because people will drive across the country. But, they will not take 5 minutes to check their heart rate and rhythm.

In my spare time, I enjoy reading books, watching movies, and working out at the gym. I also love watching The Colts, Pacers and NASCAR. A few years ago, I ran in my first 10 mile race in Southport, Indiana. I am also doing the BAA Marathon in Boston on April 21, 2019. My goal is to complete 20 miles in 4 hours and 15 minutes.

The reason I wanted to be an Entrepreneur is because as I grew up I wanted to learn to become a billionaire. My friend, Brooke McQueen, was the CEO of GenTech Performance Products. She was so passionate about the company and about life, so I wanted to learn from her.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
Well, I am 50 years old. I grew up in the South during the sixties and seventies. I was the oldest child in the family and was expected to learn all the responsibility that was demanded. From a very young age I was dependable and held all the “adult” responsibilities that were needed. I watched my siblings closely, and took on the duties that were needed. I believe that was the best thing that ever happened to me. I got to experience responsibility from a very early age and therefore I have a comfort zone that allows me to take on almost anything.

I have also had a major roadblock in my life in which I believe has also greatly helped shape my character. My mother passed away on January 2nd, 1984 and that was the single worst experience of my life. I had always been the “good” son, I did everything right, I never got into trouble, and always stayed focused and on the right track. But here was my mother, dying of cancer before my eyes. I was the one who washed and changed her body, I was the one who cut her hair, took care of her hygiene, and did everything for her.

I can not describe to you the emotions that went through me. I was angry, resentful, and very emotional, but I was focused on doing everything for her, so that I could be the best son I could be. But I was also so angry. I thought “Why do I have to do this? Why couldn’t it be someone else? How is this fair? Why me? How come none of my other friends have to go through this?” But I also realized that my mother needed me. I didn’t have a choice. This was happening to me. I made a choice in life to be the best son I could be and now here was my chance to do just that.

This is the most vivid memory I have and is my single most important experience in my life. All that I have achieved in my life stems from this moment and has helped define who I am as a person.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I am 50 years old, I was born in Augusta, Georgia to a middle class African American family. My father was a home inspector and real estate agent. My mother was a nurse. It was at first for a middle class family in 1970's that they divorced and both remarried and I have since helped raise a brother and a sister.

In my early years, I grew up in a relatively calm environment and never experienced any great tragedies. One incident that always sticks with me as I say is once I fell into the swimming pool as a toddler. I was immediately rescued and lived to tell about it.

I earned a Bachelor's degree from Georgia State in Criminal Justice and a Master's from the University of the District of Columbia in Criminal Justice Management. I also earned an MBA from Capella University. In 2009, I was laid off from my longtime job and found it difficult finding employment.

I later became a Community Health Worker at Catholic Charities, right outside of DC. I loved that role and enjoyed helping the disabled, poor, and elderly to access healthy food, housing, jobs and create networks of fellow community members to help each other to lead quality lives. Unfortunately, during the Trump administration, Catholic Charities was unable to win any more contracts. Since the pandemic I have moved on to become a Waiter.

Lastly, I often think of my mother who I visited on a regular basis at the nursing home. I was a shadow for a lot of my life and I never wanted to have the same frustrations and tragedies in my own life and want to lead a better life for myself and my daughter.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I am 55 years old. I grew up in Texas, and have never left the USA. I majored in Electrical Engineering in college. My parents taught me the value of hard work, and encouraged me to work hard and to be diligent. My mother taught me to be persistent, and to not take ‘no’ for an answer.

My husband and I love to travel and adventure together. We have explored Europe extensively. We have also hiked in the mountains of the Sierra Nevada, and we have camped in multiple National Parks. When we go somewhere, we never take a map – just a car and a good sense of adventure. We find roads to travel that others have not yet found.

My husband and I also have ‘Hammocking Weekend’. For Hammocking Weekend, we take our two hammocks and a few small personal items, and we go to the most isolated campground we can find in any local State or National Park. There are no road signs, no street lights, no cars, no phones, no nothing. It’s us, the hammocks, and nature. When we hammock, we never take anything out of our hammocks with us except the phone to take a photo of the view. Everything else – like food and drink – stays in the hammock. And so, for hammocking weekend, I stay with my husband in the hammocks for 24 hours – just hanging around and relaxing with him in the woods. It is our very favorite weekend of the year.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: My name is Constance Smith. I am 58 years old. I am married with two children. I grew up in Shelbyville, Kentucky. I am mostly Caucasian but some European. Some events that made me who I am are my life's experiences. I have been through many things in my life to include high-school, college, and university. I have worked my way up from getting a degree in Criminal Justice, getting my Bachelor's and a Master's in Social Work, then teaching as an instructor at the university level. I am currently the Director of Child Advocacy Services in Norfolk, Nebraska, and as you can see, I have made something of myself. I have worked hard to better myself and my family, and I have taken the steps I needed to in order to achieve my goals. In addition to education, my own hobbies consist of crochet. I do this in my free time. I enjoy my family and I love helping people. I enjoy work that impacts the community in a positive way. I like helping others to fulfill their potential, and I find pleasure in their success. My family also plays a big part in who I am, and is part of my core values. I am a sociable person and I love meeting new people, but I also like my space and alone time to unwind at times as well. I am easy to approach and I don't like being alone. I am the type of person who likes to think of others first and that is just my nature. I grew up doing that and it helps me with my career because my goal is to help families and children to better their lives so they can have a positive outcome in the future.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I grew up in Randfontein, Johannesburg, South Africa. I have been married to Heddenia for 42 years and we have 2 children, Mark and Sarah. Sarah has a PHD in Endocrinology and is a lecturer at UCT. Mark is in the IT industry. Our son Andrew, Heddenia’s brother, and his wife Gerda live in Singapore. Our parents are both in their eighties and our daughter and son-in-law are both doctors, each specialising in Anesthesiology. I see little of them at present, so I communicate with them mainly by e-mail and WhatsApp.

I am 75 years old. I obtained my Bachelor of Commerce degree in accounting and then served articles with the accounting firm Price Waterhouse. I moved on to audit various businesses and specialised in Estate Agency as one of the more complex businesses. The main clients in that industry were the four major Estate Agencies, Chas Everitt, Gresswold, Harrel Williams, Jantze, and the other large real estate agencies such as Seaboard Properties in Springs and Midvaal Auctioneers. Later I changed to a securities firm and negotiated listings on the Johannesburg Stock Exchange, of which I was an active client. At that time there were 500 companies listed in South Africa, all the other international companies having departed for London or the USA. My practice consisted mainly of placing equity and debenture shares. I formed a close relationship with Miltons Beit and Company, which became Beits. I have been on the boards of most the South African listed companies in the Liberty Group (a unit trust, life assurance, broking and estate agency group in South Africa).
