I was President of the Rand Association of Real Estate Agents and became actively involved in the Property Brokerage Association of South Africa. I was chairman of its legal committee which drafted the legislation and regulations for the industry which became the Estate Agency Affairs Act of 1976. I then joined the Zimbabwe property industry and was president of the Real Estate Institute of Zimbabwe.

I was a member of the Executive Committee of the Central African Association of Real Estate Agents in Zimbabwe and was also a board member of the Central African Development Foundation in Zimbabwe, of which Sir Garry Carrier was chairman. I was an expert witness in many cases in the Zimbabwean courts as an expert in Estate Agency and investment property. In 2004 I sold my practice to Johnson and Jeoffreys in Harare.

In 2005 I became an advisor to a Norwegian Pension Fund for a feasibility study on the creation of a mining investment fund in Africa. Through that I came into contact with Francois Granger, an Oxford graduate, who had been working in Iraq and Cyprus with Red Cross and Red Crescent. He asked me to come on board with his dream of replicating Palabora Mining on the African continent. We started in 2005 by investing 45.6 million in Kagem, which now has a market cap of over $1 Billion.

Our mines would be built in conjunction with local communities. Mining is necessary for the economy of the country and in order to create these investments in a new industry, a lot of capital is needed. The companies are usually a Joint Venture between the local community, the government, and investors, and operated by a management team drawn from the local community. These new communities are initially dependent on the mining activity for jobs, food and housing. The communities are better off than previously, the mine is meeting its minimum social corporate responsibility obligations. It then becomes a mission for the mine to become self-sufficient and sustainable on the host country’s Government and the mine’s own investment, so that there is no longer a need for subsidy. Then the mine can go on to become a well-run profitable industry, supplying minerals to international markets.

For the first 3 years of operations there is no pay-back to investors because all of the initial investment is in the various infrastructure that is needed for mining. The initial and very substantial investment from external sources is in a region where there is no power source, roads, and other infrastructure, as well as in initial mining activities. The later pay-back starts to come in at about year 3 when the revenue from sales in the market are channeled back to investors.

Although the coal is being used in the electricity supply (conventional coal fired generation) we do not plan on mining coal which is used in the generation of electricity in the same power station. This is because most of the power stations in Africa are coal fired. Our mines will be producing coal for domestic use in Southern African countries and for export to the Indian sub-continent where coal is not widely used.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am in my 60s and live in the same neighborhood I have always lived in. I am not rich and by some standards might even be considered homeless. However, I could spend thousands of dollars more per month if I wanted. I am happy with my life style.

I am from the backwoods of this country and grew up with very little. On a few occasions, we were starving in the woods and going to school on an empty stomach. We had a small brown paper bag for dinner a couple of nights every week. Breakfast on some days was just a big bowl of Kool-Aid™ mixed with powdered milk.

My two brothers were thin and we worried about them catching a cold. I could play around outside all day but if I got too cold, we had to come in the house. If my brother played outside in the cold, he might catch a chill and be sick for a week.

On the day before payday, my mother would spend my whole allowance in the grocery store because she just could not resist those long stems of red roses for only 29 cents a stem. I would have rather had bread and milk for dinner, but I did not dare protest because I did not want to take them away from her. We were lucky to have even 79 cents to last her until payday. A lot of times, we would not have any money for awhile.

I started school when I was five. Like everyone else, I came into this country in the bottom of a big iron can and after a long boat ride. I was picked up by a social service agency at six weeks old and given to an older couple in a rural area of north Georgia. This was in 1941.

There was no TV, no radio, and very few books in the whole town except in the school. The food was pretty basic consisting of what was raised in the garden and caught from the creek, pond, or field.

The postman would stop by our little house and hand deliver letters and packages from my mother to me. I used to look forward to them coming. My mother wrote me every Saturday. It made me very sad to know that she was in a big room with a lot of other people just like me. We could not go into the kitchen and get something to eat. She used to tell me not to worry about her, that she would be fine.

She wrote me letters every week, usually on Saturday because she knew that is when I would be out walking down the road with my doll in my arms hoping to see a letter on the road on my way back home. I learned how to read so I could read her letters from her mother to me. I would put them in a drawer and pull them out as I got the chance. When we moved, I brought them with me. The older I got, the more I used to hide them so she would think I was more popular than I was. I was so proud of those letters.

I had bad eyesight when I was young. We used to go to a forested area to pick mulberries. It was a popular spot for us to be. It was not uncommon to have 12 to 15 other kids in my father’s group that we would meet. Sometimes other groups would show up. We would hide among the large oak trees and be covered with mulberries. It was fun. So much fun.

My father would go into the woods and stay for hours and hours, sometimes days. Later, I found out he had picked up a fungus from the eucalyptus. He was eventually cured of it. I was told he could not work when it first started.

I started going to school when I was five. There were no books in school. The teacher took us in the library and we picked out five books to read and take home. There were only a few books in the whole library. I used to have my mother help me find things in the book that was found in nature. At the end of each day, I would go home with a book. My mother read to me a lot when I was a child and I still love to hear her tell the stories about our life in the field.

When I was in the first grade, my mother fell sick. My father went to North Carolina to pick blackberries. My older brother took my younger brother and I to the old primitive country school house. Our teacher was not there and we did not know what to do. We sat there for a long time, talking to each other. Soon, other kids came into the school house. We ran home to tell our father, and he came and got us. I realized then that when there was nothing else to do, I could read my mother’s letters to me. They were like my friends in that little building.

One time, we saw a man hanging from a tree. I did not know what he did but I knew it was bad. It was when white people were trying to kill all the Indians. After that, we started getting up in the middle of the night because the whites would come into the town and run away with the folks. The town was named after the Indian tribe living there. We were called Natches Town.

We lived in the poorest part of the town. There was no room, no plumbing, and no electricity. During the rainy season, everything flooded. The water would keep us from going to school. We would eat cookies and drink coffee with sugar in it and it would make us so sick. My father got paid in food stamps for picking cotton. He would have to line up every Sunday to get his payment. He would start the day by going to town to get the food and then go home. We had no cars.

I ran away from home. I stopped by a store on the way to get something to eat. I had no money and could not get the food without money or credit. I ran away to Chattanooga and got a job and caught the train back home. I went back home because I could not survive any longer on my own. Even though I was homeless, I enjoyed living away from there. The adults were always trying to play control games. We just needed time to grow up and they needed to be out of our lives. My father got sick again and we did not have any money. It was a three day ride on the bus. My mother hated the bus.

I was thirteen and started to school. There were books and no one bothered us. I loved math and made good grades. A lot of the guys hated me because I was getting to be the teacher’s pet. I was used to being in the minority. Some of the guys were picking on me.

My teacher liked me but he could see the guys were making fun of me so he told me, “Chazie, you have a big ass and you need to wear dresses to hide that ass.” I told him I was not going to wear a dress because I wanted to be a farmer. I had not realized the way guys were reacting to me. Some guys were much bigger than me. They would push me down to the ground. I was young, weak, and scared. I never fought them. I had decided not to hide my ass, but I did hide the fact that I could read better than a lot of the boys.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am 66 years old, and a member of the “Silent Generation.” I attended Roseville Public Schools, graduating in 1964. I did not like school, but on occasion I did pass some tests. I got kicked out of high school in 1963 because I ditched my freshman composition class for a full week, and was not able to complete my three page paper. I completed a semester at MCTC during my junior year, and then my senior year I was accepted to Michigan State University.

As a child I read quite a bit. I have a small library, mostly biographies, containing material about Thomas Jefferson, Abraham Lincoln, Ulysses Grant, Robert E. Lee, Ben Franklin, Mark Twain, Edgar Allen Poe, Warren Buffett, Antonin Scalia, and a few others. Reading the Old Testament was where I got my morality from. When I was 18 I read Paulo Freire’s, Pedagogy of the Oppressed. It changed my outlook. What I learned in school was a distraction, but not something I needed to forget. I keep in my head that we are all imprisoned and we do what we must to survive. I have found this to be more of a negative than a positive, because the religious establishment has a hold on many people. This was the way that it was in the South, where I grew up. My mother and father were independent Baptists, but also charismatic in their faith.

Those that taught me in school did not teach me the history of the USA. I learned it on my own. I love history. When I was in the army, 1969, I served on a nuclear launch crew, at Gunter’s Landing, Alabama. It was an interesting experience, but I did not work on the missile, only a standby crew. I worked on the Norden bombsights in nuclear bombers and my other duty was as a security policeman on the flight line. After the army I lived in California for the majority of my life, but during those 47 years I did move a lot. I now live in St. Cloud, Minnesota.

I have worked as a missionary in Guatemala, Honduras, Nicaragua, El Salvador, Costa Rica, Panama, Belize, and Mexico. I also worked for the United States Housing Authority and with an EPA program that inspected and removed asbestos. I worked as an MSHA Mine Foreman and OSHA compliance officer. I worked for the Department of Air Force, Air Force Reserve, and for 20 years I served in the Army Reserve, as a Military Police Officer, a unit commander, a public affairs officer, and traveled the United States. Since retiring from the military I have worked with many kinds of people.

My father was a bridge crane operator, steel worker, and superintendent for much of his life. He worked for a long time at Pennsylvania Power and Light Company in Jim Thorpe, Pennsylvania. He was an old style Christian fundamentalist, racist, and patriot. My mother worked as a secretary and in the supermarket industry. She was a Southern Baptist and an authoritarian. My brother is a Baptist minister.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I am 77 years old, born in the town of Balcarce, Argentina. I began a 45-year career in real estate brokerage and investing in the 1960’s with my husband, Dr. Saul Brenner, who was an obstetrician in the Chicago area. We expanded our real estate holding with the acquisition of apartment buildings, shopping centers, a professional office building, and retail strips centers. In the 1970’s, we began to invest in rental and owner-occupied apartments in Fort Lauderdale, Florida. We became very involved in our Jewish community in Chicago and Fort Lauderdale, including taking board and committee positions in many Jewish organizations. In 1996, Saul passed away; at that time, my now-16 year old granddaughter, Kalena, came to live with me. Over the next ten years, I became a caregiver to two of my grandchildren, Kalena and Oscar, who had been orphaned at birth. During that time, I was fortunate to participate in both undergraduate and graduate programs at the University of the District of Columbia in Washington, D.C., while developing my consulting business in organizational development and succession planning for Jewish not-for-profit organizations. When Kalena became of age to make decisions regarding her life, she decided to move to Israel, but I remained in the United States. I continue to own and manage my property in Ft. Lauderdale, Florida, and I devote a lot of time to my social justice work, which I see as my legacy in Saul’s memory. I am the founder of the Israeli – American Jewish Democratic Alliance (IAJDA). See www.IAJDA.org. I had previously founded, in 1982, the first Chicago – Tel Aviv Sister City Project, in conjunction with Mayor Jane Byrne.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I will tell you my short story. I was born in 1948, so I’m already more than 70 years old. I was born in Beijing, China. I grew up during the time of the revolution and I was raised by my grandfather. I loved him very much and he had a great influence on me.

There were many incidents during the Cultural Revolution and I survived all of them. When I was seven years old I remember when people came to our house and started to burn things. My grandmother was a good person. She believed in the new philosophy so they left her alone. But my grandfather was against them and he burned his books that were about evolution and different things. He burned those books in front of the people and because of that they punished him. They kicked him in the head and he lost his memory for a few days.

That is a very difficult memory for me to talk about. So I never wanted to be an activist, or a public figure. When I was young I was shy and I never liked to talk in front of people. Then I met my husband and he encouraged me and supported me. He gave me more confidence. Now I talk a little, even though I still feel shy.

People ask me about my life and I tell them what I have seen with my own eyes. I saw with my own eyes the violence in Tiananmen Square in 1989. I had never seen the violence. My husband and I went there and we saw it. We couldn’t believe it was happening in our country. We had heard of the violence, but we had never seen it before.

So I tell people, “I have experienced all of this. I saw it with my own eyes. I have seen the power of the people, the power of people’s movements. I have seen the struggle for freedom and the desire for democracy. I have seen the destruction and the lies and the violence.” I tell people my story because I want people to understand that this is China and this is us, the people of China. I want people to know that there are people here who want to live free, who want democracy, who want to choose their own way of life. I tell them because I want them to understand what is going on in China. We have a choice. We can live in freedom. We can be free. We can choose our own path.

This is what I tell people. I am not here to make money. I am here to share my story and my life. I want people to understand China, and what is happening here. This is the real China. This is the true China. We are not interested in your money. We are not interested in your money. We want you to know who we are, and what we are trying to do, to be free. This is the real China.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I was born 29 January 1942 in Rochester, Minnesota, USA. I am 66 years old and grew up in at various places around the world as the son of a career US Navy man (Ensign Robert Earl Gilbert, World War II and post-war veteran.) I read "The Wonderful Wizard of Oz" at 4, fell in love with books in a big way. We moved to a small rural Iowa town when I was seven. I made friends with a scrawny, slow-witted, loner kid who had somehow been kept back a grade. This was Ted Naftalin, who has become a friend and colleague of Bill Clinton and a software developer. We went our separate ways when I went to college; but once Clinton's presidential campaign and administration matured, his White House made contact and we became friends. I had the most amazing life imaginable from age 4 to 22.

I had to invent a life and personality from that point on. I made my way from the southwest suburbs of Chicago to UCLA as a part-time janitor and full-time student. I took the job because I needed the money and most of the time people did not go to bed until long after I got to work. I was all set to join the Southern California Tech Cosmopolitan Singles Club when I was asked to leave an apartment building after hitting a rent collector with a snow shovel. Three hours after that, I got my first UNIX account. I got a PhD from UC Berkeley for a thesis about supporting larger file systems on computers that started with four 9-track tape drives. I worked as a systems programmer for five years. I divorced, and took a job writing a computer program that was needed by the Clinton Administration's new Administrative Office of the US Courts. I subsequently did three full-scale conversions of court management applications to Linux/Java.

I met my second wife (for 27 years now) in 1986 in Los Angeles, near the building where they shoot CSI. We met at a showing of this abysmal straight-to-video sci-fi B movie, "The Visitor". My wife, Susann (she also posts as "Beth") agreed to a speed date (2 hours) and because we both speak Swiss German and could not speak English, there was plenty of time to talk. Two weeks later she came to Los Angeles (from Switzerland) for a vacation and I asked her to stay. She said, "Come and get me," and I did. She was hired (through a fairly awkward set of personal introductions) to teach computer programming at Cal Poly Pomona. We have three sons (two of them at UC Berkeley at the same time).

I began to write using a computer when I was seven years old on a Teletype MkXII with a keyboard that could be wired to transmit the keys struck in different ways. My brother Bob said that I was a writer even when I was walking. His own first words were the title of Moby Dick, "Call me Ishmael". My mother once asked Bob why he decided to apply for medical school, given that he never seemed to mind the sight of blood. He said that he became a doctor because it was expected of him and he wanted to do something different. That was me with computers. I had had a Commodore PET, an Osborne-1, an IBM Portable PC, a Gypsy 45 and a KIM-1 before I used a computer on a mainframe at UCLA in 1970, and I was fascinated by them immediately.

My favorite US President is Abraham Lincoln. He escaped from slavery, fought the Civil War, and attempted to force the Southern states to be re-united. He succeeded in the first two objectives and lost the third; but a century and a half later the remaining states are beginning to voluntarily do something Lincoln never could have achieved. I am a committed Democrat because I am a Democrat, but I am not a single-issue voter. My environmental views can be summed up by my memory of being in Los Angeles on a Sunday, watching the cars go by. I saw two Jaguars, 19 Corvettes and 3 Rolls-Royces in a 3-block span. It made a lasting impression on a science geek.

My main political interests are improving the judicial branch of the government. I donate (very lightly) to the Democratic National Committee and contribute to several minor progressive-oriented sites (spans in the New York Times and New Yorker are more expensive than a good computer once was). My political views generally mesh closely with Obama's; but I also respect Hillary Clinton's tenacity and sense of justice. I attended an Obama campaign event at UCLA and volunteered on the day he signed up presidential nominees. I subsequently played a semi-vital role in getting one of my friends to contribute to his campaign, making it possible for an urban Spanish-speaking Jamaican woman who works as a cook to have a primary vote. I think that both the campaigns I did on his behalf made a modest, but unique and demonstrable impact. There is little going on to the same extent in my neighborhood, so I really wish I could do more.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
Life is a series of experiences, some good, some bad. When I was 5 years old, my grandfather died. I was devastated. When I was 18, I lost my first boyfriend to cancer. That was the worst pain I had ever known. When I was 30, I had a bad motorcycle accident and I thought I was going to die. I was frightened and sad and angry and frustrated. When I was 46, I was laid off and I was scared to death that I might have to get a ‘real’ job. When I was 57, I got a letter from my doctor telling me I had a disease. When I was 64, I broke my hip. Now that I am 69, I will begin chemotherapy again because my breast cancer has returned. And now that I am 73, I am living in a nursing home.

For many people, questions are designed to elicit a predictable response. These questions include “Tell me about yourself” or “How are you today?” This is usually a greeting, not a probe. Many professionals don’t like to be asked a question that forces them to think about their life experiences because it’s not on the agenda for that day. For those who do like these questions, the questions generally elicit the most interesting, surprising and compelling answers.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
OK. I was born in Chicago, IL in 1987. I was raised by my 75 year old grandma. She took me in when my parents abandoned me as a baby. Yes, I like cats. In fact, my cat has been my best friend since 2001. My grandma taught me everything I know, including how to wipe my ass and play Candyland. We lived in a small town, so there wasn’t much going on. But I have fond memories of walking to school, hanging out with my grandma, and playing in the street with the neighborhood children. There is a large park in town with a lake and a boat rental, so we would visit that a lot. My grandma died in the summer of 1996. She dropped dead while watching Kojak. I didn’t understand what it meant for her to die. I just understood that she wasn’t around anymore. I remembered we had a funeral at the St. Patrick’s cathedral. I’m not religious, but I’m not a heathen either. I grew up a tomboy because I had no sisters. My grandma loved me to death, but I was self sufficient from a young age, so I never depended on my grandma for anything. I think of grandma as my mom, since I have no recollection of my real mother.

Because I was raised by my grandmother, I spent a lot of time outdoors. We spent most of our time at the park. After grandma died, I went back to live with my father. He died in 2005 from a drug overdose. He was addicted to opioids, especially methadone. I think I found a love for the outdoors when I was with my grandmother. I loved to spend time at the park. The park is where I built the foundation of my outdoorsmanship and my general sense of freedom. I was always outside playing baseball with the neighborhood kids. Sometimes we would go swimming, fishing, or camping. My childhood was pretty regular. Nothing exceptional happened during this time.

After my mother, father, and grandmother died, I moved in with my aunt. My aunt did the best she could, but she worked a lot and I didn’t get the attention I needed to grow into a confident and happy adult. I started smoking marijuana in the 7th grade, though I didn’t really understand the consequences. I was a big stoner. Whenever I would get into trouble, I would blame my marijuana smoking.

After a while, the marijuana became a more prevalent influence in my life than the people who mattered. By the time I was in high school, I was smoking all day, every day. I sold marijuana in high school and dropped out in the 11th grade. I didn’t go to college because I was a dealer, but I had a ton of friends going to state university. I figured I would pursue an outdoors career after I spent some time running my marijuana business. I played D1 lacrosse and I was a pretty big deal on the field. I would stick any of my competitors who had a different color uniform. I was the enforcer, but I was also known to be the fastest player on the team. Of course, I never played an actual game. I got into a lot of trouble off the field, but that’s a story for another day.

At first, I sold marijuana because it was a good way to earn money, and it allowed me to stay high all day. But then my best friend was killed by a cop after a car chase. I was only 14, so I did not have a driver’s license, but I still hid the car keys when my dad was under the influence. I knew he was going to be arrested if he drove again, but I was worried about the consequences, so I hid his keys for him. I had to be responsible, since my parents were absent and my grandma died when I was little. My dad was really angry when he found his keys were missing. He had a lot of anger and resentment issues that he took out on me. I often got slapped across the face or slapped on the top of my head. If I had done something wrong, then I would have been punched or knocked in the face. I was 14 when I got my first black eye.

I really started smoking marijuana because it was something to do. I was bored, and I had no real family to go home to. In high school, I had some bad experiences with alcohol. I never had a lot of confidence growing up. But when I had a few beers, I was the life of the party. When I was drunk, my words were my actions and my actions were my decisions. I was rarely responsible. My friends on the lacrosse team encouraged me to smoke marijuana every day. When I got suspended from school for carrying a bong in my backpack, that’s when I decided to quit playing lacrosse and go to work. I was suspended for one semester. That wasn’t long enough, since I had accumulated 18 absences in the first semester of high school. If I could do it again, I would have told that cop to fuck off and not stolen the bag with the bong. But I did what I thought I was supposed to do. I was taught to respect and obey authority, and I felt that if I was trying to break the law, then I should be charged. But I couldn’t predict the future. I know now that you shouldn’t run from the police if they ask for ID. You should obey all lawful orders.

So when I started work, I dropped out of high school. By the end of high school, I had a felony for manufacturing and distributing marijuana. I was only 15 years old when I started working. I was just trying to make money so that I could buy weed. I started working for a good family friend. I was recruited to work at his family business by a couple of guys from high school. We would drive around the city, looking for people to run drugs for us. I was always apprehensive about this. Sometimes I would pretend I didn’t see them. If I ran drugs for them, I would get money, and I could use some of the money to buy weed. It was a win/win for me.

My first boss was a tall skinny white guy with a ponytail. He was the criminal mastermind. But the day I was forced into carrying a gun, that was when I lost all trust in him and in myself. I lost my confidence and started to doubt myself. I was really scared the first time I had to use the gun. I couldn’t even see the man I was shooting at. After that day, I no longer saw my job as working for my boss. I was his enemy. If my boss was shot, that would mean that we lost a boss and gained a friend. That’s when I started to think that we could make more money by working for ourselves and not our bosses. That’s when I started to develop a plan. I knew what I had to do, and I knew how to do it. I stole some firearms and used the firearms to steal other firearms. We could not risk being in a position where a bank robbery would go wrong. But if we could steal enough firearms, then we would be OK.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: My name is Hillary Clinton and I am 69 years old. I was born in Chicago Illinois to Anthony and Edith Rodham and I was raised mainly in Chicago. I have 2 brothers and 1 sister. I first went to college in Texas but eventually attended college at Wellesley in Massachusetts. In Texas, I attended the University of Wellesley School and majored in foreign policy. After I got my degree, I attended Yale and got my law degree. I went on to practice law in Arkansas for 12 years until I decided to run for office in Arkansas. I am married to Bill Clinton. I am also a mother to 1 and a grandmother to 4. When I think of what makes me the person I am today, I think of my mother, Edith. She raised me to believe that anything was possible and she pushed me to take risks. I believed in her so much that I only applied to schools that I had a chance of getting into. Once I got to college, I realized that anything is possible if you believe in it. I ran for office in Arkansas and had no idea that I would be successful. I believe that these events define me as a person and are what matter most to me. My family matters to me the most and is something that has always mattered to me. I am still married to Bill and we have four grandchildren. I also love my job. I love helping people and being able to improve their lives. I believe that caring about what matters to me has made me the person I am today.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I grew up in a home that emulated the United States in the forties and fifties. Polite. Honest. Frugal. Loyal. A man with a plan. A woman with a family. We weren’t wealthy, but we always had everything we needed. I’m now 66 years old. I’ve been married for 42 years and have two kids. They’re in their twenties and I’m thrilled I have sons. A daughter would have been tough. They’re good kids and we enjoy a close relationship, but I’m happy that they’re boys. My father passed away several years ago. It was a shock for my mother and they were happily married for 57 years. He had a stroke and she took care of him for two years, but one night he just slumped over and passed on. There were no signs. He had a doctor’s appointment the next day. It was almost like he was saying goodbye to my mother before he went to the next place. My mother was a good woman. Now she’s in a senior apartment and that’s what she needs, but she misses him terribly.

I’ve worked for a plumbing supply company for thirty years, starting in the warehouse and moving through the ranks to become the manager of the outside operations. I was a golf nut back in college, but haven’t played much the last thirty years. Truth be told, I don’t miss it. I grew up as an only child in Philadelphia and have been an avid reader since grade school. I read novels. The New York Times, the Wall Street Journal, sports magazines, car magazines, whatever. I’m a big fan of soccer. I like cricket too, but no one else does. I used to go to the London games every year, but can’t really do that anymore. The annual trips are fine, but now I have grandkids and I’m trying to see how much of the world I can show them.

One of the first big world trip for my grandkids was visiting a mutual friend who was working in Hong Kong on a large Disney movie. I guess it took us a week to get settled once we got there. The kids were recovering from being on an airplane for 27 hours. I was being notified for the first time of chronic constipation, excruciating knee pain, and being told that the bills for everything that I had done were being sent to me. The number was in excess of $10,000. I was staggered. I thought I had heard that there was a shortage of water in Hong Kong? Every day, after about 4 or 5 pm, the entire area was completely devoid of water. We had the first 5 star hotel in Hong Kong and I went to the bar for a beer. The barmaid, where he said he was a Midwestern country boy, he was from Iran, asked me how he could help. I thought to myself: this is the first 5 star hotel and I’m asking the bartender how to get a glass of water? As I go for my credit card, I explained that we had a major water problem and that the city was completely out of water, and would he accept a drawdown on my charges at the hotel? He said we have bottled water and it is 400 dollars. I offered the bartender one of my best poker expressions, which was, that there is a caveat to accept my charge card and it is an all-inclusive charge. If I can use all the hotel’s facilities, but the bill is too much, can I just have some crackers and water? He agreed to an all-inclusive fee and checked his rates. He said that my credit card limit was too low, that my limit was 30,000, but after taxes, room and board, and service charge, he came up with a credit card limit of 385,000 dollars. I said, take the entire amount. Just take it. I want to show these kids what it’s like to go on vacation. My credit score just increased 60 points after I paid the credit card company off. My banking institution loved the cash back program, and I spent the next several years at different Disney hotels in the UK and France in my free 25 days a year to visit my mutual friend there, who had taken on 90 pounds and shaved his head.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I am a 65-year old male Jew. I grew up in the city and lived in a Jewish neighborhood. I am the only child of my mother. My father was not involved in my life. I was academically gifted. I attended the University of Notre Dame for the first half of my undergraduate education. After the accident of 2011, I had to return home to my mother for support.

During my undergraduate career, I was a strong Christian. My father was an Episcopalian minister. I was baptized and confirmed. I attended a Montessori school and was taught Hebrew. I ate dairy with meat. I had no allergic reactions. At the time, I didn’t even know how to pray.

During my sophomore year at Notre Dame, I changed because I befriended a black friend. And being in a relationship with my first Catholic girlfriend, the charismatic spirit affected me. I began to enjoy the personality of Jesus.

I left school in 1982 due to the death of my mother. Due to severe depression, I got only a partial education. In order to survive, I became a professional cartoonist, earned 3 College Associates and Graduate Credits.

I re-married in 2006 to a Christian Believer. Unfortunately, my second wife died of breast cancer in 2009.

In 2011, a near death accident resulted in a mystical experience. I became a Catholic. I was baptized at the age of 62.

I became affiliated with Unam Sanctum in 2013. There is much more to my story, but I just wanted to give you an overview.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I was born and raised in the Midwest in the suburbs of Chicago and have lived the majority of my life in the northern suburbs of Chicago, just outside the city itself. I am 65 years old. I grew up in a moderately middle-class family. I have two younger sisters and my parents were still married up until my father’s death 3 years ago. I attended Catholic school until I was in the 7th grade and transferred to public school so that I could attend a much larger high school. In my junior year of high school, I met my husband and three years later we were married. I majored in finance in college and have worked for the last 47 years as a project manager for a large corporation. I have an interesting story as to how I ended up in this career. I have always lived near a large lake and enjoy being on the water. Some friends invited me to go waterskiing one day when I was in my early 20s. I had never tried waterskiing before and I ended up being pulled out of the lake by the boat. This episode made me see that I had a fear of heights and I promptly sought out psychoanalysis to help me deal with this fear. The psychoanalyst was a married couple who, among other things, worked with Project Management Teams in this large corporation, IBM, and decided that I should take over some of their accounts. From this, I became a technical project manager and have been doing this for 47 years. I also trained to be a psychoanalyst and ended up stopping after my training analysis due to a number of work challenges, including a work wife. Other life events that have made me who I am include the loss of my sister, moving for my husband to take a new job, being widowed by my husband, taking up golf, and taking on a boyfriend who moved in with me and eventually moved out. In my leisure time I enjoy water skiing, reading and traveling, often to foreign places such as India, Israel, Greece, and Italy, often taking my friends on trips with me so that I don’t have to go alone. Although these places are beautiful and I have enjoyed many aspects of my travels, I am an atheist and I don’t understand what people find so great about religious sites. I have, however, gotten a lot of good writing done on these trips, or just sitting in the lobby of the hotel.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I am 73 years old, married to a wonderful lady named Rosalie since 1981. I worked for 35 years as an educator, starting as a teacher in LAUSD then moving to what was then called the University of Southern California School of Education. I was there until 1991, then moved to National University where I worked 13 years until retiring in 2004. I currently teach part time at CSUF and write a blog every week. Rosalie and I love to travel, go to movies, read the newspaper, enjoy our two grandchildren, watch CNN, eat organic food, and try to fix up a vintage house in Fullerton. The house has become a labor of love. We have a dog and 6 chickens in the back yard, fresh organic eggs every day. We have lived in the same house in Fullerton for 40 years. I have four sons and two daughter-in-laws, six grandchildren, and one great-granddaughter on the way! I have spent the past 10 years as a baseball dad, volunteering as a team parent for our boys' baseball teams. We’ve traveled all over the country for tournaments. My youngest boy has just signed a scholarship with Dixie State University in St. George, Utah.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I am 80 years old and live in an American suburb. I grew up in an American town and city. My American parents sent my sister and me to parochial schools from the ages of five and seven years old and paid substantial tuition. I am very grateful to them for having made that sacrifice and have made a practice throughout my life of never having used a government program for anything, such as unemployment insurance. At least two years after graduating from high school in 1955, I volunteered for the army and subsequently served on active duty in the European theater of operations during the Cold War and the Korean War. I have never married. I have never had a family. For the rest of my life, I will live alone in the U.S.A. I eat only the foods I grow, prepare and put on the table. I am not greedy and therefore do not consume more than I need. I do not drink alcohol or smoke. I have always used public transportation to get around. My home and most of my land is in New York State and I work in New York City. I have worked all over the U.S.A. and the last job I had was in Florida. Like my parents and grandparents before me, I live according to my own ideas and not according to the ideas of others. Like my parents and grandparents before me, I do not trust the government, particularly not the federal government. In fact, I have always thought that there should be a law against it. It is very distressing to me that so many people do not have this outlook.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
Frankly, I’m 65-years-old, I’m a woman of African descent, and I’m from Tennessee. I was reared by a single, working class mother who instilled in me a strong work ethic, a thirst for education and an aspiration for things I was told were “not for me”. I grew up in Knoxville, TN and currently reside in Chattanooga, TN.

Though I’ve engaged in volunteer work with organizations focused on serving at-risk children and families since 1995, I didn’t truly start making an impact until January 2015. My middle son was killed during a gang-related incident. I used his tragedy and the year and a half of grief that followed as an opportunity to help save others from experiencing the pain of losing a child and help make the world a better place. I decided I wanted to run for public office.

With the help of both of my parents, a boyfriend who became my husband, and my 4 children – the same children my mother helped to raise – I set out to save the lives of people that were in jeopardy of being killed like my son, J. J. was our first child killed in a gang related shooting, but at the time of his death it was being reported 23 times more than his brothers and sisters and cousins and nephews and nieces and friends.

Through my work as Director of the Community Alliance for Public Safety, a social impact organization that seeks to end gang-related violence, and a 2018 Democratic candidate for office, I’ve interviewed, and been interviewed by journalists. As a result, I’m a speaker that has been asked to speak on national media about my work and nationally syndicated television talk shows have booked me as a guest to share about my organization’s work and why it’s so important to save our children’s lives.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
My name is Short John. I’m 72. I was born and raised in Inglewood. I love to dance. “Short” is not my real name but one that my friends came up with when I was young. I learned to dance at Fat Cat’s Rhythm Club, and that was it for me. I was out of my element in the School System, and there was one Teacher that I had for Physics and one for Math. Some months later I found out the Teacher was traveling all around the USA fixing problems in the School System. 

What “matters” to me is keeping my door unlocked and letting strangers in. I like to throw a party and invite everybody. This last party I had to limit it to 50 people to comply with City and County restrictions, but everybody had fun. 

But I had to turn away one person and in 29 years that was the first time that has happened to me. He had a backpack that smelled like Chinese Food.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I am 76 years old and I was born in London, England and moved to the United States in 1990. I have been married for 51 years (1969 to 2020) and have two children. I am a Veteran of the United States Army, United States Navy, United States Air Force, and United Kingdom Armed Forces (the British Army). I have a 2 year Bachelor Degree from the University of Kansas. I'm interested in genealogy, world history, European history, the political climate in Eastern Europe, Balkan region, trans-Atlantic history, British History (after all I am English), and history of warfare in Europe.

I am fascinated by culture and habit. I enjoy archaeology and traveling to explore historical places and sites. The Military History Network welcomes anyone and any idea. We welcome contributing members and technical help.

I have had an extremely interesting life. In addition to serving in the United States Armed Forces, the British Armed Forces, and participating in Nuclear Weapons Testing, I have also lived for a period of time in the Paris of the Orient: Cairo, Egypt, Israel, Jordan, Turkey, Cyprus, Afghanistan, and Jordan. It was a great time to travel. I have worked in a lot of different occupations and also worked with all of the major NATO powers and foreign powers.

After my time as a Captain in the US Army Air Defense Artillery, I traveled around the world playing soccer. It was one of the most interesting and wonderful times of my life. I played for an American military team and we played teams from RAF Lakenheath, RAF Digby, RAF Mildenhall, RAF Upper Heyford, RAF Alconbury, RAF Downhams, 1st Allied Division, the Belgian American Youth, British West Indians, Royal Air Force, and NATO teams.

I lived and played soccer in 20 different countries and went to more than 80 soccer venues including:

Belgium, the Netherlands, Turkey, Spain, France, Scotland, Argentina, Germany, Mexico, Finland, Morocco, Switzerland, Egypt, and Luxembourg.

I speak Russian (University Level - read, speak, understand and write), French (University Level - read, speak, understand and write), and German (University Level - read and speak). My English is extremely good. I have a bachelor's degree from the University of Kansas. I am applying to grad school to study international relations at the University of Cambridge. I live in Cambridge, England and it is where I went to school and university in the late 1980's.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I am of average height with blonde hair and blue eyes. I am 65 years old, a town which is located in the southern region of the United States of America. I grew up with both parents and one sibling. There were no problems in my family. My parents were fairly average in comparison to the parents of my friends. I attended school and it didn't take long before I discovered that I had a knack for mathematics and that this would be my preferred career area. I am a decisive individual and decided early in life that I wanted to be a business person. I obtained my bachelors degree and went on to work as an associate. Later on, I specialized in finance, and I now own a number of companies. I am most proud of my business acumen and feel that I have achieved an awful lot in my life. I am married and am currently enjoying all that the "golden years" have to offer me. I spent my working life on the west coast where the temperatures are fairly warm, and I enjoy spending time in my retirement home during the cold winter months. The most important events in my life include my marriage , which went on for the next 45 years, and the death of my wife, my wife's mother, my only sibling, and my father.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I am the Chinese president, 72 years old, I was born in Shanghai, suffered from poverty in my early years due to wars, while Japan gave money to nearby families, I wasn't so lucky. Since then, I have to support myself and my family. However, fate favors me. With the assistance of my school friends, I was able to attend college. 4 years later, I became a very powerful media representative in China. After ten years of working, I was transferred to the marketing team of an insurance company. 6 years later, I became the company's president. Recently, my press position is very low, and I cannot see the light at the end of the tunnel. No clear goal, no different thinking, and difficult challenges. My solution is to change industry. Worked in enterprises and financial institutions that could solve my problems and allow me to use my skill sets. After 5 years, I am very satisfied with my career, I am a VP of SSI, sales strategies and 95 percent success rate.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I was born in 1927. I grew up during the Great Depression and WWII. I had a difficult life but now I’m 87 years old. I never went to college. I was one of the unfortunate. All I did for the first twenty seven years of my life was to work to make a living, to survive in a dog eat dog world. I had worked for thirty six months in a large airplane factory, at eleven to fourteen dollars a week. At that time I decided that I couldn’t survive on that kind of income in the labor market so I applied to every branch of the service to get a job. I had a high school education but no college. I applied to get a good technical job. None of the branches would hire me because I was nearsighted. I had 20/50 vision in my left eye and 20/60 vision in my right eye. I was told that I was too small to be in the army because I was under five feet tall. Finally I was offered a job with the army air corps by the examiner. He told me that they would take me as a sharpshooter but that wasn’t a suitable job for me. So I was enlisted as a typist in 1944. I had taken only one typing course in my high school years. I was sent to Fort Wolters, Texas, a helicopter training center for the US Army. They taught us to type on an old forty wpm IBM typewriter. My job was to type four line orders, for example, for multiple rockets. I was an enlisted man. I couldn’t do anything else but write the orders and then put them on a bulletin board on base. I was very unhappy about this job because I wanted to do something that required higher education. I was offered a job as a technical writer but it was too late to get any education in aviation. I had signed the papers to enlist as a student officer in the helicopter program for 15 months. I didn’t make it through the program. I had to do pushups and anything else they wanted me to do on the lower right hand edge of a long table every night for two weeks until I was kicked out of the program. I didn’t have the physical strength for it and they were aware of that. As a result, I had to go back to my job as a typist on base. I was ashamed and embarrassed. I thought of getting out of the army in the end of 1945.

It was clear that I wasn’t going to get anywhere in the army as an enlisted man. But I couldn’t get out. I had taken only one typing course in high school for two weeks in my senior year. I couldn’t go to a civilian college because I had no money. Then I figured out that the army wouldn’t let me get out either. They would keep me on until I was 45 years old or they would let me stay with them until I died. I decided to study on my own. They provided me a Typewriting Reference Manual by the American Institute of Business. I had no instruction so I would go to the annex where the briefs for the day were posted on big charts and I would memorize each one. It was often a great battle for me. You can’t imagine how difficult it was to memorize a four line brief. After two weeks, I knew every bit of the manual. I passed the Typewriter Operator’s Certificate. I knew the syllabary, I knew the basic symbols, I knew everything. I had no direction or vision at all. I couldn’t imagine how to make a living, but I had no money anyway. But I had knowledge.

When I finished my tour in Korea, I was an S-3 officer and the army had a big meeting of the pilots. They had no idea what the hell to do with me. I decided to ask for discharge with an honorable discharge but I would need a discharge from an honorable program. They suggested that I do a tour with the army corps in Korea. I would have to stay at Fort Benning for a year to prepare for the tour. I would stay in the program and take ROTC, getting two days of off base liberty each week and living on base. My mother was dying of tuberculosis at the time. I decided that I didn’t want to go overseas. I wanted to take a course in engineering at the university of Missouri. They would take me as a provisional student, for six weeks. If I made it through the six weeks, they would grant me admission with provisional admission status. I took the entrance exam for one hour and twenty two minutes. I passed the exam. They gave me provisional admission. I got down there on 1 August 1952. I didn’t know anything about mechanical engineering. The university tried to get me to change to business but I refused. I had already put out more than enough money to get an engineering degree.

I didn’t speak a word of English. I would read the dictionary page by page until I knew one page. I couldn’t read until I was eight years old. My dad had promised me that I could go to a certain school if I were a good student. I wanted to go to the school so much that I did everything he told me to do to get an education. He told me that I must learn to read. He read to me until I had memorized more than a hundred pages and so I learned to read. I developed a thousand page bank of vocabulary. It took me nearly five years to do that. I had no money for a teacher. I had a dictionary for one hundred and twenty one days. I would study one word for a day, write one sentence and then learn to use the sentence to build more sentences until I knew the word. I took the electrical engineering course offered by the university of Missouri but I took it as a mechanical engineering student. I had mechanical engineering as my major. I went home every weekend to take care of my mother. I managed to get a part time job in the spring. I worked in a wire place in the garage. I would make 75 cents an hour. I would make wire, bent the ends of wire, put them together in bundles, sell the bundles for a penny each, and then make another 75 cents an hour. I was all alone from 5 am to 3 pm on Sunday. After one year, they gave me admission with full admission status.

In 1956, I graduated with a BS degree in engineering. My parents came to see me graduate in spite of being so ill that she couldn’t come to the graduation. That was nearly thirty years ago. I was working in an air force base in Korea then. I had developed hundreds of little factories for the air force. I was responsible for 2000 aircraft but I was a non commissioned officer. The commanding general told me that I had to get an engineering degree. I had to go to civilian school. I took the GRE exam, got out, and went back to school to take my degree and my PhD. I was going to get an MBA degree. I was working as a CEO for a Korean corporation and the CEO at the time of Dow Chemical said that if I went there he would sponsor me for a PhD in engineering.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
This is my life; the warts and all version. A good friend, a wonderful loving woman to my wife, who is my best friend.

Our relationship is 41 years of wonderful and sometimes crappy memories. The most recent crappy memory has really thrown a wrench in my game. I am 68 years old. I grew up on a small farm, sheep and citrus. I am very much a country boy and hated the big city that we lived in.

The big wrench in my game happened when I was diagnosed with Parkinson’s. My wife and I had a move across country planned. We sold our house and planned on traveling and spending time with our kids. That did not happen.

To back up the story a little. There was an election and the person I really wanted to win, won. I never voted in an election before but had to vote on this one.

The person that won did things that I was sure I would not like and for some reason I had a bit of a temper tantrum. That resulted in me being chastised and cut off from the family for a couple of years.

When I complained about having my hands shake, the doctor offered Prozac. I am not going there; I had a bad relationship with an alcohol problem and I am sure Prozac and booze are not a good mix.

They decided to do some serious research and found out that I had Parkinson’s. It is amazing how your personal and family life goes to pieces when you get this news. Especially when your wife is no longer speaking to you because you said bad things about the person who won the election.

Being able to see her “sister” in the last part of her life and seeing how she was treated was very important to both me and my wife.

The drug that we have agreed I need is something to try to quell the tremors and this drug is made for Parkinson’s. Like most drugs it had serious side effects that I did not want to live with. For some reason I could not work up enough sympathy for the family.

This is where the problem happened. For some reason I have no empathy or patience for this and it got the best of me. One day I was getting ready to take some of my vital meds and she came along and I was trying to get out of the way for her. She refused to let me leave until I said something nice to her and was going to slap me. I told her that if she does it is all over.

As my wife was over top of me on the bathroom counter, I tried to get up and “smack” her. The hit did not happen but the fall did. As I fell I kicked up and kicked her knee causing her to scream in pain.

One thing I do not like, I do not like people being in pain and so I picked her up and put her on the bed. She looked at me, and you would think she would say thank you. No way. She is on the bed and screaming. Get this, she is screaming, “Leave me alone you sick bastard, I am going to die anyway and you will be sorry.”

At this point my wife gets mad at me and I have to go and pick her up and put her in the chair. This is crazy she is 92 years old and the healthiest person I know. Someone with Parkinson’s (which I have) would give their life to be in the same shape she is.

Then this happened. I was going to see my daughter and do a last bit of socializing with her. My wife asked me when I was coming home. I said I did not know. I knew in my heart that I did not want to be home so I lied about coming home the same day.

I came home, still liking my daughter, and to talk to her a bit. When I got home, she is standing in the dining room.

“I AM NOT TALKING TO YOU. GO TO YOUR ROOM.” This is in front of a neighbor, my 39 year old son, and my brother in law and sister.

This is not what I wanted to do. I said to her please try to understand. She is going to die anyway and I want to see my daughter. “NO, I AM NOT TALKING TO YOU.”

I get smart and say that it is not much use trying to talk to someone who will not listen. She said, “NO, YOU CAN’T TALK TO ME NOW AND YOU CAN’T HAVE A CAKE EITHER.” She has been doing this a lot lately.

This is nuts she is 92 and I am 68. We have done this dance for the last 40 years. Now the latest, we always used to have coffee and cake in the morning, now she only wants cake, not coffee and she wants me to have a coke. She has been doing this a bit more lately.

My point is this. You are screwing up my life. You are taking advantage of my kindness. You are 92 for Christ’s sake and do not seem to be able to see that I am trying to “take back” my life. You may live another 10 years but you only have a few good years left. Then I can take care of you.

If you want to get technical, I had shingles when I was in the hospital and the itchiness that I suffered from was the most horrible thing I had ever felt, and that was more than 1 year after I had it. This makes no sense.

She must know that. I have a good body, no illness, that I know of. My hands are working great. (Thank you CBD and weed). Life is good.

My wife has changed her tune, way to late. My daughter has forgiven me and has even said sorry.

I finally am realizing that the only one I have hurt was me. I am also thinking of going to my doctor for Prozac because I need to get rid of this anger, which is not doing me any good.

I just hope that I do not have to take drugs like my dad and his sister. They were not “great” people. They were funny as hell though.

I guess it is a dilemma because you have lost someone and you need to let go. At the same time, I want to go. I keep my knife and gun handy because if she tells me to go, I have a place that I can go, but I am afraid that I may cut the wrong artery or do something worse. So I find ways to keep the pressure off until she calms down. I don’t think I am a very bad person and I am not an idiot.

I think that the people who are crucifying me are abusing their power. The lawyer has gotten a kick out of putting me in this position and leaving me to stew. How they are sleeping at night I don’t know. I wonder if they were having their mom treated the way they are treating me, what would they say.

I love my wife to death and do not want to see her suffering and so the job goes to me. We made a terrible mistake but that is the family; if something was wrong, you took care of it. That went away after 1956.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I’m a 65 year old, third-generation American, who was born in Sacramento, California and grew up in Mission Viejo, a part of the fast-growing Orange County. My parents, and their parents, all came to America between 1905 and 1920 from Europe. Their family name is Notkin, but my grandfather, through being drafted in the US Army, was renamed by an incompetent sergeant during a census, and ended up as Hiram Cohen. He never explained his change to my parents, but in my second grade class, when I announced to my teacher, I became “Rene Hiram Notkin.” That was my legal name in school records until I was 16. I never changed it because I wanted to honor my grandparents who worked their fingers to the bone to come to America and give their children and grandchildren the life they had always dreamed of.

In 1968, I was a 17 year old graduating from high school and going to the University of Southern California. The college I visited felt as sterile and uptight as it seemed to my parents’ world had been when they were kids, so I came home and worked my way through school at UCLA for three years. Following graduation, I got a job as a commercial artist with a local ad agency. Being an artist was always my dream job, and I was great at it. I began getting some regional, even national, recognition, but in 1972 I was asked by some friend who were well-connected in the industry, to join them as a copywriter in the Los Angeles office of a world renowned agency.

I refused. I had no interest in selling anyone anything, I was still young and more interested in my own art than in the demands of advertising, and I had no interest in what I saw as the superficial, insular and elitist world of advertising. Besides, I had just bought a ‘69 Porsche 912 and I had a big fat boat loan that had to be paid. Life was good.

I had just finished getting my master’s degree in philosophy from UCLA, when my father died of complications from injuries from a car accident. While he was getting better in the hospital, a family friend offered to pay for me to stay in an apartment near the hospital, so I could finish my dissertation and take my comprehensive exams. I was looking for a place to live, and I liked the place I rented, so I moved out of my parents’ house and became a “renter.” It seemed like a great idea at the time, so I asked my parents if they were willing to sell their house. They did so.

It was 1975, and things were good for a while. By 1977, my income was less than 100% of what I needed to pay my bills. Now I’m getting a little vague on the details, so I’ll make them up. In 1977, I had a great career with a local agency. I had great co-workers, we were doing good work, and we had lots of clients who paid us well. But one of my clients wanted to build a national agency and persuaded me to join them, selling one of my co-workers with me. It was going to be great! I’d be a partner. The agency was going to do great! It was going to be great!

My friend who had originally urged me to join them left shortly after I did, so I was basically alone. My partners were no help. They took their money and moved on. We went from doing great work for great clients to doing Mediocre work for average clients. The owners spent more than the profit to try to make the business work. The management was incompetent and the employees were depressed. Our clients left, and we were in trouble.

For my 32nd birthday, in June 1980, I bought a house. I had learned something about houses from my dad. Houses are a way to generate enough income to keep your head above water. Houses can pay for themselves and they can be a long-term investment. A house can have multiple stories, garages, yards and huge gardens in the back.

In a company like the one I was in, you had to believe you were somehow smarter, better or more capable than anyone else in order to demand higher pay. It was even better if you were part owner of a piece of the business so you would get even more. This is called “wage slavery,” and I hated it. Many of the guys I worked with were handsome, funny, charismatic, charming and highly intelligent. They could all have had careers as stand up comedians and agents for male models. Instead, they were depressed 18-hour-a-day workers. Most were probably total workaholics, and all did exactly what they were told. Most had pretty ugly wives, but they didn’t care because they were living in the moment, not planning for the future. They were in their early 30s, but all had hair loss at the temples or on top, so, while they were still attractive, the chinks in their armor were visible to anyone looking closely. I didn’t want to be like any of them, so I decided to make some money, do the boring work, and pay myself first, then pay my house to pay for itself.

And it was a good decision. In September 1982, an old girlfriend came back into my life. She was fresh out of law school and in need of a project. Her mother was a single mom, and her mom’s house was a total wreck. She was hesitant to move back in with her mom because the place was such a mess, but she needed to be nearby to help her. I had two houses of my own, so I said I’d be happy to remodel the home so she could move back in with her mom. I estimated the job at six months and got paid in advance. You know the rest of the story from Chapter Three, and the rest is history.

In 1985, I left my original firm and went to work at another agency where I worked as a copywriter and managed to write a piece that won an award. It was one of only three awards in the history of the client’s magazine. My mother thought it was the most important thing I had ever written. My dad didn’t care. What was important to him was that I make enough money so I could support myself and not have to worry about being poor.

By the time I finished my six month project with my girlfriend’s mother, I had already turned my attention to a new project. After I moved in to the 1890 house, I noticed that the former owners had evidently been busy during their last few years living there. They had filled several hundred boxes full of possessions that they had removed from the basement and attic. I managed to find a really great auction house in a nearby city and sold everything for $7500. I added the money to my other savings, and still had a budget for the house I could stick to.

I was in my late 30s and I was starting to get gray hair, but I looked and acted young. A decade later, I started painting. I started taking classes at the Pasadena Art Center College of Design on a night schedule, then at the Institute of Contemporary Art in downtown LA.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
"I was born in Los Angeles in 1946 and graduated high school in 1964. I have lived in Los Angeles for 56 years except for 8 years in Sacramento and 2 years in Kansas during that period. I am 71 years old.

"I have always been an Anglophile and love learning about British culture, history and daily life. I lived in England 4 times, was a student at the University of Durham for one year, spent summers in college at University College Birmingham and LSE and for 6 years was a student at University College, London. I have been a college professor for 48 years at Los Angeles City College, Stanford University, U.S.C., and University of California, Santa Barbara. I have been an international educator for 51 years, teaching at universities in South Korea, Taiwan, New Zealand, China, Thailand, and Bulgaria.

"In 1995 I decided to complete my MBA degree at the University of New Haven in Connecticut and worked to complete my thesis at UCLA's Anderson School of Business Management in 2005 and 2006. I became fascinated with how email, blackberries and IPad had changed our lives in America and I wanted to understand how the Internet and digital life is changing British lives. When I traveled to England, I could not believe the difference between US and UK business methods, banking, design, etc. I wanted to know more about 'how and why' of these differences. I completed the training in London in 2007 and began researching the United Kingdom for the United States, China and South Korea. "

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
My name is Major General Paul E. Vallely, USA (Ret), I am 65, born and raised on the South side of Chicago, Illinois. My mother died in 1936 at 43 yrs of age, I was 6 yrs old. I left home in my 16th year to work to support my father and brothers. After the war, I spent 33 yrs in the Army and was with Patton in Germany and I had the honor of placing the first American flag on Berlin, Germany at the Brandenburg Gate. I served my country with honor, saw many things that were wonderful and painful. I have been a husband, father and grandfather now for 42 years. God has blessed my family and my career with opportunities many can only dream of. I am who I am because God has seen fit to use my life to make a difference. In my 33 yrs in the Army I fought in 3 wars and earned 7 medals, including 3 Purple Hearts, the Silver Star, Bronze Star and Legion of Merit.

I worked in a manual labor factory most of my early life and was raised in a poor immigrant neighborhood in Chicago, and my family have always been democratic party members. I have worked as an instructor at the U.S. Military Academy at West Point, a Professor at the National War College, Department of Defense Chair at the University of Baltimore, and as a Staff Officer on the White House National Security Council. My tours of duty included Vietnam, Germany, Korea, India, Cuba, Panama, Poland, and Iraq.

I have now come full circle in my life and now am grateful to be fighting for truth and freedom and for the Constitution I swore to protect.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text:
I'm in my 60s and live in New Jersey. I am the son of Italian immigrants and moved to NJ as a child where I lived in a railroad apartment with my two parents. We had very little money, so I looked after my mother's post office business (She sold stamps and had a PO box) and waited on customers. In school I was academically inclined and at age 8 started going to church on a regular basis. I joined the scouts in elementary school and later became the first Eagle Scout in my school. Both my parents were hard working people and stressed the importance of education. After high school, I joined the Army as a volunteer (before the draft was ended) and served in combat in Vietnam during 1968 and 1969. As a result of my service, I was injured in 1969 (Before the first draft calls) and was demoted in rank. I was officially discharged from the Army as a Private (Airborne) in 1970 and was honorably discharged. The Army decided I was unfit for duty after I was wounded.

Some time later, I married and started working in the travel business and had a successful career with Pan Am, raising my family. Later, I went into the insurance business in 1985 and had a successful career there.

Then in 1995, my son was diagnosed with a degenerative brain disorder and I founded a Foundation to raise awareness and support for other families.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am in my 60s, and I have been fortunate to travel across three continents. The experiences I’ve had have shaped me profoundly, and are made even richer by knowing my roots extend back into antiquity. As I write this, I’m sitting in a small cottage overlooking a lush field in the southern French village of Bédarieux. The nightly sound of a 400-year-old family business (imagine the story that place could tell), a gurgling stream below, my friendly neighbors (we’ve agreed to become blood brothers), and the feeling of being immersed in a warm community are only a few of the very pleasant surprises of my stay here. Today I depart for Rome, a journey that was initially only a fantasy but is now reality.

At the risk of sounding like the product of a Gene Rodenberry fiction-writing workshop, if one could travel through time and leave the traveler free to change history, it would be with the intent to make a lasting difference by being present at the moment when a new idea was hatched, or was the first to recognize and take advantage of a new discovery. For me, this reality belongs to technology.

I am an engineer by training, and an IT professional who specialized in architecture and process design before my career drifted into the management and strategy side of the industry. If I could go back and touch one moment in history, it would be to rewrite the Gutenberg Bible using modern technology and methods. “Why would you do that, and what’s the point?” you might ask. There are a few, but the most important reasons are the same as my first couple of paragraphs. It’s the fact that this singular event, the printing press, represents the first opportunity for humans to make a permanent, lasting impression on those that would follow. What began as a way to mass-produce the Word of God gave rise to the very information society of which I am part. By harnessing this power, in partnership with modern tools like computer-based systems, networked high-speed printers and modern papermaking, we have the chance to make the mark we’ll leave on history ever lasting. And, we can use technology to ensure that the “lessons” of the past that have been crafted by humanity’s myths and legends are available to everyone.

What do I do with my time? This is always a tough question to answer, mostly because time is the most precious resource we have. For the past three decades, I have worked for a high-tech company that published software. I can offer my 1000-word answer, but the topic is more complicated than that. In short, I consider myself an eternal learner. My public-sector experience as a staff member for a telecommunications industry association sparked my interest in community-building and nonprofit administration. I’ve spent many years working for non-profits that study, document and restore antique and outmoded technologies. My efforts on behalf of these organizations have included managing event planners, installing and maintaining networks and computer systems, publishing newsletters, contributing to publications, raising funds, and speaking to interested audiences.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text: I am an 80 year-old retired politician. I live in Washington DC, but I was raised in California during the Great Depression of the 1930s. I went to college in the Bay Area at Berkeley where I got a Bachelor's Degree. I began as a Democrat but I switched to being a Republican when I saw how ineffective they were on policy. When I was younger, I played football with my friends, worked construction for a while until I got hurt, and thought I wanted to be a lawyer but then changed my mind because I saw how the courts are corrupt. My biggest problem was the death of my father when I was 28. My mom died two years later and that left my brother and me with just my step-mother who hated us. She was a real mean, hurtful person. She blamed us for being poor, poor enough that I couldn't go to college without taking out loans. On the other hand, the role of my wife and daughter are huge for me. My wife really transformed my life and helped me along in politics. My daughter was my reason for becoming president. She was killed in a terrorist attack by Al-Qaeda.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy?
text:
I am an 81 year old retired British subject. My mother was born in California. She lived with her grandmother till her parents came back from the First World War. I was in the college cadets during the Second World War. After leaving school, I joined the Air Force. After the war, I stayed in the British Overseas Air Force Service. I married a Welsh lady and we had two children. After retirement, I worked at a bank. The time at the bank was hard and boring.

I used to play cricket. My heroes were R. R. Bowden, C. K. Nayudu, Keith Miller and David warner. My favorite sports are Cricket, Swimming and Yachting. I love swimming to stay fit. I have been sailing to make sure my wife knows she's mine and I'm hers. I am passionate about justice. I believe that justice and fair play for everyone is very important. I enjoy talking to people, especially talking to the old and their life. I am passionate about understanding India’s colonial history. Justice and fair play is what I want. For that, I also want liberty for the people of Indian Sub-continent so they can live freely and happily.

question: Tell me about yourself. How old are you? Where did you grow up? What events made you who you are? What matters to you and what do you enjoy? Please describe in detail.
text: I am a 68 year old male in overall good health. I was born in April 1952 and grew up in the Detroit area. I joined the US Air Force at age 19 and in 1972 left the Air Force after four years. My tour of duty took me to Korea and Japan and to Washington D.C. where I worked on the space shuttle launch schedule for four years. Afterwards I returned to Michigan to attend college. Over the next 40 years I worked in many jobs. Some jobs were as service writer at car dealerships. At other times I was trained in various areas of the automotive field to become a GM diagnostic technician and later a GM training instructor. I also did some tutoring for middle schoolers, trained those interested in being a machinist, worked as a service manager and in the shipping room at a hydraulic hose manufacturing company, became certified to weld, worked in plumbing and also in an MRI department. I took a break to pursue a career in music; I formed a metal band that featured a female vocalist in 1986 and we performed for a time at local clubs and at a few festivals. Although I can’t use it as a source of income anymore, I still love music and I love to play the guitar. I am a fan of science fiction, astronomy and science and I am a avid chess player. I read about 100 books a year; novels, nonfiction, magazines, comics, and I am a big fan of Marvel and DC Comics. I also enjoy camping, fishing, hiking, all nature, and I am a good gardener and proficient at math, electronics, and automotive work.
